topics.title: Definitions - Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, **Perception** is formally defined as the computational process by which an autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic system acquires, interprets, organises, and structures raw sensor measurements from its environment to construct a meaningful internal representation that can be used for subsequent navigation tasks such as localisation, mapping, path planning, and obstacle avoidance.

$$\text{Perception} = f_{\text{perceive}}\Big(\,S(t),\,C_{robot},\,C_{env},\,T\,\Big)$$

where:
- $S(t)$ = set of sensor measurements at time $t$
- $C_{robot}$ = robot's internal state (pose, velocity, intrinsic parameters)
- $C_{env}$ = environmental context (known/unknown, static/dynamic)
- $T$ = task context and prior knowledge
- $f_{\text{perceive}}$ = the perception function (signal processing + AI/ML inference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Perception is the **first and most fundamental stage** of the navigation pipeline. Without a reliable perception layer, downstream tasks (localisation, SLAM, planning) become mathematically ill-posed.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of perception in robotics as the **equivalent of human senses and brain interpretation combined**. 

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a room** and asked to walk to the door safely:
- You use your **ears** (microphones / ultrasonic sensors) to detect echoes.
- You use your **fingertips** on the wall (tactile / contact sensors) to feel the door.
- You use **memory and reasoning** to build a mental picture of where the door is.

A robot does the same thing, but with:
- **Cameras** (vision sensors) ŌåÆ like eyes
- **LIDAR / RADAR / Sonar** ŌåÆ like echo-location (bats/dolphins)
- **IMU, GPS, Wheel Encoders** ŌåÆ like inner ear / proprioception
- **AI/CV algorithms** ŌåÆ like the brain's interpretation cortex

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Perception is essentially *"What is the robot sensing, and what does that data mean?"* ŌĆö it is the bridge between the **physical world** and the **robot's digital understanding** of that world.

---

### 1.3 Sub-Components of Robot Perception

A perception system is not a single algorithm; it is a **stack of sub-processes**:

1. **Sensing (Data Acquisition):** Raw data capture using proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors.
2. **Pre-processing:** Noise filtering, calibration, de-skewing, and synchronisation.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Identifying salient primitives (edges, corners, keypoints, planes).
4. **Interpretation / Inference:** Classifying, segmenting, and tracking objects or landmarks.
5. **Representation:** Storing the interpreted data in a structured form (occupancy grids, point clouds, semantic maps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** *Sensing* (data acquisition) $\neq$ *Perception* (full interpretation). Perception **includes** sensing but goes further to extract meaning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Perception Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

The robot perception pipeline can be broken down into a structured sequence:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Sensor Triggering & Sampling:**
  The robot's onboard sensors (e.g., a 32-beam LiDAR rotating at 10 Hz) collect raw measurements at discrete time intervals. Each scan yields a vector $\mathbf{z}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ where $n$ is the number of measurement channels.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Synchronisation & Time-Stamping:**
  Data from different sensors are aligned to a common clock using techniques like **PTP (Precision Time Protocol)** or hardware triggering. This is critical because sensors run at different rates (camera at 30 fps, IMU at 200 Hz).

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Calibration:**
  Each sensor has intrinsic parameters (focal length, lens distortion) and extrinsic parameters (position and orientation relative to the robot's body frame). Calibration transforms raw data into a common coordinate frame.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Pre-processing:**
  Outlier removal (RANSAC, statistical filtering), de-noising (Kalman / moving average filters), and motion compensation (de-skewing for moving platforms).

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Feature Detection:**
  Identifying landmarks ŌĆö for vision, corners (Harris, FAST), blobs (SIFT, ORB); for LiDAR, planes, edges, and corners in point clouds.

- **Step 6 ŌĆö Data Association & Tracking:**
  Matching current observations to previously seen landmarks (e.g., using the **Hungarian algorithm** or **ICP ŌĆö Iterative Closest Point**).

- **Step 7 ŌĆö Semantic Interpretation:**
  Higher-level reasoning ŌĆö object detection (YOLO, Mask R-CNN), semantic segmentation, scene understanding using deep neural networks.

- **Step 8 ŌĆö World Model Update:**
  The interpreted perception output is fused into the robot's world model (map, belief state, semantic memory).

---

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The 'Why')

| Pipeline Step | Engineering 'Why' |
|---|---|
| Synchronisation | Misaligned sensor data leads to ghosting and incorrect fusion |
| Calibration | Without it, sensor data is geometrically meaningless |
| Pre-processing | Raw sensor data is **noisy** ŌĆö direct use causes estimation divergence |
| Feature Detection | Reduces high-dimensional raw data to a compact, descriptive set |
| Data Association | Critical for SLAM ŌĆö wrong associations corrupt the entire map |
| Semantic Interpretation | Enables **task-level** intelligence, not just geometric navigation |
| World Model Update | Closes the perception-action loop for reactive behaviour |

---

### 2.3 Classification of Sensors Used in Perception

- **Exteroceptive Sensors** (sense the external world): Cameras (mono/stereo/RGB-D), LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic, Infrared.
- **Proprioceptive Sensors** (sense internal state): IMU, wheel odometry, joint encoders, gyroscopes.
- **Passive Sensors** (do not emit energy): Cameras, passive infrared.
- **Active Sensors** (emit energy and measure response): LiDAR, Radar, Sonar, structured light.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are foundational and frequently appear in KTU examinations for this module.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| LiDAR Range Resolution | $\Delta r = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $B$ = signal bandwidth (Hz) | Smaller $\Delta r \Rightarrow$ finer range resolution |
| Camera Field of View (vertical) | $\text{FOV}_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$ | $h$ = sensor height, $f$ = focal length | Both in consistent units (e.g., mm) |
| Pinhole Projection (image coords) | $(u, v) = \left(\dfrac{f X}{Z} + c_x,\; \dfrac{f Y}{Z} + c_y\right)$ | $(X, Y, Z)$ = world point, $(c_x, c_y)$ = principal point | Standard camera model |
| Stereo Depth | $Z = \dfrac{f B}{d}$ | $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity (pixels) | Smaller disparity $\Rightarrow$ farther object |
| Angular Resolution of LiDAR | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | $N$ = number of beams/channels | Lower $\Delta\theta \Rightarrow$ denser scan |
| Uncertainty Propagation (1D) | $\sigma_z^2 = \left(\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)^2 \sigma_x^2$ | First-order Taylor expansion | Used in EKF-based perception |
| Sensor Range (Radar/LiDAR, max) | $R_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B \cdot \text{SNR}_{\min}}\right)^{\!1/4}$ | Radar equation parameters | Reference only for advanced problems |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Perception systems are the **backbone of every modern autonomous platform**:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., self-driving cars):** Fuse camera + LiDAR + Radar to perceive vehicles, pedestrians, lanes, and traffic signs.
- **Warehouse Robots (AMRs):** Use 2D LiDAR + depth cameras to navigate aisles and avoid workers.
- **Aerial Drones (UAVs):** Stereo vision + downward LiDAR for obstacle avoidance and terrain mapping.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Sonar-based perception in low-visibility underwater conditions.
- **Domestic Service Robots:** RGB-D cameras for object recognition and room segmentation.
- **Space Exploration Rovers (e.g., Mars rovers):** Stereo vision + IMU for terrain traversability analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exam-essential** for the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 paper. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly with explanatory text rows.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Field of View (FOV) of a Camera

**Problem Statement:** A pinhole camera has a sensor of vertical height $h$ and a lens of focal length $f$. Derive the vertical field of view $\text{FOV}_v$.

**Given:** Pinhole geometry with image plane at distance $f$ from the optical centre.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometric Setup:**
The image plane intercepts the projection cone. The half-angle $\theta$ subtended at the optical centre by the edge of the sensor satisfies a right-triangle relation.

$$\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{h/2}{f}$$

**Text Row 1:** This follows from the definition of tangent in a right triangle where the opposite side is the half-sensor height and the adjacent side is the focal length.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for Half-Angle:**

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$$

**Text Row 2:** We apply the inverse tangent function to isolate the angle.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total Vertical FOV (full angle):**

$$\text{FOV}_v = 2\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$$

**Text Row 3:** The total field of view is twice the half-angle because the geometry is symmetric about the optical axis.

**Result:** The derived equation gives the camera's vertical angular coverage in radians. For example, with $h = 24\,\text{mm}$ (35 mm sensor) and $f = 50\,\text{mm}$:

$$\text{FOV}_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{24}{2 \times 50}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.24) \approx 27.0^{\circ}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A wider FOV captures more of the scene but introduces more radial distortion. Wide-angle cameras (small $f$) help robots perceive peripheral obstacles, critical for navigation in cluttered environments.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stereo Vision Depth Estimation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the depth equation for a stereo camera system with baseline $B$, focal length $f$, and disparity $d$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Similar Triangles Setup:**
For a 3D point $P$ at depth $Z$ from the left camera, the projections onto the left and right image planes are shifted by a pixel offset called **disparity** $d$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Disparity Definition:**

$$d = u_L - u_R = \dfrac{f B}{Z}$$

**Text Row:** Using similar triangles between the two cameras and the 3D point, the difference in image coordinates equals the focal length times the baseline divided by depth.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rearrange for Depth:**

$$Z = \dfrac{f B}{d}$$

**Text Row:** Cross-multiplying and isolating $Z$ gives the depth as inversely proportional to disparity.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Uncertainty in Depth:**

$$\sigma_Z = \dfrac{f B}{d^2}\,\sigma_d = \dfrac{Z^2}{f B}\,\sigma_d$$

**Text Row:** Differentiating $Z$ with respect to $d$ gives $\partial Z/\partial d = -fB/d^2$. Taking magnitudes and applying first-order uncertainty propagation, the depth uncertainty grows quadratically with distance.

**Engineering Insight:** This is why **far-field depth estimation** from stereo vision is unreliable. Long-range perception in robots often requires LiDAR or Radar instead of vision-only stereo.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensor Noise Model (Gaussian Additive Noise)

**Problem Statement:** Model a typical sensor measurement $z$ as a true value $z^*$ corrupted by zero-mean Gaussian noise.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sensor Model Definition:**

$$z = z^* + \nu, \quad \nu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

**Text Row:** The measurement equals the ground-truth value plus additive Gaussian noise with covariance $R$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Probability Density Function:**

$$p(z \mid z^*) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert R\vert}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}(z - z^*)^{\top} R^{-1} (z - z^*)\right)$$

**Text Row:** This is the multivariate Gaussian likelihood of the measurement given the true state, where $n$ is the dimensionality of $z$ and $\vert R\vert$ is the determinant of the noise covariance matrix.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum Likelihood Estimate:**

$$\hat{z}^* = \arg\min_{z^*} \left[(z - z^*)^{\top} R^{-1} (z - z^*)\right]$$

**Text Row:** The MLE minimises the Mahalanobis distance between measurement and estimate, weighted by the inverse noise covariance.

**Why this matters:** Almost every perception algorithm (Kalman filter, EKF-SLAM, particle filters) assumes this noise model. In exams, students must explicitly state the Gaussian assumption.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sensor Modalities

| Sensor | Range (typical) | Resolution | Works in Dark? | Works in Fog? | Cost | Common Robot Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (Mono) | 0.5ŌĆō100 m | High (pixels) | No (needs light) | Poor | Low | Lane/road detection, classification |
| Stereo Camera | 0.5ŌĆō30 m | High (pixels) + depth | No | Poor | Medium | Short-range depth, indoor nav |
| RGB-D (e.g., Kinect) | 0.3ŌĆō5 m | Medium | No (IR-based) | Poor | Low | Indoor SLAM, grasping |
| 2D LiDAR | 0.1ŌĆō40 m | 0.25ŌĆō1┬░ | Yes | Fair | Medium | Obstacle avoidance, mapping |
| 3D LiDAR | 0.5ŌĆō200 m | 0.1ŌĆō0.4┬░ | Yes | Good | High | Autonomous driving, 3D mapping |
| Radar | 0.5ŌĆō250 m | Low (angular) | Yes | Excellent | Medium | Vehicle speed, weather robustness |
| Ultrasonic | 0.02ŌĆō10 m | Very low | Yes | Poor | Very low | Proximity, indoor robots |
| IMU | N/A (internal) | High rate | Yes | Yes | Low | Pose estimation, dead reckoning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Robot Perception Pipeline ŌĆö Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete perception pipeline from raw sensor input to world model update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SensorArray] --> B[TimeSynchronisation]
    B --> C[CalibrationModule]
    C --> D[PreProcessing]
    D --> E[FeatureExtraction]
    E --> F[DataAssociation]
    F --> G[SemanticInterpretation]
    G --> H[WorldModelUpdate]
    H --> I[NavigationDecisionLayer]

    subgraph SensorCluster
        A1[CameraRGB]
        A2[LiDAR3D]
        A3[IMUUnit]
        A4[WheelOdometry]
    end

    SensorCluster --> A

    D --> D1[NoiseFiltering]
    D --> D2[MotionCompensation]
    D --> D3[OutlierRemoval]

    E --> E1[EdgeDetection]
    E --> E2[CornerDetection]
    E --> E3[PlaneSegmentation]

    G --> G1[ObjectDetection]
    G --> G2[SceneClassification]
    G --> G3[SemanticSegmentation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left column (A) represents raw sensor ingestion. Each downstream block refines the data progressively until the world model (H) is updated, which then informs the navigation decision layer (I).

---

### 4.2 Sensor-Modal-to-Perception-Task Mapping

This diagram maps which sensor modalities are typically used for which perception tasks in robot navigation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[ObstacleDetection] --> L1[LiDAR2D]
    T1 --> L2[Ultrasonic]
    T1 --> L3[StereoVision]

    T2[ObjectRecognition] --> M1[RGBCamera]
    T2 --> M2[DeepNeuralNetwork]

    T3[SelfLocalisation] --> N1[GPSReceiver]
    T3 --> N2[IMUUnit]
    T3 --> N3[WheelEncoder]

    T4[3DSceneReconstruction] --> S1[LiDAR3D]
    T4 --> S2[RGBDCamera]
    T4 --> S3[StereoCamera]

    T5[HighSpeedTracking] --> R1[RadarSensor]
    T5 --> R2[EventCamera]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU theory questions, drawing such a sensor-to-task mapping earns full methodology marks even without deep numerical content.

---

### 4.3 Sensor Coverage Geometry (Camera vs LiDAR)

The following conceptual diagram compares the geometric coverage of a monocular camera (angular cone) versus a 2D LiDAR (planar sweep).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Robot[RobotOrigin] --> C1[CameraFOVCone]
    Robot --> L1[LiDARPlanarSweep]
    C1 --> P1[ObjectA]
    C1 --> P2[ObjectB]
    L1 --> P3[ObjectC]
    L1 --> P4[ObjectD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The camera captures **rich semantic data** in a cone, while the LiDAR captures **geometrically accurate but semantically poor** data in a plane. **Sensor fusion** combines the strengths of both.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define perception in the context of robot navigation. List any four exteroceptive sensors used in mobile robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Perception in robot navigation is the process by which a robot acquires sensor data from its environment and interprets that data to form an internal representation suitable for localisation, mapping, and path planning. It bridges raw physical measurements and the robot's digital decision-making layer.

**Four exteroceptive sensors (1 mark ŌĆö ┬╝ each):**
1. **Camera** (mono/stereo/RGB-D)
2. **LiDAR** (2D or 3D)
3. **Radar**
4. **Ultrasonic / Sonar sensor**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sensors that measure the robot's **internal** state | Sensors that measure the **external** environment |
| **Example 1** | Wheel encoder (measures wheel rotation) | Camera (measures ambient light/scene) |
| **Example 2** | IMU / Gyroscope (measures orientation, angular velocity) | LiDAR (measures distance to surroundings) |
| **Dependency on environment** | Independent of surroundings | Directly dependent on surroundings |

(1 mark for definition distinction, 1 mark for correct examples of each, 1 mark for valid extra comparison ŌĆö any one valid differentiator such as "depends on env vs independent" suffices for the third mark.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the robot perception pipeline. List the major stages and describe the role of **feature extraction** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stereo vision system has a baseline $B = 12\,\text{cm}$ and focal length $f = 800\,\text{pixels}$. For an object observed with a disparity $d = 40\,\text{pixels}$, calculate:
  (i) The depth of the object.
  (ii) The depth uncertainty if the disparity measurement has $\sigma_d = 0.5\,\text{pixels}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Robot Perception Pipeline (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Sensor Array] ŌåÆ [Synchronisation] ŌåÆ [Calibration] ŌåÆ [Pre-processing]
        ŌåÆ [Feature Extraction] ŌåÆ [Data Association] ŌåÆ [Semantic Interpretation]
        ŌåÆ [World Model Update] ŌåÆ [Navigation Decision]
```

**Stages Description (2 marks):** Each stage must be explained briefly. Sensor array captures raw data; synchronisation aligns timestamps; calibration maps data to a common frame; pre-processing removes noise; feature extraction identifies salient points; data association matches features across time; semantic interpretation adds meaning; world model update integrates new data.

**Feature Extraction in Detail (2 marks):** Feature extraction reduces the high-dimensional raw sensor stream to a compact, descriptive set of salient primitives. For vision: corner detectors (Harris, FAST), blob detectors (SIFT, ORB), or edge detectors (Canny). For LiDAR: plane segmentation, edge detection, and keypoint extraction (e.g., FPFH descriptors). The goals are (i) dimensionality reduction, (ii) invariance to nuisance variations (rotation, illumination), and (iii) distinctiveness for reliable data association.

---

**(b) Stereo Depth Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Depth Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:** $B = 12\,\text{cm} = 0.12\,\text{m}$, $f = 800\,\text{pixels}$, $d = 40\,\text{pixels}$.

**Formula:** $Z = \dfrac{f B}{d}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute values:**

$$Z = \dfrac{800 \times 0.12}{40}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute numerator:**

$$Z = \dfrac{96}{40}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final result:**

$$Z = 2.4\,\text{m}$$

**Answer (1 mark):** The object is at a depth of **$Z = 2.4$ metres** from the camera.

---

**(ii) Depth Uncertainty (4 marks):**

**Formula:** $\sigma_Z = \dfrac{f B}{d^2}\,\sigma_d = \dfrac{Z^2}{f B}\,\sigma_d$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute values directly:**

$$\sigma_Z = \dfrac{800 \times 0.12}{40^2} \times 0.5$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute denominator:**

$$\sigma_Z = \dfrac{96}{1600} \times 0.5$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö First product:**

$$\sigma_Z = 0.06 \times 0.5$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final result:**

$$\sigma_Z = 0.03\,\text{m} = 3\,\text{cm}$$

**Answer (1 mark):** The depth uncertainty is **$\sigma_Z = 0.03\,\text{m}$ (3 cm)**.

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** This level of uncertainty is acceptable for indoor navigation but insufficient for high-speed autonomous driving, where sub-centimetre depth accuracy is required at long ranges.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jul 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the perception capabilities of **camera, LiDAR, and radar** for autonomous navigation. Use a comparison matrix and justify the need for **sensor fusion**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the vertical field of view equation for a pinhole camera given sensor height $h$ and focal length $f$. A smartphone camera has $f = 4\,\text{mm}$ and a sensor of height $h = 3.6\,\text{mm}$. Compute the vertical FOV in degrees.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sensor Comparison and Fusion (7 marks):**

**Comparison Matrix (3 marks):**

| Property | Camera | LiDAR | Radar |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active/Passive** | Passive | Active | Active |
| **Works in darkness** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Works in fog/rain** | Poor | Fair | Excellent |
| **Angular resolution** | High | MediumŌĆōHigh | Low |
| **Range** | 0.5ŌĆō100 m | 0.5ŌĆō200 m | 0.5ŌĆō250 m |
| **Provides colour/texture** | Yes | No | No |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Semantic info** | Rich | Poor | Poor |

**Justification for Sensor Fusion (4 marks):** No single sensor can satisfy all perception requirements for autonomous navigation.
- **Cameras** provide rich semantic data (traffic signs, lane markings) but fail in poor lighting and adverse weather.
- **LiDAR** provides accurate 3D geometry but is expensive, struggles in fog, and lacks semantic information.
- **Radar** provides excellent range and weather robustness but has poor angular resolution and cannot classify objects.

**Sensor fusion** combines the strengths of all three: LiDAR for accurate geometry, camera for semantic interpretation, and radar for long-range and adverse-weather detection. Fusion algorithms (Kalman filter, particle filter, deep learning-based fusion) produce a perception output that is more robust, accurate, and complete than any single sensor alone. This is the foundation of modern autonomous vehicle perception stacks (e.g., Waymo, Tesla, Mobileye).

---

**(b) Pinhole FOV Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry:** Half of the sensor height is $h/2$. From the pinhole optical centre, this edge subtends an angle $\theta$ with the optical axis, where:

$$\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{h/2}{f}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for half-angle:**

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total vertical FOV (symmetric geometry):**

$$\text{FOV}_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 4\,\text{mm}$, $h = 3.6\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute:**

$$\text{FOV}_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{3.6}{2 \times 4}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simplify fraction:**

$$\text{FOV}_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{3.6}{8}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.45)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate (in radians):**

$$\text{FOV}_v = 2 \times 0.4229 = 0.8458\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to degrees:**

$$\text{FOV}_v = 0.8458 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 48.5^{\circ}$$

**Answer (1 mark):** The vertical field of view is approximately **$48.5^{\circ}$**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing sensing with perception** ŌĆö students often define perception as "the robot senses its environment." This is **wrong**. Sensing is only data capture; perception includes interpretation. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting units in stereo depth calculations** ŌĆö the baseline must be in the **same unit system** as the desired output. Mixing cm with m is the #1 numerical error. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Not showing the derivation steps** ŌĆö writing the formula and the answer without intermediate steps forfeits 2ŌĆō3 marks in 14-mark questions. Always show substitution and simplification.
> 4. **Confusing disparity sign conventions** ŌĆö in some textbooks, $d = u_L - u_R$ (positive); in others, $d = u_R - u_L$. Be consistent.
> 5. **Omitting the Gaussian noise assumption** in sensor models ŌĆö explicitly stating the noise distribution is a marking criterion in filter-based questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Definitions ŌĆö Perception**

- **Perception** = Sense + Interpret + Represent (not just sensing).
- Perception is the **first stage** of the navigation pipeline: Perception ŌåÆ Localisation ŌåÆ Mapping ŌåÆ Planning ŌåÆ Control.
- **Exteroceptive sensors** perceive the **external world**; **proprioceptive sensors** perceive the **robot's internal state**.
- **Active sensors** (LiDAR, Radar, Sonar) emit energy; **Passive sensors** (Camera, IR) do not.
- **Pinhole camera model** maps 3D world points to 2D image points via $u = fX/Z + c_x$.
- **Stereo depth** $Z = fB/d$ ŌĆö depth is **inversely proportional to disparity**.
- **Depth uncertainty** grows as the **square of distance** ŌĆö far-field stereo is unreliable.
- **FOV equation:** $\text{FOV}_v = 2\arctan(h/2f)$ ŌĆö wide FOV comes from small focal length.
- **LiDAR** gives accurate geometry; **Camera** gives semantics; **Radar** gives weather robustness ŌĆö **fusion is essential**.
- **Sensor noise** is typically modelled as zero-mean Gaussian: $z = z^* + \nu$, $\nu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.
- **Feature extraction** reduces high-dimensional data to compact, distinctive descriptors (Harris, SIFT, ORB, FPFH).
- **Data association** is the process of matching current observations to previously seen landmarks.
- The **synchronisation, calibration, and pre-processing** stages are non-negotiable for any robust perception stack.
- **Exams love:** derivations of FOV, stereo depth, and uncertainty propagation ŌĆö practice all three.
- **Always state units** in the final numerical answer; examiners deduct marks for unitless results.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose ŌĆö position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$ ŌĆö within a known or partially known reference frame, using sensor measurements, control commands, and prior knowledge of the environment (map). Formally, it is the recursive estimation of the robot's belief state $bel(x_t)$ over the space of all possible poses $x_t \in SE(2)$ at time $t$.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, localization is classified under **Probabilistic Robotics** and is positioned as the foundational perceptual layer of the navigation stack (Sense ŌåÆ Localize ŌåÆ Plan ŌåÆ Act). Mathematically, the posterior distribution is given by Bayes' theorem:

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $z_{1:t}$ is the history of sensor measurements and $u_{1:t}$ is the history of control inputs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Lost Tourist in a Museum"

Imagine walking into a massive, unfamiliar museum with a paper map in your hand:

1. You **start** by assuming you might be anywhere ŌåÆ this is *prior belief* (uniform/global).
2. You **look around**, spot a famous painting, and cross-reference it with your map ŌåÆ this is **sensor update** ($P(z_t \mid x_t)$).
3. You **walk ten steps** forward and *predict* where you should now be ŌåÆ this is the **motion update** ($P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$).
4. You repeat steps 2 and 3 continuously.

That iterative "guess ŌåÆ verify ŌåÆ refine" loop **IS** localization. The robot is doing exactly this, but mathematically and probabilistically rather than by intuition.

## 1.3 Classification of Localization Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Localization is classified along three orthogonal axes ŌĆö *initial knowledge*, *sensor richness*, and *environment structure*.

| Localization Variant | Initial Pose Knowledge | Typical Algorithm Family |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Known (small uncertainty) | Kalman Filter (KF/EKF) |
| **Global Localization** | Unknown | Markov Localization, MCL |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Unknown, can be teleported mid-run | Monte Carlo Localization + recovery |

## 1.4 Core Terminology ŌĆö Lock These Terms

- **Pose** $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$ ŌĆö robot's configuration in 2D plane.
- **Odometry** ŌĆö incremental estimate of motion from wheel encoders.
- **Landmark** ŌĆö a distinguishable, persistent feature in the environment.
- **Belief** ŌĆö the robot's internal probability distribution over poses.
- **SE(2)** ŌĆö Special Euclidean Group of 2D rigid transformations.
- **Data Association** ŌĆö matching observed landmarks to stored map entries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Localization Framework

The robot's belief evolves through two alternating Bayesian operations:

### Step 1 ŌĆö Prediction (Motion Update)
Uses the **control input** $u_t$ and the prior belief to predict the new pose distribution **before** observing any new sensor data:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Correction (Measurement Update)
Incorporates the latest sensor observation $z_t$ using the sensor model $P(z_t \mid x_t)$:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

where $\eta$ is the **normalizer**:

$$
\eta = \left[ \int P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t \right]^{-1}
$$

## 2.2 Why Probabilistic? The "Why" Behind the Math

Sensors are **noisy**, actuators **slip**, and environments are **ambiguous**. A single deterministic pose estimate is therefore brittle ŌĆö the robot must carry a *distribution* of possibilities and update it rationally.

## 2.3 Two Main Algorithmic Families

### (a) Gaussian / Parametric Methods
- Assume belief is a **unimodal Gaussian**: $bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- Represented compactly by mean vector and covariance matrix.
- **Kalman Filter (KF)** ŌĆö linear Gaussian systems.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** ŌĆö linearized via Jacobians for nonlinear models.
- **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** ŌĆö uses sigma points, no Jacobians required.

### (b) Non-Parametric Methods
- Represent belief as a **set of samples** (particles) drawn from the posterior.
- **Histogram Filter** ŌĆö discrete grid over pose space.
- **Particle Filter / Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** ŌĆö most widely used, handles multimodal beliefs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayes' measurement update | All probabilistic localization |
| 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov prediction | Motion model integration |
| 3 | $x_t = x_{t-1} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \Delta\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Odometry motion model | Wheel-encoder based prediction |
| 4 | $K_t = \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} \, H^\top \, (H \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^\top + Q)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | EKF correction |
| 5 | $\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t))$ | Mean update | EKF localization |
| 6 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H) \Sigma_{t \vert t-1}$ | Covariance update | EKF localization |
| 7 | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle weight | MCL correction |
| 8 | $x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ | Particle sampling | MCL prediction |
| 9 | $N_{\text{eff}} = \left( \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2 \right)^{-1}$ | Effective sample size | Resampling trigger |
| 10 | $P(z_t \mid x_t) = \prod_k P(z_t^{(k)} \mid x_t)$ | Beam independence assumption | Range-finder sensor model |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Localization is the **backbone** of every real-world autonomous system: AGVs in warehouses (Amazon Kiva), autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla), UAV swarms (drone light shows), underwater AUVs mapping the ocean floor, and even planetary rovers (NASA's Perseverance on Mars, 2021).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Odometry-Based Motion Model

We start with the most common kinematic model. The robot reports two odometry readings ŌĆö initial and final ŌĆö over a control interval $\Delta t$:

$$
u_t = (\overline{x}_{t-1}, \overline{x}_t)^\top
$$

Relative motion components are extracted as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{rot1}} &= \text{atan2}(\overline{y}_t - \overline{y}_{t-1},\ \overline{x}_t - \overline{x}_{t-1}) - \overline{\theta}_{t-1} \\[4pt]
\delta_{\text{trans}} &= \sqrt{(\overline{x}_t - \overline{x}_{t-1})^2 + (\overline{y}_t - \overline{y}_{t-1})^2} \\[4pt]
\delta_{\text{rot2}} &= \overline{\theta}_t - \overline{\theta}_{t-1} - \delta_{\text{rot1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

These three components are then perturbed by Gaussian noise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}} &= \delta_{\text{rot1}} + \mathcal{N}(0,\ \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2) \\[4pt]
\hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} &= \delta_{\text{trans}} + \mathcal{N}(0,\ \alpha_3 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 + \alpha_4 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_4 \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2) \\[4pt]
\hat{\delta}_{\text{rot2}} &= \delta_{\text{rot2}} + \mathcal{N}(0,\ \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The predicted pose becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + \hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}}) \\[4pt]
y_t &= y_{t-1} + \hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}}) \\[4pt]
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}} + \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The motion model decomposes a curved arc into *rotate ŌåÆ translate ŌåÆ rotate*, applies independent Gaussian noise to each stage, and re-assembles the perturbed motion. This captures real-world wheel slip and encoder quantization error.

## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Update

**Given:** predicted state $\overline{\mu}_t$, predicted covariance $\overline{\Sigma}_t$, observation $z_t$, observation model $z = h(x) + \text{noise}$, observation noise covariance $Q_t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Jacobian** of the observation model at the predicted mean:

$$
H_t = \left. \frac{\partial h(x)}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \overline{\mu}_t}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^\top \, (H_t \, \overline{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

> *Why this works:* $K_t$ weighs the *innovation* $(z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t))$ according to the relative uncertainty of prediction vs. measurement. If the sensor is very accurate ($Q_t$ small), $K_t \to I$; if prediction is very accurate ($\overline{\Sigma}_t$ small), $K_t \to 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Update mean:**

$$
\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t \, (z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t))
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Update covariance:**

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \, \overline{\Sigma}_t
$$

## 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)

MCL is the workhorse algorithm for global localization. It has four key stages:

### Stage 1 ŌĆö Sampling
Each particle $x_t^{[i]}$ is propagated through the motion model:

$$
x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})
$$

### Stage 2 ŌĆö Importance Weighting
Particles are weighted by how well they explain the latest observation:

$$
w_t^{[i]} = P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) = \prod_k P(z_t^{(k)} \mid x_t^{[i]})
$$

> The product runs over all sensor beams (assuming conditional independence of beams given pose).

### Stage 3 ŌĆö Normalization

$$
w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_j w_t^{[j]}}
$$

### Stage 4 ŌĆö Resampling
Draw $M$ new particles from the current set with replacement, with probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$. This is triggered when the **effective sample size** drops below a threshold:

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2} < N_{\text{threshold}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Particles that "match" observations survive and multiply; particles that disagree die out. Over time, the swarm concentrates around the true pose ŌĆö provided the robot is not kidnapped.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration ŌĆö 1D Range Sensor Model

Suppose a robot at pose $(x_r, y_r)$ observes a landmark at known map location $(x_l, y_l)$. The predicted range is:

$$
\hat{z}_t = \sqrt{(x_l - x_r)^2 + (y_l - y_r)^2}
$$

With measurement noise variance $\sigma_r^2$, the likelihood is:

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_r^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(z_t - \hat{z}_t)^2}{2\sigma_r^2} \right)
$$

If the robot reports $z_t = 5.0\,\text{m}$ and the predicted range for particle A is $\hat{z}_A = 5.1\,\text{m}$ while for particle B it is $\hat{z}_B = 7.4\,\text{m}$, then clearly particle A will receive a higher weight ŌĆö it "agrees" with the measurement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Localization Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x0] --> B[Control Input ut]
    B --> C[Motion Model Pxt ut xt-1]
    C --> D[Predicted Belief bar bel xt]
    D --> E{Sensor Measurement zt Arrives}
    E -- Yes --> F[Sensor Model Pzt xt]
    F --> G[Bayes Update bel xt]
    G --> H[Output Pose Estimate]
    H --> I[Resampling Check Neff]
    I -- Neff less than threshold --> J[Resample Particles]
    I -- Neff ok --> K[Continue Loop]
    J --> K
    K --> B
    E -- No --> C
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Decision Tree ŌĆö Which Localization Algorithm to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Localization Problem] --> B{Initial Pose Known?}
    B -- Yes, with low uncertainty --> C[Position Tracking]
    B -- No --> D{Environment Symmetric?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Global Localisation Needed]
    D -- No --> F{Belief Shape}
    F -- Unimodal Gaussian --> G[Use EKF or UKF]
    F -- Multimodal --> H[Use Particle Filter MCL]
    C --> G
    E --> H
    G --> I[Output Pose mu_t Sigma_t]
    H --> J[Output Particle Swarm]
```

## 4.3 EKF Localization Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Encoders] --> B[Odometry ut]
    B --> C[Predicted Mean mu_bar]
    B --> D[Predicted Covariance Sigma_bar]
    E[Lidar or Camera] --> F[Observation zt]
    F --> G[Innovation nu = zt - h mu_bar]
    D --> H[Kalman Gain Kt]
    C --> G
    H --> I[Updated Mean mu_t]
    H --> J[Updated Covariance Sigma_t]
    G --> I
```

## 4.4 Particle Filter (MCL) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Particles xt-1] --> B[Sample from Motion Model]
    B --> C[Predicted Particles x_bar_t]
    C --> D[Sensor Reading zt]
    D --> E[Compute Importance Weights wt]
    E --> F[Normalise Weights]
    F --> G{Neff below threshold}
    G -- Yes --> H[Resample with Replacement]
    G -- No --> I[Keep Particles]
    H --> J[Output Posterior Particles]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define localization in mobile robotics and distinguish between *local* and *global* localization. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

Localization is the process of estimating a robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within a reference frame using sensor and control data, represented formally as $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

- **Local localization (position tracking):** Robot's initial pose is approximately known; uncertainty remains small and unimodal; algorithms like EKF suffice.
- **Global localization:** Initial pose is unknown; the robot must determine its location *ab initio*; requires non-parametric methods such as MCL or Markov localization.

**(3 marks)** ŌĆö Awarded for: (i) definition 1 mark, (ii) correct distinction with example algorithm 2 marks.

### Q2. State Bayes' theorem as applied to the measurement update step of robot localization. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

where:
- $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ = predicted belief from motion model,
- $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ = sensor measurement likelihood,
- $\eta$ = normalizer ensuring probabilities sum to 1.

This equation allows the robot to fuse prior pose belief with new sensor evidence into a refined posterior.

**(3 marks)**

---

## Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A ŌĆö (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Extended Kalman Filter update equations for a 2D mobile robot localization problem. Clearly state the Jacobian computation and explain the role of the Kalman Gain. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robot at predicted pose $\overline{\mu}_t = (1.0, 2.0, 0.5)^\top$ observes a landmark at known map position $(5, 4)$. The rangeŌĆōbearing observation model is $z = (\sqrt{(x_l - x_r)^2 + (y_l - y_r)^2},\ \text{atan2}(y_l - y_r, x_l - x_r))^\top$ with noise covariance $Q = \text{diag}(0.04, 0.01)$. Given predicted covariance $\overline{\Sigma}_t = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25, 0.04)$ and measurement $z_t = (4.24, 0.79)^\top$, compute one EKF update step. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of EKF Update Equations**

The EKF linearizes the nonlinear observation model $z = h(x)$ about the current predicted mean $\overline{\mu}_t$ via the Jacobian:

$$
H_t = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right|_{\overline{\mu}_t}
$$

The Kalman Gain ŌĆö which optimally balances prediction uncertainty against measurement noise ŌĆö is:

$$
K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \overline{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

The updated state estimate and covariance are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_t &= \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t)) \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of Kalman Gain:** $K_t$ acts as a *trust factor*. When the sensor is highly accurate ($Q_t$ small), $K_t$ is large and the filter trusts the measurement more. When the prediction is highly confident ($\overline{\Sigma}_t$ small), $K_t$ is small and the filter leans on the prediction. Geometrically, it projects the innovation vector $z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t)$ into the state space, weighted by relative certainty.

**(b) Numerical EKF Step**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Predicted range and bearing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{r} &= \sqrt{(5 - 1)^2 + (4 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472 \\[4pt]
\hat{\phi} &= \text{atan2}(4 - 2,\ 5 - 1) = \text{atan2}(2, 4) \approx 0.4636\ \text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Innovation:**

$$
\nu = z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} 4.24 - 4.472 \\ 0.79 - 0.4636 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.232 \\ 0.3264 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Jacobian of observation model:**

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix}
\frac{-(x_l - x_r)}{\hat{r}} & \frac{-(y_l - y_r)}{\hat{r}} & 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{y_l - y_r}{\hat{r}^2} & \frac{-(x_l - x_r)}{\hat{r}^2} & -1
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
-0.894 & -0.447 & 0 \\
0.0995 & -0.199 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Innovation covariance** $S = H \overline{\Sigma}_t H^\top + Q$:

$$
H \overline{\Sigma}_t H^\top = \begin{bmatrix} 0.250 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.025 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \text{(diag weighting)} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.200 & -0.015 \\ -0.015 & 0.0247 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.240 & -0.015 \\ -0.015 & 0.0347 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Kalman Gain** $K = \overline{\Sigma}_t H^\top S^{-1}$:

$$
K \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.83 & 0.36 \\ -0.41 & 0.18 \\ -0.06 & -0.69 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Updated mean:**

$$
\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K \nu = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 2.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.31 \\ 0.15 \\ -0.21 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.31 \\ 2.15 \\ 0.29 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Updated covariance:**

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - KH)\overline{\Sigma}_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.087 & 0.029 & 0.008 \\ 0.029 & 0.117 & 0.024 \\ 0.008 & 0.024 & 0.028 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The covariance has shrunk ŌĆö uncertainty has decreased, confirming successful localization.

---

### QUESTION B ŌĆö (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice ŌĆö KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm with its four key stages. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of EKF in global localization. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robot uses 100 particles in MCL. After computing importance weights, the normalized weights are: $w = (0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10)$ for the first 5 particles (rest are negligible). Calculate the effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ and state whether resampling should be triggered if $N_{\text{threshold}} = 50$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Monte Carlo Localization ŌĆö Four Stages**

1. **Sampling:** Each particle $x_t^{[i]}$ is drawn from the motion model conditioned on the previous particle and the latest control: $x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$.
2. **Importance Weighting:** Each particle is weighted by the likelihood it assigns to the observation: $w_t^{[i]} = P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$.
3. **Normalization:** Weights are rescaled so that $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$.
4. **Resampling:** New particles are drawn with replacement proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$, eliminating low-weight particles and replicating high-weight ones.

**Advantages over EKF for global localization:**

- **Multimodal belief representation:** Particles can simultaneously represent multiple pose hypotheses (essential when the robot does not know where it is).
- **No Gaussian assumption:** EKF fails when belief becomes multimodal (e.g., in symmetric corridors).
- **Robust to non-linear, non-Gaussian noise.**
- **Computationally scalable** ŌĆö accuracy depends on particle count, not on map dimensionality.

**Limitation:** Suffers from the *particle deprivation* problem in the kidnapped robot scenario unless augmented with random particle injection.

**(b) Effective Sample Size Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2 &= 0.30^2 + 0.25^2 + 0.20^2 + 0.15^2 + 0.10^2 \\
&= 0.09 + 0.0625 + 0.04 + 0.0225 + 0.01 \\
&= 0.225
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2} = \frac{1}{0.225} \approx 4.44
$$

Since $N_{\text{eff}} \approx 4.44 \ll N_{\text{threshold}} = 50$, **resampling IS triggered**. In fact, the disparity among weights is severe ŌĆö only ~4 particles effectively represent the belief out of 100, indicating that most particles are wasted and resampling is urgently required to prevent particle depletion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing prediction vs. update order** ŌĆö Markov property must be clearly stated: prediction uses $u_t$, update uses $z_t$.
> 2. **Forgetting normalization constant $\eta$** in Bayes' update ŌĆö partial credit only.
> 3. **Sign errors in Jacobian** ŌĆö the $(y_l - y_r)/\hat{r}^2$ term must be positive; check atan2 conventions.
> 4. **Reporting covariance as a scalar** ŌĆö it is a $3 \times 3$ matrix in 2D localization.
> 5. **Stating EKF is "best" without context** ŌĆö EKF fails in symmetric/global scenarios; mention particle filters.
> 6. **Omitting the resampling step** in MCL ŌĆö answers without it lose 2ŌĆō3 marks easily.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ Localization = estimating pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$ as a probability distribution $bel(x_t)$.
- Ō£ģ Three flavors: **position tracking**, **global localization**, **kidnapped robot**.
- Ō£ģ Two-step Bayesian loop: **prediction (motion)** then **correction (sensor)**.
- Ō£ģ **EKF**: best for local tracking with unimodal Gaussian belief; requires Jacobian $H_t$.
- Ō£ģ **MCL / Particle Filter**: best for global localization; handles multimodal beliefs; resampling critical.
- Ō£ģ **Effective Sample Size** $N_{\text{eff}}$ triggers resampling when particle weights collapse.
- Ō£ģ Odometry noise grows **unboundedly** without sensor correction ŌĆö never rely on wheel encoders alone.
- Ō£ģ Data association errors are the #1 cause of localization failure in landmark-based methods.
- Ō£ģ The Kalman Gain $K_t$ balances **prediction trust** vs **measurement trust**.
- Ō£ģ Symmetric environments cause ambiguity ŌĆö require non-parametric filters.
- Ō£ģ Map representation (feature-based vs. grid-based) directly affects algorithm choice.
- Ō£ģ Always include normalization $\eta$ in Bayes' update expressions for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PERAT741 ŌĆō Robot Navigation** (KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned), **Cognition** refers to the higher-level information processing, reasoning, and decision-making capabilities that allow an autonomous robot to perceive its environment, build internal representations (world models), reason about goals, and select actions that accomplish those goals under uncertainty.

Formally, robot cognition is the computational process that transforms raw sensor data into a **Belief State** $b_t$ and maps that belief to an action $a_t$ such that a value or utility function $V(b_t, a_t)$ is optimized over time. It is the "thinking layer" that sits above low-level control and below the high-level task specification.

$$a_t = \pi^*(b_t) = \arg\max_{a_t} \sum_{s_{t+1}} P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)\, U(s_{t+1})$$

Where:
- $b_t$ ŌĆö Belief state at time $t$ (internal world model)
- $\pi^*$ ŌĆö Optimal cognitive policy
- $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ ŌĆö State transition probability
- $U(\cdot)$ ŌĆö Utility / reward function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 ŌĆö Cognition):** Cognition is the bridge between *Perception* (sensing) and *Action* (motor control). It encompasses **perceptionŌĆōreasoningŌĆōplanningŌĆōlearning**, and distinguishes an *autonomous* robot from a merely *teleoperated* machine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a self-driving car approaching an unmarked four-way intersection at night.

- **Without Cognition:** The car simply follows its last command ("go straight"). If a pedestrian is crossing, the car will collide because it cannot interpret the situation.
- **With Cognition:** The car **perceives** the dark shape (pedestrian), **remembers** that intersections have right-of-way rules, **predicts** that the pedestrian might continue crossing, **evaluates** braking vs. swerving, and **decides** to brake. The *act of reasoning about unobserved meaning* is cognition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Cognition in a robot is the same as *thinking* in a human. Your eyes gather photons (perception), but it is your brain that says, "That is a staircase, walk carefully." Robot cognition is the engineered equivalent of that brain.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Robot Cognition

| Pillar | Role | Example in Navigation |
|---|---|---|
| **PerceptionŌĆōAction Loop** | Continuous sensing and motion | LIDAR + wheel encoders |
| **World Modeling** | Maintain internal belief $b_t$ | Occupancy grid map |
| **Reasoning / Planning** | Decide *what* to do next | A* path planning |
| **Learning** | Improve from experience | Reinforcement learning tuning |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners in KTU frequently award marks for **clear definitions of the four pillars**. Memorize the table ŌĆö it is high-yield for Part A (3-mark) questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Cognitive Architecture

A cognitive robot architecture is typically organized into **three functional layers** (a hybrid deliberativeŌĆōreactive architecture):

1. **Reactive Layer (Lowest)** ŌĆö Sub-second reflexes such as obstacle avoidance. Uses potential fields or behaviour-based schemas. *No memory, no reasoning.*
2. **Executive / Sequencing Layer (Middle)** ŌĆö Coordinates behaviours, monitors task progress, and handles exceptions. *Short-term memory, no deep planning.*
3. **Deliberative Layer (Highest)** ŌĆö Performs global path planning, world modeling, mission-level reasoning, and learning. *Long-term memory, symbolic reasoning.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the *deliberativeŌĆōreactive hybrid* model. The **3T (Three-Tier) architecture** by Bonasso et al. and **CLARAty** by NASA-JPL are classical textbook examples ŌĆö both are fair game for theory questions.

## 2.2 The PerceptionŌĆōCognitionŌĆōAction (P-C-A) Cycle

Cognition does not operate in isolation. It is the central node of a closed loop:

$$\text{Environment} \xrightarrow{\text{Sense}} \text{Sensors} \xrightarrow{\text{Perceive}} \underbrace{b_t}_{\text{Cognition}} \xrightarrow{\text{Plan}} a_t \xrightarrow{\text{Actuate}} \text{Motors} \rightarrow \text{Environment}$$

Every iteration reduces the *epistemic uncertainty* in the belief $b_t$ and increases the robot's situational awareness.

## 2.3 Belief Representation and Bayesian Updating

The single most important cognitive operation is **belief updating** using **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(s \mid z) = \frac{P(z \mid s)\, P(s)}{P(z)}$$

In a cognitive robot:
- $P(s)$ ŌĆö **Prior** belief about world state $s$ (before sensing)
- $P(z \mid s)$ ŌĆö **Likelihood** of sensor reading $z$ given state $s$ (sensor model)
- $P(s \mid z)$ ŌĆö **Posterior** belief after incorporating $z$ (updated belief)
- $P(z)$ ŌĆö Normalizing constant (evidence)

When observations arrive sequentially, we use the **recursive Bayes filter**:

$$bel(s_t) = \eta\, P(z_t \mid s_t)\, \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})\, bel(s_{t-1})\, ds_{t-1}$$

Where $\eta$ is a normalizer. This is the *cognitive engine* of any probabilistic robot.

## 2.4 Markov Property and the POMDP Framework

A cognitive agent makes decisions assuming the **Markov property**: the future depends only on the present state, not on the full history.

$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

Because sensors are noisy and partial, the true state $s_t$ is **not directly observable** ŌĆö only an observation $z_t$ is. This gives the **POMDP** (Partially Observable Markov Decision Process) tuple:

$$\text{POMDP} = \langle S, A, Z, T, O, R, \gamma \rangle$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $S$ | Set of true world states |
| $A$ | Set of actions (motor commands) |
| $Z$ | Set of observations (sensor readings) |
| $T(s,a,s') = P(s' \mid s,a)$ | Transition function |
| $O(s',a,z) = P(z \mid s',a)$ | Observation function |
| $R(s,a)$ | Reward / utility |
| $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Discount factor |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for Module 1 ŌĆö Cognition**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_t = \arg\max_a\, U(s_{t+1})$ | Cognitive decision rule | ŌĆö |
| 2 | $P(s \mid z) = \dfrac{P(z \mid s)P(s)}{P(z)}$ | Bayes' theorem | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $bel(s_t) = \eta\, P(z_t \mid s_t) \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1},a_{t-1})\, bel(s_{t-1})\, ds_{t-1}$ | Recursive Bayes filter | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $H(X) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy (uncertainty) | **bits** |
| 5 | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s, \pi\right]$ | Value function under policy $\pi$ | Reward units |
| 6 | $Q^\pi(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\, V^\pi(s')$ | Action-value (Q) function | Reward units |
| 7 | $\text{SLAM posterior: } P(m, x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Map + pose belief | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\bigl[R(s,a) + \gamma\, V^*(s')\bigr]$ | Bellman optimality | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPING RULE:** For absolute-value or conditioning bars inside tables, I have used `$\vert$` and `$\mid$` rather than the literal pipe character `$\vert$` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Replicate this in your own answer sheets.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

Cognitive robots are deployed in:
- **Autonomous vehicles** (planning lane changes under uncertainty)
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva bots use POMDP-style reasoning)
- **Search and rescue** (reasoning about unknown building layouts)
- **Space exploration** (NASA's Mars rovers ŌĆö **CLARAty** architecture)
- **Surgical robotics** (da Vinci's cognition layer for instrument trajectory planning)

> [!TIP]
> Whenever a KTU Part B question asks *"Discuss the role of cognition in modern robots"*, cite **autonomous vehicles** and **Mars rovers** as examples ŌĆö they are credited in the official KTU answer key.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to the Recursive Bayes Filter

**Goal:** Derive the recursive belief-update equation used in cognitive robot localization.

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the prior belief.** Let $bel(s_{t-1}) = P(s_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})$ be the robot's belief about its state after incorporating all observations and actions up to time $t-1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Predict the next state using the Markov transition model.** Conditioning on $s_{t-1}$ and applying the **Markov property** (future is independent of history given the present state):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(s_t) &\;\triangleq\; P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1}) \\
&= \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})\, P(s_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-2})\, ds_{t-1} \\
&= \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})\, bel(s_{t-1})\, ds_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The overline denotes the **predicted belief** (before the new measurement $z_t$ is incorporated).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Bayes' rule using the new measurement $z_t$.** We want $P(s_t \mid z_{1:t}, a_{1:t-1})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(s_t) &= P(s_t \mid z_{1:t}, a_{1:t-1}) \\
&= \frac{P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{t-1})\, P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator is a normalizer independent of $s_t$; absorb it into $\eta$:

$$
bel(s_t) = \eta\; P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{t-1})\;\overline{bel}(s_t)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the prediction from Step 2 into Step 3.** This is the **complete recursive Bayes filter**:

$$
\boxed{\,bel(s_t) = \eta\, P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{t-1}) \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})\, bel(s_{t-1})\, ds_{t-1}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the student:** The integral *propagates* the old belief through the motion model; the multiplication *corrects* it with the new sensor reading; $\eta$ *renormalizes* it to a valid probability. This three-step structure (predict ŌåÆ correct ŌåÆ normalize) is the cognitive heart of every probabilistic robot.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bellman Optimality for Cognitive Decision-Making

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the value of a state under an optimal policy $\pi^*$:**

$$
V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} Q^*(s, a)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand $Q^*(s,a)$ using the expected future reward over all possible next states $s'$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a)\, V^*(s') \\
&= R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combine to get the **Bellman optimality equation** for the cognitive agent:**

$$
\boxed{\,V^*(s) = \max_{a} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\, V^*(s') \right]\,}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The optimal cognitive policy is therefore:**

$$
\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\, V^*(s') \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Bellman equation is the *cognitive optimum*. It says: *Choose the action that gives the best immediate reward plus the best discounted future outcome.* Every KTU 2024 question on "decision-making in robots" maps directly to this equation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cognitive Operations vs. Navigation Tasks

| Navigation Task | Required Cognitive Function | Governing Formula | Cognitive Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Localization** | Belief updating | Recursive Bayes filter (Eq. above) | Deliberative |
| **Mapping (SLAM)** | Joint map + pose estimation | $P(m, x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Deliberative |
| **Path planning** | Search in belief space | $A^*$, $D^*$-Lite, RRT* | Deliberative |
| **Obstacle avoidance** | Reactive control | Artificial potential field | Reactive |
| **Exploration** | Information-gain maximization | $I(m; z) = H(m) - H(m \mid z)$ | Executive |
| **Fault recovery** | Anomaly detection + replanning | Belief divergence threshold | Executive |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a **hall-mark answer template** for the 14-mark KTU question *"Explain cognition in robot navigation with suitable functions."* Reproducing this table (with one example per row) secures full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cognitive Architecture of a Navigation Robot

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts the flow of information from the physical world through the cognitive layers to motor actuation. Every block is a functional module; arrows are information/control flows.

```mermaid

    G --> H[Motor Controllers: Wheel Drivers and Manipulators]

    D --> I[Long Term Memory: Map Database]
```

### 4.2 Three-Tier (3T) Hybrid Architecture ŌĆö Detailed View

```mermaid

        D1[Global Planner: A Star and D Star Lite]

```

### 4.3 PerceptionŌĆōCognitionŌĆōAction Closed Loop

```mermaid

    P1[Perception: Sense] --> P2[World Model: Update Belief]
    P2 --> P3[Reasoning: Predict and Plan]

    P5 --> P1
    style P1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa9,color:#000
```
