<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Odometry** is a fundamental *dead-reckoning* localization technique used in mobile robotics wherein the pose (position and orientation) of a wheeled robot is estimated incrementally by measuring the rotation of its drive wheels over time. The word *odometry* is derived from the Greek words **"hodos" (meaning journey/travel)** and **"metron" (meaning measure)**, literally translating to "measurement of travel."

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the odometry sensor refers to the integration of **wheel-mounted rotary encoders** (typically optical incremental encoders or Hall-effect sensors) coupled with a **kinematic model** of the robot to compute the displacement of the robot's reference point frame, typically expressed as:

$$p(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ \theta(t) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$

where $x$ and $y$ represent the planar Cartesian coordinates of the robot in the global reference frame, and $\theta$ denotes the heading angle measured counter-clockwise from the global $X$-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"Odometry is the use of data from the rotation of a robot's wheels to estimate change in position over time. It is a method of relative position estimation that relies on internal proprioceptive sensors rather than external references."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Blindfolded Walk

Imagine you are blindfolded in the middle of a large room and asked to walk to the opposite wall. You have **no eyes (no exteroceptive sensors like GPS or cameras)**, but you can:
1. **Count your footsteps** (analogous to counting encoder ticks).
2. **Feel the angle at which you turn your body** (analogous to measuring wheel differential).
3. **Remember the direction you were facing when you started** (the initial pose).

Each footstep adds to your cumulative estimate. This is exactly what odometry does. The robot "remembers" where it started, and by continuously integrating wheel rotations, it estimates where it *should* be. However, like the blindfolded walker, every tiny slip, miscounted step, or imperfect turn **compounds into growing uncertainty**, which is why odometry is classified as an open-loop, drift-prone technique.

### 1.3 Classification of Odometry Sensors

| Sensor Class | Operating Principle | Resolution | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical Incremental Encoder** | Slotted disk + photodiode | 100ŌĆō10000 CPR | Differential drive robots |
| **Hall-Effect Encoder** | Magnetic field variation | 1ŌĆō100 CPR | Low-cost hobby robots |
| **Potentiometric Encoder** | Resistive voltage divider | Continuous | Servo feedback (rare for odometry) |
| **Resolver** | Electromagnetic induction | Infinite (analog) | Industrial AGVs |

> [!NOTE]
> The most prevalent KTU-referenced sensor is the **quadrature optical incremental encoder**, which produces two square-wave channels (A and B) phase-shifted by 90┬░, allowing direction discrimination.

### 1.4 Why Odometry Matters in Robot Navigation

Odometry is the **backbone** of any navigation stack. It serves three critical roles:
1. **Short-term localization**: Provides real-time pose estimates between slower exteroceptive sensor updates (e.g., GPS, SLAM corrections).
2. **Motion control feedback**: Forms the inner loop of velocity and trajectory controllers.
3. **Sensor fusion seed**: Acts as the prediction step in Extended Kalman Filters (EKF) and particle filters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Differential Drive Robot Model

The most commonly analyzed platform in KTU examinations is the **differential drive (DD) mobile robot**, which has two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axis, plus a passive caster or ball wheel for balance.

Let the following parameters be defined:
- $r$ = nominal wheel radius (m)
- $b$ = wheel base / track length = distance between the two drive wheels (m)
- $v_L$, $v_R$ = linear velocities of the left and right wheels (m/s)
- $\omega_L$, $\omega_R$ = angular velocities of the left and right wheels (rad/s)
- $N$ = encoder counts per full wheel revolution (CPR)
- $\Delta t$ = sampling time interval (s)
- $N_L$, $N_R$ = number of encoder ticks accumulated during $\Delta t$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 ŌĆö Wheel Velocity Computation**
Each encoder emits $N$ pulses per revolution. By counting pulses over a fixed interval, the wheel rotation angle is recovered.

$$\Delta\phi_L = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_L}{N}, \quad \Delta\phi_R = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_R}{N}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linear Displacement per Wheel**
The arc-length swept by each wheel during $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta\phi_L, \quad \Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta\phi_R$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Robot Center Linear and Angular Velocity**
Using the kinematic constraint of the differential drive:

$$v = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2 \cdot \Delta t}, \quad \omega = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b \cdot \Delta t}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pose Update (Integration)**
The new pose is obtained by integrating the unicycle model:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{k+1} \\ y_{k+1} \\ \theta_{k+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \theta_k \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} v \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\ v \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\ \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Insight:** The pose update is a **first-order Euler integration**. Higher accuracy can be achieved with the **exact (arc-based) odometry update**, which is detailed in Section 3.

### 2.3 The Exact Odometry Update (Circular Arc Model)

When the robot's rotation is non-negligible, treating the trajectory as a straight line segment introduces error. The **exact odometry** approach models the robot's motion as a circular arc of instantaneous radius:

$$R = \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}$$

The change in heading angle is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

The new robot center position:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + R \cdot [\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \sin(\theta_k)]$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k - R \cdot [\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \cos(\theta_k)]$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Case ŌĆö Straight Line Motion:** When $\Delta s_R = \Delta s_L$, the radius $R \to \infty$. In this limit, the exact update gracefully reduces to the straight-line Euler update, preserving numerical stability.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel rotation | $\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi N_{ticks}}{N_{CPR}}$ | rad | Encoder-based |
| 2 | Wheel linear displacement | $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta\phi$ | m | Arc length |
| 3 | Robot linear velocity | $v = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2\Delta t}$ | m/s | Centroid velocity |
| 4 | Robot angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b \Delta t}$ | rad/s | About ICC |
| 5 | ICC radius | $R = \frac{b(\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)}{2(\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)}$ | m | Instantaneous Center of Curvature |
| 6 | Heading change | $\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$ | rad | Per time step |
| 7 | Euler position update | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k \Delta t$ | m | Approximate |
| 8 | Euler position update | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v\sin\theta_k \Delta t$ | m | Approximate |
| 9 | Euler heading update | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \Delta t$ | rad | Approximate |
| 10 | Exact position update | $x_{k+1} = x_k + R[\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta_k]$ | m | Preferred |
| 11 | Exact position update | $y_{k+1} = y_k - R[\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \cos\theta_k]$ | m | Preferred |
| 12 | Forward kinematic map | $\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2} & \frac{r}{2} \\ \frac{r}{b} & -\frac{r}{b} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$ | m/s, rad/s | Differential drive |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in warehouses use wheel odometry fused with LiDAR for SLAM.
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA's *Perseverance*, *Curiosity*) rely on visual-inertial-odometry (VIO) but ground-truth their motion with wheel encoders.
- **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** (Roomba) use odometry as the primary localization source.
- **Self-Driving Cars** use encoder odometry of the drive shaft for fine-grained motion prediction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Encoder Ticks to Pose Update

**Problem Statement:**
A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheel base $b = 0.30$ m, and encoders with resolution $N = 500$ CPR. During a sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, the left encoder recorded $N_L = 25$ ticks and the right encoder recorded $N_R = 35$ ticks. Starting pose: $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$. Compute the new pose using the exact odometry update.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute wheel rotation angles.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi_L &= \frac{2\pi \cdot N_L}{N} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 25}{500} = \frac{\pi}{10} \text{ rad} = 0.3142 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi_R &= \frac{2\pi \cdot N_R}{N} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 35}{500} = \frac{7\pi}{50} \text{ rad} = 0.4398 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The wheel rotation is proportional to the tick count. Dividing by the CPR normalizes to a fraction of a full revolution ($2\pi$ rad).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute wheel linear displacements.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= r \cdot \Delta\phi_L = 0.05 \cdot 0.3142 = 0.01571 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_R &= r \cdot \Delta\phi_R = 0.05 \cdot 0.4398 = 0.02199 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Arc length $s = r\theta$ for pure rolling without slip.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute heading change $\Delta\theta$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.02199 - 0.01571}{0.30} = \frac{0.00628}{0.30} = 0.02094 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Since the right wheel traveled farther than the left, the robot turned left (positive heading change in our convention).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute ICC radius $R$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L} = \frac{0.30}{2} \cdot \frac{0.02199 + 0.01571}{0.02199 - 0.01571}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.15 \cdot \frac{0.03770}{0.00628} = 0.15 \cdot 6.003 = 0.9005 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The robot follows an arc of radius $\approx 0.90$ m about the ICC.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the exact pose update.**
Since $\theta_0 = 0$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= x_0 + R[\sin(\theta_0 + \Delta\theta) - \sin(\theta_0)] = 0 + 0.9005 \cdot [\sin(0.02094) - 0]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0.9005 \cdot 0.02094 = 0.01886 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= y_0 - R[\cos(\theta_0 + \Delta\theta) - \cos(\theta_0)] = 0 - 0.9005 \cdot [\cos(0.02094) - 1]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= -0.9005 \cdot [0.99978 - 1] = -0.9005 \cdot (-0.000220) = 0.000198 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \theta_0 + \Delta\theta = 0 + 0.02094 = 0.02094 \text{ rad} \approx 1.2^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Pose:** $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) \approx (0.0189 \text{ m}, \, 0.0002 \text{ m}, \, 0.0209 \text{ rad})$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Error Propagation in Odometry (Covariance Growth)

**Problem Statement:**
Show that the covariance of the pose estimate grows unboundedly with distance traveled, demonstrating the open-loop nature of odometry.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define error in wheel displacement.**
Let $\delta_s_L$ and $\delta_s_R$ be zero-mean Gaussian errors with covariance:
$$\begin{aligned}
E[\delta s_L^2] &= k_r^2 \vert \Delta s_L \vert, \quad E[\delta s_R^2] = k_r^2 \vert \Delta s_R \vert
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The error variance is proportional to distance traveled ŌĆö a standard model for slip-related odometric noise.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Propagate to pose error via Jacobian.**
The pose update is a nonlinear function of $(\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R)$. Using first-order Taylor expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \delta x \\ \delta y \\ \delta \theta \end{bmatrix} = J \begin{bmatrix} \delta s_L \\ \delta s_R \end{bmatrix}, \quad J = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial x}{\partial s_L} & \frac{\partial x}{\partial s_R} \\ \frac{\partial y}{\partial s_L} & \frac{\partial y}{\partial s_R} \\ \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial s_L} & \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial s_R} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Covariance update rule.**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{k+1} = J P_{\Delta s} J^T + P_k
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The pose covariance $P_k$ is the sum of previous uncertainty plus the newly injected process noise. Since $P_k$ only grows, **the pose uncertainty is monotonically non-decreasing** ŌĆö this is the mathematical statement of odometric drift.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Sources of Odometric Error

| Category | Source | Systematic (Bias) | Non-Systematic (Random) | Mitigation |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Geometric** | Unequal wheel diameters | Ō£ō | ŌĆö | Calibration, factory measurement |
| **Geometric** | Wheelbase mis-measurement | Ō£ō | ŌĆö | UMBmark calibration |
| **Geometric** | Misalignment of wheels | Ō£ō | ŌĆö | Mechanical alignment |
| **Kinematic** | Encoder resolution finite | ŌĆö | Ō£ō | Use higher CPR encoders |
| **Kinematic** | Sampling rate too low | ŌĆö | Ō£ō | Increase loop rate |
| **Operational** | Wheel slip on wet/loose surface | ŌĆö | Ō£ō | Friction modeling, IMU fusion |
| **Operational** | Skidding during turns | ŌĆö | Ō£ō | Reduce acceleration, sensor fusion |
| **Environmental** | Uneven floor, bumps | ŌĆö | Ō£ō | Suspension, IMU integration |
| **Mechanical** | Wheel deformation over time | Ō£ō | ŌĆö | Periodic replacement |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Odometry Signal Processing Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Rotation] --> B[Optical Encoder]
    B --> C[Quadrature Decoder]
    C --> D[Tick Counter N_L N_R]
    D --> E[Wheel Angle Calculation]
    E --> F[Linear Displacement Delta_S]
    F --> G[Forward Kinematics Model]
    G --> H[Velocity v and Angular omega]
    H --> I[Pose Integrator]
    I --> J[Robot Pose x y theta]
    J --> K{Sensor Fusion}
    K --> L[Corrected Pose Estimate]
```

### 4.2 Differential Drive Kinematic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Commanded Left Wheel Velocity omega_L] --> N1[Left Motor + Gearbox]
    M2[Commanded Right Wheel Velocity omega_R] --> N2[Right Motor + Gearbox]
    N1 --> O1[Left Encoder]
    N2 --> O2[Right Encoder]
    O1 --> P[Encoder Reading Aggregator]
    O2 --> P
    P --> Q[Compute Delta_S_L and Delta_S_R]
    Q --> R[Kinematic Inversion: Solve for v and omega]
    R --> S[Pose Update Step]
    S --> T[Updated Pose x_k_plus_1 y_k_plus_1 theta_k_plus_1]
```

### 4.3 Sources of Error Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Odometric Error Sources] --> SYS[Systematic Errors]
    ERR --> RAN[Non Systematic Errors]
    SYS --> S1[Unequal Wheel Diameter]
    SYS --> S2[Wheelbase Offset]
    SYS --> S3[Misalignment]
    RAN --> R1[Wheel Slip]
    RAN --> R2[Encoder Quantization]
    RAN --> R3[Floor Unevenness]
    S1 --> DRIFT[Pose Drift]
    S2 --> DRIFT
    S3 --> DRIFT
    R1 --> DRIFT
    R2 --> DRIFT
    R3 --> DRIFT
    DRIFT --> MIT[Mitigation via Sensor Fusion with IMU and GPS]
```

### 4.4 Coordinate Frame Relationships in Odometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WF[World Frame X_W Y_W] -->|Translation and Rotation| RF[Robot Frame X_R Y_R]
    RF -->|Encoder 1 measures| WL[Left Wheel Contact]
    RF -->|Encoder 2 measures| WR[Right Wheel Contact]
    WL --> IC[Instantaneous Center of Curvature ICC]
    WR --> IC
    IC -->|Radius R| ARC[Motion Arc]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define odometry. Mention any two inherent limitations of wheel odometry as a localization method.*

**Model Answer:**
Odometry is a relative position estimation technique in mobile robots that computes the robot's pose by integrating the rotation of its drive wheels, typically measured via incremental encoders.

*Limitations:*
1. **Unbounded error growth (drift):** Errors accumulate indefinitely because the technique is open-loop and there is no external correction.
2. **Sensitivity to wheel slip:** Any loss of traction (on wet, sandy, or inclined surfaces) introduces unmeasured motion that violates the no-slip rolling assumption, corrupting the pose estimate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is the role of a quadrature encoder in the odometry pipeline? Why is the dual-channel A-B configuration preferred over a single-channel design?*

**Model Answer:**
A quadrature encoder is an optoelectronic sensor that converts the angular position of a shaft into a train of digital pulses. In odometry, it measures the rotation of each drive wheel, which is then converted to linear displacement.

*The dual-channel A-B configuration is preferred because:*
1. It enables **direction discrimination** ŌĆö the phase relationship between channels A and B indicates clockwise vs. counter-clockwise rotation.
2. It allows **fourfold resolution multiplication** by detecting both rising and falling edges of each channel, yielding $4N$ counts per revolution instead of $N$.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [14 Marks]** ŌĆö Differential Drive Odometry Computation

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]**

A differential drive mobile robot has the following specifications:
- Wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m
- Wheel base $b = 0.40$ m
- Encoder resolution $N = 1000$ counts per revolution
- Sampling period $\Delta t = 0.2$ s

**(a)** [7 Marks] In one sampling interval, the left encoder produces 80 ticks and the right encoder produces 120 ticks. If the initial pose is $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$, compute the updated pose using the **exact odometry update equations**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Repeat the calculation using the **approximate (Euler) odometry update** and compare the two results. Identify the conditions under which the Euler approximation becomes acceptable.

---

**Solution to Q-A(a) ŌĆö Exact Odometry Update**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Wheel rotation angles:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi_L &= \frac{2\pi \cdot 80}{1000} = 0.5027 \text{ rad}, \quad \Delta\phi_R = \frac{2\pi \cdot 120}{1000} = 0.7540 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linear displacements:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= 0.08 \cdot 0.5027 = 0.04021 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta s_R = 0.08 \cdot 0.7540 = 0.06032 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Heading change:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.06032 - 0.04021}{0.40} = \frac{0.02011}{0.40} = 0.05027 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö ICC radius:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{0.40}{2} \cdot \frac{0.06032 + 0.04021}{0.06032 - 0.04021} = 0.20 \cdot \frac{0.10053}{0.02011} = 0.20 \cdot 5.000 = 1.000 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply exact update with $\theta_0 = 0$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 1.000 \cdot [\sin(0.05027) - 0] = 1.000 \cdot 0.05024 = 0.05024 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= 0 - 1.000 \cdot [\cos(0.05027) - 1] = -1.000 \cdot [0.99874 - 1] = 0.001262 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.05027 = 0.05027 \text{ rad} \approx 2.88^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Exact Pose:** $(0.05024, \, 0.00126, \, 0.05027)$ in $(m, m, rad)$.

---

**Solution to Q-A(b) ŌĆö Euler Approximation & Comparison**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute average linear velocity and angular velocity:**
$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2 \Delta t} = \frac{0.06032 + 0.04021}{2 \cdot 0.2} = \frac{0.10053}{0.4} = 0.2513 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b \Delta t} = \frac{0.02011}{0.40 \cdot 0.2} = \frac{0.02011}{0.08} = 0.2514 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Euler update:**
$$\begin{aligned}
x_1^{Euler} &= x_0 + v \cos(\theta_0) \Delta t = 0 + 0.2513 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.2 = 0.05027 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1^{Euler} &= y_0 + v \sin(\theta_0) \Delta t = 0 + 0.2513 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.2 = 0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1^{Euler} &= \theta_0 + \omega \Delta t = 0 + 0.2514 \cdot 0.2 = 0.05027 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Comparison Table:**

| Quantity | Exact | Euler | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x$ (m) | 0.05024 | 0.05027 | 0.00003 (0.06%) |
| $y$ (m) | 0.001262 | 0 | 0.001262 |
| $\theta$ (rad) | 0.05027 | 0.05027 | 0 (identical) |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Acceptability Condition:**
The Euler approximation is acceptable when $\Delta\theta$ per step is small (typically $< 5^\circ$ or $\approx 0.087$ rad). Here $\Delta\theta = 0.0503$ rad $\approx 2.88^\circ$, which is well below the threshold, hence Euler yields acceptable accuracy. For high-speed turning or low control rates, the exact formulation is mandatory.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]** ŌĆö Sources of Error & Sensor Fusion

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Resit]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Classify the sources of error in wheel odometry into **systematic** and **non-systematic** categories. Provide two specific examples for each category and explain their physical origins.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain why odometry alone cannot provide reliable long-term localization. Describe how fusion with an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** using a Kalman Filter mitigates the drift problem.

---

**Solution to Q-B(a) ŌĆö Error Classification**

**Systematic Errors** (deterministic, repeatable, can be calibrated out):
1. **Unequal wheel diameters:** Manufacturing tolerances cause $\Delta r_L \neq \Delta r_R$. Even a 1% radius mismatch produces $\approx 2\%$ heading error over a 5 m run.
2. **Wheelbase offset:** The actual distance between wheels differs from the value used in software, biasing $\Delta\theta$ computations.

**Non-Systematic Errors** (random, environment-dependent, cannot be calibrated):
1. **Wheel slip during acceleration:** The no-slip rolling constraint $\Delta s = r\Delta\phi$ is violated, causing undercounting of true motion.
2. **Floor irregularities:** Bumps and cracks cause transient wheel bouncing, producing spurious encoder pulses.

---

**Solution to Q-B(b) ŌĆö Why Odometry Fails Long-Term & IMU Fusion**

**Why Odometry Fails:**
Odometry is a **dead-reckoning (open-loop)** technique. Each pose update integrates previous errors, so the covariance grows without bound:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{k+1} = P_k + Q_k, \quad \text{where } Q_k \succ 0 \text{ always}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the pose estimate becomes increasingly unreliable over long trajectories.

**IMU Fusion via Kalman Filter:**
An IMU measures linear acceleration and angular velocity directly, providing *absolute* (or short-term accurate) motion data. The Kalman Filter fuses both:

- **Prediction step** uses odometry: $\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1|k-1}, u_k)$.
- **Update step** corrects with IMU: $\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k(z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k|k-1}))$.

The IMU complements odometry because its errors are *bounded* over short horizons (no integration drift in acceleration over short windows), while odometry errors are *bias-dominated* but bounded in the short term. Together, the filter suppresses the unbounded growth of odometric drift.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert CPR to radians:** Many students write $\Delta\phi = N_{ticks}/N$ (dimensionless) instead of $\Delta\phi = 2\pi N_{ticks}/N$ (radians). This gives a factor-of-$2\pi$ error.
> 2. **Mixing Euler and Exact updates:** Examiners specifically allocate marks for the *exact* (arc-based) derivation. Writing Euler when the question demands exact loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Right wheel faster than left ŌćÆ positive heading change (left turn) **only** if you adopt the standard convention. Always declare your convention explicitly at the start of the solution.
> 4. **Ignoring the straight-line edge case:** When $\Delta s_L = \Delta s_R$, $R \to \infty$ and the formula breaks. Examiners expect the student to mention this limit.
> 5. **Confusing $b$ with $d$:** Wheelbase $b$ is center-to-center, not from wheel to wheel edge.
> 6. **Neglecting units in the final answer:** Always specify (m, m, rad) or (m, m, deg).

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Odometry = proprioceptive dead-reckoning** using wheel encoder data ŌĆö no external reference required.
- The differential drive robot has two independent drive wheels; its motion is governed by $v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ and $\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}$.
- Encoder ticks convert to wheel rotation via $\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi N_{ticks}}{N_{CPR}}$, then to displacement via $\Delta s = r \Delta\phi$.
- **Two update formulations exist:** the approximate **Euler update** (straight-line) and the preferred **exact update** (circular arc). Use the exact update whenever $\Delta\theta$ is significant.
- The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** is the pivot point about which the robot rotates, located at distance $R = \frac{b}{2}\frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}$ from the robot center.
- Errors are classified as **systematic** (deterministic, calibratable) and **non-systematic** (random, environment-driven).
- **Odometric drift is mathematically inevitable** because pose covariance grows monotonically.
- **Sensor fusion** (IMU + odometry + GPS/LiDAR via EKF or particle filter) is the standard remedy.
- Quadrature encoders provide **direction sense** and **4├Ś resolution** via dual-channel edge detection.
- Always declare the **sign convention** for $\theta$ and the **reference point** of the robot (center, wheel, or sensor frame) before solving numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gyroscope** is a heading sensor that measures **angular rate (┬░/s or rad/s)** or **angular displacement (┬░ or rad)** about one or more axes, based on the principles of **conservation of angular momentum**, the **Coriolis effect**, or the **Sagnac effect**. In robotic navigation, gyroscopes form the rotational core of an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** and provide **yaw (heading), pitch, and roll** information essential for **dead-reckoning**, **attitude estimation**, and **sensor fusion with GPS/LiDAR**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT741, Module 1):**
> Heading sensors are categorized as **absolute** (e.g., magnetic compass, GPS) and **relative/inertial** (e.g., gyroscopes, IMU). Gyroscopes belong to the **inertial class** and report *change* in orientation rather than absolute heading.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Spinning Top

Imagine a child spinning a top on a table. The top stubbornly resists being tipped over ŌĆö even when you push it sideways, it does not fall; instead, it slowly **wobbles (precesses)** around the vertical axis. This is exactly how a **mechanical gyroscope** behaves. Its spinning rotor stores **angular momentum**, and any attempt to rotate the platform produces a *resisting* (precessional) torque proportional to the applied rotation. A **MEMS gyroscope** uses a similar idea in miniature: a tiny vibrating mass is forced to oscillate, and when the chip rotates, the **Coriolis force** deflects the mass sideways in proportion to the rotation rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Physical Principles Used by Gyroscopes:**
> 1. **Angular momentum (mechanical gyros)** ŌĆö Conservation of $L = I\omega$.
> 2. **Coriolis effect (MEMS gyros)** ŌĆö Apparent force on a moving body in a rotating frame.
> 3. **Sagnac effect (optical gyros: RLG, FOG)** ŌĆö Path-length difference for counter-propagating light beams in a rotating loop.

## 1.3 Classification of Gyroscopes (KTU Board Favourite)

| Class | Examples | Measured Quantity | Typical Drift |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Spinning Mass)** | Rotor + Gimbal, HRG (Hemispherical Resonator) | Angular rate / attitude | $\sim 0.001 \ ^\circ/\text{hr}$ |
| **Optical (Sagnac-based)** | RLG (Ring Laser Gyro), FOG (Fiber Optic Gyro) | Angular rate | $\sim 0.01 \ ^\circ/\text{hr}$ |
| **MEMS (Vibrating Structure)** | Tuning-fork, Wine-glass resonator | Angular rate | $\sim 1\text{ŌĆō}10 \ ^\circ/\text{hr}$ |
| **Quantum (Emerging)** | Atom interferometer, Nuclear MRG | Angular rate | $\sim 10^{-6} \ ^\circ/\text{hr}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For a **mobile robot** (wheeled/quadruped), a **MEMS IMU** is preferred (cheap, small). For an **autonomous car / aerial drone**, a **tactical-grade MEMS or FOG** is used. For **aerospace/submarine navigation**, **RLG or HRG** dominates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics ŌĆö The Three Pillars

### Pillar 1 ŌĆö Angular Momentum (Mechanical Gyroscopes)

A symmetric rotor spinning at angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ about its symmetry axis stores angular momentum:
$$\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$$
where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg┬Ęm┬▓). When the platform is forced to rotate at $\vec{\Omega}$ (the *precession* rate), the rotor axis tilts by an angle $\theta$ and obeys:
$$\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{L}$$
The magnitude of the precession rate is therefore:
$$\Omega = \frac{\tau}{L \sin\theta}$$
For a perfectly orthogonal case where $\theta = 90^\circ$ (gimbal arrangement), $\sin\theta = 1$, and $\Omega$ is directly proportional to the external torque.

### Pillar 2 ŌĆö Coriolis Effect (MEMS Gyroscopes)

A proof mass $m$ vibrating with linear velocity $\vec{v}$ in a frame rotating at $\vec{\Omega}$ experiences an apparent **Coriolis force** perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{\Omega}$:
$$\vec{F}_c = 2m \, (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$
The resulting displacement of the mass is measured capacitively, and the *sense-electronics* output voltage is:
$$V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot \Omega$$
where $K$ is the **scale factor** (V┬Ęs/┬░ or mV/┬░/s).

### Pillar 3 ŌĆö Sagnac Effect (Optical Gyroscopes)

Light is split into two coherent beams that traverse a closed loop of area $A$ in **opposite directions**. If the loop rotates at $\vec{\Omega}$, the co-rotating beam travels a *longer* path than the counter-rotating beam by:
$$\Delta L = \frac{4 A \Omega}{c}$$
The corresponding time difference is:
$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta L}{c} = \frac{4 A \Omega}{c^{2}}$$
This produces an **optical phase shift**:
$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi \, c \, \Delta t}{\lambda} = \frac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$
For a **Fiber Optic Gyro (FOG)** of coil length $L_c$ and $N$ turns, the area is $A = N \pi r^{2}$, and the sensitivity is amplified by a factor $N$ (since each turn adds an extra path).

## 2.2 Performance Metrics (Must-Know for KTU)

- **Bias (offset)**: Output when $\Omega = 0$; varies with temperature.
- **Scale-factor error**: Deviation of the slope $K$ from nominal.
- **Bias stability / Drift**: Random walk of the bias, expressed in $^\circ/\text{hr}$ or $^\circ/\sqrt{\text{hr}}$.
- **Angle Random Walk (ARW)**: White-noise component; integration over time yields orientation error.
- **Bandwidth**: Maximum rotation rate measurable (Hz).
- **g-Sensitivity**: Output drift under linear acceleration (cross-coupling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A *single-axis rate gyro* measures $\dot{\psi}$ (yaw rate). A *three-axis gyro* (combined in an IMU) gives the full attitude vector using Euler angles or quaternions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$ | $L$ = angular momentum, $I$ = inertia, $\omega$ = spin rate | Mechanical rotor |
| 2 | $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{L}$ | Precession torque relation | Mechanical gyro |
| 3 | $\Omega_{p} = \dfrac{\tau}{L \sin\theta}$ | Precession rate (rad/s) | Gimbaled gyro |
| 4 | $\vec{F}_{c} = 2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$ | Coriolis force (N) | MEMS gyro |
| 5 | $y(t) = \dfrac{2 m \Omega \, v_{0}}{k} \sin(\omega_{d} t)$ | Coriolis displacement (m) | MEMS proof mass |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot \Omega + b$ | Sensor model with bias $b$ | All gyros |
| 7 | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c}$ | Sagnac path difference (m) | RLG / FOG |
| 8 | $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ | Sagnac phase shift (rad) | FOG |
| 9 | $\Delta \phi_{FOG} = \dfrac{8 \pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ | FOG with $N$ turns | FOG |
| 10 | $\theta(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \Omega(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Angle from rate integration | Dead reckoning |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\theta} = \text{ARW} \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Orientation random-walk error | Drift analysis |
| 12 | $\Omega_{\text{lock-in}} \approx \dfrac{c \lambda}{4 \pi r L_{c}}$ | Lock-in threshold (RLG) | Optical gyro |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) on aircraft, ships, and autonomous vehicles integrate gyroscope output to compute **roll-pitch-yaw** when GPS is unavailable (e.g., tunnels, indoors, underwater). A tactical-grade IMU with $0.01 \ ^\circ/\text{hr}$ drift gives a position error of $\approx 1$ nautical mile per hour without aiding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Precession Rate of a Mechanical Gyroscope

**Statement:** Show that the precession angular velocity of a symmetric spinning rotor is $\Omega_{p} = \dfrac{\tau}{L \sin\theta}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define angular momentum vector.**

A symmetric rotor of mass moment of inertia $I$ spinning at $\omega$ stores:
$$\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply an external torque.**

Suppose a torque $\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}}$ is applied perpendicular to $\vec{L}$. Newton's 2nd law for rotation states:
$$\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize that $\vec{L}$ changes direction, not magnitude.**

The torque is orthogonal to $\vec{L}$, so $\vert \vec{L} \vert$ stays constant; only the **direction** of $\vec{L}$ changes. Over a small time $dt$, the tip of $\vec{L}$ sweeps an arc:
$$\vert d\vec{L} \vert = L \sin\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Define precession rate.**

The precession rate is $\Omega_{p} = \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$. Substituting the magnitude of torque:
$$\tau = \frac{\vert d\vec{L} \vert}{dt} = L \sin\theta \, \Omega_{p}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $\Omega_{p}$.**
$$\boxed{\Omega_{p} = \frac{\tau}{L \sin\theta}}$$

For a **gimbaled** gyro with $\theta = 90^\circ$, $\sin\theta = 1$, and the precession is a **direct measure** of the applied torque (and hence the platform's angular displacement).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Coriolis Displacement in a MEMS Gyroscope

**Statement:** A MEMS proof mass of mass $m$ vibrates along the $x$-axis with velocity $v(t) = v_{0}\cos(\omega_{d} t)$ inside a frame rotating at $\Omega$ about the $z$-axis. Show that the steady-state displacement along $y$ is $y(t) = \dfrac{2 m \Omega v_{0}}{k}\sin(\omega_{d} t)$, where $k$ is the effective spring constant of the secondary sense-mode.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write Coriolis acceleration.**

The Coriolis acceleration is:
$$\vec{a}_{c} = 2 (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$
With $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{k}$ and $\vec{v} = v_{0}\cos(\omega_{d} t)\,\hat{i}$:
$$\vec{a}_{c} = 2 \Omega v_{0}\cos(\omega_{d} t)\,(\hat{k} \times \hat{i}) = 2 \Omega v_{0}\cos(\omega_{d} t)\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Coriolis force on the mass.**
$$\vec{F}_{c} = m \vec{a}_{c} = 2 m \Omega v_{0}\cos(\omega_{d} t)\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Model sense-mode as a springŌĆōmassŌĆōdamper.**

The deflection $y(t)$ along $\hat{j}$ obeys:
$$m\ddot{y} + c \dot{y} + k y = F_{c}(t) = 2 m \Omega v_{0}\cos(\omega_{d} t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assume light damping; use sinusoidal steady-state.**

For a lightly damped system at resonance ($\omega_{d} = \sqrt{k/m}$), the response lags the force by $90^\circ$, and the amplitude is $F_{0}/k$:
$$y(t) = \frac{2 m \Omega v_{0}}{k}\sin(\omega_{d} t)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Capacitive readout.**

The sense-electronics convert $y$ into a voltage $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{C_{0}}{d}\,y \cdot G$ (where $G$ is amplifier gain), yielding:
$$V_{\text{out}} = \underbrace{\left(\frac{2 m v_{0} C_{0} G}{d\,k}\right)}_{K}\, \Omega$$
The constant $K$ is the **scale factor** of the MEMS gyroscope.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Sagnac Phase Shift in a Ring Laser / Fiber Optic Gyroscope

**Statement:** Two counter-propagating light beams travel around a circular loop of radius $r$ in a frame rotating at $\Omega$. Derive the optical phase difference between them.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Circumference of the loop.**
$$C = 2 \pi r$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Path of the co-rotating beam.**

The co-rotating beam effectively travels a longer path because the loop moves forward during transit. The time for the co-rotating beam to complete one loop is:
$$t_{\text{cw}} = \frac{C + r \Omega \, t_{\text{cw}}}{c} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{\text{cw}} = \frac{C}{c - r\Omega}$$
Using the first-order binomial expansion ($r\Omega \ll c$):
$$t_{\text{cw}} \approx \frac{C}{c}\left(1 + \frac{r\Omega}{c}\right) = \frac{2\pi r}{c}\left(1 + \frac{r\Omega}{c}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Path of the counter-rotating beam.**
$$t_{\text{ccw}} = \frac{C}{c + r\Omega} \approx \frac{2\pi r}{c}\left(1 - \frac{r\Omega}{c}\right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Time difference.**
$$\Delta t = t_{\text{cw}} - t_{\text{ccw}} = \frac{4 \pi r^{2} \Omega}{c^{2}} = \frac{4 A \Omega}{c^{2}}$$
(using loop area $A = \pi r^{2}$)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Phase difference.**

Each beam has frequency $f = c/\lambda$, so the phase difference is:
$$\Delta \phi = 2 \pi f \, \Delta t = \frac{2 \pi c}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{4 A \Omega}{c^{2}} = \frac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö FOG enhancement with $N$ turns.**

In a fiber coil of $N$ turns, each turn adds an extra Sagnac phase, so:
$$\boxed{\Delta \phi_{FOG} = \frac{8 \pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** For $A = 100 \text{ cm}^{2}$, $\lambda = 1.55 \,\mu\text{m}$, $\Omega = 1 \ ^\circ/\text{hr} = 4.85 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad/s}$:
> $\Delta \phi \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ rad ŌĆö extremely small. Therefore FOGs need $N \sim 10^{3}\text{ŌĆō}10^{4}$ turns to push the phase shift into the $\mu$rad range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Orientation Error from Angle Random Walk

**Statement:** If a gyroscope has ARW = $N$ $^\circ/\sqrt{\text{hr}}$, find the standard deviation of orientation error after time $t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Model the integrated white noise.**

The orientation is obtained by integrating rate noise $\eta(t)$ (white, PSD = $N^{2}$):
$$\theta(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \eta(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Variance of the integral.**

For zero-mean white noise, $\langle \eta(\tau_{1})\eta(\tau_{2}) \rangle = N^{2}\delta(\tau_{1} - \tau_{2})$. Thus:
$$\sigma_{\theta}^{2} = N^{2} \int_{0}^{t}\int_{0}^{t}\delta(\tau_{1}-\tau_{2})\,d\tau_{1} d\tau_{2} = N^{2} t$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Standard deviation.**
$$\sigma_{\theta} = N \sqrt{t}$$
This is the **angle random walk** formula, central to IMU-error budgeting in KTU board problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow of Gyroscope Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GYROSCOPES FOR ROBOT NAVIGATION] --> B[SPINNING MASS]
    A --> C[OPTICAL SAGNAC]
    A --> D[VIBRATING STRUCTURE MEMS]
    A --> E[QUANTUM EMERGING]

    B --> B1[Classical Rotor Gimbal]
    B --> B2[Hemispherical Resonator HRG]
    B --> B3[Dynamically Tuned Gyro DTG]

    C --> C1[Ring Laser Gyro RLG]
    C --> C2[Fiber Optic Gyro FOG]
    C --> C3[Interferometric FOG]

    D --> D1[Tuning Fork MEMS]
    D --> D2[Wine Glass Resonator]
    D --> D3[Comb Drive Capacitive]

    E --> E1[Atom Interferometer]
    E --> E2[Nuclear Magnetic Resonance]

    B1 --> F1[Angular Momentum I omega]
    C1 --> F2[Sagnac Path Difference]
    D1 --> F3[Coriolis Force 2m Omega cross v]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each branch ends in the *physical principle* it exploits, which is the standard KTU board answer for "classify gyroscopes" type questions.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a MEMS Gyroscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Angular Rate Input Omega] --> DRV[Drive Oscillator Loop]
    DRV --> PM[Proof Mass Vibrates along X]
    PM --> CF[Coriolis Force on Y Axis]
    CF --> SM[Sense Mode Spring Mass]
    SM --> CAP[Capacitive Pickoff]
    CAP --> DEMOD[Synchronous Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> AMP[Amplifier and Filter]
    AMP --> OUT[Voltage Vout K times Omega plus Bias]
    TEMP[Temperature Sensor] --> COMP[Bias Compensation]
    COMP --> AMP
```

**Interpretation for KTU:**
1. The drive loop forces the mass to oscillate at resonance.
2. External rotation $\Omega$ creates Coriolis force along the sense axis.
3. The sense-mode displacement is read out capacitively.
4. A demodulator extracts the in-phase component proportional to $\Omega$.
5. Temperature compensation corrects bias drift.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Superluminescent Diode SLD Source] --> P1[Polarizer]
    P1 --> SPL[Coupler Splits into CW and CCW Beams]
    SPL --> MOD[Pockels Cell Phase Modulator]
    MOD --> COIL[Fiber Coil N Turns Radius r]
    COIL --> SPL
    SPL --> DET[Photodetector]
    DET --> SIG[Signal Processing and Closed Loop Electronics]
    SIG --> MOD
    SIG --> VOUT[Digital Output Angular Rate]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Closed-loop FOG** uses a Pockels-cell phase modulator to introduce a feedback phase that *cancels* the Sagnac shift. This linearizes the output and improves scale-factor stability ŌĆö a frequent Part-B question.

---

## 4.4 Mechanical Gyroscope Gimbal Schematic (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROTOR[Spinning Rotor I omega] --> GIM1[Outer Gimbal Yaw]
    GIM1 --> GIM2[Inner Gimbal Pitch]
    GIM2 --> BASE[Base Platform Roll]
    BASE --> TORQ[Torque Applied tau ext]
    TORQ --> PREC[Precession at Rate Omega p]
    PREC --> GIM1
```

The three gimbals provide **three degrees of rotational freedom**, isolating the rotor spin axis from the platform motion ŌĆö the foundation of inertial navigation platforms used in ships and aircraft.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model]
**Define a gyroscope. List any two types of gyroscopes used in robotic navigation, stating one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A gyroscope is a heading sensor that detects **angular rate** or **angular displacement** by exploiting the conservation of angular momentum, the Coriolis effect, or the Sagnac effect.

| Type | Advantage |
|---|---|
| **MEMS Gyroscope** | Small size, low cost ($<\$5$), low power ŌĆö ideal for mobile robots and drones. |
| **Fiber Optic Gyro (FOG)** | No moving parts, high accuracy ($\sim 0.01\ ^\circ/\text{hr}$), immune to vibration ŌĆö ideal for autonomous vehicles. |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for types + advantages.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the Sagnac effect? Write the expression for the Sagnac phase shift and identify the variables.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sagnac effect is the phenomenon in which two coherent light beams, traversing a closed loop in opposite directions, experience a **path-length difference** proportional to the loop's rotational rate $\Omega$.

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$
where $A$ = loop area, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $c$ = speed of light, $\Omega$ = rotation rate.

*(3 marks: 1 effect, 1 formula, 1 variable list.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 End-Semester pattern, attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Ō£ģ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö MEMS & FOG Focus

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a MEMS gyroscope** based on the **Coriolis effect** with a neat block diagram. Define **scale factor** and **bias stability**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):**
   A MEMS gyro consists of a silicon proof mass suspended by compliant springs above a substrate. The mass is driven into linear oscillation along the *drive axis* (X) by an electrostatic comb-drive at its resonant frequency $\omega_{d}$. Capacitive combs on the orthogonal *sense axis* (Y) detect displacement.

2. **Working Principle ŌĆö Coriolis Effect (3 marks):**
   - When the chip rotates at $\Omega$ about the Z-axis, the vibrating mass experiences Coriolis force: $F_c = 2 m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$ along Y.
   - This excites a secondary *sense-mode* oscillation whose amplitude is proportional to $\Omega$.
   - The capacitive pickoff measures Y-displacement; synchronous demodulation recovers the rate signal.
   - Governing equation: $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot \Omega + b$.

3. **Definitions (2 marks):**
   - **Scale factor $K$** ŌĆö Slope of inputŌĆōoutput curve (V┬Ęs/┬░); indicates sensitivity.
   - **Bias stability** ŌĆö Measure of how much the offset drifts over time at zero input, typically expressed in $^\circ/\text{hr}$.

**(b)** **Derive the Sagnac phase-shift expression** for an optical gyroscope and explain how a **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)** amplifies this shift. Mention **two advantages** of FOG over RLG. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Derivation (4 marks):** (follow Section 3.3 above)
   - $t_{\text{cw}} \approx \dfrac{2\pi r}{c}\left(1 + \dfrac{r\Omega}{c}\right)$, $t_{\text{ccw}} \approx \dfrac{2\pi r}{c}\left(1 - \dfrac{r\Omega}{c}\right)$
   - $\Delta t = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c^{2}}$, $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$

2. **FOG amplification (2 marks):**
   In a fiber coil of $N$ turns, each turn contributes its own Sagnac shift; total shift = $N \times$ (single loop shift):
   $$\Delta \phi_{FOG} = \frac{8 \pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$
   With $N \sim 10^{3}\text{ŌĆō}10^{4}$ and coil length $L_c = 100\text{ŌĆō}1000$ m, sensitivity reaches $\mu$rad level.

3. **Two advantages of FOG over RLG (1 mark):**
   - No **lock-in problem** (RLG needs dithering; FOG does not).
   - Solid-state ŌåÆ rugged, immune to shock and vibration, no high-voltage discharge path needed.

---

### Ō£ģ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Mechanical & RLG Focus

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, describe the **construction and working of a mechanical gyroscope**. Derive the **precession rate** in terms of angular momentum and applied torque. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** A high-speed rotor (30,000+ rpm) is mounted in **gimbals** (inner, outer) that isolate the spin axis from base motion. Three gimbals = three rotational DOF.

2. **Working (2 marks):**
   - Rotor spin stores $\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$ along the spin axis.
   - Platform rotation applies torque $\vec{\tau}$ on the gimbals.
   - The rotor axis *precesses* at rate $\Omega_{p}$ about the torque direction.
   - Precession is the *indicator* of platform angular rate.

3. **Derivation (3 marks):** (follow Section 3.1 above)
   - $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$, geometry gives $\vert d\vec{L} \vert = L \sin\theta \, d\phi$, hence $\Omega_{p} = \dfrac{\tau}{L \sin\theta}$.

**(b)** **Explain the lock-in problem in a Ring Laser Gyro (RLG).** How is it overcome using **mechanical dithering**? Compare RLG and FOG in any **four** points. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Lock-in problem (2 marks):**
   At very low rotation rates ($\Omega < \Omega_{L}$), the two counter-propagating laser beams in an RLG **mode-lock** (couple via back-scatter from mirrors). The output becomes *non-linear* and the gyro cannot detect rates below $\sim 100\ ^\circ/\text{hr}$. This threshold is the lock-in rate $\Omega_{L} \approx \dfrac{c\lambda}{4\pi r L_c}$.

2. **Dithering (2 marks):**
   The entire RLG block is mounted on a **piezoelectric dither motor** that shakes it sinusoidally at $\sim 400$ Hz with amplitude exceeding $\Omega_{L}$. This shifts the operating point out of the lock-in band, and the *true* rotation rate is recovered by subtracting the known dither signal in post-processing.

3. **Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | RLG | FOG |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Gas laser (He-Ne) in cavity | Coiled optical fiber |
| Moving parts | Yes (dither motor) | None (solid-state) |
| Lock-in | Yes (needs dithering) | No |
| Sensitivity | Very high (navigation-grade) | High (tactical to strategic) |
| Cost | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Robustness | Vibration-sensitive | Excellent |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the Sagnac derivation ŌĆö write $A$ in m┬▓, $\Omega$ in rad/s, $\Delta\phi$ in rad.
> 2. **Confusing ARW with bias stability** ŌĆö ARW is a *white-noise* quantity ($^\circ/\sqrt{\text{hr}}$); bias stability is a *1Žā drift* ($^\circ/\text{hr}$).
> 3. **Drawing MEMS gyros as 2D rectangles** ŌĆö examiners expect a *block-level functional diagram* with Drive / Sense / Demodulator / Output blocks.
> 4. **Saying "gyroscope measures angle"** ŌĆö strictly, *rate gyros* measure $\dot{\theta}$; angle is obtained by integration.
> 5. **In the precession derivation, dropping the $\sin\theta$ factor** ŌĆö for a non-orthogonal gimbal, you must retain it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **gyroscope** is an *inertial* heading sensor measuring **angular rate** (or via integration, angle).
- [x] Three physical principles: **angular momentum (mechanical)**, **Coriolis (MEMS)**, **Sagnac (optical)**.
- [x] Key formulas: $\Omega_{p} = \dfrac{\tau}{L\sin\theta}$, $\vec{F}_{c} = 2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$, $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$.
- [x] FOG amplifies Sagnac shift by factor $N$ (number of fiber turns).
- [x] RLG suffers from **lock-in** at low rates ŌåÆ solved by **dithering**.
- [x] MEMS gyros are *cheap and small*; FOG/RLG are *accurate and expensive*.
- [x] Sensor model: $V_{\text{out}} = K \Omega + b$ (with $K$ = scale factor, $b$ = bias).
- [x] Orientation error from ARW grows as $\sigma_{\theta} = N \sqrt{t}$ ŌĆö critical for dead-reckoning accuracy.
- [x] A *three-axis gyro* (with 3-axis accelerometer) forms a **6-DOF IMU** ŌĆö used for robot attitude estimation via **complementary** or **Kalman filters**.
- [x] KTU favourite answer: "Sagnac effect is independent of the medium inside the loop; it depends only on the **enclosed area** and **rotation rate**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** An electronic device that measures and reports a body's specific force (acceleration), angular rate, and (optionally) magnetic field orientation, using a combination of **accelerometers**, **gyroscopes**, and sometimes **magnetometers**. It forms the primary dead-reckoning sensor stack for any autonomous robot, drone, AGV (Autonomous Guided Vehicle), or marine vessel.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, an IMU is treated as the **fundamental proprioceptive sensor** of a mobile robot ŌĆö one that does not depend on external infrastructure (unlike GPS or beacons) and can therefore operate in GNSS-denied environments (tunnels, underwater, indoor warehouses, space).

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Human Inner Ear

Think of the human **vestibular system** (the inner ear). It contains:

- **Otolith organs (utricle & saccule)** ŌĆö tiny hair cells under a gelatinous mass that deflect when the head accelerates linearly. This is your body's *accelerometer*.
- **Semicircular canals** ŌĆö three fluid-filled loops oriented in 3D. When you rotate your head, the fluid lags behind, deflecting hair cells. This is your body's *gyroscope*.

An IMU does exactly this in silicon: tiny MEMS proof masses deflect under acceleration, and vibrating structures (like a tuning fork) detect Coriolis forces during rotation. The robot "knows" how it is moving by feeling its own inertia, not by looking outside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** A tactical-grade IMU achieves drift on the order of **0.01 ┬░/hr**, while a MEMS consumer-grade IMU (e.g., in a smartphone) drifts around **10ŌĆō50 ┬░/hr**. This drift rate directly determines how long an IMU can navigate autonomously before error grows unbounded.

### Why IMUs Matter for Robot Navigation

Robot navigation requires continuously answering two questions:
1. *Where am I?* (Localization)
2. *Which way am I facing?* (Orientation)

An IMU provides the **raw time-integrated kinematic state** ŌĆö velocity by integrating acceleration, and orientation by integrating angular velocity. This is the mathematical backbone of the **Inertial Navigation System (INS)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Sub-Sensors

### (a) Accelerometer
Measures **specific force** $f$ ŌĆö the non-gravitational acceleration acting on the sensor. It cannot distinguish between true linear acceleration and the reaction force due to gravity.

$$f = a - g$$

where $a$ is the true acceleration vector in the inertial frame and $g$ is the gravitational vector. The accelerometer reads the force per unit mass required to accelerate the proof mass.

### (b) Gyroscope
Measures **angular velocity** $\omega$ (rad/s or ┬░/s) about its sensitive axis. MEMS gyroscopes use the **Coriolis effect**: a vibrating proof mass experiences a perpendicular force proportional to the rotation rate.

$$\vec{F}_{Coriolis} = -2m (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$$

where $m$ is the proof mass, $\omega$ is the angular velocity, and $v$ is the velocity of the proof mass.

### (c) Magnetometer (Optional, 9-DOF IMU)
Measures the Earth's magnetic field vector, providing an **absolute heading reference** to correct gyroscope drift. Often listed as part of an "AHRS" (Attitude and Heading Reference System).

## 2.2 Types of IMU Architectures

| Architecture | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Gimbaled / Platform IMU** | Sensors mounted on a stabilized platform held level by gimbals and torque motors | Strategic-grade marine/military navigation |
| **Strapdown IMU** | Sensors rigidly bolted to the vehicle body; outputs $f$ and $\omega$ directly | Almost all modern robotics, drones, phones |
| **MEMS IMU** | Micro-electro-mechanical systems; tiny silicon structures etched on a chip | Consumer, automotive, drones |
| **Ring Laser / Fiber-Optic Gyro (RLG/FOG)** | Optical Sagnac effect | Aerospace, surveying |
| **Hemispherical Resonator Gyro (HRG)** | Quartz hemispherical shell vibration | High-end tactical/space |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Equations

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Force | $f = a - g$ | m/s┬▓ | Accelerometer output |
| 2 | Coriolis Force | $F_c = 2m(\omega \times v)$ | N | MEMS gyro principle |
| 3 | Velocity (integrated) | $v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t f(\tau)\,d\tau$ | m/s | Drift grows linearly |
| 4 | Position (double integrated) | $p(t) = p_0 + \int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau$ | m | Drift grows as $t^2$ |
| 5 | Orientation update | $q_{k+1} = q_k \otimes \exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\Omega(\omega_k)\Delta t\right)$ | quaternion | Strapdown integration |
| 6 | Euler angle update | $\dot{\phi} = \omega_x,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega_y,\ \dot{\psi} = \omega_z$ | rad/s | For small rotations |
| 7 | Allan Variance noise floor | $\sigma(\tau) = \sqrt{N^2/\tau + B^2 + K^2\tau^2}$ | varies | Sensor noise characterization |
| 8 | Sagnac phase shift (FOG) | $\Delta\phi = \tfrac{8\pi A}{c\lambda}\Omega$ | rad | Optical gyro principle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam tip:** Position error from a stand-alone IMU grows as **$t^2$** because acceleration is integrated twice. This is why pure inertial navigation is *dead reckoning* ŌĆö errors compound unboundedly without external aiding (GPS, LiDAR, vision, wheel odometry).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Aerial Robotics (Drones):** IMUs run at **400ŌĆō2000 Hz** to stabilize flight controllers (PX4, Ardupilot). They feed the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) along with GPS.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** IMUs fill in the gap when GPS is lost (tunnels, urban canyons) and provide high-frequency motion data between sparse camera/LiDAR updates.
- **Marine Navigation:** Ring Laser Gyros in submarines provide heading accuracy of ~0.001┬░ without any external reference.
- **AR/VR Headsets:** IMUs track head orientation at 1000 Hz, fusing with cameras for low-latency pose estimation.
- **Spacecraft & Satellites:** Star trackers are aided by IMUs for attitude determination between star sightings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Specific Force to Position (Strapdown Mechanization)

This is the central derivation every KTU student must master. We show how the raw accelerometer and gyroscope readings are converted into position, velocity, and orientation.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Sensor Output Model

The accelerometer mounted on the vehicle (body frame $b$) measures specific force resolved in the body frame:

$$\tilde{f}^b = f^b + b_a + n_a$$

where $b_a$ is the accelerometer **bias** (a slowly varying offset) and $n_a$ is wide-band **measurement noise**. The gyroscope reads:

$$\tilde{\omega}^b_{ib} = \omega^b_{ib} + b_g + n_g$$

where $\omega^b_{ib}$ is the angular velocity of the body frame $b$ relative to the inertial frame $i$, expressed in $b$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Attitude Update (Quaternion Integration)

We rotate the body specific force into the navigation frame $n$ (typically NED ŌĆö North-East-Down):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^n &= C_b^n \, f^b \\
&= (I - [\boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n \times]\Delta t)\, (I + [\boldsymbol{\omega}_{nb}^b \times]\Delta t)\, f^b
\end{aligned}
$$

The quaternion differential equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}_b^n &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, q_b^n \otimes \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \omega_{nb}^b \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\omega)\, q_b^n
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Omega(\omega) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_x & -\omega_y & -\omega_z \\ \omega_x & 0 & \omega_z & -\omega_y \\ \omega_y & -\omega_z & 0 & \omega_x \\ \omega_z & \omega_y & -\omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

The discrete-time update is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{k+1} &= q_k \otimes \exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\,\Omega(\omega_k)\,\Delta t\right) \\
&\approx q_k \otimes \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_k \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

followed by **re-normalization** $q \leftarrow q / \Vert q \Vert$ to prevent drift in unit norm.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Velocity Update

Using Newton's law in the rotating Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed frame (neglecting Earth rotation for short durations):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{v}^n &= C_b^n f^b - g^n \\
v_{k+1}^n &= v_k^n + \left[C_b^n(k)\,f^b_k - g^n\right]\Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Position Update

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{p}^n &= v^n \\
p_{k+1}^n &= p_k^n + v_k^n \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\left[C_b^n(k)\,f^b_k - g^n\right]\Delta t^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Error Propagation (Crucial for Exam!)

If the accelerometer has a constant bias $\delta f$, the position error after time $t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta v(t) &= \delta f \cdot t \\
\delta p(t) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\delta f \cdot t^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **$t^2$ error growth**. A bias of just **0.01 m/s┬▓** (typical MEMS) gives **0.5 m** position error in **10 s**, and **45 m** in **3 minutes** ŌĆö which is why MEMS-only navigation is useless for long durations without aiding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Coriolis Force in a MEMS Gyroscope

A MEMS gyroscope consists of a proof mass $m$ oscillating with velocity $v$ along drive axis $x$. When the device rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$ about the sensitive axis $z$, the mass experiences a Coriolis force perpendicular to both $v$ and $\Omega$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{F}_{Coriolis} &= -2m\,(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}) \\
&= -2m\,(\Omega \hat{z} \times v_x \hat{x}) \\
&= -2m\Omega v_x\, \hat{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

This force displaces the proof mass along the $y$-axis. The displacement is measured capacitively and is proportional to $\Omega$, giving the angular rate measurement.

## 3.3 Allan Variance ŌĆö Sensor Noise Characterization

Allan variance $\sigma_A^2(\tau)$ is the standard tool to characterize IMU noise. For a cluster time $\tau$:

$$
\sigma_A^2(\tau) = \frac{1}{2(M-1)} \sum_{k=1}^{M-1}\left(\bar{y}_{k+1}(\tau) - \bar{y}_k(\tau)\right)^2
$$

A log-log plot of $\sigma_A(\tau)$ versus $\tau$ reveals five noise regimes:
- **Quantization Noise** ($Q$) ŌĆö slope $-1$ at small $\tau$
- **Angle Random Walk** ($N$) ŌĆö slope $-1/2$ (white noise floor)
- **Bias Instability** ($B$) ŌĆö slope $0$ (minimum point, key spec)
- **Rate Random Walk** ($K$) ŌĆö slope $+1/2$
- **Rate Ramp** ($R$) ŌĆö slope $+1$ (deterministic drift)

The **bias instability** $B$ (in ┬░/hr or m/s/ŌłÜhr) is the most cited IMU specification in datasheets.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Drift Behavior | Typical MEMS | Tactical Grade |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | Linear in time | 10ŌĆō50 ┬░/hr | 0.01 ┬░/hr |
| Velocity | $v$ | Linear in time | 0.1 m/s/ŌłÜhr bias | 0.001 m/s/ŌłÜhr |
| Position (INS) | $p$ | Quadratic in time | 5ŌĆō10 m/min | <1 m/hr |
| Orientation (gyro) | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ | Linear in time | 1┬░/min | 0.1┬░/hr |
| Acceleration noise | $N_a$ | White | 50 ┬Ąg/ŌłÜHz | 5 ┬Ąg/ŌłÜHz |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
