# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. Define Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) and list its primary components. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
An IMU is an electronic device that measures a body's specific force, angular velocity, and (optionally) magnetic field, providing **9 Degrees of Freedom (9-DOF)** when fully equipped. Its primary components are:

1. **Accelerometer (3-axis)** ŌĆö measures specific force in m/s┬▓
2. **Gyroscope (3-axis)** ŌĆö measures angular velocity in rad/s
3. **Magnetometer (3-axis)** ŌĆö measures magnetic field strength, used for absolute heading

Secondary components include temperature sensors for thermal compensation and signal-conditioning ASICs.

### Q2. Why does pure inertial navigation drift? **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
Pure inertial navigation requires **double integration** of accelerometer readings to obtain position. Any residual error ŌĆö such as accelerometer bias $\delta f$, scale-factor error, or axis misalignment ŌĆö gets integrated once into velocity (linear drift) and twice into position (**quadratic $t^2$ drift**). Similarly, gyroscope bias produces unbounded heading error. Without external aiding (GPS, vision, LiDAR), errors compound monotonically with time, making stand-alone IMUs unsuitable for long-duration navigation.

---

## Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a MEMS accelerometer with neat diagrams. Discuss its key error sources. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A MEMS accelerometer consists of a tiny silicon proof mass suspended by compliant flexures. When the device accelerates, the proof mass deflects relative to the housing due to inertia. The deflection $\Delta x$ is governed by Newton's second law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx &= -m\,a_{ext} \\
\Delta x_{steady} &= -\frac{m\,a_{ext}}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

The deflection is measured using **differential capacitance** between interdigitated comb fingers. The change in capacitance $\Delta C$ is proportional to displacement, and hence to external acceleration. Modern MEMS accelerometers (e.g., ADXL345, LSM6DSO) integrate the MEMS structure and ASIC on a single die.

**Key Error Sources:**

1. **Bias Offset** ŌĆö constant offset in output even at zero input (e.g., $\pm 50$ mg for MEMS).
2. **Scale Factor Error** ŌĆö nonlinearity between input acceleration and output voltage.
3. **Axis Misalignment** ŌĆö sensitivity axes not perfectly orthogonal (typically $< 1┬░$).
4. **Cross-axis Sensitivity** ŌĆö acceleration on one axis produces output on another.
5. **Temperature Drift** ŌĆö bias changes with temperature, requiring thermal calibration.
6. **Mechanical Shock & Vibration** ŌĆö induces high-frequency noise (vibration rectification error).

**(b)** Derive the position-error equation for an accelerometer with constant bias $\delta f$ and show that position drift grows as $t^2$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Let true specific force be $f(t)$ and measured be $\tilde{f}(t) = f(t) + \delta f$, where $\delta f$ is a constant bias.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True velocity:}\quad v(t) &= \int_0^t f(\tau)\,d\tau \\
\text{Measured velocity:}\quad \tilde{v}(t) &= \int_0^t (f(\tau) + \delta f)\,d\tau \\
\tilde{v}(t) - v(t) &= \int_0^t \delta f\,d\tau = \delta f \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

Now integrate again to get position:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True position:}\quad p(t) &= \int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau \\
\text{Measured position:}\quad \tilde{p}(t) &= \int_0^t \tilde{v}(\tau)\,d\tau \\
\delta p(t) &= \tilde{p}(t) - p(t) = \int_0^t \delta f \cdot \tau\,d\tau \\
\delta p(t) &= \frac{1}{2}\,\delta f \cdot t^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The position error grows as **$\delta p(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta f\,t^2$** ŌĆö i.e., **quadratically with time**. This is why MEMS IMUs must be fused with GPS or vision to bound error growth.

**Numerical Example:** If $\delta f = 0.01$ m/s┬▓ (typical MEMS bias), then in $t = 60$ s, the position error is $\delta p = 0.5 \times 0.01 \times 3600 = \mathbf{18\ m}$ ŌĆö unacceptable for any practical navigation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working of a MEMS gyroscope based on the Coriolis effect. Derive the expression for the Coriolis force experienced by the proof mass. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A MEMS gyroscope has a proof mass $m$ driven to oscillate along the drive axis $x$ with velocity $v_x = v_0 \sin(\omega_d t)$. The drive mechanism is electrostatic or piezoelectric.

**Operation:** When the device rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$ about the sensitive axis $z$, the proof mass experiences a **Coriolis force** perpendicular to both the drive motion and the rotation axis. This deflects the mass along the sense axis $y$, which is measured capacitively.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{F}_{Coriolis} &= -2m\,(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}) \\
\text{Let}\quad \vec{\Omega} &= \Omega \hat{z},\quad \vec{v} = v_x \hat{x} \\
\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} &= \Omega v_x\,(\hat{z} \times \hat{x}) = \Omega v_x\, \hat{y} \\
\therefore\ \vec{F}_{Coriolis} &= -2m\Omega v_x\, \hat{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

The displacement along $y$ is measured, and since $v_x$ is known from the drive signal, the rotation rate $\Omega$ is extracted. The magnitude of the sense-axis displacement is:

$$
y(t) = \frac{2\Omega\,v_0}{\omega_d} \sin(\omega_d t)
$$

Examples: Bosch BMG250, InvenSense ICM-42688, STMicroelectronics LSM6DSOX.

**(b)** What is Allan Variance? Sketch a typical log-log plot of $\sigma_A(\tau)$ versus $\tau$ and identify the five noise regimes. Explain the significance of **Bias Instability**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Allan Variance is a time-domain technique used to characterize the frequency stability and noise processes of an oscillator or inertial sensor. It is defined over cluster times $\tau = mT_0$:

$$
\sigma_A^2(\tau) = \frac{1}{2(M-1)} \sum_{k=1}^{M-1}\left(\bar{y}_{k+1}(\tau) - \bar{y}_k(\tau)\right)^2
$$

where $\bar{y}_k(\tau)$ is the average output over the $k$-th cluster.

**Five Noise Regimes** (from small $\tau$ to large $\tau$):

| Regime | Slope on log-log plot | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Quantization Noise $Q$ | $-1$ | Sensor ADC resolution limit |
| Angle Random Walk $N$ | $-1/2$ | White Gaussian noise floor |
| Bias Instability $B$ | $0$ | 1/f flicker noise, electronics drift |
| Rate Random Walk $K$ | $+1/2$ | Long-term correlated noise |
| Rate Ramp $R$ | $+1$ | Deterministic sensor aging |

**Significance of Bias Instability $B$:**
It is the **minimum point** of the Allan deviation curve and represents the best achievable bias stability of the sensor. For tactical-grade gyros, $B$ is around **0.01 ┬░/hr**; for MEMS gyros, it ranges from **1ŌĆō10 ┬░/hr**. $B$ determines the optimal averaging time over which the sensor can be trusted for integration before 1/f flicker noise dominates. Engineers use $B$ to set the Kalman filter process noise in INS/GPS fusion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing specific force with acceleration.** The accelerometer does NOT measure acceleration in the inertial frame; it measures $f = a - g$. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract gravity** in the velocity update equation. Always write $v_{k+1} = v_k + (C_b^n f^b - g^n)\Delta t$.
> 3. **Calling IMU an "odometer" or "GPS alternative".** IMUs alone cannot bound drift. State explicitly that IMUs require external aiding for long-term navigation.
> 4. **Skipping re-normalization of quaternions.** Without $q \leftarrow q / \Vert q \Vert$, numerical drift breaks the unit-norm constraint.
> 5. **Mixing up Angle Random Walk and Bias Instability.** ARW has slope $-1/2$, BI has slope $0$. State both the slope and the unit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IMU = Accelerometer + Gyroscope (+ optional Magnetometer)**. A 6-DOF IMU has 3 accelerometers + 3 gyros; a 9-DOF IMU adds a 3-axis magnetometer.
- **Accelerometer measures specific force** $f = a - g$, **not pure acceleration**. This is the #1 conceptual trap.
- **Gyroscope measures angular velocity** $\omega$ (rad/s), **not angle directly**. Angle is obtained by integration.
- **MEMS gyros use the Coriolis effect**: $\vec{F}_c = -2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$.
- **Strapdown integration** uses quaternion updates: $q_{k+1} = q_k \otimes \exp(\tfrac{1}{2}\Omega(\omega)\Delta t)$, followed by re-normalization.
- **Velocity update:** $v_{k+1} = v_k + (C_b^n f^b - g^n)\Delta t$.
- **Position update:** $p_{k+1} = p_k + v_k\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}(C_b^n f^b - g^n)\Delta t^2$.
- **Position error from accelerometer bias grows as $\delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta f\,t^2$** ŌĆö quadratic growth is why pure INS drifts.
- **Velocity error grows linearly** with time from a constant bias.
- **Allan Variance** is the standard tool to characterize IMU noise; identify **five regimes** by slope on a log-log plot.
- **Bias Instability** $B$ is the minimum of the Allan deviation curve and is the most-cited IMU spec.
- **MEMS IMUs** dominate robotics due to small size and low cost but suffer from large bias and drift; **Tactical/Strategic IMUs** (RLG, FOG) are used where drift must be minimal.
- **Sagnac effect** is the principle behind optical gyros (RLG/FOG): $\Delta\phi = \tfrac{8\pi A}{c\lambda}\Omega$.
- **IMUs alone cannot perform long-term navigation** ŌĆö they must be fused with GPS, LiDAR, cameras, or wheel odometry via a Kalman Filter (EKF/UKF).
- Typical MEMS IMU update rate: **400ŌĆō2000 Hz**; tactical-grade: **100ŌĆō1000 Hz**.
- Always remember to **subtract gravity** in the navigation-frame velocity update; this is a frequent exam marker.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Sensors - ToF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distance Sensors - ToF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 ŌĆö Distance Sensors: Time-of-Flight (ToF)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance sensor** is a non-contact ranging device that determines the distance to a target object by measuring the time interval between the emission of a signal (electromagnetic wave or acoustic wave) and the reception of its reflection from the target. The fundamental governing relation for a monostatic (co-located transmitter and receiver) ToF system is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $d$ is the line-of-sight distance to the target, $c$ is the propagation speed of the emitted signal in the medium, and $\Delta t$ is the measured round-trip time. The factor of **2** in the denominator accounts for the signal traversing the path twice (sensor ŌåÆ target ŌåÆ sensor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** ToF is classified under *Active Distance Sensors* because the sensor supplies its own illumination energy (laser pulse, IR burst, ultrasonic chirp, RF chirp), in contrast to passive sensors (e.g., cameras) that rely on ambient radiation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine standing at the edge of a deep canyon and shouting toward the opposite wall. The **echo** that bounces back carries one piece of information: *how long* did it take to return? That elapsed time, multiplied by the speed of sound and divided by two (because the sound went there *and* came back), tells you the canyon width.

A ToF sensor does the exact same thing ŌĆö but with **light (LiDAR/Laser)**, **radio (Radar)**, or **sound (Ultrasonic)** instead of your voice. Bats and dolphins perfected this technique millions of years ago; engineers simply re-implemented it with silicon photodetectors, picosecond timers, and MEMS microphones.

| Signal Medium | Carrier Wave Type | Typical Speed $c$ |
|---|---|---|
| Light in vacuum / air | EM wave | **$c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** |
| Sound in air @ 20 ┬░C | Acoustic wave | **$v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed of light is roughly one million times faster than sound.** This is why lightning is seen almost instantly but thunder arrives late ŌĆö and it is also why LiDAR must measure picosecond-scale intervals while ultrasonic sensors can comfortably work with millisecond timers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Signal Emission:** The transmitter (laser diode, IR LED, ultrasonic transducer, RF antenna) emits a short, well-defined pulse or modulated continuous wave (CW) into the environment.
2. **Free-Space Propagation:** The wavefront expands according to the inverse-square law and travels toward the target at speed $c$.
3. **Reflection / Scattering:** The target reflects (specular or diffuse) a fraction of the incident energy back toward the receiver aperture.
4. **Echo Reception:** The detector (photodiode, photomultiplier, microphone, RF mixer) intercepts the returning wavefront.
5. **Time Interval Measurement:** A high-precision chronometer (TDC ŌĆö Time-to-Digital Converter, or ADC-based phase detector) registers $\Delta t$.
6. **Distance Computation:** The embedded microcontroller evaluates $d = c \Delta t / 2$ using calibrated values of $c$ for the medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of 2?** The measured $\Delta t$ is the *round-trip* travel time. The signal covers distance $d$ going **out** and another distance $d$ coming **back**, so total path $= 2d$. Solving gives $d = c \Delta t / 2$.

### 2.2 Two Main ToF Measurement Modes

| Mode | What is Measured | Equation | Typical Sensors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pulse ToF (dToF)** | Direct time delay $\Delta t$ of a short pulse | $d = \dfrac{c \Delta t}{2}$ | LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic |
| **Continuous-Wave ToF (iToF)** | Phase shift $\varphi$ of an amplitude-modulated CW | $d = \dfrac{c \varphi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$ | PMD / iToF cameras, phase-shift laser rangefinders |

where $f_{mod}$ is the modulation frequency of the emitted carrier.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-way distance | $d = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$ | m | Monostatic ToF (single sensor) |
| 2 | Phase-shift distance | $d = \dfrac{c \, \varphi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$ | m | iToF, unambiguous range $\le \dfrac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$ |
| 3 | Maximum unambiguous range | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, PRF}$ | m | Pulse repetition frequency = PRF |
| 4 | Spatial resolution (pulse width $\tau$) | $\delta d = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | m | Two targets closer than $\delta d$ appear merged |
| 5 | Wavelength of carrier | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | Determines scattering behaviour |
| 6 | Speed of sound in air | $v \approx 331 + 0.6 \, T$ | m/s | $T$ in ┬░C |
| 7 | Round-trip-time / metre (light) | $\Delta t / m \approx 6.67$ ns | ŌĆö | Useful sanity check |
| 8 | Round-trip-time / metre (sound) | $\Delta t / m \approx 5.83$ ms | ŌĆö | Useful sanity check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** ToF is the **workhorse ranging modality** in mobile robotics ŌĆö it powers autonomous-vehicle LiDAR stacks (Velodyne, Ouster, Hesai), drone altimeters, warehouse-AMR safety scanners (SICK, Hokuyo), and even smartphone face-ID dot projectors (Apple Face-ID, structured-light iToF).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Round-Trip Distance Equation

**Given:** A monostatic ToF sensor at position $S$ emits a signal at clock time $t_0$ and the reflected echo from a target at position $T$ is detected at clock time $t_1$. The signal propagates at speed $c$ through a homogeneous medium.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Outward journey.** The signal leaves the sensor at $t_0$ and reaches the target after covering the one-way distance $\vert S - T \vert = d$. The arrival time at the target is:

$$t_{target} = t_0 + \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Instantaneous reflection (assumption).** For an ideal point target with negligible reflection delay, the wavefront is re-radiated at $t_{target}$:

$$t_{target} = t_0 + \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Return journey.** The reflected wavefront travels the same distance $d$ back to the sensor, arriving at:

$$t_1 = t_{target} + \frac{d}{c} = t_0 + \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = t_0 + \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $d$.** Subtract $t_0$ from both sides and isolate $d$:

$$\Delta t \equiv t_1 - t_0 = \frac{2d}{c}$$

$$d = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example ŌĆö LiDAR Ranging

**Problem:** A robotic LiDAR emits a 905 nm laser pulse. The return echo is detected after $\Delta t = 100$ ns. Compute the distance to the target.

**Given:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\Delta t = 100 \times 10^{-9}$ s.

**Solution:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \times (100 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ m}$$

The target is **15 metres** away.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example ŌĆö Ultrasonic Ranging with Temperature Compensation

**Problem:** An ultrasonic sensor on a mobile robot emits a 40 kHz chirp. The echo returns after $\Delta t = 25$ ms. The ambient temperature is 30 ┬░C. Calculate the distance and the wavelength of the emitted acoustic wave.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute speed of sound at 30 ┬░C:**

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute distance:**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{349 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{8.725}{2} = 4.3625 \text{ m} \approx 4.36 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{sound}}{f} = \frac{349}{40 \times 10^{3}} = 8.725 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 8.73 \text{ mm}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Optical LiDAR | Ultrasonic | Millimetre-Wave Radar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier speed $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | $\approx 343$ m/s | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| Practical range | 0.1 ŌĆō 250 m | 0.02 ŌĆō 10 m | 0.1 ŌĆō 250 m |
| Range resolution | mm-level | cm-level | cm-level |
| Beam divergence | very narrow (mrad) | wide ($\sim 15┬░$ŌĆō$30┬░$) | medium ($\sim 3┬░$ŌĆō$8┬░$) |
| Weather robustness | degraded by fog/snow | degraded by wind/temp. gradients | excellent (penetrates fog/rain) |
| Cost (2024) | high | very low | medium |
| Robotic use case | mapping, SLAM, AV perception | short-range obstacle avoidance | outdoor SLAM, ACC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid ŌĆö ToF Operational Block Architecture

```mermaid

    subgraph TX["Transmitter Chain"]
        clkA["Timing Clock / MCU"]
        drvA["Pulse Driver / Modulator"]
        emitA["Emitter LASER LED USound Antenna"]
    end

    subgraph ENV["Propagation Medium"]
        pathA["Free Space Air"]
        tgtA["Target Object"]
    end

        tdcA["TDC or Phase Detector"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["Signal Processor"]
        calcA["Distance Calculator"]
        outA["Digital Distance Output"]
    end

    clkA --> drvA --> emitA
    emitA -.->|"Emit pulse"| pathA
    pathA --> tgtA
    tgtA -.->|"Reflected echo"| detA
    detA --> ampA --> tdcA
    clkA -.->|"Start / Stop trigger"| tdcA
    tdcA --> calcA --> outA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid ŌĆö Decision Flow: Choosing a ToF Modality

```mermaid

    startA["Need distance measurement?"] --> rangeQ{"Required range?"}
    rangeQ -- "Less than 10 m, low cost" --> usA["Use Ultrasonic ToF"]

    rangeQ -- "Outdoor, fog / dust" --> radA["Use mmWave Radar ToF"]

    radA --> endA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid ŌĆö Pulse-vs-CW ToF Timing Comparison

```mermaid
    end

    subgraph CW["Continuous Wave iToF timing"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
Define a **Time-of-Flight (ToF) distance sensor**. State the fundamental distance equation and identify the two physical parameters that must be known to compute range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Time-of-Flight distance sensor is an active ranging device that measures the elapsed time between emission of a signal and reception of its echo from a target, then converts that time into a line-of-sight distance using the propagation speed of the medium. The governing equation is

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

The two parameters required are **(i)** the propagation speed $c$ of the carrier wave (light, sound, or radio) in the medium, and **(ii)** the measured round-trip time interval $\Delta t$. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
List **three advantages** and **two limitations** of LiDAR-based ToF sensors used in mobile robot navigation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Advantages:* (i) very high spatial resolution (mm-level), (ii) long measurement range up to hundreds of metres, (iii) direct generation of accurate 2D / 3D point clouds without complex post-processing. (1┬Į marks)
*Limitations:* (i) performance degrades in fog, rain, and airborne dust due to scattering, (ii) higher cost and mechanical/optical complexity compared with ultrasonic sensors. (1┬Į marks)

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

### **Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the working principle of a **pulse-based Time-of-Flight distance sensor**. Clearly state the role of each block and derive the distance equation from first principles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The pulse-based ToF system consists of a timing clock, a pulse driver, an emitter (laser / LED / ultrasonic transducer), a free-space propagation channel, a target, a detector, a Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC), and a distance calculator. The clock fires the pulse driver, which energises the emitter. The emitter launches a short pulse of energy at clock instant $t_0$. The pulse propagates through the medium at speed $c$ and strikes the target. The target reflects a portion of the energy back, which the detector intercepts at instant $t_1$. The TDC measures $\Delta t = t_1 - t_0$ and feeds it to the calculator. The derivation proceeds as in Section 3.1 above:

$$\Delta t = t_1 - t_0 = \frac{2d}{c} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$$

Marks: Block diagram ŌĆö 3, derivation steps ŌĆö 3, explanation ŌĆö 1. **Total = 7 marks**

**(b)** A scanning 2D LiDAR used on an autonomous mobile robot has a maximum unambiguous range of 60 m. If the operating wavelength is 905 nm, determine **(i)** the pulse repetition frequency (PRF), **(ii)** the maximum allowable pulse width to maintain a range resolution of 0.30 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
**(i)** For an unambiguous range $d_{max}$, the round-trip time is $\Delta t_{max} = 2 d_{max} / c$.

$$\Delta t_{max} = \frac{2 \times 60}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{120}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 400 \text{ ns}$$

The pulse repetition frequency is the reciprocal:

$$PRF = \frac{1}{\Delta t_{max}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = 2.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii)** Range resolution is governed by pulse width $\tau$:

$$\delta d = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{2 \, \delta d}{c}$$

$$\tau = \frac{2 \times 0.30}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{0.6}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ns}$$

Marks: (i) ŌĆö 3, (ii) ŌĆö 4. **Total = 7 marks**

---

### **OR**

### **Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **LiDAR, Ultrasonic, and Radar ToF sensors** under the headings: principle, range, accuracy, weather robustness, and typical robotic application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | LiDAR ToF | Ultrasonic ToF | mmWave Radar ToF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier | Near-IR laser pulse | 20ŌĆō400 kHz acoustic pulse | 76ŌĆō81 GHz FMCW / pulse |
| Range | 0.1 ŌĆō 250 m | 0.02 ŌĆō 10 m | 0.1 ŌĆō 250 m |
| Accuracy | ┬▒ 1ŌĆō3 cm | ┬▒ 1ŌĆō5 cm | ┬▒ 5ŌĆō10 cm |
| Weather robustness | poor in fog/rain | poor in wind/temperature gradients | excellent |
| Robotic application | mapping, SLAM, AV perception | short-range obstacle avoidance | outdoor SLAM, ACC | 

Marks: tabulation + commentary ŌĆö 7. **Total = 7 marks**

**(b)** An **ultrasonic ToF sensor** mounted on a service robot emits a 25 kHz pulse. The echo from a wall is received after 11.66 ms. The ambient temperature is **20 ┬░C**. Compute **(i)** the distance to the wall, **(ii)** the wavelength of the emitted wave, **(iii)** the percentage error in range if the firmware neglects temperature compensation and uses the default 343 m/s value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
**(i)** Speed of sound at 20 ┬░C:

$$v_{20} = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = 331 + 12 = 343 \text{ m/s}$$

(Note: in this case the firmware default equals the true value, so the compensation error is zero.)

Distance:

$$d = \frac{v_{20} \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 11.66 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{4.00}{2} = 2.00 \text{ m}$$

**(ii)** Wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{20}}{f} = \frac{343}{25 \times 10^{3}} = 1.372 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 13.72 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii)** Percentage error:

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\vert d_{assumed} - d_{actual} \vert}{d_{actual}} \times 100 = \frac{\vert 2.00 - 2.00 \vert}{2.00} \times 100 = 0\%$$

However, had the temperature been 30 ┬░C ($v = 349$ m/s), the same $\Delta t$ would yield:

$$d_{true} = \frac{349 \times 11.66 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.034 \text{ m} \quad \text{and} \quad d_{assumed} = 2.000 \text{ m}$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.034}{2.034} \times 100 \approx 1.67\%$$

Marks: (i) ŌĆö 2, (ii) ŌĆö 2, (iii) ŌĆö 3. **Total = 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2.** Students routinely write $d = c \Delta t$ instead of $d = c \Delta t / 2$. This is the single most common error in Module 1 papers. **Always show the round-trip argument.**
> 2. **Mixing up speed of light and speed of sound.** A LiDAR question with $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and an ultrasonic question with $v = 343$ m/s are *not interchangeable*. Markers will instantly deduct 1 mark for using the wrong constant.
> 3. **Ignoring temperature compensation.** Ultrasonic ToF accuracy falls by Ōēł 0.17 % per ┬░C. Examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark problems ŌĆö quote the formula $v = 331 + 0.6 T$.
> 4. **Skipping units in final answers.** Always write `m`, `ns`, `Hz`, `MHz` explicitly. Missing units can cost a half-mark even when the numerical value is correct.
> 5. **Confusing pulse repetition frequency (PRF) with carrier frequency.** PRF is the *rate* at which pulses are emitted; carrier frequency is the optical / acoustic frequency *within* each pulse. They differ by 6ŌĆō10 orders of magnitude.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ToF Definition:** Active range measurement based on the round-trip travel time of a wave emitted by the sensor.
- **Master Equation:** $d = c \, \Delta t / 2$ (always include the divide-by-two).
- **Two flavours:** *Pulse / direct ToF (dToF)* measures $\Delta t$ directly; *Continuous-wave / indirect ToF (iToF)* measures phase shift $\varphi$.
- **Phase-shift variant:** $d = c \, \varphi / (4 \pi f_{mod})$ with unambiguous range $c / (2 f_{mod})$.
- **Spatial resolution:** $\delta d = c \, \tau / 2$, governed by transmitted pulse width $\tau$.
- **Physical constants to memorise:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s at 20 ┬░C.
- **Temperature correction:** $v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6 \, T$ m/s, $T$ in ┬░C.
- **Unambiguous range:** $d_{max} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$.
- **Sensor families compared:**
  - *LiDAR* ŌåÆ long range, mm accuracy, fragile in fog.
  - *Ultrasonic* ŌåÆ cheap, short range, cm accuracy, weather-sensitive.
  - *mmWave Radar* ŌåÆ robust to weather, moderate accuracy, indispensable outdoors.
- **Robot-nav use cases:** obstacle avoidance, 2D/3D mapping, SLAM, terrain following, altimetry.
- **Always state the factor of 2, the units, and the medium-specific speed in your derivations.**
- **Examiner traps to avoid:** wrong $c$, missing factor of 2, mixing PRF with carrier frequency, omitting units, neglecting temperature compensation for ultrasonic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Shift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phase Shift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, **Phase Shift** is formally defined as the angular displacement (measured in radians or degrees) between two periodic signals of identical frequency, or between a transmitted and a received waveform, expressed as a fraction of the signal's full cycle. It quantifies the relative timing offset introduced when a wave propagates through a medium, reflects off a surface, or passes through an electronic/electromechanical system.

Mathematically, for a continuous sinusoidal reference signal and a measured signal of the same frequency $f$, the phase shift $\phi$ is the argument offset in their angular representations. In navigation systems, phase shift is exploited as a **high-precision proxy for time delay**, which can be converted into distance, bearing, or angular displacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, Phase Shift is treated as the foundational measurement primitive that enables **range estimation (ranging)**, **angle-of-arrival (AoA) determination**, and **encoder-based odometry** in mobile robots. Mastery of its continuous-wave (CW) and modulated forms is a direct prerequisite for the localization algorithms covered in Module 2.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two children running at the **exact same speed** around a circular track. If one child started running 10 seconds after the other, they will be at the **same angular speed** but at **different positions** on the track at any given moment. The angular "gap" between them ŌĆö measured as an arc on the circle ŌĆö is analogous to the **phase shift**.

In robotics:
- The **track** is the cycle of a transmitted radio/acoustic/optic wave.
- The **delayed child** is the returning echo or the signal reaching a secondary antenna.
- The **arc gap** is the phase shift, which tells the robot *how much farther* the signal had to travel.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| $v_{s}$ | Speed of sound in air (20┬░C) | **343 m/s** |
| $f$ | Carrier frequency | Hz (varies by sensor) |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength | m |
| $\phi$ | Phase shift | radians (or degrees) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems frequently use $v_{s} = 343$ m/s for ultrasonic/narrowband acoustic sensors and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for RF/optic systems. Always read the question carefully for the medium.

### 1.4 Why Phase Shift Matters in Robot Navigation

A robot must answer three continuous questions: *Where am I? Where am I going? How do I get there?* Phase shift is one of the most precise, low-latency physical observables a robot can use to answer these:

1. **Ranging (distance measurement):** A transmitted continuous wave reflects off an obstacle; the round-trip phase shift reveals the distance with sub-millimeter precision (limited only by the wavelength).
2. **Bearings (angle of arrival):** Two antennas separated by a known baseline observe the same incoming wave; the inter-antenna phase difference directly yields the angle of incidence.
3. **Wheel Odometry:** Optical or magnetic encoders on wheel shafts produce pulse trains whose **phase relationship** between channels A and B (the quadrature phase) indicates direction of rotation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematics of a Phase-Shifted Signal

Any narrowband signal can be written as a sinusoid. A reference transmitted wave is:

$$s_{t}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f t)$$

After reflecting from a target at distance $d$ (or being received by an antenna after a propagation delay $\tau$), the wave becomes:

$$s_{r}(t) = A_{r} \cos(2\pi f (t - \tau)) = A_{r} \cos(2\pi f t - \phi)$$

The argument $- \phi$ inside the cosine is the **phase shift**, and by the chain rule of trigonometry:

$$\phi = 2\pi f \tau$$

This single relationship is the cornerstone of every phase-based navigation sensor.

### 2.2 Structured Logic: How a Robot Uses Phase Shift

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Excite the medium:** The robot (or an infrastructure beacon) emits a known continuous-wave (CW) or modulated carrier at frequency $f$.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Capture the delayed replica:** A receiver on the robot (or a synchronized receiver) captures the wave after it has traveled.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Mix / Correlate:** A mixer multiplies the incoming signal by a local replica. Low-pass filtering leaves only a term proportional to $\cos(\phi)$ or $\sin(\phi)$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Recover $\phi$:** An arctangent operation (in I/Q demodulators) recovers the unambiguous phase from the in-phase and quadrature components.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to physical quantity:** Substitute $\phi$ into the appropriate geometric model (range, AoA, encoder angle) to obtain the desired navigation observable.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" Box:** The I/Q (In-phase/Quadrature) demodulation is needed because $\cos^{-1}$ alone cannot distinguish between $+\phi$ and $-\phi$. Using both $I = \cos\phi$ and $Q = \sin\phi$, the $\text{atan2}(Q, I)$ function recovers the full 360┬░ with correct sign.

### 2.3 Three Navigation Applications of Phase Shift

#### A) Continuous-Wave (CW) Phase-Based Ranging
Used when time-of-flight is too short to measure directly (e.g., short-range RF or laser interferometry). A phase shift of one full $2\pi$ corresponds to one wavelength of travel.

$$d = \frac{\phi}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{\phi \, c}{4\pi f}$$

(The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for round-trip travel.)

#### B) Phase Difference of Arrival (PDOA / Interferometry)
For bearing estimation, two antennas separated by a baseline $L$ observe the same far-field wave arriving at angle $\theta$ off the broadside. The path difference $\Delta r = L \sin\theta$ produces a phase difference:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi L \sin\theta}{\lambda}$$

Solving for the bearing:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda \, \Delta\phi}{2\pi L}\right)$$

#### C) Quadrature Encoder Phase (Odometry)
Optical incremental encoders emit two pulse trains, A and B, that are 90┬░ (a quarter cycle) out of phase ŌĆö hence "quadrature". By examining which channel leads the other, the robot's firmware determines forward vs. reverse rotation. Each full cycle of channel A corresponds to one fixed angular increment $\Delta\theta = 2\pi / N$, where $N$ is the lines-per-revolution count.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\phi = 2\pi f \tau$ | Phase shift from time delay |
| 2 | $\tau = d / v$ | Time delay from distance and propagation speed $v$ |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{\phi \, c}{4\pi f}$ | CW range from round-trip phase |
| 4 | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi L \sin\theta}{\lambda}$ | Interferometric AoA phase difference |
| 5 | $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda \, \Delta\phi}{2\pi L}\right)$ | Bearing from PDOA |
| 6 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength |
| 7 | $\Delta\theta_{\text{enc}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | Encoder angular resolution per pulse cycle |
| 8 | $v_{\text{wheel}} = r \cdot \dot{\phi}_{\text{enc}}$ | Wheel linear velocity from encoder phase rate |
| 9 | $f_{\text{Doppler}} = \dfrac{2 v \cos\alpha}{\lambda}$ | Doppler frequency (phase rate due to motion) |
| 10 | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | Unambiguous range for FMCW radars |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Radar (77ŌĆō81 GHz):** Uses FMCW chirps; the **beat frequency's phase** discriminates objects separated by sub-cm distances, enabling ADAS and autonomous vehicles.
- **Indoor BLE/UWB Beacons:** Phase-based ranging complements time-of-flight for centimeter-accurate indoor positioning in warehouses and hospitals.
- **LiDAR Interferometry:** Dual-frequency laser phase comparison enables vibration-tolerant terrain mapping for aerial and space robots.
- **Encoder-based SLAM:** Every wheel encoder is, at its core, a phase-shift counter. The integrated phase history forms the odometry backbone of virtually every differential-drive mobile robot.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Distance from Round-Trip Phase Shift

**Problem Statement:** A mobile robot emits a 2.4 GHz continuous wave toward a wall. The reflected wave is received with a phase shift of $\phi = 2.4\pi$ radians relative to the transmitted signal. Compute the wall's distance. (Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

We start with the fundamental phase-delay relationship for a one-way trip:

$$\phi_{\text{one-way}} = 2\pi f \tau$$

For a reflected (round-trip) wave, the signal traverses the distance $d$ twice, so the total time delay is:

$$\tau = \frac{2d}{c}$$

Substituting into the one-way phase relation, the **total observed phase shift** becomes:

$$\phi = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f d}{c}$$

This is rearranged for the unknown distance $d$:

$$d = \frac{\phi \, c}{4\pi f}$$

**Numerical evaluation** (with $f = 2.4 \times 10^9$ Hz and $\phi = 2.4\pi$ rad):

$$d = \frac{(2.4\pi)(3 \times 10^8)}{4\pi (2.4 \times 10^9)}$$

The $\pi$ terms and the $2.4$ factors all cancel cleanly:

$$d = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^9} = \frac{3}{40} = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d = 7.5 \text{ cm}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II: Angle of Arrival from Phase Difference of Arrival (PDOA)

**Problem Statement:** A robot has two antennas spaced $L = 0.10$ m apart, receiving a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi signal from an access point. The measured inter-antenna phase difference is $\Delta\phi = 1.2$ rad. Find the angle of arrival $\theta$ relative to the antenna broadside.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The wave arrives at angle $\theta$ from the broadside. The extra path length to the further antenna is the projection of the baseline onto the direction of arrival:

$$\Delta r = L \sin\theta$$

This extra path corresponds to a phase difference:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi \Delta r}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi L \sin\theta}{\lambda}$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda \, \Delta\phi}{2\pi L}\right)$$

We first compute the wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

Substituting the numbers:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{(0.125)(1.2)}{2\pi (0.10)}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.15}{0.6283}\right)$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.2387) = 13.81┬░$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 13.8┬░}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III: Ambiguity Range of Phase-Based Ranging

A critical limitation in phase-based ranging is the **$2\pi$ ambiguity**: the receiver can only measure $\phi$ modulo $2\pi$, so any integer multiple of $2\pi$ added to the true phase yields the same measurement. The corresponding maximum unambiguous range $R_{\max}$ is reached when the round-trip phase accumulates exactly $2\pi$:

$$\frac{4\pi f R_{\max}}{c} = 2\pi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_{\max} = \frac{c}{2f} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Numerical Check** for $f = 2.4$ GHz:

$$R_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.0625 \text{ m} = 6.25 \text{ cm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why pure CW ranging at 2.4 GHz is unusable beyond ~6 cm! Practical systems (FMCW radar, UWB impulse radios) introduce frequency modulation or pulse timing to extend the unambiguous range while keeping the fine phase resolution of the carrier.

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Encoder Tick ŌåÆ Wheel Displacement

**Problem Statement:** A differential-drive robot has wheels of radius $r = 0.05$ m. Each wheel carries an incremental encoder with $N = 500$ lines per revolution. The microcontroller counts $n = 125$ phase cycles of the encoder's I/Q signal. Compute the linear distance traveled by one wheel.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Each line on the encoder disk produces one full electrical cycle (one A-channel rising edge to the next). The angular resolution per cycle is:

$$\Delta\theta_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{2\pi}{N} = \frac{2\pi}{500} \text{ rad}$$

For $n$ counted cycles, the wheel rotates by:

$$\theta_{\text{wheel}} = n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N} = 125 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{500} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$

The arc length (linear distance) on the wheel surface is:

$$s = r \cdot \theta_{\text{wheel}} = 0.05 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = 0.025\pi \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{s \approx 0.0785 \text{ m} = 7.85 \text{ cm}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Phase-Shift Modality

| Modality | Best Range | Best Resolution | Limitation | KTU Exam Tip |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CW RF Phase | $\lambda / 2$ | sub-mm | Severe ambiguity | Show $\lambda/2$ derivation |
| FMCW Chirp | $c / (2 \Delta f)$ | mm to cm | Sweep linearity | State $R_{\max}$ formula |
| Ultrasonic PDOA | tens of m | cm | Multipath in rooms | Use $v_{s} = 343$ m/s |
| Optical Encoder | unbounded (by counting) | $\leq 0.01┬░$ | Slip on wheels | Quote $2\pi / N$ per pulse |
| UWB Impulse | $10$ŌĆō$100$ m | cm | Regulatory limits | Mention IEEE 802.15.4z |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Phase-Shift-Based Ranging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Osc[Oscillator f] --> Splitter[Power Splitter]
    Splitter --> TX[Transmit Antenna]
    Splitter --> Mixer[Reference Mixer Port]
    TX -. Wave propagation .-> Target[Target Object]
    Target -. Reflected wave .-> RX[Receive Antenna]
    RX --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    LNA --> Mixer
    Mixer --> LPF[Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> ADC[I and Q ADC]
    ADC --> DSP[Phase Estimator atan2 Q I]
    DSP --> Range[Range Output d]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Quadrature Encoder Signal Flow (Odometry)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Disc[Encoder Disk with N lines] --> ChA[Channel A Photodiode]
    Disc --> ChB[Channel B Photodiode]
    ChA --> CompA[Comparator Schmitt Trigger]
    ChB --> CompB[Comparator Schmitt Trigger]
    CompA --> Quad[Quadrature Decoder Logic]
    CompB --> Quad
    Quad --> Count[Up Down Counter]
    Count --> Tick[Position Delta per Tick]
    Tick --> Odom[Wheel Odometry Module]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Phase Difference of Arrival Angle Finder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source[Far Field Emitter] --> Wave[Incoming Wavefront angle theta]
    Wave --> Ant1[Antenna 1]
    Wave --> Ant2[Antenna 2 baseline L]
    Ant1 --> RX1[Receiver 1]
    Ant2 --> RX2[Receiver 2]
    RX1 --> Ph1[Phase Measurement phi1]
    RX2 --> Ph2[Phase Measurement phi2]
    Ph1 --> Sub[Subtracter]
    Ph2 --> Sub
    Sub --> Delta[Delta phi]
    Delta --> AoA[Angle of Arrival theta = asin lambda dphi over 2 pi L]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Architecture: Phase-Shift Subsystems Inside a Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhysLayer[Physical Layer Sensors]

    end

        Demod[I and Q Demodulation]

        Filt[Kalman Filter on phi]
    end
    subgraph NavLayer[Navigation Layer]
        Odom[Odometry]
        Local[Localization]
        Map[Mapping]
        Plan[Path Planning]
    end
    Enc --> Demod
    Sonar --> Demod

    Lidar --> Demod
    Demod --> Unwrap
    Unwrap --> Filt
    Filt --> Odom
    Filt --> Local
    Odom --> Map
    Local --> Plan
```
