## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "phase shift" in the context of sinusoidal signals. How is it mathematically related to the time delay and the frequency of the signal?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Phase shift $\phi$ is the angular offset, expressed in radians, between two sinusoidal signals of identical frequency, or between a transmitted wave and its received replica. It arises because the received signal is delayed in time by $\tau$ relative to the transmitted one. The relationship is $\phi = 2\pi f \tau$, where $f$ is the carrier frequency. **(1 mark)** for the definition, **(1 mark)** for the formula, **(1 mark)** for explaining the cause (time delay).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Sample]**
*A robot uses an incremental optical encoder with 360 lines per revolution on a wheel of radius 4 cm. How many encoder pulses must be counted to confirm that the wheel has rotated through exactly 90┬░?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Angular resolution per pulse = $360┬░ / 360 = 1┬░$ per line **(1 mark)**. For 90┬░ rotation, the required number of pulses is $90 / 1 = 90$ pulses **(1 mark)**. In terms of phase cycles, this corresponds to 90 full A-channel cycles, equivalent to 360 quadrature counts (since each cycle yields 4 counts) **(1 mark)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the distance $d$ between a robot and a target, given that the robot transmits a continuous wave of frequency $f$ and measures a round-trip phase shift $\phi$ on the reflected wave. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot operates a 5.8 GHz CW radar for obstacle detection. The receiver measures a round-trip phase of $3.6\pi$ radians. Determine: (i) the distance to the obstacle, and (ii) the maximum unambiguous range of this sensor. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the transmitted signal be $s_t(t) = A\cos(2\pi f t)$. The wave propagates to the target at distance $d$ and returns, incurring a total time delay of:

$$\tau = \frac{2d}{c} \quad \text{(2 marks ŌĆö assumption: line-of-sight, c = speed of light)}$$

The received signal is therefore:

$$s_r(t) = A_r\cos(2\pi f(t - \tau)) = A_r\cos(2\pi f t - \phi) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

By definition, the phase shift is:

$$\phi = 2\pi f \tau \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Substituting $\tau$:

$$\phi = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f d}{c} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \frac{\phi c}{4\pi f} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i)** Substituting $f = 5.8 \times 10^9$ Hz, $\phi = 3.6\pi$ rad:

$$d = \frac{(3.6\pi)(3 \times 10^8)}{4\pi (5.8 \times 10^9)} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

$$d = \frac{3.6 \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 5.8 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^9}{2.32 \times 10^{10}} = 0.04655 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 4.66 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}}$$

**(ii)** The maximum unambiguous range corresponds to $\phi = 2\pi$:

$$R_{\max} = \frac{c}{2f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5.8 \times 10^9} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$R_{\max} \approx 0.02586 \text{ m} = 2.59 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Conclusion:** The measured 4.66 cm exceeds the unambiguous range, meaning the true distance is wrapped around. KTU systems typically resolve this by sweeping the frequency (FMCW).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Phase Difference of Arrival (PDOA) for angle-of-arrival estimation. Derive the expression for the bearing angle $\theta$ in terms of the baseline $L$, the wavelength $\lambda$, and the measured inter-antenna phase difference $\Delta\phi$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dual-antenna robot receiver (baseline $L = 0.25$ m) operates at 900 MHz. It receives a signal from a far-field source with an inter-antenna phase difference of $\Delta\phi = 1.05$ rad. Find: (i) the angle of arrival relative to broadside, and (ii) the maximum baseline $L_{\max}$ that can be used without introducing a $2\pi$ phase ambiguity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and Derivation (7 marks):**

PDOA uses two (or more) antennas separated by a known baseline $L$. A plane wave arriving from bearing $\theta$ relative to the antenna broadside travels an extra geometric path $\Delta r$ to the further antenna **(1 mark)**.

Geometric projection gives:

$$\Delta r = L \sin\theta \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

This extra path corresponds to a phase lead/lag of:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi \Delta r}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi L \sin\theta}{\lambda} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda \, \Delta\phi}{2\pi L}\right) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Assumption: far-field (plane-wave) source so that wavefronts are parallel across the baseline **(1 mark)**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Wavelength: $\lambda = c / f = (3 \times 10^8) / (900 \times 10^6) = 0.333$ m **(1 mark)**.

**(i)** Substituting $\Delta\phi = 1.05$ rad and $L = 0.25$ m:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{(0.333)(1.05)}{2\pi (0.25)}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.35}{1.5708}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.2228) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 12.88┬░ \quad \text{(1 mark)}}$$

**(ii)** To avoid $2\pi$ phase ambiguity, the maximum allowed phase difference is $\Delta\phi = \pi$ (any larger would wrap into a different cycle) **(1 mark)**. The maximum baseline that produces at most $\pi$ phase difference at end-fire ($\theta = 90┬░$, where $\sin\theta = 1$) is found from:

$$L_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{0.333}{2} = 0.1667 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in round-trip CW ranging.** Many students write $d = \phi c / (2\pi f)$ instead of $d = \phi c / (4\pi f)$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Using degrees instead of radians** in the arctan2 / arcsin step of PDOA. Always confirm the calculator is in radian mode when computing $\theta$.
> 3. **Ignoring the $\lambda / 2$ ambiguity condition.** If $L \geq \lambda / 2$, the bearing becomes ambiguous. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you do not state this restriction.
> 4. **Confusing phase cycles with quadrature counts** in encoder problems. One full A-channel cycle = 4 quadrature counts. State this explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phase shift = $2\pi f \tau$** ŌĆö the single most important relationship in this module.
- **Round-trip CW range:** $d = \phi c / (4\pi f)$; remember the factor of 2 for the return path.
- **PDOA bearing:** $\theta = \sin^{-1}(\lambda \Delta\phi / (2\pi L))$; valid for $L \leq \lambda / 2$ to avoid ambiguity.
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f$ for RF/optic, $\lambda = v_s / f$ for acoustic ($v_s \approx 343$ m/s).
- **Maximum unambiguous range** for pure CW: $R_{\max} = \lambda / 2$, hence FMCW is used in real radars.
- **Encoders:** One full A-channel pulse cycle corresponds to $2\pi / N$ rad of wheel rotation, where $N$ is the encoder's lines-per-revolution.
- **Direction detection** in encoders is done by the **90┬░ phase relationship** between channels A and B (quadrature decoding).
- **Doppler shift** is just a *time-varying phase*: $f_D = \dot{\phi} / (2\pi)$.
- **Units checklist:** phase in **rad**, frequency in **Hz**, distance in **m**, baseline in **m**, $\lambda$ in **m**.
- **Assumption box:** Always state "line-of-sight, far-field, narrowband" assumptions before writing a phase-based formula ŌĆö examiners award marks for these.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic ŌĆö "P.R.E.S.S.":** **P**ropagation delay $\rightarrow$ **R**adian shift $\rightarrow$ **E**xtract via I/Q $\rightarrow$ **S**ubstitute geometry $\rightarrow$ **S**olve for unknown. This five-step pipeline applies to *every* phase-based navigation problem KTU can set.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triangulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triangulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Triangulation** is a geometric localization technique in robot navigation where the position of an unknown point (the robot) is determined by measuring angles to two or more known reference points (landmarks or beacons) whose positions are already known in a global reference frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Triangulation is the process of determining the location of a point by measuring the angles to that point from two known points at either end of a fixed baseline, rather than measuring distances directly. It is a purely angular, range-free localization strategy fundamentally rooted in the geometry of triangles.*

Formally, given:
- Two landmarks $L_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $L_2(x_2, y_2)$ in a global 2D coordinate frame.
- Two angular measurements $\alpha$ (angle measured at $L_1$ relative to the baseline $\overline{L_1 L_2}$) and $\beta$ (angle measured at $L_2$ relative to the same baseline).

The robot's global position $R(x, y)$ is uniquely determined as the third vertex of the triangle $\triangle L_1 R L_2$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Forest Explorer Analogy:**
> Imagine you are lost in a forest at night. You spot two lighthouses on a coastline whose GPS coordinates you memorized earlier. You cannot walk to them to measure how far away they are, but you have a compass. You measure the compass bearing to Lighthouse A, then turn and measure the bearing to Lighthouse B. If you draw a line on your map from Lighthouse A in the direction you saw it, and another line from Lighthouse B in the direction you saw it, **the point where those two lines cross is exactly where you are standing**. That intersection method is Triangulation. The robot replaces your eyes and compass with onboard sensors (cameras, lidar, sonar) that return angles to known beacons.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Geometric Constants

- **Baseline ($b$):** The known distance between the two reference landmarks, expressed in **meters (m)**. Standard values in indoor robotics: $b \in [0.5, 10] \text{ m}$.
- **Angles ($\alpha, \beta$):** Measured in **radians (rad)** for computation or **degrees (┬░)** for human interpretation. Conversion: $1^\circ = \pi/180 \text{ rad}$.
- **Angular Resolution:** The smallest distinguishable angle by the sensor, typically in the range of **0.1┬░ to 1┬░** for modern encoders, vision systems, and phased-array sonars.

### 1.4 Triangulation vs. Trilateration ŌĆö A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Triangulation | Trilateration |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Quantity | **Angles (bearing)** | **Distances (range)** |
| Geometric Shape | Triangle of angles | Triangle of sides |
| Sensor Type | Camera, compass, sonar array | LiDAR, UWB, RSSI, wheel odometry |
| Sensitivity | Highly sensitive to angular error at long range | Sensitive to range error at short range |
| Indoor Use | Common (vision-based) | Common (UWB, BLE) |
| Outdoor Use | Common (theodolites, GPS-like) | Common (satellite ranging) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"Tri-An-Gulation uses ANGLES, Tri-Lat-Eration uses LATERAL distances."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles ŌĆö The "Why" and "How"

The mathematical bedrock of triangulation is the **Angle-Side-Angle (ASA) congruence postulate** in Euclidean geometry. This postulate guarantees that if we know:

1. The length of one side of a triangle (the **baseline** $b$ between $L_1$ and $L_2$).
2. The two angles adjacent to that side ($\alpha$ at $L_1$ and $\beta$ at $L_2$).

ŌĆ”then the triangle is **uniquely determined up to reflection**, meaning the position of the third vertex $R$ is fixed.

#### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Known World Anchor**: Two landmarks $L_1$ and $L_2$ are surveyed in advance and stored in the robot's map. Their coordinates $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ are constants.
2. **Baseline Vector Computation**: The system computes the baseline vector $\vec{b} = L_2 - L_1$ and its length $b = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$.
3. **Baseline Orientation**: The bearing (compass direction) of the baseline is computed as $\theta_{12} = \text{atan2}(y_2 - y_1, x_2 - x_1)$.
4. **Angular Observation**: The robot's sensor measures the absolute bearing $\theta_1$ to $L_1$ and $\theta_2$ to $L_2$. The relative angles are $\alpha = \theta_{12} - \theta_1$ and $\beta = \theta_2 - \theta_{12}$ (with appropriate sign conventions).
5. **Geometric Resolution**: Apply the **Law of Sines** to find distances $d_1 = \overline{L_1 R}$ and $d_2 = \overline{L_2 R}$.
6. **Coordinate Reconstruction**: Convert polar offsets back to Cartesian coordinates to yield $(x, y)$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use angles, not distances?** Angular sensors (cameras, compasses, beam-steering sonars) are often cheaper, smaller, and consume less power than ranging sensors. Angle measurement is independent of the target's reflectance or material properties.
- **Why two landmarks and not one?** A single angle gives a ray (an infinite set of possible positions). Two rays intersect at a single point, removing ambiguity.
- **Why a third landmark is sometimes used?** To resolve the **mirror-image ambiguity** (the geometric solution has two possible intersection points, symmetric about the baseline). A third observation collapses this to a single correct point.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $b = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ | **Baseline length** between landmarks | m |
| 2 | $\theta_{12} = \text{atan2}(y_2-y_1, x_2-x_1)$ | **Bearing of baseline** $L_1 \rightarrow L_2$ | rad |
| 3 | $\alpha = \theta_{12} - \theta_1$ | **Angle at landmark $L_1$** | rad |
| 4 | $\beta = \theta_2 - \theta_{12}$ | **Angle at landmark $L_2$** | rad |
| 5 | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha - \beta$ | **Interior angle at robot $R$** | rad |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d_1}{\sin \beta} = \dfrac{d_2}{\sin \alpha} = \dfrac{b}{\sin \gamma}$ | **Law of Sines** for triangle $\triangle L_1 R L_2$ | ŌĆö |
| 7 | $d_1 = \dfrac{b \sin \beta}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}$ | **Range from $L_1$ to $R$** | m |
| 8 | $d_2 = \dfrac{b \sin \alpha}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}$ | **Range from $L_2$ to $R$** | m |
| 9 | $x = x_1 + d_1 \cos(\theta_1)$, $\;y = y_1 + d_1 \sin(\theta_1)$ | **Cartesian position of $R$** | m |
| 10 | $\sigma_x \approx \dfrac{d_1^2 \sigma_\alpha}{b \sin^2(\alpha+\beta)}$ | **Position uncertainty** (linearized error propagation) | m |
| 11 | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}/\sigma_\alpha$ | **Geometric Dilution of Precision** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Tip:** In the formula sheet, never confuse the *interior angle* $\gamma$ at the robot with the *measured angles* $\alpha, \beta$ at the landmarks. $\gamma$ is computed, not measured. A common 1-mark loss in KTU valuation is writing $\gamma = \alpha + \beta$ instead of $\gamma = \pi - (\alpha + \beta)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS Augmentation (Pseudolite Systems):** Indoor GPS receivers triangulate from pseudolites fixed on ceilings.
- **Marine & Aerial Surveying:** Theodolites and total stations have used triangulation since the 19th century.
- **Robot Soccer (RoboCup):** Vision-based localization of the ball relative to field landmarks.
- **AR/VR Headsets:** Inside-out tracking using corner features of a room.
- **Drone Swarms:** Each drone acts as a moving landmark for its neighbors, enabling **relative triangulation** without GPS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Position from Two Angular Measurements (ASA Method)

**Problem Setup:** Given two known landmarks $L_1 = (0, 0)$ and $L_2 = (b, 0)$ placed conveniently on the x-axis, and the robot measures angles $\alpha$ (at $L_1$, measured from the +x baseline to the line $L_1 R$) and $\beta$ (at $L_2$, measured from the ŌłÆx baseline to the line $L_2 R$). Derive the robot's coordinates $(x, y)$.

**Step 1: Identify the Triangle.**
The three vertices are $L_1(0,0)$, $L_2(b, 0)$, and $R(x, y)$. The interior angle at $R$ is the angle $\angle L_1 R L_2$, denoted $\gamma$.

**Step 2: Apply the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem.**
The sum of interior angles in any planar triangle equals $\pi$ radians (180┬░).

$$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$$

**Step 3: Isolate the angle at the robot.**

$$\gamma = \pi - \alpha - \beta$$

**Step 4: Apply the Law of Sines.**
The Law of Sines states that for any triangle with sides $a, b, c$ opposite to angles $A, B, C$:

$$\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C}$$

In our triangle, side $b$ (the baseline $\overline{L_1 L_2}$) is opposite to angle $\gamma$ at $R$. Side $d_1 = \overline{L_1 R}$ is opposite to $\beta$ at $L_2$. Side $d_2 = \overline{L_2 R}$ is opposite to $\alpha$ at $L_1$.

$$\frac{d_1}{\sin \beta} = \frac{d_2}{\sin \alpha} = \frac{b}{\sin \gamma}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $d_1$.**

$$d_1 = \frac{b \sin \beta}{\sin \gamma}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $\gamma = \pi - \alpha - \beta$ and apply the co-function identity.**
Recall that $\sin(\pi - \theta) = \sin \theta$. Therefore:

$$\sin \gamma = \sin(\pi - \alpha - \beta) = \sin(\alpha + \beta)$$

Thus:

$$d_1 = \frac{b \sin \beta}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}$$

**Step 7: Compute the robot's coordinates using the polar-to-Cartesian transform from $L_1$.**
The line from $L_1$ to $R$ makes an angle $\alpha$ with the +x axis (since $L_1$ is at the origin and the baseline lies along the +x direction). Therefore:

$$x = 0 + d_1 \cos \alpha = \frac{b \sin \beta \cos \alpha}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}$$

$$y = 0 + d_1 \sin \alpha = \frac{b \sin \beta \sin \alpha}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}$$

**Step 8: Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\;x = \frac{b \sin \beta \cos \alpha}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}, \quad y = \frac{b \sin \beta \sin \alpha}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check:** As $\alpha \to 0$, the robot is along the baseline extension. Then $y \to 0$ and $x \to b \sin \beta / \sin \beta = b$, which is correct (robot is at $L_2$). As $\beta \to 0$, $y \to 0$ and $x \to 0$, which is correct (robot is at $L_1$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Position from Two Bearings (Line-Intersection Method)

**Problem Setup:** Given two known landmarks $L_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $L_2(x_2, y_2)$, and the robot measures the absolute bearings $\theta_1$ (to $L_1$) and $\theta_2$ (to $L_2$) in the global frame. Derive $(x, y)$ via the intersection of two bearing lines.

**Step 1: Bearing Line Equations.**
A line passing through $(x_0, y_0)$ at bearing $\theta$ is parameterized by the slope $\tan \theta$. In implicit form:

$$(y - y_0) \cos \theta = (x - x_0) \sin \theta$$

This is preferred over slope-intercept form because $\tan \theta$ is undefined when $\theta = \pi/2$.

**Step 2: Write the equation of the line passing through $L_1$ at bearing $\theta_1$.**

$$(y - y_1) \cos \theta_1 = (x - x_1) \sin \theta_1 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 3: Write the equation of the line passing through $L_2$ at bearing $\theta_2$.**

$$(y - y_2) \cos \theta_2 = (x - x_2) \sin \theta_2 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 4: Formulate the 2├Ś2 Linear System.**
Rearranging (I) and (II) into standard form $Ax + By = C$:

$$\text{(I):} \quad x \sin \theta_1 - y \cos \theta_1 = x_1 \sin \theta_1 - y_1 \cos \theta_1$$

$$\text{(II):} \quad x \sin \theta_2 - y \cos \theta_2 = x_2 \sin \theta_2 - y_2 \cos \theta_2$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sin \theta_1 & -\cos \theta_1 \\ \sin \theta_2 & -\cos \theta_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \sin \theta_1 - y_1 \cos \theta_1 \\ x_2 \sin \theta_2 - y_2 \cos \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Apply Cramer's Rule.**
The determinant of the coefficient matrix is:

$$D = \sin \theta_1 (-\cos \theta_2) - \sin \theta_2 (-\cos \theta_1) = \sin \theta_2 \cos \theta_1 - \sin \theta_1 \cos \theta_2 = \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_1)$$

This determinant is non-zero provided the two bearing lines are not parallel, which is the geometric condition for a unique intersection.

**Step 6: Compute the numerator determinants.**

$$D_x = (x_1 \sin \theta_1 - y_1 \cos \theta_1)(-\cos \theta_2) - (x_2 \sin \theta_2 - y_2 \cos \theta_2)(-\cos \theta_1)$$

$$D_x = -x_1 \sin \theta_1 \cos \theta_2 + y_1 \cos \theta_1 \cos \theta_2 + x_2 \sin \theta_2 \cos \theta_1 - y_2 \cos \theta_2 \cos \theta_1$$

**Step 7: Simplify and solve.**

$$\boxed{\;x = \frac{D_x}{\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}, \quad y = \frac{D_y}{\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}\;}$$

where $D_y$ is computed analogously by replacing the second column of the coefficient matrix with the RHS vector.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students frequently divide by $\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_1)$ without checking if the bearings are nearly parallel ($\theta_2 \approx \theta_1 + n\pi$). This leads to numerical instability and "**blow-up**" of the computed position. Always verify that $\vert \theta_2 - \theta_1 \vert > \epsilon$ for some small $\epsilon \approx 10^{-4}$ rad.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Uncertainty Propagation (GDOP)

**Why this matters for KTU:** The Examiner frequently awards a 7-mark question that requires you to derive the **positional uncertainty** of a triangulated fix, given the angular noise of the sensor.

**Setup:** Assume angle measurements have independent Gaussian noise with standard deviation $\sigma_\alpha = \sigma_\beta = \sigma_\theta$. The position $(x, y)$ depends on the angles. We seek $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ via the **linearized error propagation formula**:

$$\sigma_x^2 \approx \left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial \alpha}\right)^2 \sigma_\alpha^2 + \left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial \beta}\right)^2 \sigma_\beta^2$$

**Step 1: Express position in symmetric form using both landmarks.**

$$x = x_1 + d_1 \cos \alpha, \quad \text{where } d_1 = \frac{b \sin \beta}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\partial x / \partial \beta$.** Treat $\alpha$ as constant, differentiate $d_1$ with respect to $\beta$:

$$\frac{\partial d_1}{\partial \beta} = b \cdot \frac{\cos \beta \sin(\alpha+\beta) - \sin \beta \cos(\alpha+\beta)}{\sin^2(\alpha+\beta)} = \frac{b \sin \alpha}{\sin^2(\alpha+\beta)}$$

**Step 3: Form the partial derivative of $x$.**

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \beta} = \frac{\partial d_1}{\partial \beta} \cos \alpha = \frac{b \sin \alpha \cos \alpha}{\sin^2(\alpha+\beta)}$$

**Step 4: Form $\partial x / \partial \alpha$ analogously.** Differentiate both $d_1(\beta)$ and the $\cos \alpha$ factor.

After full expansion (and assuming $\sigma_\alpha = \sigma_\beta = \sigma_\theta$):

$$\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 \approx \frac{d_1^2 \sigma_\theta^2}{\sin^2(\alpha+\beta)}$$

**Step 5: Define GDOP.**

$$\text{GDOP} = \frac{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}}{\sigma_\theta} = \frac{d_1}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The position error is minimized when $\alpha + \beta = \pi/2$, i.e., when the triangle is **right-angled at the robot**. This is the classic **"broadside" or "perpendicular" geometry** used in surveying to achieve the most accurate triangulation fix. KTU examiners love testing this optimal geometry.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robot observes landmark $L_1(0, 0)$ at bearing $\theta_1 = 60^\circ$ and landmark $L_2(10, 0)$ m at bearing $\theta_2 = 300^\circ$. Find the robot's position using the ASA method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute baseline length.
$b = \sqrt{(10-0)^2 + 0^2} = 10$ m.

**Step 2:** Compute baseline bearing.
$\theta_{12} = \text{atan2}(0, 10) = 0^\circ$.

**Step 3:** Compute the relative angles.
$\alpha = \theta_{12} - \theta_1 = 0^\circ - 60^\circ = -60^\circ$. The magnitude is $\vert \alpha \vert = 60^\circ$.
$\beta = \theta_2 - \theta_{12} = 300^\circ - 0^\circ = 300^\circ$. Modulo $360^\circ$, the interior angle is $360^\circ - 300^\circ = 60^\circ$.

**Step 4:** Apply the formula.

$$x = \frac{10 \cdot \sin 60^\circ \cdot \cos 60^\circ}{\sin(60^\circ+60^\circ)} = \frac{10 \cdot (0.8660) \cdot (0.5)}{\sin 120^\circ} = \frac{4.330}{0.8660} = 5.00 \text{ m}$$

$$y = \frac{10 \cdot \sin 60^\circ \cdot \sin 60^\circ}{\sin 120^\circ} = \frac{10 \cdot 0.75}{0.8660} = \frac{7.50}{0.8660} \approx 8.660 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The robot is at $(5.00, 8.66)$ m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Configuration of Triangulation

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical 2D geometric layout of the triangulation problem, identifying the two landmarks, the robot, the baseline, the angular observations, and the unknown position to be solved.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["L1 X1 Y1 LANDMARK A KNOWN"]
    L2["L2 X2 Y2 LANDMARK B KNOWN"]
    R["R X Y ROBOT UNKNOWN"]
    BL["BASELINE b METERS"]
    A["ANGLE ALPHA AT L1"]
    B["BETA ANGLE AT L2"]
    G["GAMMA AT ROBOT"]
    SOL["SOLVE FOR X Y"]

    L1 -- "D1 RANGE" --> R
    L2 -- "D2 RANGE" --> R
    L1 --- BL
    BL --- L2
    L1 -. "ALPHA" .-> R
    L2 -. "BETA" .-> R
    R -. "GAMMA" .-> L1
    R -. "GAMMA" .-> L2
    A --> SOL
    B --> SOL
    BL --> SOL
    SOL --> R
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow of a Triangulation Pipeline

This block diagram captures the **signal-processing and computational pipeline** that a robot's onboard computer executes to perform a single triangulation fix. Each node represents a distinct functional stage, and the arrows depict data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SENSOR HARDWARE CAMERA OR SONAR ARRAY"]
    S2["FEATURE EXTRACTION CORNER OR BEACON DETECTION"]
    S3["LANDMARK IDENTIFICATION MATCH TO STORED MAP"]
    S4["ANGULAR MEASUREMENT COMPUTE BEARINGS THETA1 THETA2"]
    S5["BASELINE FETCH RETRIEVE L1 L2 FROM MAP"]
    S6["GEOMETRIC SOLVER APPLY LAW OF SINES OR CRAMER RULE"]
    S7["POSITION ESTIMATE X Y OUTPUT"]
    S8["UNCERTAINTY ESTIMATOR GDOP COMPUTATION"]
    S9["KALMAN FILTER FUSION WITH PRIOR POSE"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S7
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Choosing the Solution Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"ARE BOTH BEARINGS ABSOLUTE IN GLOBAL FRAME"}
    Q2{"ARE BOTH ANGLES RELATIVE TO BASELINE"}
    M1["USE BEARING INTERSECTION CRAMER RULE"]
    M2["USE ASA LAW OF SINES"]
    M3["CONVERT BEARING TO RELATIVE ANGLE THEN USE LAW OF SINES"]
    END["OUTPUT POSITION X Y"]

    Q1 -- YES --> M1
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2
    Q2 -- YES --> M2
    Q2 -- NO --> M3
    M1 --> END
    M2 --> END
    M3 --> END
```

### 4.4 Error Sensitivity Heat-Map (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1["OPTIMAL GEOMETRY ALPHA PLUS BETA EQUALS 90 DEGREES"]
    Z2["DEGRADED GEOMETRY ROBOT NEAR BASELINE EXTENSION"]
    Z3["SINGULAR CASE BEARINGS NEARLY PARALLEL"]
    E1["MINIMAL POSITION ERROR"]
    E2["MODERATE POSITION ERROR"]
    E3["BLOW UP COMPUTATIONALLY UNSTABLE"]

    Z1 --> E1
    Z2 --> E2
    Z3 --> E3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to "draw the geometry of triangulation" in an exam, always label: (1) the baseline with its length $b$, (2) the two measured angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$ with curved arrows, (3) the interior angle $\gamma$ at the robot, and (4) the two range lines $d_1, d_2$. A well-labeled diagram is worth 1ŌĆō2 marks by itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define triangulation as used in robot navigation. List any two sensors commonly used to obtain the angular measurements required for triangulation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Triangulation is a localization technique in which a robot determines its position by measuring the angles (bearings) from two or more known reference points (landmarks) whose global coordinates are already known. The intersection of the two bearing lines uniquely defines the robot's position in the plane.

**Sensors (1 mark):** Any two of the following:
- Monocular or stereo **camera** (computes bearing from pixel coordinates and camera intrinsics).
- **Digital compass / magnetometer** (measures Earth's magnetic bearing to a landmark).
- **Beam-steering sonar or ultrasonic array** (returns angle of the strongest echo).
- **Phased-array radar / LiDAR** (returns angle of return pulse).
- **Encoder on a rotating turret** (mechanical angle to a retroreflector).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between triangulation and trilateration in the context of robot localization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Triangulation | Trilateration |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Quantity | **Angles** to known landmarks | **Distances** to known landmarks |
| Geometry | Triangle with known ASA | Triangle with known SSS |
| Required Data | 2 angles + 1 baseline | 3 distances |
| Common Sensors | Camera, compass, sonar | LiDAR, UWB, RSSI, GPS |
| Sensitivity | Long-range sensitive to angular noise | Short-range sensitive to range noise |

(3 marks distributed: 1 mark for the conceptual distinction, 1 mark for sensor contrast, 1 mark for measurement quantity contrast.)

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Rule:** Each Part B question must contain two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`

**A mobile robot is operating in a 2D indoor environment mapped with known landmarks. Two landmarks are located at $L_1 = (0, 0)$ m and $L_2 = (8, 0)$ m. The robot's onboard vision system measures the bearings to these landmarks as $\theta_1 = 45^\circ$ (to $L_1$) and $\theta_2 = 315^\circ$ (to $L_2$) relative to the global +x axis.**

**(a)** Using the **ASA Law of Sines** formulation, derive and compute the **exact (x, y) position** of the robot. Show all intermediate steps and clearly identify the interior angles $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$.

**(b)** Suppose the angular sensor has a Gaussian noise of $\sigma_\theta = 0.5^\circ$ on each measurement. **Compute the GDOP** and the resulting position uncertainty $\sigma_r = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}$. Comment on whether the geometry is favorable.

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the baseline.
$b = \sqrt{(8-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 8$ m.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Baseline bearing.
$\theta_{12} = \text{atan2}(0, 8) = 0^\circ$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute relative interior angles.
At $L_1$: $\alpha = \theta_{12} - \theta_1 = 0^\circ - 45^\circ = -45^\circ$. The interior angle magnitude is $\vert \alpha \vert = 45^\circ$. The robot is "above" the baseline, so we use $\alpha = 45^\circ$ as the interior angle.

At $L_2$: The robot bearing to $L_2$ is $\theta_2 = 315^\circ$. The interior angle at $L_2$ is the angle between the line $L_2 \to L_1$ (bearing $180^\circ$) and the line $L_2 \to R$ (bearing $315^\circ - 180^\circ = 135^\circ$ measured locally, but we need the interior angle). The difference is $180^\circ - 135^\circ = 45^\circ$. So $\beta = 45^\circ$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Interior angle at robot.
$\gamma = 180^\circ - 45^\circ - 45^\circ = 90^\circ$.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Apply Law of Sines.

$$d_1 = \frac{b \sin \beta}{\sin \gamma} = \frac{8 \cdot \sin 45^\circ}{\sin 90^\circ} = \frac{8 \cdot 0.7071}{1} = 5.657 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark, completes 7):** Convert to Cartesian.

$$x = 0 + d_1 \cos \theta_1 = 5.657 \cdot \cos 45^\circ = 5.657 \cdot 0.7071 = 4.00 \text{ m}$$

$$y = 0 + d_1 \sin \theta_1 = 5.657 \cdot \sin 45^\circ = 5.657 \cdot 0.7071 = 4.00 \text{ m}$$

**Answer (a):** Robot position is $(x, y) = (4.00, 4.00)$ m.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the GDOP formula.

$$\text{GDOP} = \frac{d_1}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)} = \frac{d_1}{\sin \gamma}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute values.

$$\text{GDOP} = \frac{5.657}{\sin(90^\circ)} = \frac{5.657}{1} = 5.657$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert angular noise to radians.
$\sigma_\theta = 0.5^\circ \times (\pi/180) = 0.00873$ rad.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute position uncertainty.
$\sigma_r = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_\theta = 5.657 \times 0.00873 = 0.0494$ m $\approx 4.94$ cm.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Geometric commentary.
Since $\alpha + \beta = 90^\circ$, the triangle is **right-angled at the robot**, which is the **optimal geometry** that minimizes GDOP. The position uncertainty is approximately 5 cm, which is excellent for indoor navigation.

---

#### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`

**A surveying robot mounted on a wheeled base must localize itself using two beacons. The beacons are at $B_1 = (2, 3)$ m and $B_2 = (12, 8)$ m in a global frame. The robot's sensor returns absolute bearings $\theta_1 = 120^\circ$ to $B_1$ and $\theta_2 = 30^\circ$ to $B_2$.**

**(a)** Solve the triangulation problem using the **line-intersection (Cramer's Rule) method**. Derive the closed-form expressions for $x$ and $y$, and compute the numerical result. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **mirror-image ambiguity** in two-landmark triangulation. Describe one additional measurement or constraint that can be used to resolve this ambiguity, with a brief justification. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Form the two bearing-line equations.

Line 1: $(y - 3) \cos 120^\circ = (x - 2) \sin 120^\circ$
$$-0.5 (y - 3) = 0.8660 (x - 2)$$

Line 2: $(y - 8) \cos 30^\circ = (x - 12) \sin 30^\circ$
$$0.8660 (y - 8) = 0.5 (x - 12)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Rearrange into standard form.
Line 1: $-0.8660 x - 0.5 y = -1.7320 - 1.5 = -3.2320$
Line 2: $0.5 x - 0.8660 y = 6.0 - 6.9282 = -0.9282$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute determinant.

$$D = \sin(30^\circ - 120^\circ) = \sin(-90^\circ) = -1$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply Cramer's Rule.

$$x = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} -3.2320 & -0.5 \\ -0.9282 & -0.8660 \end{vmatrix}}{-1} = \frac{(-3.2320)(-0.8660) - (-0.5)(-0.9282)}{-1} = \frac{2.7989 - 0.4641}{-1} = \frac{2.3348}{-1} = -2.33$$

**Step 5 (1 mark, completes 7):** Compute $y$ analogously.

$$y = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} -0.8660 & -3.2320 \\ 0.5 & -0.9282 \end{vmatrix}}{-1} = \frac{(-0.8660)(-0.9282) - (-3.2320)(0.5)}{-1} = \frac{0.8038 + 1.6160}{-1} = \frac{2.4198}{-1} = -2.42$$

**Answer (a):** Robot position is approximately $(x, y) = (-2.33, -2.42)$ m.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition of mirror-image ambiguity.
The two-landmark triangulation problem, as a pure geometry problem, admits **two solutions** that are mirror images of each other across the baseline $\overline{B_1 B_2}$. If we only know $\alpha$ and $\beta$, the triangle $\triangle B_1 R B_2$ is congruent to a second triangle $\triangle B_1 R' B_2$ on the opposite side of the baseline. Mathematically, the line-intersection method yields one point if the two bearing lines are oriented in a specific way, but if the bearings are taken in absolute form without side-information, the two solutions are equally valid geometrically.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Resolution via a third landmark.
A third beacon $B_3$ is observed, yielding a third bearing $\theta_3$. The third bearing line intersects one of the two mirror solutions but not the other (in general position). The point lying on all three lines is the true position. This is the standard **multi-lateration/multi-angulation** approach used in GPS and surveying.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Alternative ŌĆö Prior pose / dead reckoning.
If the robot has a prior estimate of its position from wheel odometry or an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), the mirror solution that lies closer to the prior estimate is selected. The Bayesian filter (e.g., Kalman or particle filter) combines the prior and the new bearing measurements to disambiguate. This is computationally cheap and avoids the need for a third landmark.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Validation.
Always verify the chosen solution lies in a physically plausible region of the map (e.g., not inside a wall, not outside the room boundary). Map-based gating is a robust final check.

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Triangulation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (ŌĆō2 marks):** Forgetting that bearings are measured **clockwise from +x** in robotics, so the formula $\alpha = \theta_{12} - \theta_1$ requires careful modular arithmetic (mod $360^\circ$ or $2\pi$). A negative angle is often correct.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\gamma$ with $\alpha + \beta$ (ŌĆō2 marks):** The interior angle at the robot is $\gamma = \pi - \alpha - \beta$, **NOT** $\alpha + \beta$. Many students write the latter, and Examiners deduct a full 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Missing the $\sin(\alpha+\beta)$ Co-function Step (ŌĆō1 mark):** Failing to apply $\sin(\pi - \theta) = \sin \theta$ in the Law of Sines denominator is a recurring one-mark loss.
>
> 4. **No Diagram (ŌĆō1 mark):** KTU evaluators explicitly state that a properly labeled geometry diagram is mandatory. Skipping it costs at least 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Units in Final Answer (ŌĆō1 mark):** Always write units (m, rad, ┬░) next to numerical answers.
>
> 6. **Singular Geometry Ignored (ŌĆō1 mark):** Not mentioning that the solution is invalid when bearings are parallel (denominator = 0) is considered incomplete by strict examiners.

---

### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Triangulation uses angles**, not distances, to known landmarks.
- Ō£ģ **Two landmarks** are the minimum for a 2D fix; a third resolves the **mirror-image ambiguity**.
- Ō£ģ The **baseline $b$** between landmarks is the known side opposite the unknown position.
- Ō£ģ Interior angles satisfy $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$.
- Ō£ģ **Law of Sines** gives: $d_1 = b \sin \beta / \sin(\alpha+\beta)$.
- Ō£ģ Position in Cartesian: $x = x_1 + d_1 \cos \theta_1$, $y = y_1 + d_1 \sin \theta_1$.
- Ō£ģ **Bearing-intersection method** uses Cramer's Rule on the 2├Ś2 system; the determinant is $\sin(\theta_2 - \theta_1)$.
- Ō£ģ **Optimal geometry** is the **right-angle at the robot** ($\alpha + \beta = 90^\circ$), minimizing GDOP.
- Ō£ģ Position uncertainty $\sigma_r = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_\theta$, where GDOP $= d_1 / \sin(\alpha+\beta)$.
- Ō£ģ Common sensors: **camera, compass, sonar, LiDAR, phased-array radar**.
- Ō£ģ Always check for **parallel bearings** (singularity) and add a **third observation** if ambiguity exists.
- Ō£ģ Triangulation differs from trilateration: angles vs. distances, ASA vs. SSS triangles.
- Ō£ģ The **Law of Sines** is the central theorem; memorize the formula and the ASA construction.
- Ō£ģ Applications span **GPS, surveying, AR/VR, drone swarms, RoboCup, and industrial AGVs**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Rangefinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ultrasonic Rangefinder** (also called an *ultrasonic distance sensor* or *sonic transducer*) is a non-contact, active ranging instrument used extensively in mobile robotics for measuring the distance between the sensor and a target object. It operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle using high-frequency acoustic waves in the ultrasonic band, typically between **20 kHz and 200 kHz**, with the most common robotic implementations using **40 kHz, 50 kHz, 58 kHz, 200 kHz, or 300 kHz** carrier frequencies.

The transducer is constructed around a **piezoelectric ceramic element** (most commonly lead zirconate titanate, abbreviated PZT) that mechanically vibrates when excited by an alternating electrical signal, thereby converting electrical energy into acoustic (sound) energy, and vice versa during reception. A single transducer usually performs both transmit and receive functions in a time-multiplexed (half-duplex) manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot:** *Ultrasonic rangefinders are active sensors that emit acoustic pulses and estimate range by measuring the round-trip time of the reflected echo, exploiting the known propagation velocity of sound in air.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Robot Bat"

Imagine a person standing blindfolded in a long corridor. To find the wall in front of them, they shout "Hello!" and listen for the echo. The longer the echo takes to return, the farther the wall. This is exactly how a bat navigates caves at night ŌĆö a biological implementation of ultrasonic echolocation.

In a robot:

- The **robot's "shout"** is a short burst of high-frequency sound (a *chirp* or *ping*), typically a 10ŌĆō20 cycle pulse at 40 kHz.
- The **"echo"** is the acoustic energy that scatters off the target surface and returns to the same transducer.
- The **"counting time"** is performed by a precision timer, often inside a microcontroller, that records the elapsed microseconds between transmission and echo reception.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used in Ultrasonic Ranging**
> - **Speed of sound in dry air at 20 ┬░C: c = 343 m/s** (Ōēł 0.343 mm/┬Ąs).
> - **Speed of sound in dry air at 0 ┬░C: c = 331.4 m/s.**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (used in some hybrid systems): g = 9.81 m/s┬▓.**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure reference: 101.325 kPa.**

## 1.3 Why Ultrasonic Sensors are Indispensable in Mobile Robotics

| Property | Consequence for Robot Navigation |
|---|---|
| Insensitive to color, transparency, or surface finish of target | Works on glass, polished metal, dark plastic equally well. |
| Penetrates dust, fog, smoke, and moderate rain | Preferred over LiDAR in industrial/dusty environments. |
| Independent of ambient light (works in total darkness) | Reliable for 24/7 warehouse or night-time patrol robots. |
| Low cost (Ōēł Ōé╣200 ŌĆō Ōé╣2,000 per module for hobbyist units) | Enables dense array placement on low-budget AGVs. |
| Centimeter-level resolution achievable | Sufficient for obstacle avoidance and wall-following. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mentor Tip:** In exam answers, always state the **fundamental operating principle in one crisp sentence first** (Time-of-Flight of an acoustic pulse), and only then proceed to equations, components, and limitations. Examiners reward clarity at the top of an answer.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The ultrasonic ranging process is decomposed into **six discrete phases**. A mobile robot's perception loop invokes these phases at a configurable refresh rate (commonly 10 Hz to 50 Hz).

1. **Trigger / Excitation Phase:** The host microcontroller raises a `TRIG` pin high for **Ōēź 10 ┬Ąs** (industry-standard HC-SR04 convention). This causes the on-board driver to apply a 10-cycle, 40 kHz burst to the piezoelectric transmitter.
2. **Acoustic Wavefront Propagation Phase:** The transducer converts the electrical burst into a compressional pressure wave in the air. The wavefront expands as a **cone-shaped beam** with a divergence half-angle *╬Ė* (typically 15┬░ to 30┬░).
3. **Target Interaction Phase:** When the wavefront strikes an obstacle, two phenomena occur:
   - **Specular reflection** ŌĆö if the surface is flat relative to the wavelength and the incidence angle is shallow, energy may reflect away from the sensor (causing a *miss*).
   - **Diffuse (Lambertian) scattering** ŌĆö energy is scattered in all directions, with a portion returning along the incident path. This is the desired scenario for ranging.
4. **Echo Reception Phase:** The returning pressure wave strikes the same (or a paired) piezoelectric element, which converts it back into a small alternating voltage (typically a few millivolts).
5. **Signal Conditioning Phase:** The weak echo is amplified (gain Ōēł 60ŌĆō80 dB), band-pass filtered around the carrier frequency (e.g., 40 kHz ┬▒ 2 kHz) to suppress environmental noise, and then fed to a comparator to produce a clean digital pulse.
6. **Distance Computation Phase:** A timer (often the microcontroller's hardware capture/compare unit) measures the time interval **╬öt** between the leading edge of transmission and the leading edge of the received echo. Distance is computed as:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the round trip (transmit + return).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the 1/2 Factor ŌĆö KTU Favourite Question

The acoustic pulse must travel from the sensor to the target **and back**. If we na├»vely computed `d = c ┬Ę ╬öt`, we would obtain the *total* distance traversed by the pulse, which is **twice** the actual one-way separation between sensor and target. Hence the division by 2.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Exam Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-way distance | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of sound, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time | metres (m) | **Most asked equation** in this module. |
| 2 | Speed of sound in air (linearized) | $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$ = ambient temperature | m/s, ┬░C | Valid for $-20\,┬░C \le T \le 40\,┬░C$. |
| 3 | Speed of sound (with humidity correction) | $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 T + 0.0124 H$ | $H$ = relative humidity in % | m/s, ┬░C, % | Used for high-precision outdoor robots. |
| 4 | Speed of sound (with pressure correction) | $c = c_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{T_K}{T_{K,0}}}$ | $T_K$ = absolute temperature in K | m/s, K | Most thermodynamically rigorous form. |
| 5 | Minimum measurable distance (blind zone) | $d_{\min} = c \cdot t_{\text{ringdown}}$ | $t_{\text{ringdown}}$ = transducer ringing time | m, s | Determined by mechanical Q-factor of the piezo. |
| 6 | Maximum measurable distance | $d_{\max} = c \cdot t_{\text{timeout}} \,/\, 2$ | $t_{\text{timeout}}$ = programmable echo window | m, s | Set by SNR and absorption limits. |
| 7 | Beam radius at distance $d$ | $r(d) = d \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = half-angle of beam | m, rad | Defines effective footprint of the sensor. |
| 8 | Echo amplitude decay (1/r┬▓ law) | $p(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ | $p$ = received pressure, $r$ = distance | Pa, m | Governs maximum range in free field. |
| 9 | Wavelength of acoustic wave | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $f$ = carrier frequency | m, Hz | Determines surface roughness sensitivity. |
| 10 | Angular resolution (rough) | $\Delta\phi \approx \dfrac{c}{f \cdot D}$ | $D$ = transducer aperture | rad, m, Hz | Analogous to optical Rayleigh criterion. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in Amazon/FedEx warehouses use arrays of 12ŌĆō16 ultrasonic sensors for **bumper-level obstacle detection** during shelf navigation.
- **Self-driving car prototypes (1990s ŌĆö ARGO, VaMP)** used ultrasonic rangefinders for **close-range parking aid** before LiDAR became affordable.
- **Service robots** (Roomba, Husqvarna Automower) employ downward-facing ultrasonic sensors for **stair-edge detection** to prevent falls.
- **Industrial automation** uses ultrasonic sensors for **liquid level measurement** in chemical tanks where optical sensors fail due to foam or vapor.
- **Medical robotics / surgical navigation** uses high-frequency (>1 MHz) ultrasonic transducers for tissue depth profiling ŌĆö a sister application of the same ToF principle.

## 2.5 Key Components Inside a Typical Module (HC-SR04 Reference)

- **Piezoelectric transmitter (TX)** ŌĆö emits the acoustic pulse.
- **Piezoelectric receiver (RX)** ŌĆö detects the returning echo (some modules use a single element in time-multiplexed mode).
- **MAX232 / driver transistor array** ŌĆö amplifies the 5 V logic pulse to ~20 Vpp required to excite the piezo.
- **Band-pass filter & LM324 op-amp chain** ŌĆö conditions the weak echo.
- **74HC04 hex inverter** ŌĆö shapes the comparator output into a clean 5 V digital pulse.
- **Ceramic resonator or crystal** ŌĆö generates the 40 kHz carrier.
- **ABS plastic housing** ŌĆö provides mechanical isolation and acoustic impedance matching.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Fundamental Range Equation from First Principles

**Statement to Prove:** The one-way distance to a target is given by

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Given:** Acoustic wave propagates at constant speed $c$ in still air, the pulse reflects off a stationary target, and the total elapsed round-trip time is $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of uniform speed.**

For a wave traveling at constant speed $c$, the distance covered in time $t$ is:

$$s = c \cdot t$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply to the outward leg.**

During the outward journey from sensor to target, the wave covers a distance $d$ in time $t_1$:

$$d = c \cdot t_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply to the return leg.**

Assuming the target is stationary (no Doppler effect) and the medium is homogeneous, the return journey also covers the same distance $d$ in the same time:

$$d = c \cdot t_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total round-trip time.**

The total measured time $\Delta t$ is the sum of both legs:

$$\Delta t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $d$.**

Multiplying both sides by $c/2$:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Speed of Sound as a Function of Temperature

**Starting Point (Thermodynamic Identity):** For an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic sound propagation,

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \, R \, T_K}{M}}$$

where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats (Ōēł 1.4 for dry air), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol┬ĘK) is the universal gas constant, $M$ = 0.02896 kg/mol is the molar mass of dry air, and $T_K$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Evaluate the constant term.**

$$\gamma \cdot \frac{R}{M} = 1.4 \times \frac{8.314}{0.02896}$$

Let us compute the numerical value:

$$\frac{8.314}{0.02896} = 287.05 \,\,\text{J/(kg┬ĘK)}$$

Multiplying by 1.4:

$$1.4 \times 287.05 = 401.87 \,\,\text{J/(kg┬ĘK)}$$

So:

$$c = \sqrt{401.87 \cdot T_K}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to Celsius for engineering convenience.**

Recall the identity $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ where $T_C$ is the Celsius temperature. Hence:

$$c = \sqrt{401.87 \cdot (T_C + 273.15)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linearize about 0 ┬░C (a Taylor series about $T_C = 0$).**

At $T_C = 0$:

$$c_0 = \sqrt{401.87 \times 273.15} = \sqrt{109{,}769.4} = 331.32 \,\,\text{m/s}$$

This matches the empirical value **331.4 m/s** at 0 ┬░C.

**Step 4 ŌĆö First-order Taylor expansion.**

For a function $f(T_C) = \sqrt{a + bT_C}$, the linearized form is:

$$f(T_C) \approx f(0) + T_C \cdot f'(0)$$

Differentiating:

$$f'(T_C) = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{a + bT_C}}$$

Evaluated at $T_C = 0$:

$$f'(0) = \frac{401.87}{2 \times 331.32} = \frac{401.87}{662.64} = 0.6066 \,\,\text{(m/s)/┬░C}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final linearized expression.**

$$\boxed{c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T_C \,\,\text{m/s}}$$

This is the **engineering-standard** form used in all KTU problems. It yields:

- At $T_C = 20$ ┬░C: $c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 20 = 343.4$ m/s Ō£ō
- At $T_C = 40$ ┬░C: $c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 40 = 355.4$ m/s
- At $T_C = -20$ ┬░C: $c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times (-20) = 319.4$ m/s

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Beam Radius at a Given Distance

**Setup:** The transducer emits a conical beam with half-angle $\theta$ (in radians). The beam's axis is aligned with the surface normal of the target.

**Geometry of the cone.** Consider the cross-section of the beam in any plane containing the axis. The cross-section is a triangle with apex at the sensor and apex angle $2\theta$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Relate beam radius to distance.**

For a target at one-way distance $d$ along the axis, the perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis to the beam edge is, from basic trigonometry:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{r}{d}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $r$.**

$$r = d \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Resulting footprint diameter.**

The total beam footprint diameter $D_{\text{foot}}$ at distance $d$ is twice $r$:

$$D_{\text{foot}}(d) = 2 d \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

**Numerical Example (HC-SR04, $\theta \approx 15┬░$):**

- At $d = 1$ m: $D_{\text{foot}} = 2 \times 1 \times \tan(15┬░) = 2 \times 0.2679 = 0.536$ m
- At $d = 4$ m: $D_{\text{foot}} = 2 \times 4 \times 0.2679 = 2.143$ m

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why a wall 4 m away is "seen" by a 40 kHz sensor as occupying a region about 2.1 m wide ŌĆö a crucial consideration when robots navigate narrow corridors.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Minimum Measurable Distance (Blind Zone)

**Physical Origin:** After a transmit pulse, the piezoelectric element continues to oscillate at its natural resonant frequency for a short duration even after the excitation voltage has been removed. This residual oscillation, called **ringdown**, generates acoustic noise that overwhelms any close-by echo.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Quantify ringdown time.**

The decay of the mechanical oscillation follows an exponential envelope characterized by the **mechanical Q-factor**:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\pi f t / Q}$$

The ringdown time $t_{\text{ringdown}}$ is conventionally defined as the time for the amplitude to fall to 1% (i.e., $-40$ dB) of the initial value:

$$\frac{A(t_{\text{ringdown}})}{A_0} = 0.01 = e^{-\pi f t_{\text{ringdown}} / Q}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $t_{\text{ringdown}}$.**

Taking natural logarithms:

$$\ln(0.01) = -\frac{\pi f \, t_{\text{ringdown}}}{Q}$$

$$-4.605 = -\frac{\pi f \, t_{\text{ringdown}}}{Q}$$

$$t_{\text{ringdown}} = \frac{4.605 \, Q}{\pi f}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to blind distance.**

During the ringdown period, the sensor cannot detect echoes. The closest detectable target distance is:

$$d_{\min} = \frac{c \cdot t_{\text{ringdown}}}{2} = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \frac{4.605 \, Q}{\pi f}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $f = 40$ kHz, $Q = 20$, $c = 343$ m/s:

$$t_{\text{ringdown}} = \frac{4.605 \times 20}{\pi \times 40{,}000} = \frac{92.1}{125{,}664} = 7.33 \times 10^{-4} \,\,\text{s} = 733 \,\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$d_{\min} = \frac{343 \times 733 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.126 \,\,\text{m} \approx 12.6 \,\,\text{cm}$$

This matches the published **HC-SR04 minimum range of Ōēł 2 cm to 5 cm** (practical specs are often better than the theoretical $-40$ dB criterion).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Maximum Range and Inverse-Square Law

**Principle:** The received acoustic intensity falls off as the inverse square of distance (geometric spreading) plus an additional exponential absorption term:

$$I(r) = \frac{I_0}{r^2} \cdot e^{-2 \alpha r}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **acoustic absorption coefficient** of air (in Np/m), and the factor of 2 in the exponent accounts for the round trip.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set received intensity equal to the detection threshold $I_{\min}$.**

$$\frac{I_0}{r_{\max}^2} \cdot e^{-2 \alpha r_{\max}} = I_{\min}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve implicitly (transcendental).**

This equation has no closed-form solution, but for typical low-absorption conditions ($\alpha r \ll 1$), we can neglect the exponential:

$$r_{\max} \approx \sqrt{\frac{I_0}{I_{\min}}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Resulting maximum range.**

The HC-SR04, for example, has $r_{\max} \approx 4$ m, which corresponds to an $I_0 / I_{\min}$ ratio of $16$ W/m┬▓ (i.e., the sensor can detect an echo that is 1/16th of the transmitted intensity).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Operation of an Ultrasonic Rangefinder

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **end-to-end signal flow** during one ranging cycle, from trigger pulse to digital distance output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Microcontroller TRIG Pin Rising Edge] --> A2[Oscillator 40 kHz Burst Generator]
    A2 --> A3[Driver Amplifier 20 Vpp]
    A3 --> A4[Piezoelectric Transmitter Element]
    A4 --> A5[Air Medium Wavefront Propagation]
    A5 --> A6[Target Object Surface]
    A6 --> A7[Diffuse Reflection Back Toward Sensor]
    A7 --> A8[Piezoelectric Receiver Element]
    A8 --> A9[Low Noise Preamplifier]
    A9 --> A10[Bandpass Filter 40 kHz Center]
    A10 --> A11[Comparator With Threshold]
    A11 --> A12[Digital Echo Pulse]
    A2 -.Time Stamp T0.-> A13[High Resolution Timer]
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14[Compute Dt Equals T1 minus T0]
    A14 --> A15[Apply Range Formula D equals c Dt divided by 2]
    A15 --> A16[Output Distance Value to Robot Planner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows denote **forward signal flow**. The dotted arrow denotes a *time-stamp reference* (TŌéĆ is captured at the start of burst transmission, TŌéü at the leading edge of the echo comparator output). The microcontroller uses the difference TŌéü ŌłÆ TŌéĆ as the round-trip interval.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Robot Mounted Multi-Sensor Array

When a robot employs multiple ultrasonic sensors, the cross-talk issue becomes critical. The diagram below maps the system architecture that prevents interference.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Host Microcontroller] --> B2[Sensor 1 TX RX Unit]
    B1 --> B3[Sensor 2 TX RX Unit]
    B1 --> B4[Sensor 3 TX RX Unit]
    B1 --> B5[Sensor 4 TX RX Unit]
    B1 --> B6[Time Division Multiplexer Scheduler]
    B6 --> B2
    B6 --> B3
    B6 --> B4
    B6 --> B5
    B2 --> B7[Distance Buffer 1]
    B3 --> B8[Distance Buffer 2]
    B4 --> B9[Distance Buffer 3]
    B5 --> B10[Distance Buffer 4]
    B7 --> B11[Obstacle Map Fusion Module]
    B8 --> B11
    B9 --> B11
    B10 --> B11
    B11 --> B12[Navigation Planner Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Talk Mitigation Strategy:** The Time-Division Multiplexer (TDM) scheduler ensures that only one sensor transmits at any given instant. The other sensors remain in pure *listen* mode and reject any echoes that do not correspond to their own transmission slot.

## 4.3 Beam-Pattern Schematic ŌĆö Conceptual Illustration

Although Mermaid cannot draw true 2-D acoustic pressure maps, the following **labelled radial plot** can be rendered in the answer script using ASCII to convey the idea:

```
                            Sensor
                              |
                              v
                             /|\
                            / | \
                           /  |  \
                          /   |   \
                         /    |    \    <-- Beam Cone (half-angle theta)
                        /     |     \
                       /      |      \
                      /       |       \
                     /  Echo  |  Echo  \
                    / returns| returns \
                   /    here |     here \
                  /         \|/          \
       _________/___________( )________________  Target Surface
```

The radius of the **footprint circle** at distance $d$ is $r = d \tan(\theta)$, and a target smaller than this circle located near the axis is reliably detected, while a target smaller than this circle located near the periphery may be missed due to side-lobe insensitivity.
