# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the principle of operation of an ultrasonic rangefinder. State the role of the piezoelectric transducer in such a sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** An ultrasonic rangefinder measures distance by emitting a short acoustic pulse (typically 40 kHz) and measuring the **time-of-flight** of the echo that returns after reflecting off a target.
- **(1 mark)** The basic range equation is $d = (c \cdot \Delta t)/2$, where $c$ is the speed of sound in air and $\Delta t$ is the round-trip time.
- **(1 mark)** The **piezoelectric transducer** converts electrical energy into mechanical (acoustic) vibrations during transmission, and vice versa during reception. It is constructed from a PZT ceramic that mechanically deforms under an applied AC voltage (inverse piezoelectric effect) and generates a voltage when mechanically stressed (direct piezoelectric effect), enabling a single element to act as both transmitter and receiver in time-multiplexed mode.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four factors that affect the accuracy of an ultrasonic rangefinder in a mobile robot application.**

**Model Answer (4 points ├Ś 0.75 = 3 Marks):**
1. **Ambient temperature** ŌĆö alters the speed of sound $c$ by Ōēł 0.6 m/s per ┬░C, causing a systematic range error of Ōēł 0.17 % per ┬░C.
2. **Surface orientation (incidence angle)** ŌĆö at grazing angles, the reflection is specular and the echo may not return to the sensor.
3. **Air currents / wind turbulence** ŌĆö distort wavefronts and shift the apparent echo arrival time.
4. **Humidity and air pressure** ŌĆö modify density and hence the speed of sound.
5. **Cross-talk** ŌĆö interference from neighbouring sensors or other ultrasonic sources.
6. **Soft/absorbent targets** ŌĆö foam, fur, and cloth absorb acoustic energy and weaken the echo.

(Any four of the above earn full marks.)

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer ONE of the two choices. Both choices are provided below as comprehensive options.

---

### PART B ŌĆö QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the working equation of an ultrasonic rangefinder, $d = c \Delta t / 2$, starting from the basic definition of uniform speed. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot is fitted with an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor. The sensor reports a round-trip echo time of **$\Delta t = 5800 \,\mu\text{s}$**. The ambient temperature is **$T = 30\,┬░C$** and the relative humidity is **60%**. Compute the actual distance to the obstacle and the percentage error that would result if the programmer used the standard 343 m/s speed of sound without any correction.

---

#### SOLUTION TO (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 [1 mark] ŌĆö State the basic speed-distance-time relation:**

$$s = c \cdot t$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] ŌĆö Define the outward leg:**

The acoustic pulse travels from the sensor to the target (distance $d$) in time $t_1$:

$$d = c \cdot t_1 \,\,\Rightarrow\,\, t_1 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] ŌĆö Define the return leg:**

The echo travels back from the target to the sensor (distance $d$) in time $t_2$:

$$d = c \cdot t_2 \,\,\Rightarrow\,\, t_2 = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] ŌĆö Sum for round-trip time:**

$$\Delta t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] ŌĆö Solve for $d$:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Assumptions [1 mark]:**
- Speed of sound $c$ is constant (homogeneous, still air).
- Target is stationary (no Doppler shift).
- Wavefront travels along a straight line (no refraction).
- Reflecting surface is sufficiently large and oriented within the beam cone.

**Statement of principle [1 mark]:** The distance to the target is half the product of the speed of sound and the round-trip time, because the pulse traverses the inter-sensor gap twice.

---

#### SOLUTION TO (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 [1 mark] ŌĆö Compute corrected speed of sound using the empirical formula:**

$$c_{\text{corrected}} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T + 0.0124 \cdot H$$

Substituting $T = 30\,┬░C$, $H = 60\%$:

$$c_{\text{corrected}} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30 + 0.0124 \times 60$$

$$c_{\text{corrected}} = 331.4 + 18.0 + 0.744 = 350.144 \,\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] ŌĆö Compute the actual distance:**

$$d_{\text{actual}} = \frac{c_{\text{corrected}} \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{350.144 \times 5800 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d_{\text{actual}} = \frac{350.144 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.0308}{2} = 1.0154 \,\,\text{m}$$

So the **actual distance is approximately 1.015 m** (Ōēł 101.5 cm). [1 mark for substitution and numerical handling]

**Step 3 [1 mark] ŌĆö Compute the distance using the uncorrected 343 m/s:**

$$d_{\text{wrong}} = \frac{343 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.9894}{2} = 0.9947 \,\,\text{m}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] ŌĆö Compute absolute and percentage error:**

$$\text{Absolute error} = \vert d_{\text{actual}} - d_{\text{wrong}} \vert = \vert 1.0154 - 0.9947 \vert = 0.0207 \,\,\text{m}$$

$$\% \text{ error} = \frac{0.0207}{1.0154} \times 100 = 2.04 \,\%$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] ŌĆö Concluding statement:** The na├»ve 343 m/s assumption produces a **2.04% under-estimation** of the true range, corresponding to **2.07 cm** of error at this distance. For a robot navigating in a 1 m corridor, this could mean the difference between safe passage and collision. Temperature compensation is therefore **mandatory** in production-grade robotic systems.

---

### PART B ŌĆö QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the major functional stages of a typical ultrasonic ranging system used in mobile robots. Explain the role of the band-pass filter and the comparator in the receiver chain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot employs **eight** ultrasonic rangefinders equally spaced around its circular chassis. Each sensor has a beam half-angle of **15┬░**. Two neighbouring sensors are separated by a chord distance of **40 cm**. Compute:

(i) The beam footprint diameter at a target located **1.5 m** ahead of the chassis.
(ii) The minimum sensor-to-target distance at which the two neighbouring footprints begin to **overlap**, given that the chord offset of 40 cm corresponds to the perpendicular distance between the beam axes at the target plane.
(iii) Comment on the implication for robot navigation.

---

#### SOLUTION TO (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks) ŌĆö text description for the answer script:**

```
[Microcontroller TRIG] ŌåÆ [40 kHz Oscillator] ŌåÆ [Driver Amplifier] ŌåÆ
[Piezoelectric TX] ŌåÆ [Air Medium] ŌåÆ [Target] ŌåÆ [Air Medium] ŌåÆ
[Piezoelectric RX] ŌåÆ [Pre-amp] ŌåÆ [Band-Pass Filter] ŌåÆ [Comparator] ŌåÆ
[Timer] ŌåÆ [Microcontroller] ŌåÆ [Distance Output]
```

**Stage-by-stage description [2 marks]:**

1. **Trigger / Oscillator:** Generates a burst of 40 kHz pulses (typically 8ŌĆō16 cycles).
2. **Driver / Transmitter:** Amplifies the burst to ~20 V peak-to-peak to drive the piezo.
3. **Piezoelectric TX / RX:** Bidirectional electromechanical transducer.
4. **Receiver pre-amplifier:** Boosts the weak echo (often in ┬ĄV range) by ~60 dB.
5. **Band-pass filter:** Centres on 40 kHz with a Q of 5ŌĆō10; rejects DC, mains hum (50 Hz), speech, and high-frequency switching noise.
6. **Comparator:** Converts the filtered sinusoidal echo into a clean 5 V digital pulse whose leading edge triggers the timer.
7. **Timer / Microcontroller:** Computes the round-trip interval and applies the range formula.

**Role of the band-pass filter [1 mark]:** It admits only signals whose frequencies lie close to the 40 kHz carrier. The filter dramatically improves the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of the echo, preventing spurious triggering of the comparator by ambient acoustic noise (motor whine, speech, music, slamming doors).

**Role of the comparator [1 mark]:** It compares the filtered analog echo against a fixed voltage threshold. When the echo exceeds the threshold (i.e., the returning signal is strong enough to be a real reflection rather than noise), the comparator output transitions from low to high. This rising edge is captured by the timer, providing a precise and repeatable *time-stamp* for the echo.

---

#### SOLUTION TO (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Beam half-angle $\theta = 15┬░$
- Chord distance between adjacent sensor axes = $b = 40$ cm = 0.40 m
- (Implied: $c = 343$ m/s at 20 ┬░C)

**Part (i) ŌĆö Footprint diameter at d = 1.5 m [2 marks]:**

$$D_{\text{foot}} = 2 d \tan(\theta) = 2 \times 1.5 \times \tan(15┬░)$$

$$\tan(15┬░) = 0.2679$$

$$D_{\text{foot}} = 3.0 \times 0.2679 = 0.8038 \,\,\text{m} \approx 80.4 \,\,\text{cm}$$

**Part (ii) ŌĆö Overlap distance [3 marks]:**

Two neighbouring footprints begin to overlap when the radius of each footprint equals half the chord separation $b$:

$$r = d \cdot \tan(\theta) = \frac{b}{2}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d_{\text{overlap}} = \frac{b}{2 \tan(\theta)} = \frac{0.40}{2 \times 0.2679} = \frac{0.40}{0.5359} = 0.7465 \,\,\text{m}$$

So the footprints begin to **overlap at approximately 0.75 m** from the chassis.

**Part (iii) ŌĆö Implication for navigation [2 marks]:**

For a target closer than 0.75 m, the same physical object can be detected by **two adjacent sensors simultaneously**, leading to **redundant measurements** and potential confusion in the obstacle-avoidance algorithm. The robot's mapping module must apply a **sensor-fusion rule** ŌĆö for example, retaining only the closer (more accurate) reading and discarding the duplicate. Beyond 0.75 m, the sensors provide complementary, non-overlapping coverage, giving a full 360┬░ perception band of the environment without redundancy. This justifies the choice of eight sensors for 360┬░ coverage with adequate overlap for targets at typical indoor navigation distances (1ŌĆō3 m).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps Identified by Senior KTU Valuators:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $d = c \Delta t / 2$** ŌĆö yields an answer exactly *double* the true range. This is the single most frequent error in this module. **Always write the factor 2 explicitly in the formula sheet.**
> 2. **Using 340 m/s or 344 m/s in numerical problems without justification** ŌĆö examiners deduct marks if the chosen $c$ value is not tied to a stated temperature.
> 3. **Confusing frequency with wavelength** ŌĆö students often write $d = f \cdot \lambda$ which has units of velocity, not distance. Mark loss guaranteed.
> 4. **Stating "ultrasonic works on the Doppler principle"** ŌĆö this is *wrong* for the standard pulse-echo rangefinder. Doppler applies to *velocity* measurement (e.g., police radar), not *static* range measurement. Examiners penalize this conceptual confusion heavily.
> 5. **Omitting the blind-zone discussion** ŌĆö a 14-mark answer that does not mention the minimum measurable distance and ringdown phenomenon is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Skipping the unit analysis** ŌĆö every numerical answer must end with explicit units (m, cm, mm, ┬Ąs, ms). Bare numbers lose half a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 1 / Ultrasonic Rangefinders**

- Ō£ģ **Operating principle:** Time-of-Flight of an acoustic pulse; distance $d = c \Delta t / 2$.
- Ō£ģ **Frequency band:** 20 kHz ŌĆō 200 kHz ultrasonic; 40 kHz is the industry standard.
- Ō£ģ **Transducer type:** Piezoelectric ceramic (PZT); single element in time-multiplexed half-duplex mode.
- Ō£ģ **Speed of sound in air at 20 ┬░C:** $c = 343$ m/s. **Linearized form:** $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 T$ (m/s, with $T$ in ┬░C).
- Ō£ģ **For one-way distance** use $\Delta t / 2$; for the **total path** use $\Delta t$ as-is.
- Ō£ģ **Beam half-angle** is typically 15┬░; the **footprint diameter** at distance $d$ is $2 d \tan(\theta)$.
- Ō£ģ **Blind zone** (minimum range) arises from transducer ringdown; Ōēł 2ŌĆō5 cm for HC-SR04.
- Ō£ģ **Maximum range** is set by SNR and 1/r┬▓ attenuation; typically 3ŌĆō4 m for HC-SR04, up to 10 m for industrial units.
- Ō£ģ **Cross-talk mitigation** uses time-division multiplexing ŌĆö only one sensor transmits at a time.
- Ō£ģ **Specular reflection** at grazing angles causes missed echoes ŌĆö incidence angle is a key design parameter.
- Ō£ģ **Major advantages:** cheap, robust, works in darkness/dust/poor light, transparent surfaces OK.
- Ō£ģ **Major limitations:** poor angular resolution, cross-talk, temperature sensitivity, soft-surface absorption, slow refresh rate compared to LiDAR.
- Ō£ģ **Standard exam numbers to memorize:** $c = 343$ m/s, $\theta = 15┬░$ for HC-SR04, blind zone Ōēł 2ŌĆō3 cm, max range Ōēł 4 m.
- Ō£ģ **Equations you must be able to derive blindfolded:** Range equation, speed-of-sound vs. temperature, blind-zone equation, beam footprint equation.
- Ō£ģ **Cross-relations:** Same ToF principle is used in radar and LiDAR; the only difference is the wave-propagation medium (radio vs. light vs. sound).

---

**End of Notes ŌĆö Module 1 / Ultrasonic Rangefinders**
**Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | PERAT741 Robot Navigation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground-Based Beacons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ground-Based Beacons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ground-Based Beacons** are fixed, externally mounted reference points (transmitters or reflectors) deployed at known coordinates within an operational environment to enable a mobile robot to determine its own position, orientation, or both, through the measurement of geometric relationships (distance, angle, or signal strength) between the robot's onboard sensors and the beacon network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A beacon-based localization system is an *absolute position estimation* technique that falls under the broader category of **exteroceptive sensing**, where the robot infers its global pose by referencing infrastructure external to its own chassis, as opposed to **dead reckoning (proprioception)** which suffers from unbounded error accumulation.

The defining attributes of a ground-based beacon system are:

| Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Beacon Position** | Fixed, known a priori and stored in a workspace map |
| **Measurement Type** | Range (distance), Bearing (angle), or Signal Intensity |
| **Sensor Coupling** | Requires Line-of-Sight (LOS) or acoustic/RF propagation path |
| **Error Behavior** | **Bounded** ŌĆö error does not grow unbounded with travel distance |
| **Reference Frame** | Global (world coordinates) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Lighthouse and the Sailor:**
> Imagine a sailor lost in fog at sea. The sailor cannot see the shore, but can detect signals from **three lighthouses** whose exact positions are marked on a nautical chart. By measuring the *angle* to each lighthouse (with a compass) and the *time delay* of the light flash, the sailor plots three intersecting arcs on the chart and instantly knows the ship's global position ŌĆö even without having ever been there before. The robot in a beacon system plays the role of the sailor; the beacons are the lighthouses, and triangulation/trilateration is the chart-plotting geometry.

### 1.3 Classification of Ground-Based Beacons

Ground-based beacons are broadly classified based on the physical medium of signal propagation:

1. **Optical Beacons** ŌĆö Visible/infrared light emitters or retroreflective markers detected by onboard cameras.
2. **Radio Frequency (RF) Beacons** ŌĆö Wi-Fi, UWB, RFID, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) transmitters.
3. **Acoustic / Ultrasonic Beacons** ŌĆö Piezoelectric transducers emitting high-frequency sound pulses.
4. **Magnetic Beacons** ŌĆö Active coils generating identifiable magnetic field signatures.
5. **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) Beacons** ŌĆö Space-based, but functionally identical to ground beacons in geometry (e.g., GPS).

### 1.4 Why Use Beacons? ŌĆö Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem with Dead Reckoning:** Wheel odometry integrates small errors over time ŌåÆ after traveling **100 m**, an uncalibrated robot can be off by **>5 m** (5% error). In contrast, beacon-based localization yields errors typically bounded to **<10 cm** regardless of distance traveled. This is why beacons are the *de facto* industrial standard for AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Localization Principles

A beacon system solves one of two fundamental geometric problems:

- **Triangulation** ŌĆö Uses **angles (bearings)** to two or more known beacons.
- **Trilateration** ŌĆö Uses **distances (ranges)** to two or more known beacons.
- **Hybrid (Angulation + Ranging)** ŌĆö Combines both for redundancy and improved accuracy.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle (Generic Beacon System)

1. **Surveying Phase (Offline):** The beacon positions $\mathbf{b}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ are surveyed with high-precision instruments (theodolite, total station) and stored in the robot's workspace map.
2. **Sensing Phase (Online):** The robot's onboard receiver measures a geometric quantity $z_i$ relative to beacon $i$.
3. **Modeling Phase:** Each measurement is expressed as $z_i = h_i(\mathbf{x}) + v_i$, where $\mathbf{x}$ is the robot pose, $h_i(\cdot)$ is the measurement model, and $v_i$ is measurement noise.
4. **Estimation Phase:** A nonlinear least-squares solver (e.g., Gauss-Newton, Levenberg-Marquardt) minimizes the residual sum:
$$\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( z_i - h_i(\mathbf{x}) \right)^2$$
5. **Publishing Phase:** The estimated pose $\mathbf{x}^*$ is fed to the navigation stack for path planning and control.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS ŌĆö MEMORIZE THESE FOR ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range from Time-of-Flight (ToF) | $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = signal speed (m/s), $\Delta t$ = round-trip time (s) |
| 2 | Range from Signal Strength (RSS) | $r = r_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_0 - P_r}{10 n}}$ | $P_r$ = received (dBm), $n$ = path-loss exponent |
| 3 | Bearing to single beacon | $\theta = \text{atan2}(y_i - y_r, \, x_i - x_r)$ | Angles in **radians** (convert ├Ś $\frac{180}{\pi}$) |
| 4 | 2-Beacon Triangulation (planar) | $\mathbf{x} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | $A$ = bearing matrix, $\mathbf{b}$ = beacon position vector |
| 5 | 2D Trilateration (3 beacons) | Solve system of 3 distance circles | Closed-form via **linearization** |
| 6 | Generalized Least Squares | $\mathbf{x}^* = (H^T R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T R^{-1} \mathbf{z}$ | $R$ = measurement noise covariance |
| 7 | Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (range) | $\sigma_r \geq \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{\text{SNR} \cdot \beta^2}}$ | $\beta$ = signal bandwidth (Hz) |
| 8 | Geometric Dilution of Precision | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{tr}((H^T H)^{-1})}$ | Lower is better |
| 9 | Number of beacons required (2D) | $n \geq 3$ for trilateration, $n \geq 2$ for triangulation |
| 10 | Number of beacons required (3D) | $n \geq 4$ for trilateration, $n \geq 2$ for triangulation + roll/pitch |

> [!WARNING]
> **No vertical pipes `|` inside table cells!** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX if absolute-value bars are needed. All formulas above are pipe-free for safe markdown rendering.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Industrial AGVs:** Magnetic tape + RFID beacons at workstations for ┬▒10 mm docking accuracy.
- **Hospital logistics:** UWB beacons for instrument tracking in ORs.
- **Autonomous tractors:** RTK-GPS (a beacon-like system) for sub-centimeter row guidance.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Acoustic beacons (USBL ŌĆö Ultra-Short Baseline) for subsea navigation where GPS is unavailable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 2D Trilateration from Three Beacons (Closed Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A robot at unknown position $(x, y)$ measures distances $r_1, r_2, r_3$ to three beacons located at known positions $B_1(x_1, y_1)$, $B_2(x_2, y_2)$, $B_3(x_3, y_3)$. Derive a closed-form expression for $(x, y)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the three range equations** (Euclidean distance constraint):

$$(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = r_1^2$$

$$(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = r_2^2$$

$$(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 = r_3^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Subtract Equation 1 from Equations 2 and 3 to linearize.**

Expanding Equation 2:

$$x^2 - 2 x x_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_2 + y_2^2 = r_2^2$$

Subtracting Equation 1 from the expanded Equation 2, the quadratic $x^2 + y^2$ cancels:

$$-2 x x_2 + x_2^2 + 2 x x_1 - x_1^2 - 2 y y_2 + y_2^2 + 2 y y_1 - y_1^2 = r_2^2 - r_1^2$$

Rearranging into a linear form in $x$ and $y$:

$$2(x_1 - x_2) x + 2(y_1 - y_2) y = r_2^2 - r_1^2 - x_2^2 - y_2^2 + x_1^2 + y_1^2$$

Let us define the constants:

$$A_1 = 2(x_1 - x_2), \quad B_1 = 2(y_1 - y_2)$$

$$C_1 = r_2^2 - r_1^2 - x_2^2 - y_2^2 + x_1^2 + y_1^2$$

Similarly, subtracting Equation 1 from Equation 3 yields:

$$A_2 = 2(x_1 - x_3), \quad B_2 = 2(y_1 - y_3)$$

$$C_2 = r_3^2 - r_1^2 - x_3^2 - y_3^2 + x_1^2 + y_1^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the linear system in matrix form:**

$$\begin{aligned} A_1 x + B_1 y &= C_1 \\ A_2 x + B_2 y &= C_2 \end{aligned} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \begin{bmatrix} A_1 & B_1 \\ A_2 & B_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_1 \\ C_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Cramer's rule for the closed-form solution:**

$$D = A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1$$

$$x = \frac{C_1 B_2 - C_2 B_1}{D}$$

$$y = \frac{A_1 C_2 - A_2 C_1}{D}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** $D \neq 0$ requires the three beacons to be **non-collinear** ŌĆö they must span a triangle. If $D = 0$, the system is **singular** and the robot lies on the line joining two beacons (no unique intersection).

### 3.2 Derivation: Bearing-Based Triangulation (Two Beacons)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A robot at $(x, y)$ measures bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to two beacons at $B_1, B_2$. Find the robot's position.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the bearing as a tangent:**

$$\tan \theta_1 = \frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1}, \quad \tan \theta_2 = \frac{y - y_2}{x - x_2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Cross-multiply and rearrange into linear form:**

$$-(x - x_1) \sin \theta_1 + (y - y_1) \cos \theta_1 = 0$$

$$-(x - x_2) \sin \theta_2 + (y - y_2) \cos \theta_2 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Collect into matrix form $A \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} -\sin \theta_1 & \cos \theta_1 \\ -\sin \theta_2 & \cos \theta_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -x_1 \sin \theta_1 + y_1 \cos \theta_1 \\ -x_2 \sin \theta_2 + y_2 \cos \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve via matrix inverse (provided $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2$):**

$$\mathbf{x} = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2$?** If the robot, beacon 1, and beacon 2 are collinear, both bearing vectors are parallel and $A$ becomes singular ŌĆö geometrically, the two rays never intersect at a unique point.

### 3.3 Derivation: GDOP (Geometric Dilution of Precision)

GDOP quantifies how beacon geometry amplifies range/bearing noise into position error. The standard linearized measurement model is:

$$\Delta \mathbf{z} = H \Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{v}$$

The covariance of the position estimate is:

$$P = (H^T H)^{-1} \sigma_{\text{meas}}^2$$

The GDOP is the trace of $P$ normalized by measurement variance:

$$\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left( (H^T H)^{-1} \right)}$$

For a planar trilateration with three beacons, $H$ is the $3 \times 2$ Jacobian of the range equations evaluated at the robot pose. **Beacon placement at the vertices of an equilateral triangle centered on the workspace yields the minimum GDOP (Ōēł1.154).**

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Beacon Modalities

| Modality | Range | Accuracy | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical (retroreflective)** | 5ŌĆō50 m | mmŌĆōcm | Cheap, fast | LOS required, sensitive to ambient light |
| **UWB RF** | 10ŌĆō100 m | 10ŌĆō30 cm | Penetrates walls, LOS not required | Expensive chips, FCC limits |
| **Ultrasonic** | 0.1ŌĆō10 m | 1ŌĆō5 cm | Cheap, simple | Speed of sound ŌåÆ poor at distance, multipath |
| **BLE / Wi-Fi RSS** | 5ŌĆō50 m | 1ŌĆō5 m | Reuses existing infra | Highly environment-dependent |
| **Magnetic** | 0.1ŌĆō3 m | mm | Robust to dust/smoke | Short range, metal interference |
| **GNSS (GPS/RTK)** | Global | 1 m (GPS) / 1 cm (RTK) | Truly global | Outdoor only, multi-path in urban canyons |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Beacon Localization System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace Survey\nKnown Beacon Map M] --> B[Beacon Network\nB1, B2, B3 ... Bn]
    B --> C[Signal Propagation\nRF / Optical / Acoustic]
    C --> D[Onboard Receiver\nToF / RSS / Bearing]
    D --> E[Measurement Vector\nz = r1, r2 ... ╬Ė1, ╬Ė2 ...]
    E --> F[Geometric Model\nh of x]
    A --> F
    F --> G[Nonlinear Estimator\nGauss-Newton / EKF]
    G --> H[Estimated Robot Pose\nx star, y star, theta star]
    H --> I[Navigation Stack\nPlanner + Controller]
    I --> J[Wheel Commands\nv, omega]
    J --> K[Robot Motion]
    K -->|Feedback| D
    H -->|Logging| L[Pose History\nfor SLAM / Mapping]
```

### 4.2 Trilateration Geometry ŌĆö 3-Beacon Intersection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph env["Workspace with Beacons"]
        B1["Beacon 1\n(0, 0)"]
        B2["Beacon 2\n(d, 0)"]
        B3["Beacon 3\n(d/2, h)"]
    end
    subgraph measurements["Range Measurements"]
        R1["r1 measured"]
        R2["r2 measured"]
        R3["r3 measured"]
    end
    subgraph geometry["Intersection Geometry"]
        C1["Circle 1\ncenter B1\nradius r1"]
        C2["Circle 2\ncenter B2\nradius r2"]
        C3["Circle 3\ncenter B3\nradius r3"]
    end
    subgraph result["Robot Position"]
        P["Robot Pose\nx star, y star"]
    end
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> P
    C2 --> P
    C3 --> P
    B1 --> R1
    B2 --> R2
    B3 --> R3
    R1 --> C1
    R2 --> C2
    R3 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Triangulation Geometry ŌĆö Bearing Lines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Robot at\nx, y"] -- "Bearing theta 1" --> B1["Beacon 1\nknown"]
    A -- "Bearing theta 2" --> B2["Beacon 2\nknown"]
    B1 -. "Intersection\nof rays" .-> A
    B2 -. "Intersection\nof rays" .-> A
    style A fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#dff,stroke:#333
    style B2 fill:#dff,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Beacon Selection & GDOP Heatmap (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Good["Good Geometry\nEquilateral / Symmetric\nGDOP < 2"] --> Result1["High Accuracy"]
    Bad["Poor Geometry\nCollinear Beacons\nGDOP > 10"] --> Result2["Highly Degraded Accuracy"]
    Optimal["Optimal Placement\nBeacons at Workspace Periphery\nSurrounding Work Volume"] --> Result1
    Avoid["Avoid Placement\nAll beacons on one wall\nAll beacons clustered"] --> Bad
    style Good fill:#cfc
    style Optimal fill:#9f9
    style Result1 fill:#9f9
    style Bad fill:#fcc
    style Avoid fill:#f99
    style Result2 fill:#f99
```

### 4.5 Algorithm Pipeline: From Beacon Signal to Robot Pose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Trigger Beacon\npulse or poll signal"] --> S2["Step 2: Timestamp\narrival at receiver"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute Range\nr = c * dt / 2"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Stack into\nMeasurement Vector z"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Initialize Estimate\nx0 = prior pose or dead reckoning"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Iterative Solve\nmin sum z - h of x squared"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Convergence Check\ndelta x less than epsilon"]
    S7 --> S8{"Converged?"}
    S8 -- "Yes" --> S9["Publish Pose\nto Navigation Stack"]
    S8 -- "No, iterate" --> S6
    S9 --> S10["Predict Next Step\nKalman / EKF Predict"]
    S10 --> S1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define ground-based beacons in the context of robot navigation. List any three physical media used for beacon signal propagation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Ground-based beacons are fixed reference transmitters or reflectors deployed at known, pre-surveyed positions in the robot's workspace. A mobile robot localizes itself by measuring geometric quantities ŌĆö such as range, bearing, or signal strength ŌĆö between its onboard sensors and the beacons.

**Three physical media (1 mark, 0.33 each):**
1. **Optical (visible/infrared light)** ŌĆö e.g., retroreflective markers + onboard camera.
2. **Radio Frequency (RF)** ŌĆö e.g., UWB, Wi-Fi, BLE, RFID.
3. **Acoustic / Ultrasonic** ŌĆö e.g., piezoelectric transducers using time-of-flight of sound.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Differentiate between triangulation and trilateration in beacon-based localization. State the minimum number of beacons required for each in a 2D plane.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Triangulation | Trilateration |
|---|---|---|
| Measured quantity | **Angles (bearings)** | **Distances (ranges)** |
| Sensor required | Directional (e.g., rotating antenna, camera) | Range sensor (ToF, RSS) |
| Minimum beacons (2D) | **2 beacons** | **3 beacons** |
| Minimum beacons (3D) | **2 beacons** (with known orientation) | **4 beacons** |
| Geometry | Intersection of **rays** | Intersection of **circles/spheres** |

**Key distinction (1 mark):** Triangulation needs only 2 beacons because two non-parallel lines always intersect at a unique point; trilateration needs 3 because the intersection of two circles gives two ambiguous points, and the third circle disambiguates.

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between two full questions.

---

#### Ō£Å’ĖÅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A mobile robot in a 2D warehouse measures its distance to three fixed beacons. The beacon positions and measured ranges are:

| Beacon | Position (m) | Measured Range (m) |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $(0, 0)$ | $r_1 = 5.00$ |
| $B_2$ | $(10, 0)$ | $r_2 = 5.00$ |
| $B_3$ | $(5, 8.66)$ | $r_3 = 8.66$ |

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the closed-form solution for 2D trilateration. Using the above data, compute the robot's estimated position $(x, y)$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compute the Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP) for this configuration. Comment on whether this beacon placement is good, and suggest one improvement.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the three range equations (1 mark):**

$$(x - 0)^2 + (y - 0)^2 = 5^2 = 25$$

$$(x - 10)^2 + (y - 0)^2 = 5^2 = 25$$

$$(x - 5)^2 + (y - 8.66)^2 = 8.66^2 = 75.0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Subtract Eq.(1) from Eq.(2) and Eq.(3) to linearize (2 marks):**

From (2) ŌłÆ (1):

$$-20x + 100 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 5$$

From (3) ŌłÆ (1):

$$-10x + 25 - 17.32y + 75 = 75 - 25 = 50$$

$$-10(5) + 100 - 17.32y = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 50 - 17.32y = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify the solution (1 mark):**

Distance from $(5, 0)$ to $B_1(0,0) = 5$ Ō£ō
Distance from $(5, 0)$ to $B_2(10,0) = 5$ Ō£ō
Distance from $(5, 0)$ to $B_3(5, 8.66) = 8.66$ Ō£ō

**Answer (1 mark):** The robot is located at $\boxed{(x, y) = (5.00, 0.00) \text{ m}}$.

**General derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$A_1 = 2(x_1 - x_2), \quad B_1 = 2(y_1 - y_2)$$

$$x = \frac{C_1 B_2 - C_2 B_1}{A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1}, \quad y = \frac{A_1 C_2 - A_2 C_1}{A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1}$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Build the Jacobian $H$ (3 marks):**

The range measurement Jacobian is:

$$H_{i} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x - x_i}{r_i} & \dfrac{y - y_i}{r_i} \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing row by row at $(5, 0)$:

$$H_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{5 - 0}{5} & \dfrac{0 - 0}{5} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$H_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{5 - 10}{5} & \dfrac{0 - 0}{5} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$H_3 = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{5 - 5}{8.66} & \dfrac{0 - 8.66}{8.66} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $H^T H$ (2 marks):**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad H^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$H^T H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute GDOP (2 marks):**

$$(H^T H)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1}) = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5$$

$$\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{1.5} \approx 1.225$$

**Comment (1 mark):** GDOP Ōēł 1.22 is **excellent** (close to the theoretical minimum of 1.154 for an equilateral layout). The beacons are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 10 m, which is the **gold-standard geometry** for 2D trilateration. No improvement is strictly necessary, though increasing the triangle size would reduce absolute position error proportionally.

---

#### Ō£Å’ĖÅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö ALTERNATIVE

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a ground-based beacon localization system. Explain the role of each block in the measurement-to-pose pipeline.

**(b) [7 marks]** A robot uses **time-of-flight (ToF)** of an ultrasonic pulse to measure the distance to a beacon. The pulse round-trip time is measured as $\Delta t = 29.0$ ms. The speed of sound in air is $c = 343$ m/s.
   (i) Compute the range to the beacon.
   (ii) If the temperature is $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$, recalculate the range using the temperature-corrected speed of sound $c = 331 + 0.6 T$.
   (iii) Comment on the sources of error in ultrasonic beacon ranging.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** See the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student should reproduce:

```
Survey Map ŌåÆ Beacon Network ŌåÆ Propagation ŌåÆ Receiver ŌåÆ Measurement
                                                              Ōåō
                              Estimator ŌåÉ Geometric Model
                                   Ōåō
                              Robot Pose ŌåÆ Navigation Stack ŌåÆ Wheels
```

**Description of blocks (4 marks, 1 each for 4 main blocks):**

1. **Beacon Network (Infrastructure):** Pre-surveyed fixed transmitters positioned to maximize coverage and minimize GDOP. Stores $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ in workspace map.
2. **Onboard Receiver & Measurement Block:** Hardware (microphone, photodiode, RF antenna) that converts the physical signal into a digital measurement $z_i$ ŌĆö typically a timestamped pulse, RSSI value, or pixel coordinate for bearing extraction.
3. **Geometric Model + Estimator:** Translates the measurement $z_i$ into a function $h_i(\mathbf{x})$ of the robot pose, then solves the nonlinear least-squares problem using a Gauss-Newton, Levenberg-Marquardt, or Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm to produce the optimal pose estimate $\mathbf{x}^*$.
4. **Navigation Stack Consumer:** The estimated pose is published to the motion planner and controller, which compute velocity commands to track the desired trajectory. Feedback is implicit because the next measurement cycle uses the updated pose.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Part (i) ŌĆö Range computation (2 marks):**

$$r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 0.029}{2} = \frac{9.947}{2} = 4.974 \text{ m}$$

**Part (ii) ŌĆö Temperature-corrected range (3 marks):**

$$c = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

$$r_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{349 \times 0.029}{2} = \frac{10.121}{2} = 5.061 \text{ m}$$

**Error introduced by ignoring temperature (1 mark):**

$$\Delta r = 5.061 - 4.974 = 0.087 \text{ m} \approx 8.7 \text{ cm}$$

This is a **1.7% error**, which is significant for high-precision AGV docking. Hence, ultrasonic systems must include temperature compensation in their firmware.

**Part (iii) ŌĆö Sources of error (1 mark, list any 3):**

1. **Temperature/humidity dependence** of the speed of sound (the dominant error, ~0.6 m/s per ┬░C).
2. **Multipath reflections** from walls and floors causing ghost echoes.
3. **Crosstalk** between multiple beacons firing simultaneously.
4. **Beam width / directivity** ŌĆö the cone of detection may include reflections, biasing the time-of-arrival.
5. **Air turbulence and wind** along the propagation path.

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF:** Round-trip time must be divided by **2** because the pulse travels to the beacon AND back (in active systems). Marks lost: full 7 in numerical problems.
> 2. **Not subtracting the constant $x^2 + y^2$ in trilateration:** Students forget to expand and subtract, ending up with a nonlinear system they cannot solve. Marks lost: 2ŌĆō3.
> 3. **Confusing RSSI with ToF:** RSSI gives range via the log-distance path-loss model, NOT linearly with time. Mixing them yields wrong formulas.
> 4. **Ignoring non-collinearity of beacons:** If beacons are collinear, $D = 0$ and the system is singular. Always check and state the non-collinearity assumption.
> 5. **Units:** Always convert ms ŌåÆ s, ┬░C ŌåÆ K only if formula requires, and angles to radians before applying $\tan$ or $\text{atan2}$.
> 6. **No "verification step":** KTU examiners award bonus marks when students plug the answer back into the original equations to verify.

---

### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Ground-based beacons** provide **absolute, bounded-error** localization by referencing fixed external infrastructure.
- [x] Two fundamental geometries: **triangulation (angles)** and **trilateration (distances)**.
- [x] Minimum beacons: **2 for triangulation (2D)**, **3 for trilateration (2D)**, **4 for trilateration (3D)**.
- [x] ToF range formula: $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ ŌĆö **always divide by 2** for round-trip.
- [x] RSS range formula (log-distance): $r = r_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_0 - P_r}{10 n}}$.
- [x] Ultrasonic speed of sound: $c = 331 + 0.6 T$ m/s (T in ┬░C).
- [x] Closed-form trilateration uses **linearization by subtraction** of the squared equations.
- [x] **GDOP** is minimized when beacons form an **equilateral triangle** surrounding the work volume.
- [x] Non-collinearity of beacons is a **mandatory** assumption ŌĆö otherwise $D = 0$.
- [x] Beacon systems are **LOS-sensitive** (optical/RF) or **multipath-sensitive** (ultrasonic).
- [x] **GNSS (GPS)** is conceptually a satellite-based beacon system; RTK-GPS is the high-precision variant.
- [x] Industrial standard for AGVs: magnetic beacons + RFID for ┬▒10 mm accuracy.
- [x] **Estimator choice:** Gauss-Newton for batch, EKF/UKF for recursive real-time estimation.
- [x] **Cram├®r-Rao bound** sets the theoretical floor on ranging error: $\sigma_r \geq \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{\text{SNR} \cdot \beta^2}}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** Memorize the trilateration closed-form (Cramer's rule) and the ToF range formula ŌĆö these two alone account for ~60% of Part B marks in any beacon-based localization question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Field and Optical Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Motion Field and Optical Flow # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Motion Field ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Motion Field**: The **Motion Field** is the **2D projection (onto the image plane) of the actual 3D velocity vectors** of points in the physical scene, as observed from a moving camera or a camera viewing a moving scene. It represents the *true* geometric motion of surface points in the world, projected through the camera's pinhole model onto the image sensor. Mathematically, the motion field at image point $(x, y)$ is defined as the projection of the 3D velocity $\mathbf{V} = (V_X, V_Y, V_Z)$ of the corresponding 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ onto the image plane. ## 1.2 Optical Flow ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optical Flow**: **Optical Flow** is the apparent motion of the **brightness (intensity) pattern** in an image sequence. It is the **2D velocity field** that describes how pixel intensities move between consecutive frames. Optical flow is what we can *compute from images* ŌĆö it is an *estimate* that ideally equals the motion field, but in practice may differ due to lighting changes, aperture effects, and lack of texture. Optical Flow is often denoted $\mathbf{u} = (u, v)$, where $u = \frac{dx}{dt}$ and $v = \frac{dy}{dt}$ are the horizontal and vertical image-plane velocity components. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö Sitting in a Moving Train** Imagine you are sitting inside a moving train and looking out the window: - **Trees, poles, and buildings close to the train** appear to **rush past quickly** in the opposite direction of motion ŌåÆ **Large optical flow vectors**. - **Distant mountains** appear to barely move ŌåÆ **Small optical flow vectors**. - **The sun** (at infinity) appears **stationary** ŌåÆ **Zero optical flow vector**. The **Motion Field** is what *actually happens geometrically* ŌĆö the true projected velocities of the trees and mountains. The **Optical Flow** is what your *eyes (or a camera sensor) actually perceive* as moving brightness patterns on your retina (or image sensor). | Scenario | Motion Field (True Geometry) | Optical Flow (Perceived) | |---|---|---| | Camera moves, static scene with rich texture | Smooth, well-defined | Ōēł Motion Field (accurate) | | Camera stationary, smooth rotating sphere | Edge pixels move | Constant brightness inside ŌåÆ **No optical flow detected** | | Light source flickers, static object | Object is stationary (zero motion field) | Brightness changes ŌåÆ **Optical flow ŌēĀ Motion Field** | > [!WARNING] > **Key Pitfall (Often tested in KTU exams):** Motion Field and Optical Flow are **NOT always equal**. They coincide only under ideal conditions ŌĆö rigid body motion, Lambertian (diffuse) reflectance, and uniform illumination. KTU examiners frequently ask students to distinguish between the two. --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Robot Navigation For autonomous robots (self-driving cars, drones, warehouse AGVs), visual motion estimation is **fundamental**: - **Obstacle detection**: When the robot moves, static obstacles generate predictable optical flow patterns; moving objects generate divergent patterns. - **Ego-motion estimation**: Recovering the robot's own motion from observed flow. - **Depth estimation**: Optical flow magnitude is inversely proportional to depth (closer objects ŌåÆ faster apparent motion). - **Visual odometry / SLAM**: Optical flow provides the data association between consecutive frames. --- ## 1.5 Fundamental Assumptions Underlying Optical Flow > [!NOTE] > Three core assumptions are foundational to nearly all optical flow algorithms: 1. **Brightness Constancy Assumption**: The intensity of a pixel remains constant as it moves between frames. 2. **Temporal Persistence** (Small Motion): Pixel displacements between consecutive frames are small (typically < 1 pixel). 3. **Spatial Coherence**: Neighboring pixels belong to the same surface and have similar motion (smooth flow field). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometric Derivation of the Motion Field Consider a pinhole camera with focal length $f$ observing a 3D point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ moving with velocity $\mathbf{V} = (V_X, V_Y, V_Z)$ relative to the camera. Using the perspective projection equations: $$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$ The image-plane velocity (motion field) is obtained by differentiating $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ with respect to time $t$: $$\dot{x} = \frac{dx}{dt}, \quad \dot{y} = \frac{dy}{dt}$$ ### Component-wise Expansion Applying the quotient rule to $x = fX/Z$: $$\dot{x} = f \cdot \frac{\dot{X} Z - X \dot{Z}}{Z^2}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $Z$ and substituting $x = fX/Z$ (i.e., $X/Z = x/f$): $$\dot{x} = \frac{f \dot{X} - x \dot{Z}}{Z} = \frac{f V_X - x V_Z}{Z}$$ Similarly for $\dot{y}$: $$\dot{y} = \frac{f V_Y - y V_Z}{Z}$$ This gives the canonical **Motion Field Equations**: $$\boxed{\;\dot{x} = \frac{f V_X - x V_Z}{Z}, \qquad \dot{y} = \frac{f V_Y - y V_Z}{Z}\;}$$ --- ## 2.2 Special Case: Pure Camera Translation (Translational Motion Field) When the camera translates with velocity $\mathbf{T} = (T_X, T_Y, T_Z)$ and the scene is static, then $V_X = -T_X$, $V_Y = -T_Y$, $V_Z = -T_Z$. Substituting: $$\dot{x} = \frac{-f T_X + x T_Z}{Z}, \quad \dot{y} = \frac{-f T_Y + y T_Z}{Z}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Focus of Expansion (FOE)**: When $T_Z \neq 0$, there exists a single image point $(x_0, y_0)$ where the motion field is **zero** ŌĆö this is the **Focus of Expansion**. Setting $\dot{x} = \dot{y} = 0$ gives: $$x_0 = f \cdot \frac{T_X}{T_Z}, \qquad y_0 = f \cdot \frac{T_Y}{T_Z}$$ This corresponds to the 3D direction of translation. **The FOE is a critical concept for KTU exams and robot navigation ŌĆö it directly gives the heading direction.** --- ## 2.3 The Optical Flow Constraint Equation (Brightness Constancy) Starting from the **Brightness Constancy Assumption**: $$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$$ Applying a first-order Taylor series expansion around $(x, y, t)$: $$I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt) \approx I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} dt$$ Subtracting $I(x, y, t)$ from both sides: $$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} dt = 0$$ Dividing by $dt$: $$\boxed{\;I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0\;}$$ where: - $I_x = \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}$ ŌĆö spatial gradient in $x$ - $I_y = \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}$ ŌĆö spatial gradient in $y$ - $I_t = \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$ ŌĆö temporal gradient - $u = \frac{dx}{dt}$, $v = \frac{dy}{dt}$ ŌĆö optical flow components > [!NOTE] > In **vector notation**, the constraint becomes $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} + I_t = 0$, where $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)^T$ is the spatial image gradient. --- ## 2.4 The Aperture Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aperture Problem**: A single pixel measurement provides **only one equation** (the brightness constancy equation) but has **two unknowns** ($u$ and $v$). This makes the problem **under-constrained**. Geometrically, only the **component of motion along the intensity gradient** $\nabla I$ can be determined ŌĆö motion perpendicular to the gradient is invisible through a small aperture. The component along the gradient is the **normal flow**: $$\boxed{\;u_n = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}\;}$$ The normal flow $u_n$ is what can be **directly computed** without any additional assumptions. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Significance** | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | Motion Field (general) | $\dot{x} = (f V_X - x V_Z)/Z$, $\dot{y} = (f V_Y - y V_Z)/Z$ | True projected 3D motion | | 2 | Motion Field (translational) | $\dot{x} = (-f T_X + x T_Z)/Z$ | Camera moving, static scene | | 3 | Focus of Expansion (FOE) | $x_0 = f T_X / T_Z$, $y_0 = f T_Y / T_Z$ | Point of zero flow; heading | | 4 | Brightness Constancy | $I(x,y,t) = I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt)$ | Core assumption of optical flow | | 5 | Optical Flow Constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Fundamental flow equation | | 6 | Normal Flow | $u_n = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | Flow along intensity gradient | | 7 | Lucas-Kanade Spatial Coherence | $\sum W^2 [\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} + I_t]^2$ minimized in window | Local constant flow assumption | | 8 | Horn-Schunck Smoothness | $\min \iint (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \lambda^2(\vert \nabla u \vert^2 + \vert \nabla v \vert^2) dxdy$ | Global smoothness constraint | | 9 | Depth from Flow | $Z = f V_X / (\dot{x} + x V_Z / f)$ | Recover depth from flow | | 10 | Inverse Depth Relation | $\dot{x} / x \approx -V_Z / Z$ | Approximation for large $Z$ | > **Note on units**: $f$ in pixels (or mm), $X, Y, Z$ in meters, $V$ in m/s, $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ in pixels/sec, $I$ in intensity units (0ŌĆō255 for 8-bit). --- ## 2.6 Solution Methods ŌĆö Brief Overview ### A. Lucas-Kanade Method (Sparse, Local) Assumes the flow $(\mathbf{u})$ is **constant within a small spatial neighborhood window** $W$. Solves the over-determined system $A\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{b}$ using **least squares**: $$\mathbf{u} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$ where $A = [I_x, I_y]^T$ stacked over the window, and $\mathbf{b} = -I_t$. The closed-form solution: $$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} -\sum I_x I_t \\ -\sum I_y I_t \end{bmatrix}$$ This is computable **only when the $2\times 2$ matrix is invertible** ŌĆö requires texture in at least two directions (corner-like regions). This is the well-known **Harris corner condition**. ### B. Horn-Schunck Method (Dense, Global) Adds a **global smoothness constraint** that the flow field varies smoothly. Minimizes the energy functional: $$E = \iint \underbrace{(I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2}_{\text{data term}} + \underbrace{\lambda^2 \left(\left\vert \nabla u \right\vert^2 + \left\vert \nabla v \right\vert^2\right)}_{\text{smoothness term}} \, dx\, dy$$ Results in iterative Laplacian-based PDEs: $$\left(1 + \lambda^2 (\nabla^2 u + \nabla^2 v)\right) \mathbf{u}^{k+1} = \bar{\mathbf{u}}^k - \frac{\nabla I (\nabla I \cdot \bar{\mathbf{u}}^k + I_t)}{\lambda^2 + \vert \nabla I \vert^2}$$ ### C. Feature-Based Methods (Sparse, Robust) Detect corners (Harris, SIFT, ORB), track them between frames (Lucas-Kanade pyramidal tracking), compute flow at feature points only. Used in **visual odometry and SLAM**. ### D. Modern Deep Learning Methods FlowNet, FlowNet 2.0, PWC-Net, RAFT ŌĆö supervised CNN/transformer-based methods producing dense flow fields with high accuracy. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Applications in Robotics and Beyond:** 1. **Self-Driving Cars (e.g., Tesla, Waymo)**: Optical flow detects moving objects (vehicles, pedestrians) and estimates their relative velocity. 2. **Drone Navigation**: Bio-inspired optical flow for obstacle avoidance and hovering stability (used in research drones mimicking insect vision). 3. **Visual Odometry / SLAM**: ORB-SLAM2, VINS-Mono use sparse optical flow (Lucas-Kanade on tracked features) for pose estimation. 4. **Action Recognition**: Flow fields feed into CNNs for activity recognition in surveillance. 5. **Medical Imaging**: Tracking cell motion in microscopy. 6. **Video Compression**: MPEG uses block-based motion estimation (a discrete version of optical flow). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Motion Field from 3D Velocity ### Setup Camera with focal length $f$ projects 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ to image point $(x, y)$: $$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$ The 3D point moves with velocity $(V_X, V_Y, V_Z) = (\dot{X}, \dot{Y}, \dot{Z})$. ### Step 1: Differentiate $x$ with respect to time $$\dot{x} = \frac{d}{dt} \left( f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \right)$$ Since $f$ is constant: $$\dot{x} = f \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{X}{Z} \right)$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Quotient Rule $$\frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{X}{Z} \right) = \frac{\dot{X} \cdot Z - X \cdot \dot{Z}}{Z^2}$$ ### Step 3: Combine $$\dot{x} = f \cdot \frac{\dot{X} Z - X \dot{Z}}{Z^2}$$ ### Step 4: Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by $Z$ $$\dot{x} = f \cdot \frac{\dot{X} - (X/Z) \dot{Z}}{Z}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute $x = fX/Z$, which gives $X/Z = x/f$ $$\dot{x} = f \cdot \frac{V_X - (x/f) V_Z}{Z}$$ ### Step 6: Distribute $f$ $$\dot{x} = \frac{f V_X - x V_Z}{Z}$$ ### Step 7: Repeat identically for $y$ $$\dot{y} = \frac{f V_Y - y V_Z}{Z}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\;\dot{x} = \frac{f V_X - x V_Z}{Z}, \qquad \dot{y} = \frac{f V_Y - y V_Z}{Z}\;}$$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Focus of Expansion (FOE) ### Setup For pure translational motion with the camera moving at $(T_X, T_Y, T_Z)$ relative to a static scene: $$V_X = -T_X, \quad V_Y = -T_Y, \quad V_Z = -T_Z$$ ### Step 1: Substitute into Motion Field Equations $$\dot{x} = \frac{-f T_X + x T_Z}{Z}, \quad \dot{y} = \frac{-f T_Y + y T_Z}{Z}$$ ### Step 2: Set $\dot{x} = 0$ and solve for $x$ $$0 = -f T_X + x_0 T_Z$$ $$x_0 T_Z = f T_X$$ $$x_0 = \frac{f T_X}{T_Z}$$ ### Step 3: Set $\dot{y} = 0$ and solve for $y$ $$y_0 = \frac{f T_Y}{T_Z}$$ ### Interpretation The FOE $(x_0, y_0)$ is the image point where the motion field vanishes. It corresponds to the projection of the 3D direction of camera translation onto the image plane. As $Z \to \infty$ (very far points), all motion vectors point *radially away from* (or toward) the FOE. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Optical Flow Constraint Equation ### Step 1: Brightness Constancy Assumption A pixel at $(x, y)$ at time $t$ has intensity $I(x, y, t)$. After displacement $(dx, dy)$ over time $dt$, the same physical point has the same intensity: $$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$$ ### Step 2: First-Order Taylor Expansion Expand $I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$ around $(x, y, t)$: $$I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt) = I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}dt + O(\text{higher order})$$ ### Step 3: Drop Higher-Order Terms (assuming small motion) $$I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt) \approx I(x,y,t) + I_x \, dx + I_y \, dy + I_t \, dt$$ ### Step 4: Substitute into Brightness Constancy $$I(x,y,t) = I(x,y,t) + I_x \, dx + I_y \, dy + I_t \, dt$$ ### Step 5: Simplify $$I_x \, dx + I_y \, dy + I_t \, dt = 0$$ ### Step 6: Divide by $dt$ $$I_x \frac{dx}{dt} + I_y \frac{dy}{dt} + I_t = 0$$ ### Step 7: Define Optical Flow Components $$u = \frac{dx}{dt}, \quad v = \frac{dy}{dt}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\;I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0\;}$$ --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Normal Flow ### Setup The optical flow constraint $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ can be written as a dot product: $$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} = -I_t$$ ### Step 1: Decompose Flow into Normal and Tangential Components Any 2D velocity vector $\mathbf{u} = (u, v)$ can be decomposed as: $$\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}_n + \mathbf{u}_t$$ where: - $\mathbf{u}_n$ is parallel to $\nabla I$ (along the intensity gradient) - $\mathbf{u}_t$ is perpendicular to $\nabla I$ ### Step 2: Dot Product Properties $$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} = \nabla I \cdot (\mathbf{u}_n + \mathbf{u}_t) = \nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u}_n + \underbrace{\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u}_t}_{= 0}$$ Since $\mathbf{u}_t \perp \nabla I$, the dot product vanishes. Therefore: $$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u} = \nabla I \cdot \mathbf{u}_n = -I_t$$ ### Step 3: Express Normal Flow The normal flow lies along the unit gradient direction: $$\mathbf{u}_n = u_n \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}, \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{\mathbf{n}} = \frac{\nabla I}{\vert \nabla I \vert}$$ ### Step 4: Compute Magnitude $$\nabla I \cdot (u_n \hat{\mathbf{n}}) = u_n \vert \nabla I \vert = -I_t$$ Solving for $u_n$: $$u_n = \frac{-I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\;u_n = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation**: $u_n$ represents the flow **perpendicular to image contours** (i.e., across edges). This is the only component observable at a single pixel. The tangential component (along edges) is invisible ŌĆö this is the **Aperture Problem**. --- ## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: Lucas-Kanade Optical Flow ### Step 1: Local Constant Flow Assumption Within a small spatial window $W$ centered at pixel $(x, y)$, assume the flow $(u, v)$ is constant: $$u(x', y') = u, \quad v(x', y') = v, \quad \forall (x', y') \in W$$ ### Step 2: Stack Constraints for All Pixels in Window For each pixel $(x_i, y_i) \in W$: $$I_x(x_i, y_i) u + I_y(x_i, y_i) v = -I_t(x_i, y_i)$$ In matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} I_x(x_1, y_1) & I_y(x_1, y_1) \\ I_x(x_2, y_2) & I_y(x_2, y_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(x_n, y_n) & I_y(x_n, y_n) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -I_t(x_1, y_1) \\ -I_t(x_2, y_2) \\ \vdots \\ -I_t(x_n, y_n) \end{bmatrix}$$ Denote $A \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{b}$. ### Step 3: Minimize the Least-Squares Error Minimize: $$E(\mathbf{u}) = \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in W} W(x_i, y_i)^2 \left[ I_x(x_i, y_i) u + I_y(x_i, y_i) v + I_t(x_i, y_i) \right]^2$$ where $W(x_i, y_i)$ is a window weighting function (often Gaussian). ### Step 4: Set Partial Derivatives to Zero $$\frac{\partial E}{\partial u} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial E}{\partial v} = 0$$ This yields the **normal equations**: $$A^T A \mathbf{u} = A^T \mathbf{b}$$ ### Step 5: Explicit Form of $A^T A$ (the "structure tensor") $$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$ This $2 \times 2$ matrix is the **second-moment matrix** (or **structure tensor**) of the image gradients in the window. ### Step 6: Solve for $\mathbf{u}$ $$\mathbf{u} = (A^T A)^{-1} (A^T \mathbf{b})$$ ### Step 7: Explicit Inversion $$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sum I_x^2 \sum I_y^2 - (\sum I_x I_y)^2} \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_y^2 & -\sum I_x I_y \\ -\sum I_x I_y & \sum I_x^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -\sum I_x I_t \\ -\sum I_y I_t \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 8: Invertibility Condition The matrix is invertible **iff**: $$\det(A^T A) = \sum I_x^2 \sum I_y^2 - (\sum I_x I_y)^2 > 0$$ This is satisfied only at **corner-like regions** where intensity varies in at least two non-parallel directions. > [!NOTE] > **Connection to Harris Corner Detector**: The two eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $A^T A$ represent gradient energy in the principal directions. If both eigenvalues are large, the region is a corner and flow is computable. If one is small (edge), flow is unobservable (aperture problem). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Motion Field vs. Optical Flow ŌĆö Conceptual Distinction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["3D World Scene"] P1["3D Point P moving with velocity V"] P2["Static Object Q"] end subgraph B["Camera / Sensor"] C["Pinhole Camera Model
