$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \det(2\pi R_t)^{-1/2} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(z_t - h(x_t, m))^T R_t^{-1}(z_t - h(x_t, m))\right)$$

**Noise-only case ŌåÆ unimodal (1.5 marks):** If only one pose $x^*$ in the map can plausibly explain $z_t$, then $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ has a single peak at $x^*$ whose width is governed by $R_t$. Larger noise ŌćÆ broader peak. The posterior remains unimodal.

**Aliased case ŌåÆ multi-modal (1.5 marks):** If two distinct poses $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}$ both yield $h(x^{(i)}, m) \approx z_t$, then $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ has *two peaks* of comparable height. After multiplication with the prediction in the Bayes recursion, both peaks survive in the posterior ŌĆö the robot maintains both hypotheses until subsequent measurements disambiguate.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing noise with aliasing (ŌĆō2 marks typical):** Noise = corruption of measurement value; aliasing = multiple poses giving same value. Examiners routinely deduct for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the integral in Bayes recursion (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** Writing only $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$ instead of the full $\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ loses the prediction step marks.
> 3. **Confusing EKF with particle filters on aliasing (ŌĆō2 marks):** EKF *cannot* represent a multi-modal posterior; particle filters can. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Missing the normalizer $\eta$ (ŌĆō1 mark):** The Bayes recursion is incomplete without it.
> 5. **Numerical errors in Mahalanobis / Gaussian likelihood calculations (ŌĆō1 mark each):** Show every step explicitly; do not skip the determinant evaluation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensor Noise** = corruption of the measurement value (stochastic, systematic, outlier).
- **Sensor Aliasing (Perceptual Aliasing)** = multiple distinct poses produce the same measurement.
- **Bayes filter** is the canonical tool that simultaneously handles both via a multi-modal posterior.
- The **master equation** is $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- **Gaussian noise model**: $z_t = h(x_t) + \epsilon_t$, $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.
- **Mahalanobis distance** is the natural metric for noisy multi-dimensional measurements.
- **Particle filters (MCL)** preserve multi-modal posteriors ŌåÆ required for severe aliasing.
- **EKF localization** assumes a unimodal Gaussian posterior ŌåÆ fails under aliasing.
- **Grid / histogram filters** are discrete approximations that retain multi-modality.
- **KLD-sampling** adaptively grows the particle count when posterior complexity increases.
- **Wheel odometry noise** uses the standard parameters $\alpha_1 \ldots \alpha_6$ (translational and rotational error coefficients).
- **Systematic biases** require explicit calibration or state augmentation ŌĆö they do not average out.
- **Outliers** are mitigated via robust kernels or likelihood floors, not naive Gaussians.
- Always show the **likelihood computation** explicitly in numericals ŌĆö including the determinant of $2\pi R$.
- Always include the **normalizer $\eta$** when writing the Bayes update.
- For KTU valuation: distinguish between **handling noise** (Gaussian likelihood) and **handling aliasing** (multi-modal posterior).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effector Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effector Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Effector Noise** (also called **Actuator Noise** or **Motion Noise**) in the context of robot navigation refers to the stochastic uncertainty and error inherent in the execution of motion commands by a mobile robot's actuators (effectors) ŌĆö such as DC motors, stepper motors, servo motors, and wheel encoders. When the robot's controller issues a deterministic command (e.g., "rotate left wheel at 0.5 m/s for 2 seconds"), the actual physical outcome deviates from the commanded value due to mechanical imperfections, wheel slippage, floor irregularities, gear backlash, and electronic noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Effector noise is the uncertainty in robot motion resulting from the probabilistic behavior of the robot's actuators. Unlike sensor noise (which corrupts the robot's *perception* of the world), effector noise corrupts the robot's *action* in the world. It is formally modeled as a probability distribution over the state transition function $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$, where $u_t$ is the control command and $x_t, x_{t-1}$ are consecutive robot poses.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded and asked to walk exactly 10 steps forward and then turn exactly 90 degrees right** (this is the famous "blind man's walk" problem). Even with great concentration, you will:
- Walk slightly more or less than 10 steps (distance error)
- Turn slightly more or less than 90┬░ (rotation error)
- Drift sideways unintentionally (lateral slip error)

Each repetition produces a *slightly different final position* ŌĆö this scatter is **effector noise**.

Now imagine giving the same instruction to a **wheeled robot**:
- The wheel encoders say "we have rotated 10 times", but due to wheel slippage on a wet floor, the robot may have actually moved less.
- The left and right motors may not produce exactly equal torque, causing unintended curvature.
- Gear backlash introduces a small "dead zone" before the wheel actually moves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy ŌĆö The Car Steering Problem:**
> A car's steering wheel is set to "straight", but the car drifts slightly to the left on a cambered road. This drift is *systematic* (predictable) and can be calibrated. However, the random jitter caused by wind gusts, potholes, and tire variability is *non-systematic* (random) noise ŌĆö the true "effector noise" that must be modeled probabilistically.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Wheel encoder resolution:** typically **500ŌĆō20,000 ticks/revolution**
- **Odometry drift rate:** typically **0.1% ŌĆō 10% of distance traveled** depending on surface
- **Standard motion model parameters** (Probabilistic Robotics notation by Thrun, Burgard, Fox): **$\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4, \alpha_5, \alpha_6$** ŌĆö six error parameters characterizing different sources of effector noise
- **Units:** distance in **meters (m)**, angles in **radians (rad)**, velocities in **m/s**, angular velocities in **rad/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Effector Noise Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> Effector noise is the *dual* of sensor noise. While Module 1/2 of PERAT741 covers sensor noise, this topic covers the *other half* of the robot localization problem ŌĆö the action uncertainty. KTU questions frequently ask students to (a) derive the noisy motion model, (b) compute the resulting pose covariance, and (c) distinguish between systematic and non-systematic errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Effector Noise

Effector noise is classified along two orthogonal axes:

### Axis 1: Systematic vs. Non-Systematic Errors

| Error Type | Definition | Source | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Systematic Errors** | Deterministic, repeatable bias in actuation | Wheel diameter mismatch, axis misalignment, gear-ratio inaccuracy | Calibration, kinematic correction |
| **Non-Systematic Errors** | Random, non-repeatable deviations | Wheel slip, floor bumps, sensor quantization | Probabilistic modeling (Gaussian noise) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Many exam questions test whether you understand that *systematic errors can be reduced by calibration*, while *non-systematic errors can only be characterized statistically*.

### Axis 2: Source-Based Classification

1. **Wheel Slip Errors** ŌĆö the wheel rotates but the robot doesn't move proportionally
2. **Encoder Quantization Errors** ŌĆö finite resolution of optical/magnetic encoders
3. **Gear Backlash** ŌĆö mechanical play between meshing gears
4. **Motor Torque Ripple** ŌĆö non-uniform torque output across a rotation
5. **Floor Irregularity Errors** ŌĆö bumps, cracks, slopes causing unexpected contact forces
6. **Time-Synchronization Errors** ŌĆö slight timing mismatches between left/right wheel commands

## 2.2 The Velocity Motion Model (Probabilistic Form)

The most widely used effector noise model is the **Velocity Motion Model with Noise** (Thrun, Burgard, Fox ŌĆö *Probabilistic Robotics*, MIT Press, 2005).

Given a control command:
$$u_t = \begin{bmatrix} v_t \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v_t$ is the commanded translational velocity and $\omega_t$ is the commanded rotational velocity, the *true* (noise-corrupted) velocities are:

$$v_t^{\text{true}} = v_t + \epsilon_{\alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2}$$

$$\omega_t^{\text{true}} = \omega_t + \epsilon_{\alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2}$$

$$ \gamma_t = \frac{\epsilon_{\alpha_5 v^2 + \alpha_6 \omega^2}}{\omega_t^{\text{true}}}$$

where each $\epsilon_{\sigma^2}$ is independent zero-mean Gaussian noise with variance $\sigma^2$, i.e.:

$$ \epsilon_b \sim \mathcal{N}(0, b)$$

The parameter $b$ itself is a *function* of the commanded velocities, modeling the empirical observation that **larger commands produce proportionally larger errors** (a property called *heteroscedasticity*).

## 2.3 Pose Update Equations

Starting from pose $x_{t-1} = (x, y, \theta)^\top$, the new pose is computed as:

$$ x' = x - \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \sin(\theta) + \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \sin(\theta + \omega^{\text{true}} \Delta t)$$

$$ y' = y - \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \cos(\theta) + \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \cos(\theta + \omega^{\text{true}} \Delta t)$$

$$ \theta' = \theta + \omega^{\text{true}} \Delta t + \gamma_t \Delta t$$

## 2.4 Odometry Motion Model (Alternative Form)

In practice, the *odometry motion model* is often preferred because it uses *measured* wheel rotations rather than *commanded* velocities. The control input becomes:

$$u_t = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{x}_{t-1} \\ \bar{x}_t \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\bar{x}_{t-1}$ and $\bar{x}_t$ are the odometry-reported poses at consecutive time steps. The relative motion is decomposed into a rotation, translation, and second rotation:

$$ \delta_{\text{rot1}} = \text{atan2}(\bar{y}' - \bar{y}, \bar{x}' - \bar{x}) - \bar{\theta}$$

$$ \delta_{\text{trans}} = \sqrt{(\bar{x}' - \bar{x})^2 + (\bar{y}' - \bar{y})^2}$$

$$ \delta_{\text{rot2}} = \bar{\theta}' - \bar{\theta} - \delta_{\text{rot1}}$$

Each of these is corrupted with Gaussian noise:

$$ \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}} = \delta_{\text{rot1}} - \epsilon_{\alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2}$$

$$ \hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} = \delta_{\text{trans}} - \epsilon_{\alpha_3 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 + \alpha_4 (\delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2)}$$

$$ \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot2}} = \delta_{\text{rot2}} - \epsilon_{\alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_t = [v_t, \omega_t]^\top$ | Commanded control vector (velocity model) | m/s, rad/s |
| 2 | $u_t = [\bar{x}_{t-1}, \bar{x}_t]^\top$ | Commanded control vector (odometry model) | m, m, rad |
| 3 | $\epsilon_b \sim \mathcal{N}(0, b)$ | Gaussian noise with variance $b$ | variance units |
| 4 | $b = \alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2$ | Variance scaling for translational noise | m┬▓/s┬▓ |
| 5 | $b = \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2$ | Variance scaling for rotational noise | rad┬▓/s┬▓ |
| 6 | $b = \alpha_5 v^2 + \alpha_6 \omega^2$ | Variance scaling for final rotation noise | rad┬▓/s┬▓ |
| 7 | $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | State transition probability (motion model) | 1/m┬▓ or 1/rad |
| 8 | $x' = x + \Delta x(\theta, v, \omega, \Delta t)$ | Updated x-coordinate | m |
| 9 | $\Sigma_{t} = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + V_t M_t V_t^\top$ | Covariance propagation (EKF-style) | 3├Ś3 matrix |
| 10 | $\text{drift} = k \cdot d$ | Linear odometry drift with distance $d$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize formulas #4, #5, #6 ŌĆö these three are the *core* of every motion noise question. The parameters $\alpha_1$ through $\alpha_6$ are robot-specific and must be determined experimentally via a system identification procedure (maximum likelihood estimation on logged motion data).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Effector noise modeling is critical in:
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Safety depends on accurately predicting the *probability distribution* of the car's position after a control command, not just its mean.
- **Warehouse Robotics (AMRs):** Amazon's Kiva robots must localize to within 1 cm despite thousands of noisy actuation events per hour.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA):** Mars rovers experience *extreme* wheel slip (up to 100% on sandy terrain) ŌĆö effector noise is the *dominant* error source, far exceeding sensor noise.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Sub-millimeter accuracy requires stochastic models of manipulator noise for closed-loop control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Noisy Pose Update from Velocity Model

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Deterministic Motion

For a differential-drive robot with translational velocity $v$ and rotational velocity $\omega$ over time $\Delta t$, the deterministic (noise-free) motion is given by integrating the kinematic equations:

$$ \dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$
$$ \dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$
$$ \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Solve the Differential Equations

If $\omega \neq 0$ (i.e., the robot is turning), the heading changes linearly: $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega t$. Substituting back and integrating over $[0, \Delta t]$:

$$ x(\Delta t) = x_0 + \int_0^{\Delta t} v \cos(\theta_0 + \omega \tau) \, d\tau$$

Applying the standard integral identity $\int \cos(a + b\tau) d\tau = \frac{1}{b}\sin(a + b\tau)$:

$$ x(\Delta t) = x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega} \left[ \sin(\theta_0 + \omega \Delta t) - \sin(\theta_0) \right]$$

Similarly for $y$ using $\int \sin(a + b\tau) d\tau = -\frac{1}{b}\cos(a + b\tau)$:

$$ y(\Delta t) = y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega} \left[ \cos(\theta_0 + \omega \Delta t) - \cos(\theta_0) \right]$$

And for $\theta$:

$$ \theta(\Delta t) = \theta_0 + \omega \Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** The original form uses the convention where heading is measured from the $y$-axis (not the $x$-axis), giving the equivalent compact form shown in Section 2.3. Both are mathematically equivalent ŌĆö KTU questions may use either convention.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Inject Effector Noise

Now replace the deterministic $v$ and $\omega$ with the noisy versions $v^{\text{true}}$ and $\omega^{\text{true}}$ defined in Section 2.2:

$$ v^{\text{true}} = v + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2)$$

$$ \omega^{\text{true}} = \omega + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2)$$

The pose update now becomes a *random variable* whose mean equals the deterministic update and whose variance depends on the $\alpha$ parameters and the commanded velocities.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Distribution (Special Case)

If we treat the small-noise limit, the resulting pose is approximately Gaussian:

$$ x_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{x_t}, \Sigma_{x_t})$$

where:
$$ \mu_{x_t} = f(\mu_{x_{t-1}}, v, \omega, \Delta t)$$
$$ \Sigma_{x_t} = G_x \Sigma_{x_{t-1}} G_x^\top + G_u M G_u^\top$$

Here $G_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ is the Jacobian w.r.t. the state and $G_u = \frac{\partial f}{\partial u}$ is the Jacobian w.r.t. the control, and $M$ is the diagonal noise covariance matrix with entries $(\alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2, \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2, \alpha_5 v^2 + \alpha_6 \omega^2)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Covariance Propagation for the Special Case $\omega \to 0$

When the robot moves in a *straight line* ($\omega = 0$), the kinematic equations simplify drastically. The deterministic motion becomes:

$$ x(\Delta t) = x_0 + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_0)$$
$$ y(\Delta t) = y_0 + v \Delta t \sin(\theta_0)$$
$$ \theta(\Delta t) = \theta_0$$

### Linearization

The Jacobian of the pose update w.r.t. the state is:

$$ G_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin(\theta_0) \\ 0 & 1 & v \Delta t \cos(\theta_0) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The Jacobian w.r.t. control $[v, \omega]^\top$ is:

$$ G_u = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta t \cos(\theta_0) & 0 \\ \Delta t \sin(\theta_0) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The control noise covariance is:

$$ M = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha_5 v^2 + \alpha_6 \omega^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Since $\omega = 0$, the control-noise contribution to the position covariance is:

$$ G_u M G_u^\top = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_1 v^2 \Delta t^2 \cos^2(\theta_0) & \alpha_1 v^2 \Delta t^2 \sin(\theta_0)\cos(\theta_0) & 0 \\ \alpha_1 v^2 \Delta t^2 \sin(\theta_0)\cos(\theta_0) & \alpha_1 v^2 \Delta t^2 \sin^2(\theta_0) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha_6 v^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The resulting covariance ellipse grows linearly with the square of the distance traveled ŌĆö this is why **odometry drift grows quadratically with distance** (covariance is distance┬▓, and standard deviation is distance).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot is at pose $x_0 = (0, 0, 0)^\top$ (x, y, $\theta$ in meters and radians). It is commanded to move with $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 0$ rad/s, for $\Delta t = 2$ s. The noise parameters are $\alpha_1 = 0.1$ m┬▓/s┬▓, and all other $\alpha_i = 0$. Compute (a) the expected final pose, and (b) the $2\sigma$ (95%) confidence ellipse in the $(x, y)$ plane.

### Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Expected final pose:**

Since $\omega = 0$, the robot moves straight along the x-axis. The deterministic (mean) update is:

$$ x_2 = 0 + 0.5 \times 2 \times \cos(0) = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$ y_2 = 0 + 0.5 \times 2 \times \sin(0) = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$ \theta_2 = 0 + 0 \times 2 = 0 \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{x_2} = (1.0, \ 0.0, \ 0.0)^\top}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Covariance ellipse:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2 with $G_u M G_u^\top$:

The position covariance block is:

$$ \Sigma_{xy} = \alpha_1 v^2 \Delta t^2 \begin{bmatrix} \cos^2(0) & \sin(0)\cos(0) \\ \sin(0)\cos(0) & \sin^2(0) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$ \Sigma_{xy} = 0.1 \times 0.25 \times 4 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}^2$$

Standard deviations: $\sigma_x = \sqrt{0.1} \approx 0.316$ m, $\sigma_y = 0$ m.

The $2\sigma$ (95%) confidence interval along $x$: $2 \times 0.316 \approx 0.632$ m.

$$\boxed{\text{Robot's true position lies in } x \in [0.368, \ 1.632] \text{ m with 95\% probability.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This problem illustrates a *key consequence* of effector noise ŌĆö even for a perfectly straight command, the true position is uncertain. The covariance grows with $v^2 \Delta t^2$, meaning doubling speed *quadruples* the position uncertainty, and doubling time *quadruples* it. This is why autonomous vehicles drive slowly when high precision is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Effector Noise Causal Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Commanded Control Input u_t] --> nodeB[Velocity Motor Driver]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Wheel Encoder Feedback]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Mechanical Drivetrain]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Wheel Ground Contact]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Actual Robot Motion x_t]
    nodeG[Wheel Slip] --> nodeF
    nodeH[Floor Irregularity] --> nodeE
    nodeI[Gear Backlash] --> nodeD
    nodeJ[Encoder Quantization] --> nodeC
    nodeK[Noise Sources Alpha 1 to Alpha 6] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeL[Pose Distribution N mu Sigma]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Odometry Uncertainty Ellipsoid]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Effector Noise Modeling

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Controller Module] -->|Command u_t| nodeB[Noise Injection Block]
    nodeB -->|Noisy u| nodeC[Robot Kinematic Model]
    nodeC -->|State Transition| nodeD[Pose Update x_t]
    nodeD -->|Covariance Propagation| nodeE[EKF Predict Step]
    nodeE -->|Updated Belief| nodeF[Belief Distribution]
    nodeG[Alpha Parameter Bank] -->|Calibrated Variances| nodeB
    nodeG -->|Calibrated Variances| nodeE
    nodeH[Sensor Measurement z_t] -->|Correction Step| nodeE
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Error Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot[Effector Noise] --> nodeA[Systematic Errors]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB[Non Systematic Errors]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Wheel Diameter Mismatch]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Axle Misalignment]
    nodeA --> nodeA3[Gear Ratio Error]
    nodeA --> nodeA4[Encoder Bias Offset]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Wheel Slip Random]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Floor Bumps]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Wind or External Disturbance]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Encoder Quantization]
    nodeB --> nodeB5[Motor Torque Ripple]
    nodeA1 --> nodeFix1[Fix Calibration]
    nodeA2 --> nodeFix1
    nodeA3 --> nodeFix1
    nodeA4 --> nodeFix1
    nodeB1 --> nodeFix2[Fix Probabilistic Modeling]
    nodeB2 --> nodeFix2
    nodeB3 --> nodeFix2
    nodeB4 --> nodeFix2
    nodeB5 --> nodeFix2
```

## 4.4 Uncertainty Growth Over Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeT0[Pose t0 Sigma 0] --> nodeT1[Pose t1 Sigma grows]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2[Pose t2 Sigma grows more]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Pose t3 Sigma grows further]
    nodeT3 --> nodeTN[Pose tN Sigma is large]
    nodeT0 -.->|First motion| nodeE1[Small Ellipse]
    nodeT1 -.->|After second motion| nodeE2[Medium Ellipse]
    nodeT2 -.->|After third motion| nodeE3[Large Ellipse]
    nodeT3 -.->|After Nth motion| nodeE4[Very Large Ellipse]
    nodeTN -.->|Need sensor correction| nodeSC[Sensor Update z_t]
    nodeSC -.->|Reduces Sigma| nodeT1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define effector noise in robot navigation. How does it differ from sensor noise?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Effector noise is the stochastic uncertainty associated with a robot's actuators when executing a commanded motion. When the controller issues a velocity or position command to the motors, the actual achieved motion deviates from the intended motion due to wheel slip, gear backlash, encoder quantization, and floor irregularities.

**Key distinction (1 mark each point):**
- *Sensor noise* corrupts the robot's **perception** of the world ŌĆö it adds uncertainty to the measurement $z_t$ given true state $x_t$, i.e., $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.
- *Effector noise* corrupts the robot's **action** in the world ŌĆö it adds uncertainty to the state transition $x_t$ given command $u_t$, i.e., $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.

Both are modeled as probability distributions but operate on different sides of the Bayes filter: sensor noise affects the *update* step, effector noise affects the *predict* step.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**List and briefly explain the six parameters $\alpha_1$ through $\alpha_6$ used in the velocity motion model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö 0.5 per parameter):**

The six $\alpha$ parameters in the velocity motion model quantify different sources of motion noise:

1. **$\alpha_1$** ŌĆö Variance contribution of translational velocity $v$ to translation noise.
2. **$\alpha_2$** ŌĆö Variance contribution of rotational velocity $\omega$ to translation noise.
3. **$\alpha_3$** ŌĆö Variance contribution of translational velocity $v$ to rotation noise.
4. **$\alpha_4$** ŌĆö Variance contribution of rotational velocity $\omega$ to rotation noise.
5. **$\alpha_5$** ŌĆö Variance contribution of translational velocity $v$ to the *final heading* noise.
6. **$\alpha_6$** ŌĆö Variance contribution of rotational velocity $\omega$ to the *final heading* noise.

These parameters are determined experimentally via maximum-likelihood system identification.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** With neat mathematical expressions, derive the **velocity motion model** of a differential-drive mobile robot incorporating effector noise. Clearly define all variables and explain the role of the parameters $\alpha_1$ to $\alpha_6$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot is commanded to move with $v = 0.3$ m/s and $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 4$ s. The noise parameters are: $\alpha_1 = 0.05$, $\alpha_2 = 0.02$, $\alpha_3 = 0.03$, $\alpha_4 = 0.01$, $\alpha_5 = 0.04$, $\alpha_6 = 0.02$ (units as per Probabilistic Robotics convention). The robot starts at pose $(0, 0, 0)$. Compute the standard deviation of the final pose in the $x$, $y$, and $\theta$ directions. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the control input as $u_t = [v, \omega]^\top$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the noisy true velocities as:

$$ v^{\text{true}} = v + \epsilon_{\alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2}$$
$$ \omega^{\text{true}} = \omega + \epsilon_{\alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2}$$

where $\epsilon_b \sim \mathcal{N}(0, b)$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable with variance $b$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define the additional heading noise:

$$ \gamma = \frac{\epsilon_{\alpha_5 v^2 + \alpha_6 \omega^2}}{\omega^{\text{true}}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the kinematic integration to obtain the new pose:

$$ x' = x - \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \sin(\theta) + \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \sin(\theta + \omega^{\text{true}} \Delta t)$$
$$ y' = y + \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \cos(\theta) - \frac{v^{\text{true}}}{\omega^{\text{true}}} \cos(\theta + \omega^{\text{true}} \Delta t)$$
$$ \theta' = \theta + \omega^{\text{true}} \Delta t + \gamma \Delta t$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Explain that $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ characterize the error in translation, $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$ characterize the error in rotation rate, and $\alpha_5, \alpha_6$ characterize an additional small heading drift that occurs even for purely translational motion.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the variances of the noise terms:

- Translational noise variance: $\sigma_v^2 = \alpha_1 v^2 + \alpha_2 \omega^2 = 0.05 \times 0.09 + 0.02 \times 0.04 = 0.0045 + 0.0008 = 0.0053$ m┬▓/s┬▓
- Rotational noise variance: $\sigma_\omega^2 = \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 = 0.03 \times 0.09 + 0.01 \times 0.04 = 0.0027 + 0.0004 = 0.0031$ rad┬▓/s┬▓
- Final heading noise variance: $\sigma_\gamma^2 = \alpha_5 v^2 + \alpha_6 \omega^2 = 0.04 \times 0.09 + 0.02 \times 0.04 = 0.0036 + 0.0008 = 0.0044$ rad┬▓/s┬▓

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the standard deviations:

$$ \sigma_v = \sqrt{0.0053} \approx 0.0728 \text{ m/s}$$
$$ \sigma_\omega = \sqrt{0.0031} \approx 0.0557 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$ \sigma_\gamma = \sqrt{0.0044} \approx 0.0663 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Propagate the noise to the final pose. The mean deterministic motion gives:

$$ \theta' = \omega \Delta t = 0.2 \times 4 = 0.8 \text{ rad}$$
$$ x' = -\frac{v}{\omega}\sin(0) + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(0.8) = \frac{0.3}{0.2}[\sin(0) - \sin(0.8)]$$

Wait ŌĆö re-applying the proper convention: with $\theta_0 = 0$ and the standard convention, the displacement is along an arc of radius $R = v/\omega = 1.5$ m. The arc length is $s = v \Delta t = 1.2$ m.

$$ \sigma_x \approx \sigma_v \Delta t = 0.0728 \times 4 = 0.291 \text{ m}$$
$$ \sigma_y \approx \sigma_v \Delta t = 0.291 \text{ m}$$
$$ \sigma_\theta \approx \sigma_\omega \Delta t + \sigma_\gamma \Delta t = (0.0557 + 0.0663) \times 4 = 0.488 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final answer:

$$\boxed{\sigma_x \approx 0.291 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_y \approx 0.291 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_\theta \approx 0.488 \text{ rad}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **odometry motion model** for a differential-drive robot. How is it different from the velocity motion model? Derive the expressions for the relative motion components $\delta_{\text{rot1}}$, $\delta_{\text{trans}}$, $\delta_{\text{rot2}}$ and show how noise is added to each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A robot's odometry reports the following two consecutive poses: $\bar{x}_{t-1} = (1.0, \ 2.0, \ 0.5)^\top$ and $\bar{x}_t = (2.2, \ 3.5, \ 0.9)^\top$. Compute the relative motion components. If the noise parameters are $\alpha_1 = 0.1$, $\alpha_2 = 0.1$, $\alpha_3 = 0.1$, $\alpha_4 = 0.1$, compute the variances of the noisy relative motion components. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the odometry control input as $u_t = [\bar{x}_{t-1}, \bar{x}_t]^\top$, which is the pair of odometry-reported poses.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Note the key difference from the velocity model: odometry uses *measured* motion (from encoders) rather than *commanded* motion. This makes the odometry model more accurate in practice because it captures the actual wheel rotations.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Derive the relative motion components:

$$ \delta_{\text{rot1}} = \text{atan2}(\bar{y}_t - \bar{y}_{t-1}, \ \bar{x}_t - \bar{x}_{t-1}) - \bar{\theta}_{t-1}$$

$$ \delta_{\text{trans}} = \sqrt{(\bar{x}_t - \bar{x}_{t-1})^2 + (\bar{y}_t - \bar{y}_{t-1})^2}$$

$$ \delta_{\text{rot2}} = \bar{\theta}_t - \bar{\theta}_{t-1} - \delta_{\text{rot1}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Add the Gaussian noise to each:

$$ \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}} = \delta_{\text{rot1}} - \mathcal{N}(0, \ \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2)$$

$$ \hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} = \delta_{\text{trans}} - \mathcal{N}(0, \ \alpha_3 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 + \alpha_4 (\delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2))$$

$$ \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot2}} = \delta_{\text{rot2}} - \mathcal{N}(0, \ \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2)$$

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Compute the relative motion components:

$$ \Delta x = 2.2 - 1.0 = 1.2 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta y = 3.5 - 2.0 = 1.5 \text{ m}$$
$$ \delta_{\text{rot1}} = \text{atan2}(1.5, 1.2) - 0.5 = 0.8961 - 0.5 = 0.3961 \text{ rad}$$

$$ \delta_{\text{trans}} = \sqrt{1.2^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{1.44 + 2.25} = \sqrt{3.69} \approx 1.921 \text{ m}$$

$$ \delta_{\text{rot2}} = 0.9 - 0.5 - 0.3961 = 0.0039 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the variances of the noise terms:

$$ \sigma_{\text{rot1}}^2 = \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 = 0.1 \times 0.1569 + 0.1 \times 3.690 = 0.01569 + 0.369 = 0.385 \text{ rad}^2$$

$$ \sigma_{\text{trans}}^2 = \alpha_3 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 + \alpha_4 (\delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2) = 0.1 \times 3.69 + 0.1 \times (0.1569 + 0.000015) = 0.369 + 0.0157 = 0.385 \text{ m}^2$$

$$ \sigma_{\text{rot2}}^2 = \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 = 0.385 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ (same as rot1)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Standard deviations:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{rot1}} \approx 0.620 \text{ rad}, \quad \sigma_{\text{trans}} \approx 0.620 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_{\text{rot2}} \approx 0.620 \text{ rad}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the **velocity motion model** with the **odometry motion model**. Remember: velocity model uses *commanded* $(v, \omega)$; odometry model uses *measured* pose pairs.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that the noise variance is a *function* of the command (heteroscedastic). Using a constant variance is a fatal error.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up the order of $\delta_{\text{rot1}}$, $\delta_{\text{trans}}$, $\delta_{\text{rot2}}$. The mnemonic is "**R**otate-**T**ranslate-**R**otate" (RTR) ŌĆö the robot first turns to face the new position, then translates, then turns again to its final orientation.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Computing the noise variance using the *noisy* $\hat{\delta}$ values instead of the *clean* $\delta$ values. Always use the deterministic (odometry-reported) values to compute the variance.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians (or vice versa) when computing trig functions. The KTU convention is to use radians internally.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Effector Noise:**

- Ō£ģ **Definition:** Effector noise = uncertainty in *action*, sensor noise = uncertainty in *perception*.
- Ō£ģ **Two main models:** *Velocity motion model* (uses commanded $v, \omega$) and *odometry motion model* (uses measured pose pairs).
- Ō£ģ **Six $\alpha$ parameters:** $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ for translation noise; $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$ for rotation noise; $\alpha_5, \alpha_6$ for final heading noise.
- Ō£ģ **Variance formula (heteroscedastic):** $\sigma^2 = \alpha_i v^2 + \alpha_j \omega^2$ ŌĆö variance scales with the *square* of the command magnitude.
- Ō£ģ **Odometry decomposition:** RTR pattern ŌĆö $\delta_{\text{rot1}}$ ŌåÆ $\delta_{\text{trans}}$ ŌåÆ $\delta_{\text{rot2}}$.
- Ō£ģ **Noise distribution:** Gaussian, zero-mean, but the *variance* is a function of the command.
- Ō£ģ **Drift growth:** Position uncertainty grows *quadratically* with distance traveled.
- Ō£ģ **Calibration vs. Modeling:** Systematic errors ŌåÆ calibrate; non-systematic errors ŌåÆ model probabilistically.
- Ō£ģ **Bayes filter role:** Effector noise is the input to the *prediction step* of the Bayes filter.
- Ō£ģ **Practical importance:** Odometry drift is the #1 error source in dead-reckoning; it must be corrected using sensor measurements (the *update* step).
- Ō£ģ **Higher speed ŌåÆ higher noise:** Doubling speed quadruples position uncertainty (variance scales as $v^2$).
- Ō£ģ **Real-world impact:** Critical for autonomous vehicles, planetary rovers, warehouse robots, and surgical robots.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Odometry** is the estimation of a mobile robot's instantaneous position $(x, y, \theta)$ by integrating incremental wheel rotation measurements. Because every real measurement is corrupted by deterministic (systematic) and stochastic (non-systematic) disturbances, an **Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation** is the closed-form mathematical description of how those wheel-level uncertainties propagate and accumulate into the final pose covariance $\Sigma_p$.

In KTU 2024-scheme parlance, the error model is expressed as a **propagation equation** in which the state vector $\mathbf{x}_k = [x_k,\ y_k,\ \theta_k]^\top$ is updated through a non-linear kinematic function $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k)$ and an additive error vector $\mathbf{e}_k$. Linearising $\mathbf{f}$ about the current estimate yields the **Extended Error Propagation Law**:

$$\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k) + \mathbf{e}_k$$

$$\Sigma_{k} = \nabla \mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{x}}\,\Sigma_{k-1}\,\nabla \mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{x}}^{\top} + \nabla \mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{u}}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla \mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{u}}^{\top}$$

where $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$ is the input-covariance built from the elementary wheel errors (encoder resolution, wheel-base, wheel-diameter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the End-Semester Examination (ESE), the examiner almost always awards the majority of the 14 marks for *deriving* the pose-update Jacobian $\nabla \mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{x}}$ and for *constructing* the $3 \times 3$ pose covariance $\Sigma_k$ step-by-step. Memorising the matrices without derivation yields **at most 4/14**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine a blindfolded person walking in a straight hallway with a pedometer (step counter) and a hand-held compass.

* The **pedometer** is the *left and right wheel encoders* ŌĆö it counts "steps" (wheel ticks).
* The **compass** is the *gyro / heading integration* ŌĆö it tracks orientation.
* Each step the blindfolded person takes is **slightly** too long, **slightly** too short, or **slightly** crooked.

The blindfolded walker has **no map feedback**, so every small slip compounds. After 10 m, the cumulative drift may be only a few centimetres; after 100 m it may be half a metre; after 1 km it could be many metres. This growth is **quadratic in distance** for translation error and **linear in distance** for heading error.

The **odometric error model** is the mathematical translation of this physical intuition: a function that, given the *quality* of the pedometer (encoder resolution) and the *quality* of the compass (gyro drift), tells us *how uncertain* the walker is after travelling an arbitrary distance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Differential-Drive Indoor) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Wheel-base | $b$ | $0.20 - 0.50\ \text{m}$ |
| Wheel radius | $r$ | $0.04 - 0.10\ \text{m}$ |
| Encoder ticks per revolution | $N$ | $500 - 20000$ |
| Systematic wheel-diameter error | $\varepsilon_d$ | $\pm 1 - 2\ \text{\%}$ |
| Systematic wheel-base error | $\varepsilon_b$ | $\pm 1 - 2\ \text{\%}$ |
| Resolution quantisation step | $K_{\text{res}}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi r}{N}$ metres |
| Heading uncertainty growth | $k_\theta$ | $0.01 - 0.05\ \text{rad/m}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Odometric Errors

Odometric errors fall into two broad families. The KTU 2024 syllabus is explicit that the examiner expects students to **classify every error** before applying propagation.

### 2.1.1 Systematic (Deterministic) Errors
These are *constant biases* that persist across runs and can, in principle, be calibrated away. In practice, residual bias remains because perfect calibration is impossible.

1. **Scale-factor error** due to *wheel-diameter mismatch* between left and right wheels ($\Delta D / D$).
2. **Misalignment error** of the wheel axes with respect to the robot frame.
3. **Wheel-base uncertainty** $\varepsilon_b$ (the geometric $b$ used in the kinematic equation differs from the true $b$).
4. **Encoder quantisation** ŌĆö a finite resolution $K_{\text{res}}$ that adds a bounded offset to every tick.

### 2.1.2 Non-Systematic (Stochastic) Errors
These are *random* and cannot be calibrated because they are state- and environment-dependent.

1. **Slip and skid** on uneven, wet, or sandy floors.
2. **Uneven floor contact** ŌĆö instantaneous rotation about a point other than the assumed ICC (Instantaneous Centre of Curvature).
3. **Bumps, debris, irregularities** that momentarily change the effective wheel radius.
4. **Cross-coupling** between translation and rotation through irregularities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Systematic errors dominate in *smooth indoor* environments, while non-systematic errors dominate in *outdoor / uneven* terrain. The textbook-level error model built from Jacobians is the **unified** way to handle both.

## 2.2 Odometric Pose Update (Reference Equations)

For a differential-drive robot with wheel-base $b$ and wheel radius $r$, the incremental travelled distances of the left and right wheels over one sampling interval are

$$\Delta s_r = k\,\Delta \phi_r, \qquad \Delta s_l = k\,\Delta \phi_l, \qquad k = \frac{2\pi r}{N}$$

The standard pose-update is

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}\,\cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\Delta y = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}\,\sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta x, \qquad y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta y, \qquad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mid-point heading $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ is used (not $\theta$) to make the discrete update a **second-order accurate** RungeŌĆōKutta-style approximation of the true arc. Most KTU solutions that skip this nuance lose a mark.

## 2.3 The Error Propagation Model (Elfes / Borenstein Standard Form)

The KTU 2024 reference formulation decomposes the position estimate into the *true* pose plus an additive error:

$$\mathbf{x}_k = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k + \delta \mathbf{x}_k$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1} = \mathbf{f}\!\left(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k, \hat{\mathbf{u}}_k\right)$$

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\cdot \Sigma_k \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top} + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\cdot \Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$$

The first term on the right propagates the **previous pose uncertainty**; the second term injects the **fresh control-input uncertainty**. The sum is the *worst-case* (Gaussian) covariance after the move.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below collects **every** equation a KTU 2024 student is expected to reproduce from memory in a 14-mark question. Vertical bars use $\vert$ / $\mid$ to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Remark |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Wheel-tick-to-distance | $k = \dfrac{2\pi r}{N}$ | $\text{m/tick}$ | $N$ = encoder counts per rev |
| 2 | Incremental travel | $\Delta s_r = k\,\Delta\phi_r$ , $\ \Delta s_l = k\,\Delta\phi_l$ | $\text{m}$ | $\Delta\phi$ in **radians** |
| 3 | Heading increment | $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)/b$ | $\text{rad}$ | Singular only when $b \to 0$ |
| 4 | Linear path length | $\Delta U = (\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)/2$ | $\text{m}$ | Mid-arc heading used |
| 5 | Position update (mid-arc) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta U\cos(\theta+\Delta\theta/2)$ | $\text{m}$ | Second-order accurate |
| 6 | Position update (mid-arc) | $y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta U\sin(\theta+\Delta\theta/2)$ | $\text{m}$ | ŌĆö |
| 7 | Pose Jacobian w.r.t. pose | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$ ($3\times 3$) | ŌĆö | Always near-identity |
| 8 | Pose Jacobian w.r.t. input | $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ ($3\times 2$) | ŌĆö | Scales with $\Delta U$ |
| 9 | Encoder-resolution variance | $\sigma_{\text{res}}^{2} = (k/2)^{2}$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ | Per-wheel quantisation |
| 10 | Combined input cov. | $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}} = \mathrm{diag}\!\left(\sigma_{r}^{2},\ \sigma_{l}^{2}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ | Assumes wheel errors independent |
| 11 | Pose cov. propagation | $\Sigma_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\Sigma_k\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top} + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$ | $\text{m}^{2},\text{rad}^{2}$ | Symmetric PD matrix |
| 12 | Uncertainty ellipse (95 %) | $x^{2}/\sigma_{x}^{2} + y^{2}/\sigma_{y}^{2} \le 5.99$ | ŌĆö | 2-DOF $\chi^{2}_{0.95}$ constant |
| 13 | Position error (Borenstein) | $e_{x} = a_{1}\sqrt{D^{2}} + a_{2}\,D$ , $\ e_{y} = a_{3}D$ | $\text{m}$ | $D$ = distance, $a_i$ tuned per robot |
| 14 | Heading error (Borenstein) | $e_{\theta} = a_{4}D$ | $\text{rad}$ | Linear in $D$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Autonomous AGVs in warehouses** rely on odometric error models to decide when to *re-localise* using wall-mounted fiducials.
* **Planetary rovers (e.g. NASA Curiosity-class)** run the same Jacobian-based propagation to plan dead-reckoning arcs between visual fixes.
* **Self-driving cars** couple odometry error models with IMU error models inside an Extended Kalman Filter.
* **Indoor service robots** use the position covariance $\Sigma_k$ to inflate obstacle-avoidance bubbles ŌĆö a *probabilistic* safety margin that grows with the same $\Sigma_k$ derived above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pose-Update Jacobian $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$

The pose update for a differential-drive robot, expressed as a vector function $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$, is

$$\mathbf{f}\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\\\theta\end{bmatrix},\ \begin{bmatrix}\Delta s_r\\ \Delta s_l\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}
x + \dfrac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}\cos\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{2b}\right)\\[8pt]
y + \dfrac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}\sin\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{2b}\right)\\[8pt]
\theta + \dfrac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}
\end{bmatrix}$$

For brevity define the *intermediate* quantities

$$c = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2} \quad\text{(linear path length)}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b} \quad\text{(heading increment)}$$

The Jacobian with respect to the pose $\mathbf{x} = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^\top$ is therefore

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -c\,\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)\\[4pt]
0 & 1 & \ \ c\,\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)\\[4pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Derivation commentary (row by row).**

* **Row 1 (for $x$):** Differentiating $x_{k+1} = x_k + c\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$ with respect to $x_k$, $y_k$, $\theta_k$ gives $1$, $0$, $-c\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$ because $\Delta\theta$ is *constant* with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ (it depends only on $\mathbf{u}$).
* **Row 2 (for $y$):** Symmetric to row 1, the sine derivative flips sign.
* **Row 3 (for $\theta$):** $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$ is independent of $x$ and $y$, so the entries are $0, 0, 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that in the *exact* (non-mid-arc) formulation, the third column would carry the term $c\cos\theta$ or $c\sin\theta$ in rows 1 and 2. KTU solutions that fail to use the mid-arc heading will produce an *incorrect* Jacobian, losing 2ŌĆō3 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Input Jacobian $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$

Define the four partials we will need:

$$A = \frac{\partial c}{\partial \Delta s_r} = \frac{1}{2}, \qquad \frac{\partial c}{\partial \Delta s_l} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$B = \frac{\partial \Delta\theta}{\partial \Delta s_r} = \frac{1}{b}, \qquad \frac{\partial \Delta\theta}{\partial \Delta s_l} = -\frac{1}{b}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \Delta s_r}\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2b}, \qquad \frac{\partial}{\partial \Delta s_l}\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) = -\frac{1}{2b}$$

**Row 1 of $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ (for $x$):** Differentiate $c\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$ with respect to $\Delta s_r$ and $\Delta s_l$ using the product rule.

$$\frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_r} = \frac{1}{2}\cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) - c\,\sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)\cdot \frac{1}{2b}$$

$$\frac{\partial x_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_l} = \frac{1}{2}\cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) + c\,\sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)\cdot \frac{1}{2b}$$

**Row 2 of $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ (for $y$):** Symmetric, with sine replacing cosine.

$$\frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_r} = \frac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) + c\,\cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)\cdot \frac{1}{2b}$$

$$\frac{\partial y_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_l} = \frac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) - c\,\cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)\cdot \frac{1}{2b}$$

**Row 3 of $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ (for $\theta$):** Simple ŌĆö $\theta_{k+1} = \theta + \Delta\theta$ and $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)/b$.

$$\frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_r} = \frac{1}{b}, \qquad \frac{\partial \theta_{k+1}}{\partial \Delta s_l} = -\frac{1}{b}$$

Stacking the columns:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(\theta+\Delta\theta/2) - \dfrac{c}{2b}\sin(\theta+\Delta\theta/2) & \dfrac{1}{2}\cos(\theta+\Delta\theta/2) + \dfrac{c}{2b}\sin(\theta+\Delta\theta/2)\\[10pt]
\dfrac{1}{2}\sin(\theta+\Delta\theta/2) + \dfrac{c}{2b}\cos(\theta+\Delta\theta/2) & \dfrac{1}{2}\sin(\theta+\Delta\theta/2) - \dfrac{c}{2b}\cos(\theta+\Delta\theta/2)\\[10pt]
\dfrac{1}{b} & -\dfrac{1}{b}
\end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Compact shortcut for an exam:** notice that the first column equals the second column with the sign of $c/(2b)$ flipped. Many KTU toppers remember this pattern and recover the full matrix from a single column.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Encoder-Resolution Covariance $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$

A standard quadrature encoder produces $N$ counts per wheel revolution. The smallest resolvable linear motion per tick is $k = 2\pi r/N$. Assuming a *uniform* distribution of quantisation error in $[-k/2, +k/2]$ per tick, the variance per tick is $k^{2}/12$. Since the controller typically averages over $M$ ticks in one sampling interval, the per-interval variance of the **single-wheel** distance estimate is

$$\sigma_{\text{tick}}^{2} = \frac{k^{2}}{12\,M} = \frac{1}{12\,M}\left(\frac{2\pi r}{N}\right)^{2}$$

For a conservative upper-bound analysis (and the formulation KTU prefers for hand-derivable questions), the **half-tick** bound is used:

$$\sigma_{r}^{2} = \sigma_{l}^{2} = \left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{\pi r}{N}\right)^{2}$$

Because the left and right wheel quantisation errors are statistically independent, the joint input covariance is diagonal:

$$\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}} = \begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_{r}^{2} & 0\\[2pt]
0 & \sigma_{l}^{2}
\end{bmatrix} = \left(\frac{\pi r}{N}\right)^{2}\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0\\[2pt]
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style)

A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05\ \text{m}$, wheel-base $b = 0.30\ \text{m}$, encoder resolution $N = 2000$ counts/rev, current pose $\mathbf{x}_k = [0.0,\ 0.0,\ 0.0]^\top$, and a single-step control of $\Delta\phi_r = 100$ ticks, $\Delta\phi_l = 80$ ticks.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the elementary constants.**

$$k = \frac{2\pi r}{N} = \frac{2\pi\cdot 0.05}{2000} = 1.5708\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/tick}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{tick}} = \frac{\pi r}{N} = \frac{\pi\cdot 0.05}{2000} = 7.854\times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute incremental wheel distances.**

$$\Delta s_r = k\,\Delta\phi_r = 1.5708\times 10^{-4}\times 100 = 0.015708\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta s_l = k\,\Delta\phi_l = 1.5708\times 10^{-4}\times 80 = 0.012566\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $c$, $\Delta\theta$, mid-arc heading.**

$$c = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2} = \frac{0.015708 + 0.012566}{2} = 0.014137\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b} = \frac{0.015708 - 0.012566}{0.30} = 0.010472\ \text{rad}\ (\approx 0.6^{\circ})$$

$$\theta_{\text{mid}} = \theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 0.0 + 0.005236 = 0.005236\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the new pose.**

$$x_{k+1} = 0.0 + 0.014137\cos(0.005236) = 0.014137\times 0.999986 = 0.014137\ \text{m}$$

$$y_{k+1} = 0.0 + 0.014137\sin(0.005236) = 0.014137\times 0.005236 = 7.40\times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = 0.0 + 0.010472 = 0.010472\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Build the pose Jacobian $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$ (numerically).**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -0.014137\sin(0.005236)\\[3pt]
0 & 1 & \ \ 0.014137\cos(0.005236)\\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -7.40\times 10^{-5}\\[3pt]
0 & 1 & 0.014137\\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Build the input Jacobian $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ (numerically).** Using $c/(2b) = 0.014137/(0.60) = 0.023562$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.5\cos(\theta_{\text{mid}}) - 0.023562\sin(\theta_{\text{mid}}) & 0.5\cos(\theta_{\text{mid}}) + 0.023562\sin(\theta_{\text{mid}})\\[3pt]
0.5\sin(\theta_{\text{mid}}) + 0.023562\cos(\theta_{\text{mid}}) & 0.5\sin(\theta_{\text{mid}}) - 0.023562\cos(\theta_{\text{mid}})\\[3pt]
1/b & -1/b
\end{bmatrix}$$

Numerically,

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
0.49999 & 0.49999\\
0.02369 & -0.02344\\
3.3333 & -3.3333
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Build the input covariance.**

$$\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}} = (7.854\times 10^{-5})^{2}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix} = 6.169\times 10^{-9}\,\mathbf{I}_{2}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Propagate the pose covariance (assume $\Sigma_k = 0$ initially).**

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\cdot\mathbf{0}\cdot\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top} + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top} = \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$$

Multiplying out:

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = 6.169\times 10^{-9}\begin{bmatrix}
0.49999 & 0.49999\\
0.02369 & -0.02344\\
3.3333 & -3.3333
\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}
0.49999 & 0.02369 & 3.3333\\
0.49999 & -0.02344 & -3.3333
\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma_{k+1} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
3.084\times 10^{-9} & -7.30\times 10^{-13} & 0\\[3pt]
-7.30\times 10^{-13} & 1.73\times 10^{-12} & 0\\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 1.37\times 10^{-7}
\end{bmatrix}\ \text{m}^{2},\ \text{rad}^{2}$$

**Interpretation.** The $\sigma_{x} \approx 5.55\times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$ and $\sigma_{\theta} \approx 3.70\times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}$ are the **1-Žā** standard deviations after one motion step. The 95 % uncertainty ellipse has semi-axes $\sigma_{x}\sqrt{5.99} \approx 1.36\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$ and $\sigma_{y}\sqrt{5.99} \approx 2.49\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, showing that lateral uncertainty is *orders of magnitude smaller* than along-track uncertainty ŌĆö the classic signature of a single-step odometric update.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary of the Propagation

The complete one-step covariance update in matrix form is

$$\boxed{\;\Sigma_{k+1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -c\,\sin\theta_{m}\\[3pt]
0 & 1 & \ \ c\,\cos\theta_{m}\\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}\Sigma_{k}\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0\\[3pt]
0 & 1 & 0\\[3pt]
-c\,\sin\theta_{m} & c\,\cos\theta_{m} & 1
\end{bmatrix} \;+\; \begin{bmatrix}
a & b\\[3pt]
b & a\\[3pt]
1/b & -1/b
\end{bmatrix}\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\begin{bmatrix}
a & b & 1/b\\[3pt]
b & a & -1/b
\end{bmatrix}\;}$$

where $\theta_{m} = \theta + \Delta\theta/2$ and $a, b$ are the entries of $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ defined in Section 3.2.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners appreciate it when a student *labels* the two summed matrices as "pose-propagation term" and "input-injection term" ŌĆö a one-line addition that usually adds 1 mark for communication.

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Error Propagation Pipeline

The figure below shows how raw encoder pulses are progressively transformed into a final pose covariance through the layers of the error model. Each block is a mathematical operator, and the dashed arrows represent the *covariance* signals as opposed to the *state* signals (solid arrows).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Encoders<br/>N counts per rev] --> B[Encoder Tick Counter<br/>dphi_r and dphi_l]
    B --> C[Kinematic Map<br/>delta s_r k dphi_r and delta s_l k dphi_l]
    C --> D[Mid-Arc Pose Update f x u<br/>x y theta]
    C --> E[Input Covariance Builder<br/>Sigma_u diag of pi r N squared]
    D --> F[Pose Jacobian grad f x<br/>3 by 3 matrix]
    D --> G[Input Jacobian grad f u<br/>3 by 2 matrix]
    F --> H[Covariance Aggregator<br/>Sigma new grad f x Sigma k grad f x T]
    G --> I[Covariance Aggregator<br/>Sigma inject grad f u Sigma u grad f u T]
    E --> I
    H --> J[Final Pose Covariance Sigma new<br/>3 by 3 symmetric PD]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Uncertainty Ellipse Renderer<br/>chi squared 2D 0.95]
    D --> L[Pose Output x y theta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** the *left* chain is the state-pose update; the *right* chain is the uncertainty-pose update. Both chains read from the same kinematic block in the centre. The aggregator at the bottom sums the two contributions exactly as required by the propagation equation.

## 4.2 Error-Source Classification Tree

The diagram below maps every physical error source in odometry to the mathematical term in the propagation equation that *absorbs* it. This is the **single most important organisational aid** for the KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Odometric Error Sources] --> SYS[Systematic Deterministic]
    ERR --> NONS[Non-Systematic Stochastic]
    SYS --> D1[Wheel Diameter Mismatch<br/>epsilon d]
    SYS --> D2[Wheel Base Error<br/>epsilon b]
    SYS --> D3[Encoder Quantisation<br/>pi r per N]
    NONS --> N1[Wheel Slip and Skid]
    NONS --> N2[Uneven Floor Contact]
    NONS --> N3[Bumps and Debris]
    D1 --> MAP1[Modifies delta s_r and delta s_l]
    D2 --> MAP2[Modifies b in delta theta]
    D3 --> MAP3[Builds Sigma u Diagonal]
    N1 --> MAP4[Adds Random Walk term to Sigma u]
    N2 --> MAP5[Modifies c and theta_mid]
    N3 --> MAP4
    MAP1 --> PROP[Propagation Equation<br/>Sigma k plus 1 grad f x Sigma k grad f x T plus grad f u Sigma u grad f u T]
    MAP2 --> PROP
    MAP3 --> PROP
    MAP4 --> PROP
    MAP5 --> PROP
```

## 4.3 Pose-Covariance Growth Schematic (Geometric View)

The figure below shows how the position uncertainty **ellipse** is transformed from one timestep to the next. The mid-arc heading $\theta_m$ rotates the ellipse by the same angle as the robot itself, and the kinematic map *stretches* it by the input Jacobian.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Pose at Step k<br/>x k y k theta k] --> S0[Uncertainty Ellipse Sigma k<br/>Centre at P0]
    S0 --> R1[Rotate by theta plus half delta theta]
    R1 --> S1[Mid-Arc Ellipse]
    S1 --> ST[Stretch by Input Jacobian grad f u]
    ST --> S2[Translated Ellipse Centre at P k plus 1]
    P0 --> P1[Pose at Step k plus 1]
    S2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2[Pose at Step k plus 2]
    S2 --> R2[Repeat for Next Step]
    R2 --> S3[Sigma k plus 1]
    P2 --> S3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *rotation* of the covariance matrix is performed by $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_k\,\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$. The *stretching* is performed by $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$. Conflating these two operations in an exam solution is a classic source of lost marks.

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Summary Table

| Stage | Math Symbol | Physical Meaning | Output Type |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta\phi_{r}, \Delta\phi_{l}$ | Tick counter deltas | Integer counts |
| 2 | $\Delta s_{r}, \Delta s_{l}$ | Linear travel per wheel | Metres |
| 3 | $c,\ \Delta\theta,\ \theta_{m}$ | Arc geometry | Metres / radians |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$ | Updated pose | $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| 5 | $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$ | Wheel-level uncertainty | $2 \times 2$ covariance |
| 6 | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$ | Pose-sensitivity | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 7 | $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ | Input-sensitivity | $3 \times 2$ matrix |
| 8 | $\Sigma_{k+1}$ | Final pose uncertainty | $3 \times 3$ covariance |
