# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate clearly between systematic and non-systematic errors in odometric position estimation. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Systematic errors** are *deterministic biases* that repeat identically across runs and accumulate predictably. Examples:
> 1. Wheel-diameter mismatch between left and right wheels (constant scale-factor error).
> 2. Encoder quantisation (bounded, periodic offset of magnitude $k/2$ per tick).
>
> **Non-systematic errors** are *random, environment-dependent disturbances* that cannot be calibrated away. Examples:
> 1. Wheel slip on a wet or sandy floor (state-dependent).
> 2. Uneven floor contact (instantaneous rotation about an unknown point).
>
> *Mark split:* 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for two systematic examples, 1 mark for two non-systematic examples.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Why is the mid-arc heading $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ used in the discrete pose update of a differential-drive robot, instead of the current heading $\theta$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The robot travels along a *circular arc* of length $c$ and sweep $\Delta\theta$. The true geometric displacement is the chord of that arc. Using $\theta$ instead of $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ corresponds to a **first-order Euler** discretisation, which introduces an $O(\Delta\theta^{2})$ heading error. The mid-arc heading yields a **second-order accurate** RungeŌĆōKutta-style approximation, halving the per-step integration error.
>
> *Mark split:* 1 mark for the arc interpretation, 1 mark for the order-of-accuracy argument, 1 mark for the consequence on the Jacobian.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module-2 14-mark question offers the candidate an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. The model answers below cover both choices; in the actual exam paper, only one of the two is presented.

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a differential-drive mobile robot with wheel radius $r$ and wheel-base $b$, derive the **odometric pose-update equations** relating $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \theta_{k+1})$ to $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$ and the encoder deltas $\Delta\phi_r, \Delta\phi_l$. Clearly state the assumption made about the robot's trajectory.

**Model Solution.**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Kinematic model.* The incremental linear travel of each wheel is

$$\Delta s_r = \frac{2\pi r}{N}\Delta\phi_r, \qquad \Delta s_l = \frac{2\pi r}{N}\Delta\phi_l$$

*Step 2 ŌĆö Trajectory assumption.* The robot is assumed to move along a **circular arc** of radius $R$ over the sampling interval (this is the ICC assumption). Under this assumption the heading changes by

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$$

and the linear path length is

$$c = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Pose update using mid-arc heading.* Displacement along the chord of the arc is

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + c\,\cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + c\,\sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$$

> *Mark split:* 2 marks for the kinematic model, 2 marks for stating and using the ICC/arc assumption, 3 marks for the final pose equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Now suppose the encoder on each wheel has a *uniform quantisation* of magnitude $k/2$ (where $k = 2\pi r/N$). Construct the **input covariance matrix** $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$ and explain why the two wheel errors are modelled as *independent*.

**Model Solution.**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Variance of a uniform distribution.* The quantisation error of a single tick is uniform in $[-k/2, +k/2]$, with variance

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{(k/2 - (-k/2))^{2}}{12} = \frac{k^{2}}{12} = \frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{2\pi r}{N}\right)^{2}$$

*Step 2 ŌĆö Conservative upper bound.* The KTU 2024 syllabus accepts the half-tick bound as the worst-case variance:

$$\sigma_{r}^{2} = \sigma_{l}^{2} = \left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{\pi r}{N}\right)^{2}$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Independence argument.* Left and right encoders are **physically separate hardware** mounted on opposite sides of the chassis; their quantisation events are triggered by independent track-pattern features on each wheel. There is no common noise source, so the cross-covariance is zero and the joint covariance is diagonal:

$$\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}} = \begin{bmatrix}\sigma_{r}^{2} & 0\\ 0 & \sigma_{l}^{2}\end{bmatrix} = \left(\frac{\pi r}{N}\right)^{2}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 4 ŌĆö Engineering remark.* If the wheels are coupled through a *belt-drive* or share a *common shaft*, the cross-covariance may be non-zero, in which case the off-diagonal entries must be populated. KTU problems almost always assume independent encoders.

> *Mark split:* 2 marks for variance computation, 2 marks for the diagonal construction, 2 marks for the independence argument, 1 mark for the engineering remark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the discrete pose update $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{u}_k)$, derive the **error-propagation equation**

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_k\,\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top} + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$$

Highlight the physical meaning of the two summed terms.

**Model Solution.**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Linearisation.* Let the true pose be $\mathbf{x} = \hat{\mathbf{x}} + \delta\mathbf{x}$ and the true input be $\mathbf{u} = \hat{\mathbf{u}} + \delta\mathbf{u}$. Taylor-expanding $\mathbf{f}$ to first order:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}} + \delta\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k, \hat{\mathbf{u}}_k) + \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\,\delta\mathbf{x}_k + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\delta\mathbf{u}_k$$

Subtracting the nominal update $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k+1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k, \hat{\mathbf{u}}_k)$:

$$\delta\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\,\delta\mathbf{x}_k + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\delta\mathbf{u}_k$$

*Step 2 ŌĆö Covariance of a linear combination.* If $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are independent zero-mean random vectors, then for $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{b}$:

$$\mathrm{cov}(\mathbf{c}) = \mathbf{A}\,\mathrm{cov}(\mathbf{a})\,\mathbf{A}^{\top} + \mathbf{B}\,\mathrm{cov}(\mathbf{b})\,\mathbf{B}^{\top}$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the identity.* With $\mathbf{A} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$, $\mathbf{B} = \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$, $\mathrm{cov}(\delta\mathbf{x}_k) = \Sigma_k$, $\mathrm{cov}(\delta\mathbf{u}_k) = \Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$:

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_k\,\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top} + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$$

*Step 4 ŌĆö Physical meaning of the two terms.*

| Term | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_k\,\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$ | Propagates the **uncertainty already present** in the previous pose estimate forward through the motion ŌĆö the "memory" of past errors. |
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$ | Injects **fresh uncertainty** from the current wheel-encoder noise into the pose estimate ŌĆö the "per-step" error. |

> *Mark split:* 2 marks for the linearisation, 2 marks for the covariance identity, 2 marks for the final equation, 1 mark for the physical-meaning table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot with $r = 0.04\ \text{m}$, $b = 0.20\ \text{m}$, $N = 1000$ counts/rev takes one step of $\Delta\phi_r = 50$, $\Delta\phi_l = 50$ ticks. Compute the resulting $\Sigma_{k+1}$ assuming $\Sigma_k = 0$.

**Model Solution.**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Constants.*

$$k = \frac{2\pi r}{N} = \frac{2\pi\cdot 0.04}{1000} = 2.513\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/tick}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{tick}} = \frac{\pi r}{N} = \frac{\pi\cdot 0.04}{1000} = 1.257\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

*Step 2 ŌĆö Increments.*

$$\Delta s_r = \Delta s_l = 2.513\times 10^{-4}\times 50 = 0.012566\ \text{m}$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Path length and heading.*

$$c = 0.012566\ \text{m}, \qquad \Delta\theta = 0\ \text{rad}, \qquad \theta_{m} = 0$$

*Step 4 ŌĆö Jacobians.* For pure straight motion ($\Delta\theta = 0$, $\theta_m = 0$):

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\ 0 & 1 & 0.012566\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.5\\ 0.031415 & -0.031415\\ 5 & -5\end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 5 ŌĆö Input covariance.*

$$\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}} = (1.257\times 10^{-4})^{2}\mathbf{I}_2 = 1.580\times 10^{-8}\mathbf{I}_2$$

*Step 6 ŌĆö Covariance aggregation.* Since $\Sigma_k = 0$, the pose-propagation term vanishes:

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$$

Multiplying:

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = 1.580\times 10^{-8}\begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.5\\ 0.031415 & -0.031415\\ 5 & -5\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.031415 & 5\\ 0.5 & -0.031415 & -5\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma_{k+1} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
7.90\times 10^{-9} & 0 & 0\\[3pt]
0 & 3.12\times 10^{-11} & 0\\[3pt]
0 & 0 & 7.90\times 10^{-7}
\end{bmatrix}\ \text{m}^{2},\ \text{rad}^{2}$$

*Step 7 ŌĆö Interpretation.* $\sigma_{x} \approx 8.9\times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$, $\sigma_{y} \approx 5.6\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, $\sigma_{\theta} \approx 8.9\times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}$. Even for a *straight* motion of just $1.25\ \text{cm}$, the heading uncertainty is the dominant error channel, foreshadowing the **quadratic growth of position error with distance** observed in real robots.

> *Mark split:* 1 mark for constants, 1 mark for increments, 1 mark for Jacobians, 2 marks for matrix multiplication, 2 marks for interpretation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on this topic ŌĆö avoid all of them!**
>
> 1. **Using the *current* heading $\theta_k$ instead of the mid-arc heading $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$ in the Jacobians.** This single mistake propagates into every subsequent matrix and costs 2ŌĆō3 marks. The mid-arc form is the KTU-blessed convention.
> 2. **Forgetting the transpose in the propagation equation.** The propagation is $\nabla\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma\,\nabla\mathbf{f}^{\top}$ ŌĆö a *sandwich*. Writing only $\nabla\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma$ or $\Sigma\,\nabla\mathbf{f}$ is a 1-mark deduction *each time* it appears.
> 3. **Treating wheel-diameter mismatch as a stochastic noise.** It is *systematic* and belongs in the model, not in $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$. Adding it to $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$ inflates the variance incorrectly.
> 4. **Confusing the input covariance with the pose covariance.** $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$ is a $2\times 2$ matrix (left, right wheel variances), $\Sigma_k$ is a $3\times 3$ matrix (pose). Dimensions must be consistent.
> 5. **Skipping the independence justification.** When asked to *construct* $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$, students often write the diagonal without *explaining* why the off-diagonals are zero. The independence argument is a 1-mark line item.
> 6. **Ignoring the $\chi^{2}_{2,0.95} = 5.99$ constant when quoting the "95 % uncertainty ellipse".** Many students write the ellipse equation but forget to multiply the variances by $5.99$, costing 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Read this checklist **just before** entering the exam hall. It compresses the entire module to one screen.

- Odometry = **integration of wheel rotations** to estimate pose; errors compound with travel.
- Two error families: **systematic** (calibratable, deterministic) and **non-systematic** (stochastic, environment-dependent).
- Standard pose update uses the **mid-arc heading** $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ for second-order accuracy.
- The **error-propagation equation** is the linearised sum of two terms: *pose-propagation* + *input-injection*.
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$ is a $3\times 3$ matrix with a near-identity structure; the only non-trivial off-diagonal entries are in the third column, scaled by $c\,\sin\theta_m$ and $c\,\cos\theta_m$.
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}$ is a $3\times 2$ matrix; its third row is always $[\,1/b\ \ -1/b\,]$.
- $\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}$ is **diagonal** under the independent-encoder assumption; the half-tick bound is $\sigma^{2} = (\pi r/N)^{2}$.
- The **uncertainty ellipse** is drawn using $\sigma_{x}\sqrt{5.99}$ and $\sigma_{y}\sqrt{5.99}$ for the 95 % confidence region.
- Borenstein's *practical* model: $e_{x} = a_{1}D^{2} + a_{2}D$, $e_{y} = a_{3}D$, $e_{\theta} = a_{4}D$ ŌĆö quick to evaluate in exams, but the Jacobian model is the rigorous one.
- **Always** show the linearisation step ($\mathbf{x} = \hat{\mathbf{x}} + \delta\mathbf{x}$) before writing the propagation equation.
- **Always** state the assumption (independent encoders, ICC arc, no slip) before applying the formula.
- **Always** justify dimensional consistency: $3\times 3 \cdot 3\times 3 \cdot 3\times 3$ on the left, $3\times 2 \cdot 2\times 2 \cdot 2\times 3$ on the right.
- In exam answers, **label** every term ("pose-propagation" / "input-injection") for a free 1 mark.
- A common exam "twist" is to ask for the *cumulative* covariance after $N$ steps: integrate $\Sigma_{k+1} = \Sigma_k + \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}\,\Sigma_{\mathbf{u}}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{f}^{\top}$ over $N$ steps. Be ready.
- For *straight-line* motion ($\Delta\theta = 0$), the $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}$ Jacobian simplifies to a pure rotation/translation ŌĆö exploit this symmetry to halve the derivation time in an exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Localization-Based Navigation

**Localization-Based Navigation** is a closed-loop, sensor-driven navigation paradigm in which a mobile robot continuously estimates its own pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) within a reference coordinate frame, and uses this pose estimate to dynamically plan and update its trajectory toward a goal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Localization is the process of determining the pose of a robot relative to a given map of the environment. Navigation based on this continuously updated pose is termed localization-based navigation.*

Mathematically, at every discrete time step $k$, the robot maintains a belief distribution:

$$
bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $x_t$ is the current state, $z_{1:t}$ are the sensor measurements up to time $t$, and $u_{1:t}$ are the control inputs. The robot **never trusts its initial position blindly** ŌĆö it constantly corrects itself.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Programmed Solutions

**Programmed (or Pre-scripted) Navigation Solutions** are open-loop, deterministic trajectories that are either hard-coded, taught via a teach-pendant, or pre-computed offline. The robot executes a fixed sequence of motion primitives (move forward 2 m, turn 90┬░, etc.) without any real-time verification of its actual pose.

> [!NOTE]
> These are also referred to in KTU textbooks as **Pre-Planned Paths**, **Waypoint Navigation**, or **Scripted Behaviors**. They are the dominant paradigm in industrial Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) following magnetic tape or QR codes.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Tourist vs. The Commuter":**
>
> - **Programmed Solution** = A daily train commuter. Every morning, the commuter boards the **same train, at the same platform, follows the same fixed track** to reach the office. The track never changes, stops are fixed, and the commuter never asks *"Where am I?"* If a track is blocked, the commuter is **stuck**.
>
> - **Localization-Based Navigation** = A tourist in a new city holding a **GPS-enabled smartphone with Google Maps**. The tourist constantly asks *"Where am I now?"* (localization), compares it with *"Where do I want to go?"* (goal), and dynamically chooses a route. If a road is closed, the tourist **reroutes instantly**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

The following industry-standard performance metrics are universally applied to grade any navigation system:

- **Accuracy** ŌĆö the mean positional error in **meters** or **millimeters**.
- **Repeatability** ŌĆö the standard deviation of end-pose over $N \geq 30$ trials.
- **Update Rate** ŌĆö the frequency of pose correction, typically **10 Hz ŌĆō 100 Hz**.
- **Computational Cost** ŌĆö measured in **FLOPS** or **CPU/GPU utilization %**.
- **Robustness** ŌĆö Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) in hours.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Paradigms ŌĆö A Bullet-Style Comparison

**Programmed Solutions ŌĆö Operational Logic:**
- Robot stores a sequence of $(x, y, \theta, v, \omega)$ tuples in memory.
- Execution is **deterministic**: same input ŌåÆ same output.
- No sensor feedback is used to correct drift; any error **accumulates** with distance.
- Relies on **infrastructure** (magnetic tape, RFID tags, colored lines) embedded in the environment.
- Best for: structured warehouses, assembly lines, repetitive pick-and-place.

**Localization-Based Solutions ŌĆö Operational Logic:**
- Robot uses proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMU) + exteroceptive sensors (LIDAR, camera, GPS, UWB beacons).
- Applies a **Bayesian filter** (Kalman / Particle / EKF) to fuse sensor data into a pose belief.
- Path is **re-planned online** using algorithms like A*, D*, RRT, or Potential Fields.
- Best for: dynamic warehouses, outdoor autonomous cars, search-and-rescue, planetary rovers.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

**Why does Programmed Navigation drift?**
Because wheel encoders integrate small errors $\delta v$ and $\delta \omega$ over time, producing unbounded growth in pose uncertainty ŌĆö a phenomenon called **odometric drift**.

**How does Localization counter it?**
By introducing **absolute measurements** (e.g., a LIDAR scan matching a known map, a GPS fix) that re-anchor the robot's pose and reset the integrated error.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation / Definition** | **Units / Boundary** | **When to Use** |
|--------------|---------------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Robot Pose | $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | meters, meters, radians | State vector at time $t$ |
| Odometry Update | $x_{t+1} = x_t + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_t)$ | meters | Dead-reckoning step |
| Odometry Update | $y_{t+1} = y_t + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta_t)$ | meters | Dead-reckoning step |
| Heading Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t$ | radians | Dead-reckoning step |
| Odometric Error Bound | $\sigma_{xy}^2 = k_r^2 \cdot d^2$ | $m^2$ (where $k_r$ = error constant, $d$ = distance) | Drift estimation |
| Kalman Gain | $K_t = P_t H^T (H P_t H^T + R)^{-1}$ | dimensionless | Sensor fusion weighting |
| Belief Update (Bayes) | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | probability | Core localization recursion |
| Repeatability (ISO 9283) | $RP = \pm 3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\sum (d_i - \bar{d})^2}{N-1}}$ | meters | Industrial robot grading |
| Path Length Cost | $L = \int_{0}^{T} \vert v(t) \vert \, dt$ | meters | Optimality metric |
| Control Frequency | $f_c = 1 / \Delta t$ | Hz | Real-time constraint (typically $\geq$ **10 Hz**) |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, every absolute-value bar was rendered using `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser intact. **Never use plain `|` inside a KTU study table** ŌĆö it will break the column structure during PDF export.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (KUKA, FANUC AGVs):** Mostly programmed + simple localization (line-following).
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Pure localization-based (HD-map + GPS + LIDAR + Vision).
- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** Hybrid ŌĆö coded floor QR-codes **plus** SLAM for redundancy.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Pure localization-based, since pre-installed infrastructure is impossible on Mars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Programmed Solutions Accumulate Error

We start from a single wheel encoder reading. Let $n_L$ and $n_R$ be the tick counts from the left and right wheels, $r$ the wheel radius, and $b$ the axle length.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Linear displacement of each wheel:**

$$
\Delta s_L = 2 \pi r \cdot \frac{n_L}{N_{ticks}}, \quad \Delta s_R = 2 \pi r \cdot \frac{n_R}{N_{ticks}}
$$

where $N_{ticks}$ is encoder resolution (ticks per revolution).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linear and angular velocity of the robot:**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2}, \quad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Pose update using unicycle kinematics:**

$$
x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Error propagation. Let $\sigma_L^2$ and $\sigma_R^2$ be wheel-variance.**

The variance in heading after $N$ steps:

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^2 = \left(\frac{r}{b}\right)^2 \cdot (\sigma_L^2 + \sigma_R^2) \cdot N
$$

The variance in Cartesian position (linear growth):

$$
\sigma_{xy}^2 = k_r^2 \cdot d^2
$$

where $d$ is the total distance traveled and $k_r$ is a robot-specific error constant (typically **1 % ŌĆō 5 %**). The quadratic-in-distance growth is precisely why a **purely programmed (open-loop) system cannot navigate reliably over long distances** without an external correction.

### 3.2 Derivation: How Localization Resets the Error

Suppose at time $t$ the robot's pose has uncertainty $P_t$ (a $3 \times 3$ covariance matrix). It receives a measurement $z_t$ with noise covariance $R$. The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction-update cycle is:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Prediction (using odometry $u_t$):**

$$
\bar{x}_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t), \quad \bar{P}_t = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Innovation (difference between measurement and prediction):**

$$
\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{x}_t)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Kalman Gain calculation:**

$$
K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Correction (the localization step):**

$$
x_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t \cdot \nu_t, \quad P_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{P}_t
$$

The covariance $P_t$ **shrinks** with every absolute measurement, **cancelling** the odometric drift accumulated in $\bar{P}_t$. This is the mathematical heart of localization-based navigation.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which?

| **Criterion** | **Programmed Solution** | **Localization-Based** | **Winner** |
|---------------|-------------------------|------------------------|------------|
| Static, structured factory | Excellent fit | Overkill | **Programmed** |
| Dynamic, human-shared space | Dangerous | Safe and adaptive | **Localization** |
| Cost-sensitive deployment | Low (no LIDAR) | High (sensors + compute) | **Programmed** |
| Long-range outdoor mission | Fails (drift) | Succeeds | **Localization** |
| Compute budget (embedded MCU) | Trivial | Needs GPU / NPU | **Programmed** |
| Need for re-planning on obstacle | None | Real-time | **Localization** |
| Deterministic cycle time (Takt) | Guaranteed | Variable | **Programmed** |
| Map-less deployment | Natural | Requires prior map / SLAM | **Programmed** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROBOT NAVIGATION SYSTEM] --> B{Navigation Paradigm}
    B --> C[PROGRAMMED SOLUTION]
    B --> D[LOCALIZATION-BASED SOLUTION]
    
    C --> C1[Predefined Waypoint List]
    C1 --> C2[Open Loop Controller]
    C2 --> C3[Wheel Encoders Only]
    C3 --> C4[Fixed Trajectory Execution]
    C4 --> C5[No Replanning]
    
    D --> D1[Sensor Suite: LIDAR Camera IMU GPS]
    D1 --> D2[Bayesian Filter EKF Particle]
    D2 --> D3[Pose Belief x y theta]
    D3 --> D4[Path Planner A star RRT]
    D4 --> D5[Closed Loop Replanning]
    
    C5 --> R[Reach Goal]
    D5 --> R
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#6b7280,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#059669,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Hybrid Architecture (Industry Best Practice)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PERCEPTION_LAYER [PERCEPTION LAYER]
        S1[LIDAR Scan]
        S2[Camera RGB-D]
        S3[IMU Gyro Accel]
        S4[Wheel Encoders]
    end
    
    subgraph FUSION_LAYER [FUSION LAYER]
        F1[Extended Kalman Filter]
        F2[Particle Filter SLAM]
    end
    
    subgraph PLANNING_LAYER [PLANNING LAYER]
        P1[Global Planner A star on Map]
        P2[Local Planner DWA]
    end
    
    subgraph CONTROL_LAYER [CONTROL LAYER]
        K1[PID Velocity Controller]
        K2[Motor Drivers]
    end
    
    S1 --> F1
    S2 --> F1
    S3 --> F1
    S4 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    K2 -->|Feedback| S4
    
    style PERCEPTION_LAYER fill:#dbeafe
    style FUSION_LAYER fill:#fef3c7
    style PLANNING_LAYER fill:#dcfce7
    style CONTROL_LAYER fill:#fee2e2
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Error Growth vs. Correction

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start of Mission t = 0] --> M1[Move 1 m]
    M1 --> E1[Error grows: 1 percent of 1 m = 1 cm]
    E1 --> M2[Move another 1 m]
    M2 --> E2[Error grows: 2 cm accumulated]
    E2 --> M3[Move another 1 m]
    M3 --> E3[Error grows: 3 cm accumulated]
    E3 --> LANDMARK{See landmark?}
    LANDMARK -->|No - Programmed| DRIFT[Drift continues unbounded]
    LANDMARK -->|Yes - Localization| RESET[Pose reset to map frame]
    RESET --> M4[Move forward with low error]
    DRIFT --> FAIL[Eventually lost]
    
    style START fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style LANDMARK fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    style RESET fill:#059669,color:#ffffff
    style FAIL fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between localization-based navigation and programmed navigation in mobile robots. List any two advantages of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Programmed** | **Localization-Based** |
|------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Feedback | Open-loop | Closed-loop |
| Sensor dependence | Minimal (encoders only) | Heavy (LIDAR / Camera / GPS) |
| Adaptability | None | Real-time replanning |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Two advantages each (1 mark ├Ś 2 = 2 marks):**
- **Programmed:** (i) Deterministic cycle time, (ii) No expensive sensors required.
- **Localization-Based:** (i) Robust to dynamic obstacles, (ii) Unbounded operational range (drift-corrected).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "odometric drift" and state why it limits purely programmed navigation solutions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Odometric drift is the unbounded accumulation of small errors in wheel-encoder integration that causes the robot's estimated pose to diverge from its true pose over time.
- **Mathematical form (1 mark):** $\sigma_{xy}^2 = k_r^2 \cdot d^2$ ŌĆö variance grows quadratically with distance.
- **Why it limits programmed solutions (0.5 mark):** Because programmed solutions have no absolute reference to correct this drift, leading to eventual collision or mission failure.

---

### **PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a localization-based navigation system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Bayesian filter and the path planner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and axle length $b = 0.30$ m moves for 10 s with left-wheel ticks $n_L = 5000$ and right-wheel ticks $n_R = 5200$. Encoder resolution $N_{ticks} = 1000$ ticks/rev. Compute the change in pose $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$ assuming the initial heading is $\theta_0 = 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q3(a):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Encoders + IMU + LIDAR + Camera] 
        ŌåÆ [Sensor Fusion / EKF] ŌåÆ [Pose Belief (x,y,╬Ė)] 
        ŌåÆ [Global Planner (A*)] ŌåÆ [Local Planner (DWA)] 
        ŌåÆ [PID Controller] ŌåÆ [Motors] ŌåÆ [Motion]
```

**Role of the Bayesian filter (2 marks):**
The Bayesian filter recursively estimates the robot's pose belief $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ by combining the prediction from odometry with the correction from absolute sensors. Implementations include the **Kalman Filter** (Gaussian, linear), **EKF** (nonlinear), and **Particle Filter** (non-parametric, handles multi-modal beliefs).

**Role of the path planner (2 marks):**
The global planner uses the current pose belief and a known map to compute a collision-free path from start to goal (e.g., A*, Dijkstra). The local planner (e.g., DWA ŌĆö Dynamic Window Approach) generates velocity commands that follow the global path while avoiding moving obstacles in real time.

---

**Solution to Q3(b) ŌĆö Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute wheel displacements** (1 mark):

$$
\Delta s_L = 2 \pi r \cdot \frac{n_L}{N_{ticks}} = 2 \pi (0.05) \cdot \frac{5000}{1000} = 0.1 \pi \approx 0.3142 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta s_R = 2 \pi r \cdot \frac{n_R}{N_{ticks}} = 2 \pi (0.05) \cdot \frac{5200}{1000} = 0.104 \pi \approx 0.3267 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute linear and angular displacement** (2 marks):

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2} = \frac{0.3142 + 0.3267}{2} \approx 0.3205 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.3267 - 0.3142}{0.30} \approx 0.0417 \text{ rad} \approx 2.39^\circ
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply unicycle kinematics** (2 marks):

$$
\Delta x = \Delta s \cdot \cos\left(\theta_0 + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) = 0.3205 \cdot \cos(0.0209) \approx 0.3204 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta y = \Delta s \cdot \sin\left(\theta_0 + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) = 0.3205 \cdot \sin(0.0209) \approx 0.0067 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final pose change** (2 marks):

$$
\boxed{\Delta x \approx 0.320 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta y \approx 0.007 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta \theta \approx 0.042 \text{ rad}}
$$

The robot has moved mostly forward with a small rightward curvature, consistent with $n_R > n_L$.

---

**Q4. [Internal Choice ŌĆö Alternative to Q3]**
**(a)** Compare programmed and localization-based navigation under the heads: (i) sensor requirement, (ii) computational cost, (iii) robustness to dynamic environments, and (iv) typical industrial use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial AGV following a magnetic tape (programmed mode) has a position-error constant $k_r = 0.02$ (i.e., 2 % drift per meter). It must travel 50 m. Compute: (i) the expected RMS position error at the destination, and (ii) how many **absolute-correction beacons** (each resetting error to zero) must be placed to keep the per-segment error below **5 cm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q4(a):**

| **Head** | **Programmed** | **Localization-Based** | **Marks** |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------|
| (i) Sensors | Encoders only | LIDAR + Camera + IMU + GPS | 1.5 |
| (ii) Compute | Negligible (lookup table) | High (EKF + A* + DWA) | 1.5 |
| (iii) Robustness | Low (fails on obstacle) | High (real-time replan) | 2.0 |
| (iv) Use case | Fixed-path AGVs in warehouses | Autonomous cars, drones, Mars rovers | 2.0 |

---

**Solution to Q4(b) ŌĆö Step-by-step:**

**Step (i) ŌĆö RMS error at 50 m without correction** (3 marks):

Using $\sigma_{xy} = k_r \cdot d$:

$$
\sigma_{xy} = 0.02 \times 50 = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Number of beacons** (4 marks):

Let $L$ be the spacing between beacons. The per-segment error is $\sigma_{seg} = k_r \cdot L$.

We require $\sigma_{seg} \leq 0.05$ m:

$$
0.02 \cdot L \leq 0.05 \implies L \leq 2.5 \text{ m}
$$

Number of beacons for a 50 m track:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{50}{2.5} \right\rceil - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19 \text{ beacons}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{xy} = 1.0 \text{ m, and 19 beacons are required to keep segment error} \leq 5 \text{ cm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with repeatability** ŌĆö KTU markers explicitly test this in Module 2. Accuracy = closeness to truth; repeatability = closeness of trials to each other.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** ŌĆö When computing the number of beacons, students often write $50/2.5 = 20$ but forget to subtract 1 (the first beacon is at the start).
> 3. **Using degrees instead of radians** in the unicycle model ŌĆö KTU boards deduct **1 full mark** for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Omitting the $H_t$ Jacobian** in EKF equations ŌĆö always show the observation-model Jacobian even if it is identity, as the examiner allocates marks for it.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Programmed navigation** = open-loop, deterministic, infrastructure-dependent, no replanning, drifts with distance.
- **Localization-based navigation** = closed-loop, sensor-fused, adaptive, replans online, drift-corrected.
- The **pose vector** is always $[x, y, \theta]^T$ ŌĆö never forget orientation.
- **Unicycle kinematics** govern differential-drive robots: always use the **mid-heading** $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ for higher accuracy.
- **Drift formula** $\sigma_{xy}^2 = k_r^2 d^2$ is the single most-tested equation in this module.
- **EKF cycle**: Predict ŌåÆ Innovate ŌåÆ Gain ŌåÆ Correct ŌĆö memorize this order.
- **Bayes recursion** $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ appears in **every** KTU paper.
- **Cost trade-off**: programmed = cheap but rigid; localization = expensive but flexible. The industrial trend is **hybrid** (RFID + SLAM).
- For **14-mark derivations**, always show: (1) diagram, (2) formula, (3) substitution, (4) numerical answer with **units**, (5) physical interpretation.
- **Repeatability formula** (ISO 9283) carries 1ŌĆō2 marks almost every semester ŌĆö practice it.
- **Key abbreviations**: AGV, EKF, SLAM, DWA, IMU, LIDAR, FOV, MTBF ŌĆö know each in one line.
- **Mermaid + LaTeX tip for your own answer sheets**: copy the diagrams above; they already comply with KTU 2024's structured-answer format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belief Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Belief Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Probabilistic Mobile Robotics, **Belief Representation** is the formal mathematical framework used by an autonomous agent (mobile robot) to internally encode and manipulate its incomplete or uncertain knowledge about its own state (pose ŌĆö position and orientation) and the state of its surrounding environment.

A **belief** is a probability distribution over all possible states the robot might be in, conditioned on all past sensor observations and control actions executed up to the current time step. Formally, the belief at time $t$ is the posterior distribution:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $x_t$ is the robot state, $z_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of measurements from time $1$ to $t$, and $u_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of control inputs. The **prediction-only** form (before incorporating the latest sensor reading $z_t$) is called the *prior* or **belief bar**:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PERAT741 ŌĆö Module 2):** Students must master the distinction between $\text{bel}(x_t)$ and $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$, the role of the Bayes filter in generating these beliefs, and the computational trade-offs between unimodal (parametric) and multimodal (non-parametric) belief representations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Where Are My Keys?" Problem

Imagine you woke up in the middle of the night and your spouse asks, *"Where are my car keys?"* You do not know with certainty. You form a **mental probability map**: *10% on the kitchen counter, 5% near the front door, 40% in the handbag, 20% in the car itself, 25% in some unknown location*. That mental probability distribution is your *belief* about the state of the keys.

Now suppose you walk to the kitchen counter and do NOT see the keys. Your belief must be **updated**: the probability of "kitchen counter" drops to **0%**, and the remaining probability mass is redistributed to other hypotheses. This continuous process of:
1. **Predicting** where the keys *might* be (based on prior habit), and
2. **Updating** that belief after new evidence (looking on the counter)

is **exactly** what a robot does with its pose belief, except the mathematics is rigorous and the hypotheses span a continuous 2D or 3D state space instead of a few discrete rooms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A belief is *not* a single point estimate. It is a **cloud of plausibility** over the entire state space. The shape, width, and modality of this cloud determines the algorithm used to represent and update it.

## 1.3 Why Belief Representation is Indispensable

Three engineering realities make belief representation unavoidable in robot navigation:

| Reality | Effect on Robot | Belief Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor Noise** | Wheel encoders, GPS, LIDAR all give imperfect measurements | Belief must *quantify* uncertainty |
| **Actuator Noise** | Motors do not execute commanded motion exactly | Belief must *propagate* uncertainty through motion |
| **Partial Observability** | A robot behind an obstacle cannot see what is ahead | Belief must *integrate* evidence over time |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Convention in KTU Examinations:** Beliefs are normalized so that the integral (continuous case) or sum (discrete case) over the entire state space equals **1.0**. Any unnormalized distribution must be divided by a normalizer $\eta$ (eta) to maintain this probabilistic validity.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Belief Representations

Belief representations in KTU-aligned robot navigation are broadly classified as:

1. **Parametric Representations** (assume a fixed functional form, e.g., Gaussian)
   - Kalman Filter (KF)
   - Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)
   - Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)
2. **Non-Parametric Representations** (no fixed form, sample-based or grid-based)
   - Histogram Filters (Grid-based decomposition)
   - Particle Filters (Sequential Monte Carlo)
3. **Hybrid / Topological Representations**
   - Graph-based belief propagation

Each will be analyzed rigorously in **SECTION 2**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes Filter ŌĆö The Mathematical Heart of Belief

Every belief representation ultimately implements some form of the **Recursive Bayes Filter**. The recursion alternates between a **prediction** step and a **measurement-update** step.

### 2.1.1 Prediction Step (a.k.a. Belief Propagation)

Using the law of total probability and the Markov assumption that the current state $x_t$ depends only on $x_{t-1}$ and the current control $u_t$:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Logic of the step:**
- Start with the previous posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$.
- For *every* hypothetical previous state $x_{t-1}$, compute the likelihood of arriving at $x_t$ via control $u_t$ using the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.
- Integrate (sum up) the contributions over all $x_{t-1}$ to obtain the new prior.
- **Effect:** Uncertainty *grows* because of the stochastic motion model.

### 2.1.2 Measurement Update Step (a.k.a. Belief Correction)

Incorporate the new sensor reading $z_t$ via Bayes' rule:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where the normalizer $\eta$ is:

$$\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x') \, dx' \right]^{-1}$$

**Logic of the step:**
- For each hypothetical state $x_t$, weight its prior probability by how well the actual measurement $z_t$ is *expected* at that state (the **measurement model** $p(z_t \mid x_t)$).
- **Effect:** Uncertainty *shrinks* (or shifts) because the sensor provides new information.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Belief Representation

### Pillar 1 ŌĆö Gaussian (Parametric) Representation

**Assumption:** The belief is a single **multivariate normal (Gaussian) distribution**, fully characterized by its mean vector $\mu$ and covariance matrix $\Sigma$.

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) = \det(2\pi\Sigma_t)^{-1/2} \exp\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_t-\mu_t)^T \Sigma_t^{-1}(x_t-\mu_t)\right)$$

- **Strengths:** Compact (only $n + n^2/2$ parameters for $n$-dimensional state), closed-form Bayesian update, computationally efficient.
- **Weaknesses:** **Unimodal only** ŌĆö cannot represent multiple hypotheses (e.g., a robot equally likely in two different corridors).
- **Used in:** Kalman Filter, EKF, UKF, EIF (Error-state Kalman / Information Filter).

### Pillar 2 ŌĆö Histogram Filter (Discretized Grid)

**Assumption:** The continuous state space is partitioned into a finite grid of bins, and the belief is a piecewise-constant probability mass over those bins.

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \approx \{p_{t,1}, p_{t,2}, \ldots, p_{t,K}\}, \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{t,k} = 1$$

- **Strengths:** Multimodal; conceptually simple; accommodates arbitrary (non-Gaussian) shapes.
- **Weaknesses:** **Curse of dimensionality** ŌĆö state space grows exponentially with dimensions; resolution fixed by grid.

### Pillar 3 ŌĆö Particle Filter (Sample-Based)

**Assumption:** The belief is approximated by a finite set of weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$$

- **Strengths:** Multimodal, can approximate any distribution (universal approximator), handles non-linear / non-Gaussian systems elegantly.
- **Weaknesses:** Computational cost scales with particle count $M$; susceptible to **particle deprivation** (sample impoverishment).

### Pillar 4 ŌĆö Topological / Hybrid

**Assumption:** Belief is maintained over a discrete set of "places" or "nodes" connected by edges. Common in SLAM (e.g., pose-graph SLAM).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for Module 2 examination problems.** Memorize the equations and their dimensional/normative implications.

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Posterior Belief | $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Normalized $\Rightarrow$ integrates to **1** |
| 2 | Prior (Predicted) Belief | $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ | Includes $u_t$, excludes $z_t$ |
| 3 | Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Motion model $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ |
| 4 | Bayes Filter Update | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ is normalizer |
| 5 | Normalizer | $\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \vert x') \overline{\text{bel}}(x') dx' \right]^{-1}$ | Ensures $\int \text{bel} = 1$ |
| 6 | Gaussian PDF (n-dim) | $\mathcal{N}(x; \mu, \Sigma) = \det(2\pi\Sigma)^{-1/2} \exp(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu))$ | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ symmetric PSD |
| 7 | EKF Prediction (mean) | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$ | $g$ is non-linear motion |
| 8 | EKF Prediction (cov) | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + R_t$ | $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ Jacobian, $R_t$ motion noise |
| 9 | EKF Update (mean) | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | $h$ is non-linear measurement |
| 10 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$, $Q_t$ sensor noise |
| 11 | EKF Update (cov) | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior covariance |
| 12 | Particle Belief | $\text{bel}(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$ | $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$ |
| 13 | Resampling | $x_t^{[i]} \sim$ discrete distribution over $\{x_t^{[j]}, w_t^{[j]}\}$ | Avoids weight degeneracy |
| 14 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ | Trigger resampling if $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$ |
| 15 | Information Form (mean) | $\xi_t = \Sigma_t^{-1} \mu_t$ | Dual of KF; $\xi$ = information vector |
| 16 | Information Form (cov) | $\Omega_t = \Sigma_t^{-1}$ | $\Omega$ = information matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` in the table above** (e.g., $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$) instead of the bare pipe character `|`. This is a **KTU-ENGINE formatting safeguard** to prevent the table from breaking the markdown parser.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla FSD, Waymo):** Maintain multi-modal Gaussian mixtures and particle hypotheses for lane and intersection localization.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Use histogram-filter-style grid beliefs for global localization against warehouse floor maps.
- **Indoor Service Robots (iRobot, Dyson):** Rely on particle filters for Monte-Carlo Localization (MCL) in cluttered domestic environments.
- **Aerial Drones (PX4, ArduPilot):** Use EKF on SE(2) or SE(3) manifolds for visual-inertial state estimation.
- **Subsea / Planetary Rovers (NASA Mars rovers):** Use hybrid grid + Gaussian mixtures due to computational constraints and sparse landmark data.

## 2.5 Choice-of-Representation Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Gaussian (KF/EKF) | Histogram | Particle | Topological |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modality** | Unimodal | Multimodal | Multimodal | Discrete |
| **Dimensionality** | LowŌĆōmoderate (Ōēż 6) | Low (Ōēż 3) | Any | Any |
| **Non-linear models** | Linearized only | Exact | Exact | Discrete only |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(K)$ | $O(M)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| **Storage** | Compact | $K$ floats | $M$ particles | Graph |
| **When to use** | Continuous, unimodal, smooth | Discrete, small state | Non-linear, multimodal | Place-recognition tasks |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Bayes Filter Recursion from First Principles

We will rigorously derive the prediction and update equations from the definition of the posterior and the Markov assumption.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the target posterior

By definition, the belief is the posterior over the current state given the data history:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the definition of conditional probability and split the data

We separate the most recent measurement $z_t$ from the past history:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Bayes' Rule

$$p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Markov Assumption (Conditional Independence)

If the state $x_t$ is a *sufficient statistic* of all past data, then the measurement $z_t$ is conditionally independent of $\{z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}\}$ given $x_t$, and $x_t$ is independent of $z_t$ given $u_t$ and $x_{t-1}$. This yields:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, u_t)$$

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, u_t)$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize the prior (bel-bar)

The second term in the numerator is precisely the prior:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \equiv \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Combine and normalize

Dropping the irrelevant conditioning in the measurement model and using $\eta$ for the denominator:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, u_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

This is the **measurement update** equation.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Expand the prior via the ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov equation

The prior is the prediction obtained by marginalizing out the previous state:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t, x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Apply the product rule:

$$= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Apply Markov again and recognize the previous posterior

$$= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the **prediction** equation. The full recursion is established.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Update

Assume a non-linear measurement model $z_t = h(x_t) + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ and a Gaussian prior $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Construct the joint Gaussian

Stack $x_t$ and $z_t$ into a single Gaussian random vector:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \begin{pmatrix} \bar{\mu}_t \\ h(\bar{\mu}_t) \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} \bar{\Sigma}_t & \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T \\ H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t & H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t \end{pmatrix} \right)$$

where $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ is the Jacobian linearization.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Use the conditional Gaussian identity

For a partitioned Gaussian $\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \begin{pmatrix} \mu_a \\ \mu_b \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} \Sigma_{aa} & \Sigma_{ab} \\ \Sigma_{ba} & \Sigma_{bb} \end{pmatrix} \right)$, the conditional $p(a \mid b)$ is:

$$p(a \mid b) = \mathcal{N}(a; \mu_a + \Sigma_{ab} \Sigma_{bb}^{-1}(b - \mu_b), \Sigma_{aa} - \Sigma_{ab} \Sigma_{bb}^{-1} \Sigma_{ba})$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the partitions

With $a = x_t$, $b = z_t$:

- $\Sigma_{ab} = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$
- $\Sigma_{bb} = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$
- Innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the Kalman gain

$$K_t \triangleq \Sigma_{ab} \Sigma_{bb}^{-1} = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Posterior mean

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Posterior covariance (Joseph form for numerical stability)

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$$

For EKF examinations, the simplified form is acceptable:

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Particle Filter Importance Sampling

The key challenge: in most robotics problems, the **target distribution** $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ cannot be sampled directly. We therefore sample from a **proposal distribution** $\pi(x_t)$ and correct via importance weights.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the importance weight

For a target $p(x)$ and proposal $\pi(x)$:

$$w(x) = \frac{p(x)}{\pi(x)}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Normalize the weights

$$w^{[i]}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{w^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w^{[j]}}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Sequential importance sampling for the Bayes filter

For the belief $\text{bel}(x_{0:t})$, the recursive importance weight update is:

$$w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \, p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)}{q(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{0:t-1}^{[i]}, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}$$

If the proposal $q$ is chosen to be the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, this reduces to the celebrated **Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR) filter**:

$$w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$

This is the **single most-cited equation in particle-filter localization (MCL)**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Resampling

To combat weight degeneracy, draw $M$ new particles from the discrete set $\{x_t^{[i]}\}$ with probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$. After resampling, all weights are reset to $1/M$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Representations Under Stress Tests

| Stress Condition | Gaussian (KF) | Histogram | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear motion + linear sensor | **Optimal** | Near-optimal | Near-optimal (wasteful) |
| Non-linear sensor, small noise | Acceptable (EKF) | Near-optimal | Optimal |
| **Kidnapped robot** (sudden jump) | Fails (mean in wrong valley) | Recovers if grid covers space | **Recovers robustly** |
| Symmetric corridor (global localization) | Cannot represent two peaks | Represents both peaks | **Represents all peaks** |
| High state dimension (Ōēź 6) | Excellent | Fails (curse of dim.) | Moderate (many particles needed) |
| Real-time constraint (Ōēź 50 Hz) | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate to poor |
| Very low noise (sensor nearly perfect) | Excellent | Excellent (if grid aligned) | Particle deprivation risk |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö The Bayes Filter Belief Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x t-1] --> B[Apply Motion Model p of x t given u t and x t-1]
    B --> C[Predicted Prior bel bar of x t]
    C --> D[Receive Sensor Measurement z t]
    D --> E[Apply Measurement Model p of z t given x t]
    E --> F[Compute Innovation and Likelihood]
    F --> G[Bayesian Update and Normalize]
    G --> H[Posterior Belief bel of x t]
    H --> I{Decision: Trigger Resampling or Grid Update?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Resample or Update Bins]
    I -- No --> K[Output bel of x t]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Store as New bel x t for Next Cycle]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cycle shows how belief *circulates* through the robot's perceptionŌĆōaction loop. The red dashed conceptual boundary between prior and posterior corresponds to the normalizer $\eta$ in the update equation.

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram ŌĆö Belief Representation Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Belief Representation Family] --> P[Parametric]
    R --> NP[Non Parametric]
    R --> H[Hybrid]
    P --> KF[Kalman Filter]
    P --> EKF[Extended Kalman Filter]
    P --> UKF[Unscented Kalman Filter]
    P --> IF[Information Filter]
    NP --> HF[Histogram Filter]
    NP --> PF[Particle Filter]
    PF --> MCL[Monte Carlo Localization]
    PF --> RB[Raoblackwellized Filter]
    H --> TG[Topological Grid]
    H --> GP[Gaussian Processes]
    KF --> EKF
    EKF --> UKF
```

## 4.3 Information Flow Diagram ŌĆö Multimodal Belief Evolution During Global Localization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State Space S Uniform Prior] --> S1[After First Sensor Reading Two Peaks Emerge]
    S1 --> S2[After Motion Command Peaks Shift and Spread]
    S2 --> S3[After Second Sensor Reading One Peak Dominates]
    S3 --> S4[Converged Unimodal Belief Locked Pose]
    S0 --- N0[Step 0 Initialization]
    S1 --- N1[Step 1 First Update]
    S2 --- N2[Step 2 Prediction]
    S3 --- N3[Step 3 Second Update]
    S4 --- N4[Step 4 Convergence]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each $S_i$ node represents the belief distribution's *shape* at a particular robot time step. This evolution from uniform ŌåÆ multimodal ŌåÆ unimodal is the **textbook signature of successful Monte-Carlo Localization**.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EKF Localization Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State Estimate mu and Sigma from Previous Step] --> B[Compute Motion Jacobian G t]
    B --> C[Propagate Mean mu bar equals g of u t and mu t-1]
    C --> D[Propagate Covariance Sigma bar equals G t Sigma t-1 G t trans plus R t]
    D --> E[Predicted State Prior]
    E --> F[Receive Measurement z t]
    F --> G[Compute Measurement Jacobian H t]
    G --> H[Compute Kalman Gain K t]
    H --> I[Innovation nu t equals z t minus h of mu bar t]
    I --> J[Update Mean mu t equals mu bar t plus K t nu t]
    J --> K[Update Covariance Sigma t equals I minus K t H t Sigma bar t]
    K --> L[Output Posterior Belief for Next Cycle]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Style]**

**Q:** Differentiate between the prior belief $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ and the posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$ in a Bayesian mobile robot localization context. Which one incorporates the most recent sensor measurement?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Prior $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | Posterior $\text{bel}(x_t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ |
| Information Used | Past measurements + **all** controls including $u_t$ | Past measurements + all controls + **latest $z_t$** |
| Computation Role | Output of **prediction** step | Output of **update** step |
| Uncertainty Trend | Higher (broadens due to motion noise) | Lower (sharpens due to sensor info) |
| Latest Measurement Included? | **No** | **Yes** |

The **posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$** incorporates the most recent sensor measurement $z_t$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q:** List any three non-parametric belief representations used in robot navigation. State one advantage and one limitation of the particle filter.

**Model Answer:**

Three non-parametric belief representations are:
1. **Histogram Filter** ŌĆö belief stored as discrete probability values over a fixed grid.
2. **Particle Filter** ŌĆö belief represented by a set of weighted samples (particles).
3. **Topological Representation** ŌĆö belief stored as a probability over discrete nodes/places.

**Particle Filter ŌĆö Advantage:** It can approximate *any* probability distribution (including highly multimodal, non-Gaussian forms) and handles non-linear system/measurement models without linearization.

**Particle Filter ŌĆö Limitation:** High computational cost (proportional to number of particles $M$) and susceptibility to **particle deprivation** when the true state lies in a low-probability region not covered by current samples.

---

> - **Do NOT confuse $\text{bel}$ and $\overline{\text{bel}}$ in the same answer** ŌĆö this is the single most frequent error in PERAT741 scripts and leads to direct loss of 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> - **In particle filter derivations, do NOT forget the importance-weight normalization** step $\sum w = 1$. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for "weights normalized to sum to 1".
> - **In EKF derivations, always specify the Jacobian $H_t$ and $G_t$ dimensions** in matrix terms (e.g., $2 \times 3$, $3 \times 3$ for a 2D pose state). Vague statements lose 1 mark.
> - **Provide units for noise parameters** ($Q_t$ in m┬▓, $R_t$ in m┬▓) ŌĆö many students omit this and lose 1 mark in the "neatness" category.

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice Option A

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Paper, PERAT741 Module 2]**

**Q:** Derive the recursive Bayes filter equations used to maintain a belief distribution in mobile robot localization. Using these equations, explain how a Kalman filter implements the same recursion under the assumption of Gaussian beliefs and linear-Gaussian models.

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Bayes Filter Derivation (7 Marks)

Derive the prediction and measurement update equations of the recursive Bayes filter, clearly stating the Markov assumption.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) ŌĆö Define belief**

The belief over the current state $x_t$ given all past data and controls is:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) ŌĆö Measurement update via Bayes' Rule**

Apply Bayes' theorem and separate the most recent measurement:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Markov Assumption (1 mark):** If $x_t$ is a complete summary of the past, then the measurement $z_t$ is conditionally independent of past data given $x_t$, i.e., $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, u_t)$.

This simplifies the numerator to $\eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, u_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$, where $\eta$ is the normalizer.

**Step 3 (2 marks) ŌĆö Prediction via ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov**

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) ŌĆö Recursion closed**

The two equations form a closed recursion: given $\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$ and a new control $u_t$, predict $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$, then upon receiving $z_t$, compute $\text{bel}(x_t)$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Kalman Filter as a Special Case (7 Marks)

Show that the Kalman filter equations follow when the belief is Gaussian and the models are linear-Gaussian.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption (2 marks):** Let the belief be Gaussian, $\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$, with linear-Gaussian motion and sensor models:

$$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

$$z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

**Prediction (2 marks):** Substituting the linear model into the Bayes prediction integral yields a Gaussian:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t, \; A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t)$$

Letting $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ and $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$.

**Update (2 marks):** Substituting into the Bayes update equation and applying the conditional Gaussian identity:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Hence the Kalman filter is a *closed-form, optimal* implementation of the Bayes filter under linear-Gaussian assumptions. For non-linear models, the **Extended Kalman Filter** linearizes via Jacobians $G_t, H_t$, giving the equations in Formula Sheet rows 7ŌĆō11.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice Option B

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Paper, PERAT741 Module 2]**

**Q:** With relevant equations and a clear diagram, explain the Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm as a particle-filter-based belief representation. Discuss the role of the motion model, the measurement model, and the resampling step. What is the "kidnapped robot problem" and how does MCL handle it gracefully?

#### Part (a) ŌĆö MCL Algorithm and Equations (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Belief Representation (1 mark):** MCL represents the belief by a set of $M$ weighted particles:

$$\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$$

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks total):**

1. **Initialization ($t = 0$):** Sample $M$ particles uniformly over the state space (or seeded from prior knowledge). Initialize $w_0^{[i]} = 1/M$.

2. **Motion Update (Prediction) ŌĆö 1 mark:** For each particle, sample a new pose from the motion model:
$$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$
This *propagates* particles according to the odometry command $u_t$.

3. **Measurement Update (Weighting) ŌĆö 1 mark:** For each particle, set the importance weight proportional to the likelihood of the actual measurement $z_t$:
$$w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$

4. **Normalization (1 mark):** $w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$.

5. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles from the discrete set with replacement, where $x_t^{[i]}$ is drawn with probability $w_t^{[i]}$. All weights reset to $1/M$.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** See SECTION 4.1 of these notes for the canonical Bayes-filter cycle, which MCL instantiates.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Resampling, Kidnapping, and Robustness (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Resampling Justification (2 marks):** Over time, importance weights become degenerate ŌĆö one particle has nearly all the mass while the rest contribute negligibly. The **Effective Sample Size** $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ quantifies this. When $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls below a threshold (e.g., $M/2$), resampling is triggered. This concentrates computational effort in the high-likelihood regions of the state space.

**Multimodality and Global Localization (2 marks):** A Gaussian (KF) cannot represent a belief that is "I might be in the kitchen OR the bedroom with equal probability". MCL naturally maintains this multimodal belief because particles cluster independently in *each* high-likelihood region. After sufficient sensor evidence, one cluster dominates ŌåÆ convergence.

**Kidnapped Robot Problem (2 marks):** The kidnapped robot is one that is suddenly teleported to a new, unknown location. A Kalman filter would fail because its Gaussian prior would never assign high probability to the new location. MCL handles it gracefully through **injection of random particles** (e.g., 1ŌĆō5% of $M$ particles uniformly re-sampled from the state space at each cycle). These "sentinel" particles ensure that if the true pose is in a low-probability region, some particles will eventually land there and attract the rest of the filter through their high measurement likelihood.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** MCL is robust, sample-efficient, and uniquely capable of multimodal global localization ŌĆö a clear advantage over parametric representations when operating in complex, ambiguous environments.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following 12 items the night before your KTU PERAT741 exam:**

- [ ] **Belief = Posterior:** $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$; **Bel-bar = Prior:** $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$.
- [ ] **Two-Step Recursion:** *Predict* with the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$, then *update* with the measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ and normalize by $\eta$.
- [ ] **Markov Assumption:** The state $x_t$ is a *complete sufficient statistic* of the past ŌĆö this is what makes the recursion tractable.
- [ ] **Gaussian Belief:** Fully described by mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$; closed-form Bayesian update via Kalman gain $K_t$.
- [ ] **EKF ŌēĀ KF:** EKF uses Jacobians $G_t, H_t$ to linearize non-linear models; KF assumes linear models. EKF is an *approximation*, not an exact Bayes filter.
- [ ] **Histogram Filter:** Belief is a piecewise-constant vector over a fixed grid; suffers curse of dimensionality.
- [ ] **Particle Filter:** Belief is a set of $M$ weighted samples; **importance weight** is $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) w_{t-1}^{[i]}$; resample when $N_{\text{eff}}$ drops.
- [ ] **MCL = Particle Filter for Localization:** Initializes uniformly for global localization; converges to unimodal after sufficient sensor updates.
- [ ] **Kidnapped Robot Recovery:** Inject a small fraction of random "sentinel" particles each cycle.
- [ ] **Information Filter:** Dual form using $\Omega = \Sigma^{-1}$ and $\xi = \Sigma^{-1} \mu$; useful for distributed multi-robot SLAM.
- [ ] **Modality Trade-off:** Gaussian = unimodal, compact, fast; Particle = multimodal, flexible, costly. Choose based on environment ambiguity.
- [ ] **Always normalize** the belief so that it integrates (continuous) or sums (discrete) to **1.0** ŌĆö otherwise the algorithm is mathematically invalid and will silently drift.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map Representation - Continuous Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Map Representation - Continuous Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Continuous Map Representation** is a paradigm in robot navigation wherein the environment is modelled as an **uninterrupted, infinitely resolvable geometric space** rather than as a collection of discrete, quantized cells. In this representation, every spatial feature (obstacle boundary, free-space edge, landmark) is encoded using **closed-form analytical primitives** ŌĆö typically parametric equations of points, line segments, polygons, arcs, splines, or smooth manifolds ŌĆö whose properties can be queried, differentiated, and integrated analytically at arbitrary resolution.

Mathematically, a continuous map $M_c$ of a planar workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is described as:

$$M_c = \{ P_i \,|\, P_i \in \mathcal{P}, \; i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \}$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the **primitive library** (points, lines, polygons, splines) and each $P_i$ carries a continuous parametric descriptor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Module 2:**
> Continuous representation explicitly contrasts with **grid/occupancy-grid (discrete)** representation. The examiner expects students to be able to (a) name the primitives, (b) explain when continuous is preferred, and (c) sketch at least two algorithms (e.g., Visibility Graph, Voronoi Diagram) used to build such maps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine two ways of describing the shape of India on paper:

1. **Discrete (Pixel) Map** ŌĆö You draw India using millions of tiny coloured squares on graph paper. You can only "see" India at the resolution of one square. Zoom in 1000├Ś and you still see squares.

2. **Continuous (Vector) Map** ŌĆö You describe India's boundary as a single smooth equation ŌĆö a chain of line segments and curves ŌĆö *"start at point A, go straight for 800 km to B, arc along a curve to CŌĆ”"* ŌĆö and you can compute the distance from any city to any other city to **infinite decimal precision** without ever needing a "pixel."

In robotics:
- The **pixel/graph-paper map** is the **occupancy grid** (discrete).
- The **chain-of-curve map** is the **continuous geometric map**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** A continuous representation stores the *mathematical description* of an obstacle's boundary (its geometry), not a sampled version of it. Memory is **O(n)** in the number of features, not **O(resolution┬▓)** as in grids.

### 1.3 Physical / Practical Constants & Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are routinely used in continuous representation problems:

- **Resolution-independent accuracy** ŌĆö Geometric queries have closed-form exact answers (up to floating-point precision, typically $\sim 10^{-15}$ in IEEE-754 double precision).
- **Storage complexity** ŌĆö $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for $n$ geometric primitives vs. $\mathcal{O}(r^2)$ for a grid of resolution $r$.
- **Typical workspace scale** ŌĆö Industrial mobile robots operate in workspaces of $10$ŌĆō$\mathbf{100}$ m on each side; surgical robots in $\mathbf{mm}$ŌĆō$\mathbf{cm}$ scale.
- **Standard primitive density** ŌĆö A typical office-floor continuous map may contain $50$ŌĆō$\mathbf{500}$ line segments describing walls, doors and furniture.
- **Coordinate frame** ŌĆö Usually the **World Frame** $\mathcal{F}_W$ (a global inertial reference), with sub-frames at each landmark $\mathcal{F}_{L_i}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Continuous Map Representations

Continuous maps in mobile robotics are categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Geometry Stored**
   - **Point-feature maps** ŌĆö Landmarks as $(x, y)$ or $(x, y, z)$.
   - **Line-feature maps** ŌĆö Walls as line segments $\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2)\}$.
   - **Polygon maps** ŌĆö Obstacles as closed polygons with vertex lists.
   - **Spline / curve maps** ŌĆö Smooth boundaries as B├®zier or B-spline curves.
   - **Implicit (level-set) maps** ŌĆö Surfaces defined by $f(x, y) = 0$.

2. **By Topological vs. Metric Content**
   - **Purely metric (geometric)** ŌĆö Exact coordinates of features.
   - **Purely topological** ŌĆö Connectivity graph (places = nodes, passages = edges).
   - **Hybrid topological-metric** ŌĆö Geometric maps stitched into a topological graph (most common in practice).

3. **By Uncertainty Handling**
   - **Deterministic continuous maps** ŌĆö Each primitive has fixed coordinates.
   - **Probabilistic continuous maps** ŌĆö Each primitive carries a covariance matrix $\Sigma_i$ (used in **EKF-SLAM**).

### 2.2 Standard Geometric Primitives (Building Blocks)

- **Point:** $P = (x, y)^T$
- **Line Segment:** $\mathcal{L} = \{\, P_1 + t(P_2 - P_1) \mid t \in [0, 1] \,\}$
- **Polygon (ordered vertex list):** $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$, with closing edge $v_k \to v_1$.
- **Circle:** $C = (c_x, c_y, r)$
- **Arc:** Subset of circle parameterised by angular interval $[\theta_a, \theta_b]$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Domain / Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Distance between two points | $d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Point-to-line distance (signed) | $d = \dfrac{(x_2 - x_1)(y_1 - y_0) - (x_1 - x_0)(y_2 - y_1)}{\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}}$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | Line segment parametric form | $\mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{a} + t(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}), \quad t \in [0,1]$ | dimensionless $t$ |
| 4 | Line-line intersection (2D) | Solve $\mathbf{a}_1 + t(\mathbf{b}_1 - \mathbf{a}_1) = \mathbf{a}_2 + s(\mathbf{b}_2 - \mathbf{a}_2)$ for $t, s$ | scalars |
| 5 | Cross product (parallel test) | $\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{w} = v_x w_y - v_y w_x$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | Polygon area (shoelace) | $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 7 | Point-in-polygon (ray cast) | Count edge crossings of a ray; **odd ŌćÆ inside** | Boolean |
| 8 | EKF landmark covariance update | $\Sigma_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H_k)\,\Sigma_{k \mid k-1}$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 9 | Voronoi edge condition | $\Vert P - O_i \Vert = \Vert P - O_j \Vert$ | metres (m) |
| 10 | Visibility graph edge validity | $\overline{P_i P_j} \cap O_k = \emptyset \;\; \forall k$ | Boolean |

### 2.4 Why and How of Continuous Representation

- **Why continuous?** Because real-world environments (walls, corridors) are *inherently* geometric. Storing them as exact lines/arcs preserves fidelity at any zoom level, uses dramatically less memory, and permits **closed-form planning** (e.g., shortest path, visibility checks).
- **How is it built?** Through sensor processing pipelines: extract features (laser scan ŌåÆ line segments via RANSAC/least-squares), match features across scans (ICP, data association), fuse into a global frame (EKF, pose-graph SLAM).
- **Engineering utility:** Autonomous cars (HD maps as polylines), indoor robots (floor plans as polygons), UAVs (3D point clouds fused to meshes) all rely on continuous representation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Point-to-Line-Segment Shortest Distance

Given a line segment $\overline{AB}$ with $A = (x_1, y_1)$, $B = (x_2, y_2)$ and a query point $P = (x_0, y_0)$. We derive the perpendicular distance from $P$ to the **infinite line**, then constrain to the **segment**.

**Step 1** ŌĆö Express the segment as a vector:
$$\vec{d} = B - A = (x_2 - x_1,\; y_2 - y_1)^T$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Express the vector from $A$ to $P$:
$$\vec{v} = P - A = (x_0 - x_1,\; y_0 - y_1)^T$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Compute the dot product $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{d}$:
$$\vec{v} \cdot \vec{d} = (x_0 - x_1)(x_2 - x_1) + (y_0 - y_1)(y_2 - y_1)$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Compute the squared length of $\vec{d}$:
$$\Vert \vec{d} \Vert^2 = (x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Find the scalar projection parameter $t$ of $P$ onto the infinite line:
$$t = \frac{\vec{v} \cdot \vec{d}}{\Vert \vec{d} \Vert^2}$$

**Step 6** ŌĆö Clamp $t$ to $[0, 1]$ so the projection lies on the **segment**:
$$t_{\text{clamp}} = \max(0,\; \min(1,\; t))$$

**Step 7** ŌĆö Compute the closest point $Q$ on the segment:
$$Q = A + t_{\text{clamp}}\,\vec{d}$$

**Step 8** ŌĆö The required perpendicular distance is then:
$$d_{\min} = \Vert P - Q \Vert = \sqrt{(x_0 - Q_x)^2 + (y_0 - Q_y)^2}$$

This derivation is **fundamental** for collision-checking algorithms in continuous-map planners.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Line-Segment to Line-Segment Intersection Test

Given two segments $\overline{P_1 P_2}$ and $\overline{P_3 P_4}$, we want a Boolean test for intersection in 2D.

**Step 1** ŌĆö Write both segments parametrically:
$$\mathbf{r}_1(s) = P_1 + s\,(P_2 - P_1), \quad s \in [0, 1]$$
$$\mathbf{r}_2(t) = P_3 + t\,(P_4 - P_3), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Set them equal (intersection hypothesis):
$$P_1 + s\,(P_2 - P_1) = P_3 + t\,(P_4 - P_3)$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Rearrange into a $2 \times 2$ linear system using the cross-product trick. Let
$$\mathbf{d}_1 = P_2 - P_1, \quad \mathbf{d}_2 = P_4 - P_3, \quad \mathbf{r} = P_3 - P_1$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö The denominator (determinant) is:
$$D = \mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2 = d_{1x}\,d_{2y} - d_{1y}\,d_{2x}$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Compute $s$ and $t$:
$$s = \frac{\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{d}_2}{D}, \qquad t = \frac{\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{d}_1}{D}$$

**Step 6** ŌĆö Segments intersect if and only if:
$$D \neq 0 \quad \text{(not parallel)} \;\;\text{AND}\;\; 0 \le s \le 1 \;\;\text{AND}\;\; 0 \le t \le 1$$

This is the **workhorse test** behind Visibility Graph and Voronoi-Diagram construction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Voronoi Edge Equation

For two obstacle sites $O_i$ and $O_j$, the **Voronoi edge** is the locus of points equidistant from both:

$$\Vert P - O_i \Vert = \Vert P - O_j \Vert$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö Square both sides:
$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = (x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Expand:
$$x^2 - 2x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 = x^2 - 2x x_j + x_j^2 + y^2 - 2y y_j + y_j^2$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Cancel $x^2 + y^2$ from both sides:
$$- 2x x_i + x_i^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 = - 2x x_j + x_j^2 - 2y y_j + y_j^2$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Collect $x$ and $y$ terms on one side:
$$2x(x_j - x_i) + 2y(y_j - y_i) = x_j^2 - x_i^2 + y_j^2 - y_i^2$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Factor the right side using $a^2 - b^2 = (a - b)(a + b)$:
$$2x(x_j - x_i) + 2y(y_j - y_i) = (x_j - x_i)(x_j + x_i) + (y_j - y_i)(y_j + y_i)$$

**Step 6** ŌĆö If $O_i \neq O_j$, divide by $2(x_j - x_i)$ (or use normal form) to get the **perpendicular bisector**:
$$(x_j - x_i)\,x + (y_j - y_i)\,y = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(x_j^2 - x_i^2) + (y_j^2 - y_i^2)\bigr]$$

This is a **straight line** ŌĆö proof that Voronoi edges between point obstacles are linear, hence trivially representable in a continuous map.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Continuous vs. Discrete Map Representations

| Attribute | Continuous (Geometric) | Discrete (Grid / Occupancy) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|
