For cell $i = 4$ (most likely outcome): $$\overline{bel}(x_1^{(4)}) = (0.0)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.8)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.0)(0.2)$$ $$\overline{bel}(x_1^{(4)}) = 0.0 + 0.02 + 0.16 + 0.02 + 0.0 = 0.20$$ For cell $i = 3$: $$\overline{bel}(x_1^{(3)}) = (0.1)(0.2) + (0.8)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.2) + (0.0)(0.2) + (0.0)(0.2) = 0.20$$ The predicted belief is still essentially uniform because motion alone provides no absolute reference. **Step 3 ŌĆö Measurement: Robot's range sensor reads $z_1 = 1$ m.** Sensor model $P(z_t = 1 \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$ ŌĆö the probability of measuring exactly $1$ m at each cell, assuming true distance to nearest wall: | Cell | True distance to nearest wall | $P(z_t = 1 \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | |------|------------------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | 0 m (wall adjacent) | 0.05 | | 2 | 1 m | 0.90 | | 3 | 2 m | 0.10 | | 4 | 1 m | 0.90 | | 5 | 0 m (wall adjacent) | 0.05 | **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Bayes Update (Unnormalized):** $$bel\_raw(x_1^{(i)}) = P(z_1 \mid x_1^{(i)}, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_1^{(i)})$$ | Cell $i$ | $\overline{bel}$ | $P(z_1 \mid x_1^{(i)})$ | $bel\_raw$ | |---------|-----------------|------------------------|------------| | 1 | 0.20 | 0.05 | 0.010 | | 2 | 0.20 | 0.90 | 0.180 | | 3 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.020 | | 4 | 0.20 | 0.90 | 0.180 | | 5 | 0.20 | 0.05 | 0.010 | **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Normalizer $\eta$:** $$\eta = \frac{1}{\sum_i bel\_raw(x_1^{(i)})} = \frac{1}{0.010 + 0.180 + 0.020 + 0.180 + 0.010} = \frac{1}{0.400} = 2.5$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Normalize:** $$bel(x_1) = [0.025, \, 0.450, \, 0.050, \, 0.450, \, 0.025]$$ **Interpretation:** The algorithm now believes the robot is in **cell 2 or 4** with 45% probability each. The ambiguity (symmetry of the corridor) cannot be resolved until another motion+measurement cycle breaks the symmetry ŌĆö a classic property of Markov Localization in symmetric environments. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Markov Localization vs. Variants | Property | Grid-based ML | Kalman Filter Localization | Monte Carlo Localization | |----------|--------------|-----------------------------|---------------------------| | Belief representation | Discrete grid | Single Gaussian | Set of weighted particles | | Multi-modal support | **Yes** | No (unimodal only) | **Yes** | | Handles global localization | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | | Handles kidnapped robot | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | | Computational complexity | $O(N)$ per step | $O(d^2)$ per step | $O(M)$ per step | | Memory footprint | **High** (entire grid) | **Low** (mean + covariance) | Medium ($M$ particles) | | Resolution | Bounded by grid size | Continuous | Approximates continuous | | Best use case | Small maps, teaching | Position tracking only | Large-scale real-world robots | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Markov Localization Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Time Step t Begins]) --> Init[Initialize Belief bel_x0 as Uniform 1 slash N] Init --> Loop{Loop While t less than T} Loop -->|Yes| GetU[Read Control Input u_t from Odometry] GetU --> Predict[Prediction Step Compute overline bel_x_t using Motion Model P x_t given u_t x_tminus1] Predict --> GetZ[Read Sensor Measurement z_t] GetZ --> Sensor[Compute Sensor Likelihood P z_t given x_t m for every cell] Sensor --> BayesRaw[Bayes Product Multiply Likelihood times Predicted Belief] BayesRaw --> Norm[Compute Normalizer eta as inverse of sum of raw beliefs] Norm --> Update[Normalize to obtain Posterior Belief bel_x_t] Update --> Estimate[Extract Pose Estimate arg max of bel_x_t] Estimate --> Inc[Increment t equals t plus 1] Inc --> Loop Loop -->|No| Stop([Output Trajectory of Beliefs]) ``` ### 4.2 Belief State Evolution Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InitPhase["Initialization"] A[Uniform Belief
bel_x0 equals 1 slash N
All cells equal probability] end subgraph PredPhase["After Prediction Step"] B[Shifted and Smeared Belief
overline bel_x1
Concentration near motion direction] end subgraph MeasPhase["After Measurement Update"] C[Sharp Multi-Modal Belief
bel_x1
Peaks at sensor-consistent poses] end subgraph ConvergePhase["After Several Cycles"] D[Converged Single Peak
Robot localized with high confidence] end A --> B B --> C C --> D ``` ### 4.3 Relationship Between Markov Localization and Its Variants ```mermaid flowchart TD Master[Markov Localization
General Bayes Filter Framework] --> V1[Grid-Based
Discrete belief over grid cells] Master --> V2[Kalman Filter Localization
Single Gaussian belief] Master --> V3[Monte Carlo Localization
Particle set belief] Master --> V4[Multi-Hypothesis Tracking
Multiple Gaussians belief] V1 -.->|Practical Limit| V3 V2 -.->|Cannot handle ambiguity| V3 V3 -.->|Modern Standard| App[Real Robot Deployment] ``` ### 4.4 Information Flow in the Recursive Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Known Map m
Static A Priori] --> SM[Sensor Model
P z_t given x_t m] SM --> UP[Update Step] OD[Odometry u_t] --> MM[Motion Model
P x_t given u_t x_tminus1] MM --> PR[Prediction Step] PREV[Previous Belief bel_x_tminus1] --> PR PR --> PB[Predicted Belief overline bel_x_t] PB --> UP UP --> NB[Normalized Belief bel_x_t] NB --> ST[Store as Previous Belief for Next Cycle] ST --> PR ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define Markov Localization. State the Markov assumption on which it is based.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Markov Localization is a probabilistic algorithm that estimates a robot's pose within a known map by recursively updating a probability distribution (belief) over all possible poses using sensor measurements and control inputs. The belief at time $t$ is $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. **(1 mark)** The algorithm is based on the **Markov Assumption**, which has two components: **(2 marks)** - **State Markov Assumption:** The next pose $x_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_{t-1}$ and the most recent control $u_t$, not on the full history. Formally: $P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$. - **Sensor Markov Assumption:** The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and the map $m$, not on the past. Formally: $P(z_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Differentiate between global localization and the kidnapped robot problem. How does Markov Localization handle both?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Global Localization:** The robot is placed at an unknown location and must determine where it is from scratch. The initial belief is a uniform distribution over the entire map. **(1 mark)** - **Kidnapped Robot Problem:** A more challenging variant where a robot that has already localized is suddenly teleported to an arbitrary new location mid-operation. The algorithm must detect this sudden discontinuity and re-initialize its belief. **(1 mark)** - **Markov Localization handles both** because its belief representation is inherently *multi-modal* ŌĆö it can simultaneously maintain high probability in multiple disconnected regions of the map. When kidnapping occurs, the old peak collapses (sensor disagreement), and a new peak emerges where sensor readings agree with the map. **(1 mark)** --- ### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Theory-Focused **Q3 (a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Derive the Markov Localization Bayes filter update equation from the definition of the belief, clearly stating every assumption used. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** We begin with the formal definition of the posterior belief: $$bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \quad \text{...(1)}$$ Applying Bayes' rule: $$bel(x_t) = \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ The denominator depends only on past data, so we replace it with a normalizer $\eta^{-1}$: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Applying the Sensor Markov Assumption:** Since the measurement depends only on the current pose and the static map: $$P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Applying the State Markov Assumption and Total Probability:** The predicted prior is obtained by marginalizing over all prior poses: $$P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \quad \text{...(5)}$$ Recognizing the previous belief in the integrand: $$P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1}) \quad \text{...(6)}$$ **Final Assembled Filter Equation:** $$\boxed{ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} } \quad \text{...(7)}$$ **Exam Tip:** Use **two clearly separated paragraphs** ŌĆö one for prediction derivation, one for update derivation. Examiners award partial marks generously if the logic flow is clear, even if the final algebra is not perfectly formatted. **(7 marks ŌĆö full distribution: 2 for Bayes, 2 for Markov assumptions, 2 for total probability, 1 for assembly and box)** --- **Q3 (b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Explain the three sub-problems of robot localization (position tracking, global localization, kidnapped robot). State which belief representation is needed for each and justify why. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Position Tracking (Local Localization):** **(2 marks)** The robot is given an approximate initial pose (e.g., from GPS or a known charging dock). The belief starts as a *narrow unimodal* distribution ŌĆö typically a Gaussian centered at the initial estimate. The algorithm merely tracks small subsequent motions. Because the belief is unimodal and remains so throughout, **any single-hypothesis representation suffices**, such as the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization. **2. Global Localization:** **(2.5 marks)** The robot does not know its initial position at all. The belief starts as a **uniform distribution** $bel(x_0) = \frac{1}{\vert X \vert}$ over the entire map. During operation, the belief becomes **multi-modal** as different hypotheses compete. A single Gaussian **cannot** represent multiple peaks; therefore, a **multi-hypothesis representation is mandatory**, such as a discrete grid (Grid-Based ML) or a set of weighted samples (Monte Carlo Localization / Particle Filter). **3. Kidnapped Robot Problem:** **(2.5 marks)** A robot that has already localized is suddenly teleported to a new arbitrary location. The true pose jumps discontinuously, while the robot's belief momentarily remains peaked at the *old* location. The sensor model then produces a strong mismatch, causing the belief to collapse, and the algorithm must re-converge at the new location. This requires: - A representation capable of **sudden re-initialization** (e.g., injecting random particles in MCL). - The ability to represent a **uniform prior** at any moment. Only multi-modal representations like Grid-based ML or MCL can solve this; EKF cannot. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Numerical / Applied-Focused **Q4 (a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] A robot operates in a discretized 1-D environment with 4 cells $\{A, B, C, D\}$. The initial belief is uniform: $bel(x_0) = [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]$. The robot executes control $u_1 = $ "move right" with motion model:** | $P(x_1 \mid u_1, x_0)$ | A | B | C | D | |--------------------------|---|---|---|---| | **From A** | 0.0 | 0.8 | 0.2 | 0.0 | | **From B** | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.8 | 0.2 | | **From C** | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 1.0 | | **From D** | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 1.0 | **The sensor then measures $z_1 = $ "wall at 1 m". The sensor likelihood is $P(z_1 \mid x_1) = [0.05, 0.90, 0.10, 0.05]$. Compute the posterior belief $bel(x_1)$. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Prediction (4 marks):** For each cell $i$, compute $\overline{bel}(x_1^{(i)}) = \sum_j P(x_1^{(i)} \mid u_1, x_0^{(j)}) \cdot bel(x_0^{(j)})$. For cell A: $\overline{bel}(A) = (0.0)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) = 0.0$ For cell B: $\overline{bel}(B) = (0.8)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) = 0.20$ For cell C: $\overline{bel}(C) = (0.2)(0.25) + (0.8)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) + (0.0)(0.25) = 0.05 + 0.20 = 0.25$ For cell D: $\overline{bel}(D) = (0.0)(0.25) + (0.2)(0.25) + (1.0)(0.25) + (1.0)(0.25) = 0.0 + 0.05 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.55$ Predicted belief: $\overline{bel}(x_1) = [0.00, \, 0.20, \, 0.25, \, 0.55]$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Bayes Update (Unnormalized) (1.5 marks):** $$bel\_raw(A) = 0.05 \times 0.00 = 0.000$$ $$bel\_raw(B) = 0.90 \times 0.20 = 0.180$$ $$bel\_raw(C) = 0.10 \times 0.25 = 0.025$$ $$bel\_raw(D) = 0.05 \times 0.55 = 0.0275$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Normalizer (0.5 marks):** $$\eta = \frac{1}{0.000 + 0.180 + 0.025 + 0.0275} = \frac{1}{0.2325} \approx 4.301$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Normalized Posterior Belief (1 mark):** $$bel(x_1) = [0.000, \, 0.774, \, 0.108, \, 0.118]$$ **Verification:** $0.000 + 0.774 + 0.108 + 0.118 = 1.000$ Ō£ō **Interpretation:** The robot is most likely in cell B (77.4%) because the sensor reading "wall at 1 m" strongly agrees with cell B's true distance, combined with the motion evidence that the robot moved rightward. --- **Q4 (b). [KTU University Exam - May 2024] A robot uses Grid-based Markov Localization in a 6├Ś6 m room discretized into 36 cells of 1 m ├Ś 1 m each. If the robot executes 5 localization cycles per second, and each cycle requires evaluating the motion model for 36 cells and the sensor model for 4 range readings, estimate the worst-case number of floating-point operations per second. Discuss the scaling limitation. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Operations per Cycle (2 marks):** - Motion model evaluation: 36 cells ├Ś (4 multiply-adds for 4-neighbor transition) Ōēł 144 ops - Sensor model evaluation: 36 cells ├Ś 4 readings ├Ś (1 distance + 1 Gaussian lookup) Ōēł 288 ops - Bayes product + normalize: 36 multiplies + 36 divides + 36 adds Ōēł 108 ops - **Total per cycle Ōēł 540 ops** **Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply by Cycle Rate (1 mark):** At 5 Hz: $540 \times 5 = 2700$ ops/sec. This is trivially small for a 6├Ś6 m room. **Step 3 ŌĆö Scale to Real-World Map (2 marks):** For a realistic warehouse of $100 \times 100$ m with 0.1 m resolution: - Number of cells $N = (1000)^2 = 10^6$ - Per cycle: $10^6 \times (4 + 4 + 1) \approx 9 \times 10^6$ ops - At 10 Hz: $9 \times 10^7$ ops/sec This exceeds the capability of simple microcontrollers and motivates the use of **Monte Carlo Localization** with $M \approx 100$ŌĆō$1000$ particles instead of $N = 10^6$ grid cells. **Step 4 ŌĆö Discuss Limitation (2 marks):** Grid-based ML has **$O(N)$ complexity per cycle**, where $N$ is the total number of cells. Doubling the map resolution (halving cell size) **quadruples** $N$, making the algorithm intractable for large or high-resolution maps. This is why modern robotics almost universally uses **particle-filter-based MCL**, which has $O(M)$ complexity with $M \ll N$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In derivation questions, students frequently **omit the normalizer $\eta$** or **forget to state the Markov assumptions explicitly**. Marks are specifically allocated for naming these assumptions. Also, in numerical problems, students often forget to **verify the belief sums to 1** after normalization ŌĆö this verification step is worth at least 1 mark in KTU's structured valuation key. Always box your final answer and explicitly state the units of any numerical quantity. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Markov Localization is a recursive Bayes filter for estimating robot pose $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ in a known map. - **Two Assumptions (memorize verbatim):** State Markov ($x_t$ depends only on $x_{t-1}$ and $u_t$) and Sensor Markov ($z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ and $m$). - **Two Phases per Cycle:** Prediction (motion model, convolves belief with $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$) then Update (multiplies by sensor likelihood and normalizes). - **Initial Belief:** Uniform $bel(x_0) = 1/\vert X \vert$ for global localization; tight Gaussian for position tracking. - **Master Equation:** $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$. - **Discrete Form:** Replace integral with a sum over all grid cells. - **Three Sub-Problems:** Position tracking, global localization, kidnapped robot ŌĆö only multi-hypothesis representations handle the latter two. - **Normalizer Formula:** $\eta = [\sum_i P(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t^{(i)})]^{-1}$. - **Complexity:** Grid-based ML is $O(N)$ per cycle; this scaling issue is the primary motivation for MCL. - **Real-World Use:** Waymo, Amazon AMRs, Roomba, NASA Mars rovers. - **Examiner Loves:** Asking you to derive the update equation, compute a small numerical grid example, or compare representations.

topics.title: Kalman Filter Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Kalman Filter Localization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Kalman Filter Localization** is a recursive, optimal state estimation technique used in mobile robotics to estimate a robot's pose (position and orientation) by fusing **noisy sensor measurements** with an **inaccurate motion model** in real time. It is the foundational probabilistic algorithm behind GPS navigation, autonomous vehicles, drones, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The Kalman Filter is an *optimal linear quadratic estimator* (LQE) that infers the posterior probability distribution of a Gaussian state variable by combining two sources of information: (1) a **prediction** derived from the robot's control inputs / motion model, and (2) a **measurement correction** derived from external sensor observations (e.g., GPS, LIDAR, odometry). It is mathematically optimal under the assumptions of **linear systems** and **Gaussian noise**. The filter operates on the principle that the true state $x_t$ of a system cannot be measured directly; instead, we maintain a **belief** $\text{bel}(x_t)$ over possible states, represented as a multivariate **Gaussian distribution** with mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Kalman"?** > Named after **Rudolf Emil K├Īlm├Īn** (1930ŌĆō2016), a Hungarian-American electrical engineer and mathematician who published his seminal paper *"A New Approach to Linear Filtering and Prediction Problems"* in 1960. The same algorithm powers the Apollo Guidance Computer, the Space Shuttle, and modern cell-phone IMUs. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Foggy Mountain Hike" Imagine you are hiking up a mountain in **dense fog**. You cannot see more than 1 meter ahead. | Source of Information | What it tells you | Uncertainty (Noise) | |---|---|---| | Your **step counter + compass** (Dead reckoning) | "I walked 1.2 km heading North-East" | Drift over time, slips, wind | | Your **altimeter** (GPS-like sensor) | "Based on satellites, you are at altitude 820 m" | Atmospheric interference, multi-path | - **Motion model alone** = you drift further and further from your true location (like a balloon losing its string). - **GPS alone** = each reading has random scatter (jumpy dots on a map). - **Kalman Filter** = a smart algorithm that **trusts the motion model for short distances** but **corrects it whenever a sensor reading arrives**, producing a smooth, accurate trajectory that is *better than either source alone*. > [!TIP] > **The Golden Rule of Kalman Filtering:** > Predict with the motion model ŌåÆ Measure with sensors ŌåÆ Fuse them using their *uncertainties*. Trust the prediction when sensors are unreliable; trust the sensors when the model is drifting. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology for the KTU Board Exam | Term | Notation | Meaning | |---|---|---| | State vector | $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | True pose (e.g., $[x, y, \theta]^T$) | | Control input | $u_t$ | Commanded motion $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta$ | | Observation | $z_t$ | Sensor measurement at time $t$ | | Process noise | $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | Uncertainty in motion model | | Measurement noise | $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | Uncertainty in sensor reading | | Belief mean | $\mu_t$ | Best estimate of state | | Belief covariance | $\Sigma_t$ | Uncertainty ellipse around $\mu_t$ | | Kalman Gain | $K_t$ | Adaptive weighting factor (sensor vs. model) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Phase Recursive Cycle The Kalman Filter for localization cycles between two phases, repeating at every discrete time step $t$: ### **Phase 1: PREDICTION (Time Update)** The robot applies its motion model to project the prior belief forward in time, **before** any new sensor data arrives. 1. Take the previous posterior mean $\mu_{t-1}$. 2. Apply the deterministic control $u_t$ through the state-transition function $g(\cdot)$. 3. Add zero-mean Gaussian noise with covariance $Q_t$ (control noise). 4. Propagate uncertainty through the linearized Jacobian $G_t$. ### **Phase 2: MEASUREMENT UPDATE (Correction)** A new sensor observation $z_t$ arrives and "pulls" the prediction toward reality. 1. Predict what the sensor **should have seen** using the observation function $h(\cdot)$. 2. Compute the **innovation** (residual) = actual reading ŌłÆ predicted reading. 3. Compute the Kalman Gain $K_t$: a ratio of *prediction uncertainty* to *total uncertainty*. 4. Fuse the innovation into the predicted mean, scaled by $K_t$. 5. Shrink the covariance ŌĆö confidence increases after a good measurement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark these two phases in your exam answer:** A full-marks answer must clearly separate the **Predict** block from the **Update** block. Examiners look for the words *a priori* ($\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$) and *a posteriori* ($\mu_t, \Sigma_t$). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Kalman Filter Localization > [!NOTE] > Use this table as the **last-page revision sheet** during exams. Every entry below is a high-yield item that has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU university exams. | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Prior mean (prediction) | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | $\bar{\mu}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | | 2 | Prior covariance (prediction) | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + Q_t$ | $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ | | 3 | Predicted observation | $\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ | $\hat{z}_t \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | | 4 | Innovation (measurement residual) | $y_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ | $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | | 5 | Innovation covariance (S) | $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t$ | $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$ | | 6 | Kalman Gain (K) | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$ | $K_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ | | 7 | Posterior mean (correction) | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t y_t$ | $\mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | | 8 | Posterior covariance (correction) | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | | 9 | Log-likelihood of observation | $\log p(z_t \mid x_t) \propto -\tfrac{1}{2} y_t^T S_t^{-1} y_t$ | Scalar score | | 10 | EKF linearization | $G_t \approx \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}, \;\; H_t \approx \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ | Jacobians | | 11 | Process noise (odometry) | $Q_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2, \sigma_\theta^2)$ | $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ | | 12 | Measurement noise (GPS) | $R_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_{\text{gps},x}^2, \sigma_{\text{gps},y}^2)$ | $R \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall ŌĆö Matrix dimensions:** > In the localization context, **$n = 3$** (x, y, ╬Ė) and **$k = 2$** for a 2-D GPS sensor. Writing $G_t$ and $H_t$ with **wrong dimensions** ($G$ is $3 \times 3$, $H$ is $2 \times 3$, $K$ is $3 \times 2$) is the most common reason for losing 2 marks in derivations. --- ## 2.3 Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why This Matters Beyond Exams | Domain | Application | Sensor(s) Fused | |---|---|---| | Autonomous Cars (Tesla, Waymo) | Lane-level pose tracking | IMU + GPS + LIDAR + Camera | | Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance) | Visual odometry on rocky terrain | IMU + Stereo Camera + Wheel encoders | | Drones (DJI, Parrot) | Hover stabilization, GPS-denied flight | IMU + Barometer + Optical Flow | | Subsea AUVs (REMUS, Bluefin) | Underwater navigation without GPS | DVL + INS + Terrain matching | | Cell phones | Step counting + location | Accelerometer + Gyro + Wi-Fi fingerprint | | Surgical Robots (Da Vinci) | Tool tip tracking | Encoders + Vision | The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** is the workhorse for non-linear robotics. The **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** and **Particle Filter** are advanced alternatives that handle strong non-linearities (e.g., kidnap-recovery, multi-modal posteriors). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Update Equations ### **Step 0 ŌĆö The Starting Assumption (Bayesian Foundation)** The robot's belief about its pose is a **Gaussian distribution**. The goal of localization is to compute the posterior: $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$$ We apply Bayes' rule and the Markov assumption (current state depends only on previous state and current inputs): $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ Because the prior $p(x_{t-1})$ is Gaussian and the motion/measurement models are linear with additive Gaussian noise, the resulting posterior is **also Gaussian**, and we can solve it in closed form. --- ### **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Linear Motion & Measurement Models** $$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$ $$z_t = C_t x_t + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$ Here $A_t, B_t, C_t$ are the linear transition matrices and $w_t, v_t$ are zero-mean, mutually independent Gaussian noises. --- ### **Step 2 ŌĆö Derive the Prior Mean $\bar{\mu}_t$ (Prediction Step)** Take the expectation of $x_t$ conditioned on all data up to time $t-1$: $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{\mu}_t &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + w_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}\right] \\ &= A_t \mathbb{E}[x_{t-1}] + B_t u_t + \mathbb{E}[w_t] \\ &= A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic row:** The expectation is linear; the noise has zero mean so it vanishes; the control $u_t$ is known deterministically. --- ### **Step 3 ŌĆö Derive the Prior Covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t$** Define the prediction error $\bar{e}_t = x_t - \bar{\mu}_t = A_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t$. Covariance of a sum of independent terms: $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{\Sigma}_t &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\bar{e}_t \, \bar{e}_t^T\right] \\ &= A_t \mathbb{E}\!\left[(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})^T\right] A_t^T + \mathbb{E}[w_t w_t^T] \\ &= A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + Q_t \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic row:** The covariance of $A e + w$ is $A \Sigma A^T + Q$ because $e$ and $w$ are uncorrelated. --- ### **Step 4 ŌĆö Derive the Innovation and its Covariance** The **innovation** measures how much the sensor disagrees with the prediction: $$y_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t = C_t(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) + v_t$$ $$ \begin{aligned} S_t &= \mathbb{E}[y_t y_t^T] = C_t \mathbb{E}[(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T] C_t^T + \mathbb{E}[v_t v_t^T] \\ &= C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Step 5 ŌĆö Derive the Kalman Gain $K_t$** The optimal $K_t$ is the one that minimizes the **posterior covariance trace** (sum of variances). Setting $\partial \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)/\partial K_t = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Sigma_t &= (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t C_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T \\ \frac{\partial \,\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)}{\partial K_t} &= -2 \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + 2 K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Solving: $$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t)^{-1} = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T S_t^{-1}$$ > **Logic row:** This compact matrix form is the "geometric mean" between the prior uncertainty $\bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T$ and the sensor's accuracy $S_t^{-1}$. --- ### **Step 6 ŌĆö Derive the Posterior Mean and Covariance** $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t) \\ \Sigma_t &= (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic row:** The mean shifts by a *scaled* innovation; the covariance *shrinks* (or stays the same) ŌĆö never grows ŌĆö after a measurement update. This is the **information-gathering** property of the filter. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Board Practice) **Problem:** A 1-D mobile robot uses odometry + GPS to localize. At $t = 0$, it believes it is at $x = 0$ m with variance $\Sigma = 4 \text{ m}^2$. It moves $u = 5$ m (noisy, $Q = 1$). The GPS reads $z = 5.5$ m with $R = 2$. Compute $\mu_1, \Sigma_1$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Predict:} \quad \bar{\mu}_1 &= \mu_0 + u = 0 + 5 = 5.0 \text{ m} \\ \bar{\Sigma}_1 &= \Sigma_0 + Q = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Innovation:} \quad y_1 &= z - \bar{\mu}_1 = 5.5 - 5.0 = 0.5 \text{ m} \\ \text{Kalman Gain:} \quad K_1 &= \frac{\bar{\Sigma}_1}{\bar{\Sigma}_1 + R} = \frac{5}{5 + 2} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714 \\ \text{Update:} \quad \mu_1 &= \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 y_1 = 5.0 + 0.714 \times 0.5 = 5.357 \text{ m} \\ \Sigma_1 &= (1 - K_1)\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 0.286 \times 5 = 1.43 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Belief moved from **5.0 m** toward **5.5 m** by 71.4% ŌĆö closer to GPS than to odometry, because GPS had a tighter uncertainty ($R = 2 < \bar{\Sigma}_1 = 5$). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö KF vs EKF vs UKF vs PF | Property | Linear KF | Extended KF | Unscented KF | Particle Filter | |---|---|---|---|---| | Handles non-linearity | ŌØī No | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Linearized (1st order) | Ō£ģ Sigma-point (2nd order) | Ō£ģ Fully non-linear | | Posterior representation | Single Gaussian | Single Gaussian | Single Gaussian | Multi-modal samples | | Computational cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | | Tunable parameters | $Q, R$ | $Q, R$ | $\alpha, \beta, \kappa$ | #particles $M$ | | KTU exam relevance | **Highest** | **Highest** | Medium | Low | | Fails on kidnap-recovery | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Kalman Filter Recursive Loop (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Time t-1: posterior mu_t-1, Sigma_t-1]) --> pred1[/Apply control u_t/] pred1 --> pred2[Predict mean: mu_bar_t = g mu_t-1, u_t] pred2 --> pred3[Predict covariance: Sigma_bar_t = G_t Sigma_t-1 G_t^T + Q_t] pred3 --> meas1{New sensor reading z_t?} meas1 -->|No| waitNode[Wait for next step] waitNode --> startNode meas1 -->|Yes| upd1[Predict measurement: z_hat_t = H mu_bar_t] upd1 --> upd2[Innovation: y_t = z_t - z_hat_t] upd2 --> upd3[Innovation cov: S_t = H Sigma_bar_t H^T + R_t] upd3 --> upd4[Kalman Gain: K_t = Sigma_bar_t H^T S_t^-1] upd4 --> upd5[Posterior mean: mu_t = mu_bar_t + K_t y_t] upd5 --> upd6[Posterior cov: Sigma_t = I - K_t H Sigma_bar_t] upd6 --> outputNode([Publish mu_t, Sigma_t to controller]) outputNode --> startNode ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Sensor Fusion in a Mobile Robot ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph sensorLayer["SENSOR LAYER"] imuNode[IMU - Gyroscope, Accelerometer] wheelNode[Wheel Encoders - Odometry] gpsNode[GPS Receiver - Position fix] lidarNode[LIDAR - Range scans] end subgraph preProc["PRE-PROCESSING"] noiseModNode[Noise models: Q_t motion, R_t measurement] jacobNode[Jacobian computation: G_t, H_t] end subgraph kalmanCore["KALMAN FILTER CORE"] predBlock[Prediction Block: mu_bar, Sigma_bar] updBlock[Update Block: K_t, mu_t, Sigma_t] end subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"] poseNode[Robot Pose mu_t: x, y, theta] uncNode[Uncertainty Sigma_t: 3x3 covariance ellipse] ctrlNode[Feedback to Motion Controller] end imuNode --> noiseModNode wheelNode --> noiseModNode gpsNode --> noiseModNode lidarNode --> noiseModNode noiseModNode --> jacobNode jacobNode --> predBlock predBlock --> updBlock updBlock --> poseNode updBlock --> uncNode poseNode --> ctrlNode ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic ŌĆö Uncertainty Evolution Across Cycles ```mermaid flowchart LR t0["t=0
Sigma = 4"] -->|Predict + Q| t1p["t=1 predict
Sigma = 5"] t1p -->|Update with z| t1u["t=1 update
Sigma = 1.43"] t1u -->|Predict + Q| t2p["t=2 predict
Sigma = 2.43"] t2p -->|Update with z| t2u["t=2 update
Sigma smaller"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **zig-zag pattern** is the visual signature of a healthy Kalman Filter: covariance *grows* during prediction (drift) and *shrinks* during update (correction). If your covariance monotonically increases, the filter is diverging ŌĆö typically due to mismatched $R$ or stale data. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** **Define the Kalman Filter. List its two main recursive steps.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Kalman Filter is an **optimal recursive Bayesian estimator** for linear dynamic systems corrupted by Gaussian noise. It maintains a Gaussian belief over the robot's state and updates it at every time step. > > The two main steps are: > 1. **Prediction (Time Update):** Projects the prior belief forward using the motion model $g(\cdot)$ and control $u_t$, producing a *prior* $\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$. > 2. **Measurement Update (Correction):** Fuses a sensor observation $z_t$ with the prior to produce an improved *posterior* $\mu_t, \Sigma_t$ via the Kalman Gain $K_t$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** **What is the physical meaning of the Kalman Gain $K_t$? What happens when $K_t \to 0$ and $K_t \to 1$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Kalman Gain $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ is a weighting matrix that determines how much the filter should **trust the sensor reading** relative to the **motion-based prediction**. > > - **$K_t \to 0$** (when $R_t$ is very large / sensor very noisy): The filter ignores the measurement and relies almost entirely on the motion model. $\mu_t \approx \bar{\mu}_t$. > - **$K_t \to 1$** (when $R_t \to 0$ / sensor is nearly perfect): The filter discards the prediction and adopts the sensor reading directly. $\mu_t \approx z_t$. --- ## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means you answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### ŌØō QUESTION A ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a) Derive the Kalman Filter measurement update equations starting from the linear measurement model and the minimization of the posterior covariance trace. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the linear models.** [1 mark] $$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$ $$z_t = C_t x_t + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Define the predicted observation.** [1 mark] $$\hat{z}_t = C_t \bar{\mu}_t, \quad \text{innovation } y_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Posterior form (linear fusion).** [1 mark] $$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$ $$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute and expand $\Sigma_t$.** [1 mark] $$ \begin{aligned} \Sigma_t &= (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t(I - K_t C_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T \\ &= \bar{\Sigma}_t - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T K_t^T + K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t) K_t^T \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Minimize the trace w.r.t. $K_t$.** [1 mark] Setting $\partial \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)/\partial K_t = 0$: $$-2 \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + 2 K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t) = 0$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $K_t$ and simplify.** [2 marks] $$ \begin{aligned} K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t) &= \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T \\ K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t)^{-1} \\ K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T S_t^{-1}, \quad \text{where } S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + R_t \end{aligned} $$ Substituting back, the posterior covariance simplifies to the **Joseph form**: $$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$ --- **(b) A differential-drive robot is localized with a 2-D Kalman Filter (state $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$). Initial belief: $\mu_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_0 = \text{diag}(2, 2, 0.1)$. The robot moves with $u = (1.0, 0.0, 0.5)$ and odometry noise $Q = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25, 0.05)$. Compute $\bar{\mu}_1$ and $\bar{\Sigma}_1$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the motion model (simplified, $A = I$, $B = I$).** [1 mark] For a small discrete step, the kinematic model approximates $x_t \approx x_{t-1} + u_t$: $$g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) = \mu_{t-1} + u_t$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the predicted mean.** [2 marks] $$ \bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the predicted covariance with $G_t = I$.** [3 marks] $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{\Sigma}_1 &= G_1 \Sigma_0 G_1^T + Q = I \cdot \Sigma_0 \cdot I + Q \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.25 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.15 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Physical interpretation.** [1 mark] The uncertainty has grown from (2, 2, 0.1) to (2.25, 2.25, 0.15) because no measurement correction was applied ŌĆö the robot is **less certain** of its pose after motion than before. This growth will be reversed when the next GPS or landmark observation arrives in the update step. --- ### ŌØō QUESTION B ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Alternative Module Question]** **(a) Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). Why is it needed for non-linear robot localization? Show the linearization step mathematically. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Motivation for EKF.** [2 marks] The standard Kalman Filter requires linear motion and measurement models: $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + w_t$, $z_t = C_t x_t + v_t$. However, real robot models are **non-linear**: $$x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t, \qquad z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$$ Example: A unicycle robot has the non-linear model $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$. Direct application of the linear KF yields biased estimates. **Step 2 ŌĆö The EKF idea: local linearization.** [2 marks] The EKF **linearizes** $g(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ about the current estimate using a first-order **Taylor expansion**. The non-linearity is approximated locally by a tangent plane (Jacobian). **Step 3 ŌĆö Mathematical linearization.** [2 marks] $$ \begin{aligned} G_t &= \left.\frac{\partial g(x_{t-1}, u_t)}{\partial x_{t-1}}\right|_{x = \mu_{t-1}} \quad (3 \times 3 \text{ matrix}) \\ H_t &= \left.\frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t}\right|_{x = \bar{\mu}_t} \quad (k \times n \text{ matrix}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Adapted prediction & update equations.** [1 mark] $$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t), \qquad \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + Q_t$$ $$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$ $$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)), \qquad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$ --- **(b) For a unicycle robot, derive the Jacobian $G_t$ of the motion model $g(x_{t-1}, u_t) = (x + v\Delta t \cos\theta, \; y + v\Delta t \sin\theta, \; \theta + \omega\Delta t)^T$ w.r.t. the state. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the motion model explicitly.** [1 mark] $$g(x, y, \theta) = \begin{bmatrix} x + v \Delta t \cos\theta \\ y + v \Delta t \sin\theta \\ \theta + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate each row w.r.t. $(x, y, \theta)$.** [3 marks] $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial x} &= 1, & \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial y} &= 0, & \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial \theta} &= -v\Delta t \sin\theta \\ \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial x} &= 0, & \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial y} &= 1, & \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial \theta} &= +v\Delta t \cos\theta \\ \frac{\partial g_3}{\partial x} &= 0, & \frac{\partial g_3}{\partial y} &= 0, & \frac{\partial g_3}{\partial \theta} &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Assemble the Jacobian $G_t$.** [2 marks] $$ G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & +v\Delta t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate at $\mu_{t-1} = (x_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \theta_{t-1})$.** [1 mark] $$ G_t \big|_{\mu_{t-1}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ 0 & 1 & +v\Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ > This matrix is the cornerstone of EKF-based EKF-SLAM (used in ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono on drones). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the sign** in $G_t$ derivatives ŌĆö $\partial(\cos\theta)/\partial\theta = -\sin\theta$. Carries **ŌłÆ2 marks** if wrong. > 2. **Mixing up $Q$ and $R$** ŌĆö $Q$ is the *process* (motion) noise; $R$ is the *measurement* (sensor) noise. Swapping them silently inverts the filter's behavior. > 3. **Not showing the trace-minimization step** in $K_t$ derivation ŌĆö earns **0 of 2 marks** for the gain formula unless the optimality condition is justified. > 4. **Omitting units** in numerical problems ŌĆö even if the numbers are right, missing "$m$" or "$m^2$" loses **0.5 mark** in KTU valuation. > 5. **Writing $G_t G_t^T$ instead of $G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T$** ŌĆö a very common shorthand mistake that costs full covariance marks. --- ## ­¤¦Ā TOPIC RECAP ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] Kalman Filter = **Recursive Bayesian estimator** assuming Gaussian noise and (for KF) linear models. - [x] Two phases: **PREDICT** (motion model ŌåÆ $\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$) and **UPDATE** (sensor ŌåÆ $K_t, \mu_t, \Sigma_t$). - [x] **Kalman Gain** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ is the optimal weighting between model and sensor. - [x] **Predict** grows uncertainty; **Update** shrinks it ŌåÆ characteristic **zig-zag** pattern in covariance plots. - [x] **EKF** handles non-linear models by linearizing with **Jacobians** $G_t, H_t$ at each step. - [x] Process noise $Q$ comes from odometry/motor imperfections; measurement noise $R$ comes from sensor specs (e.g., GPS $\pm 3$ m ŌåÆ $R \approx 9$). - [x] **Filter diverges** if $Q$ is too small (filter overconfident) or $R$ is too small (filter over-trusts a bad sensor). - [x] **Joseph form** $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T$ guarantees numerical symmetry. - [x] **Innovation** $y_t = z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t$ is the *residual* ŌĆö drive Kalman consistency checks from this. - [x] Used in: **GPS receivers**, **Apollo Guidance Computer**, **Mars rovers**, **autonomous cars**, **drones**, **AUVs**, **smartphone AR**. - [x] Limitation: only **unimodal** posteriors; cannot recover from **kidnapping** (use Particle Filter instead).

topics.title: Application to Mobile Robots: Kalman Filter Localization - Schematic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application to Mobile Robots: Kalman Filter Localization - Schematic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Kalman Filter Localization** is a recursive, optimal state estimation technique used by a mobile robot to estimate its **pose** (position and orientation) by fusing **noisy sensor measurements** with an **uncertain motion model**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, it is treated as the canonical solution to the **continuous localization problem** under the assumption of **Gaussian noise** and **linear (or linearized) system dynamics**.

Mathematically, the Kalman Filter maintains a **belief** $\text{bel}(x_t)$ about the robot's state $x_t$ as a multivariate Gaussian distribution characterized by a mean vector $\mu_t$ and a covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The schematic is the *gateway concept* for understanding any advanced filter (EKF, UKF, Particle Filter). Mastery of the **PredictŌĆōMeasureŌĆōUpdate** cycle is mandatory for the ESE Module 2 questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Blind Navigator in Fog"

Imagine you are driving through thick fog. You cannot see the road directly, but you have:
- A **speedometer** (control input $u_t$ ŌĆö *"I pressed the accelerator for 2 seconds"*).
- A **GPS unit** (measurement $z_t$ ŌĆö *"The device says I am 50 m east of the start"*).

Both of these are *noisy*. The speedometer reading drifts, and the GPS jumps around. You, the driver, mentally *predict* where you should be based on speed, then *correct* this prediction using the GPS. Over time, your estimate becomes more confident. This mental fusion of **prediction + correction** is exactly what a Kalman Filter does for a mobile robot. The schematic captures this two-step dance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Kalman Filter is not a single equation ŌĆö it is a **recursive cycle** (loop) of two phases: **Prediction (Time Update)** and **Correction (Measurement Update)**.

### 1.3 Why Use a Kalman Filter for Mobile Robot Localization?

| Property | Benefit to Mobile Robot |
|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Minimizes mean-squared error of state estimate under Gaussian noise |
| **Recursiveness** | Only needs previous state ŌĆö no need to store entire history |
| **Computational Efficiency** | $O(k^3)$ where $k$ is state dimension ŌĆö real-time feasible on-board |
| **Fusion Capability** | Natively combines multiple heterogeneous sensors (IMU, odometry, GPS, LiDAR) |
| **Uncertainty Quantification** | Output covariance $\Sigma_t$ is a direct measure of localization confidence |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Phase Schematic ŌĆö Operational Logic

The Kalman Filter Localization schematic is built upon the **Probabilistic Generative Law** of the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) of robot navigation:

$$P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This single integral governs the entire **Predict** phase. Let us break it down into structured operational steps.

#### Phase 1 ŌĆö Prediction (Time Update)

The robot **propagates** its belief forward in time using only the **control input** $u_t$ and the **motion model** $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.

- **Why?** Because between two sensor measurements, the robot has moved, and we must account for this motion even without new observations.
- **How?** The previous Gaussian is transformed through the (possibly non-linear) motion function. In the **linear** case, this is a simple matrix multiplication.

#### Phase 2 ŌĆö Correction (Measurement Update)

The robot **corrects** its predicted belief using the latest sensor measurement $z_t$ and the **measurement model** $P(z_t \mid x_t)$.

- **Why?** Sensors are our only window to the external world (e.g., landmark observations, GPS fixes). Without correction, prediction alone diverges due to drift.
- **How?** The predicted Gaussian is "multiplied" (via Bayes' rule) with the measurement likelihood ŌĆö which is also Gaussian ŌĆö to yield a sharper posterior Gaussian.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** you must memorize for ESE Module 2.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **State Prediction (Mean)** | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \, \mu_{t-1} + B_t \, u_t$ | $A_t \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$ state transition matrix |
| 2 | **Covariance Prediction** | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, A_t^\top + R_t$ | $R_t$ = process (motion) noise covariance |
| 3 | **Predicted Measurement** | $\hat{z}_t = C_t \, \bar{\mu}_t$ | $C_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ measurement matrix |
| 4 | **Innovation (Residual)** | $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t = z_t - C_t \, \bar{\mu}_t$ | Difference between actual and expected measurement |
| 5 | **Innovation Covariance** | $S_t = C_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^\top + Q_t$ | $Q_t$ = measurement noise covariance |
| 6 | **Kalman Gain** | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^\top \, S_t^{-1}$ | $K_t \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$ ŌĆö the fusion weight |
| 7 | **Posterior Mean (Update)** | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t$ | Final state estimate after correction |
| 8 | **Posterior Covariance (Update)** | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t \, C_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Always smaller than $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ (uncertainty shrinks) |
| 9 | **Log-Likelihood (for data association)** | $\log L = -\tfrac{1}{2} \nu_t^\top S_t^{-1} \nu_t - \tfrac{1}{2}\log \vert S_t \vert - \tfrac{m}{2}\log(2\pi)$ | Used by KTU problems on landmark matching |
| 10 | **Bilinear Interpolation (belief grid)** | $P(x \mid y) = (1-\vert x-x_1 \vert) P(x_1) + \vert x-x_1 \vert P(x_2)$ | For discrete grid-based KF (Thrun Burgard Fox, Ch. 4) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip:** The Kalman Gain $K_t$ is the *heart* of the filter. It tells the robot **"how much to trust the sensorŌĆØ** versus **ŌĆ£how much to trust the prediction.ŌĆØ** When sensor noise $Q_t \to 0$, $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ (full trust in sensor). When $Q_t \to \infty$, $K_t \to 0$ (ignore sensor, use prediction).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, Kalman Filter Localization underpins:
- **GNSS/INS fusion** in autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Mobileye).
- **SLAM front-ends** where it provides the odometry backbone before loop closure.
- **Drone stabilization** in GPS-denied environments (indoor quadcopters with optical flow).
- **Spacecraft attitude estimation** in interplanetary missions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Gain $K_t$

The Kalman Gain is *not* assumed ŌĆö it is **derived** by minimizing the **posterior trace of covariance** $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$, which is equivalent to minimizing the mean-squared error of the state estimate.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Begin with the posterior covariance after applying a generic linear correction.**

We assume the corrected state takes the form:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$

where $K_t$ is some arbitrary gain matrix we want to optimize.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the posterior covariance in terms of $K_t$.**

Substitute the measurement equation $z_t = C_t x_t + \epsilon_t$ (where $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$) into the corrected mean:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (C_t x_t + \epsilon_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$

The error $e_t = x_t - \mu_t$ between the true state and the estimate is:

$$e_t = x_t - \bar{\mu}_t - K_t C_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) - K_t \epsilon_t$$

$$\begin{aligned}
e_t &= (I - K_t C_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) - K_t \epsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

The posterior covariance is $\Sigma_t = E[e_t \, e_t^\top]$. Since $x_t - \bar{\mu}_t$ and $\epsilon_t$ are zero-mean and independent:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t C_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, (I - K_t C_t)^\top + K_t Q_t K_t^\top
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expand and collect terms in $K_t$.**

Using the identity $(I - K_t C_t)^\top = I - C_t^\top K_t^\top$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t + K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top + K_t Q_t K_t^\top \\
&= \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t + K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t) K_t^\top
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$ with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero.**

Recall the matrix differentiation rules:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial X} \text{tr}(X A) = A^\top$
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial X} \text{tr}(X A X^\top B) = B X A + B^\top X A^\top$

Applying these to $\Sigma_t$ with $S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)}{\partial K_t} &= -2 \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + 2 K_t S_t = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $K_t$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_t S_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top \\
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **optimal Kalman Gain** that minimizes the posterior mean-squared error.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Simplified Posterior Covariance (Joseph Form)

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute the optimal $K_t$ back into the expanded $\Sigma_t$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t + K_t S_t K_t^\top \\
&= \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t + \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top \\
&= \bar{\Sigma}_t - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t \\
&= (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}$$

The third and fourth terms cancel exactly because $K_t S_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top$. This is the **standard posterior covariance update** used in every textbook.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

A differential-drive robot moves with linear velocity $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$ for $\Delta t = 1 \text{ s}$. Let the state be $x = [p_x, p_y, \theta]^\top$, with $A_t = I_3$, $B_t = [\Delta t \cos\theta, \Delta t \sin\theta, 0]^\top$, $R_t = \text{diag}(0.04, 0.04, 0.01)$, prior $\mu_{t-1} = [0, 0, 0]^\top$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0.1)$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Predict the mean.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_t &= A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t = [0,0,0]^\top + [0.5, 0, 0]^\top = [0.5, 0, 0]^\top
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Predict the covariance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\Sigma}_t &= A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0.1) + \text{diag}(0.04, 0.04, 0.01) \\
&= \text{diag}(1.04, 1.04, 0.11)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Receive a GPS measurement $z_t = [0.6, 0.1]^\top$ with $C_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25)$.**

Innovation:
$$\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t = [0.6 - 0.5, \; 0.1 - 0]^\top = [0.1, 0.1]^\top$$

Innovation covariance:
$$\begin{aligned}
S_t &= C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1.04 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.04 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.29 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.29 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Compute the Kalman Gain.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1} = \text{diag}(1.04, 1.04, 0.11) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/1.29 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/1.29 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.806 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.806 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 11 ŌĆö Update the mean.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = [0.5, 0, 0]^\top + [0.0806, 0.0806, 0]^\top = [0.5806, 0.0806, 0]^\top
\end{aligned}$$

The robot now believes it is at **(0.58, 0.08)** m ŌĆö a healthy fusion of the dead-reckoning prediction (0.5, 0) and the noisy GPS fix (0.6, 0.1).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The PredictŌĆōUpdate Cycle (Core KF Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Initial Belief mu0 Sigma0] --> pred1
    ctrl[Control Input ut] --> pred1
    pred1[Predict Mean: mu_bar = A mu_prev + B ut] --> pred2
    pred2[Predict Cov: Sigma_bar = A Sigma_prev A_T + R] --> meas
    sens[Sensor Measurement zt] --> innov
    pred2 --> innov
    pred1 --> innov
    innov[Innovation: nu = zt - C mu_bar] --> kal
    innov --> innovC
    pred2 --> innovC
    sens --> innovC
    innovC[Innovation Cov: S = C Sigma_bar C_T + Q] --> kal
    kal[Kalman Gain: K = Sigma_bar C_T S_inv] --> upd1
    innov --> upd1
    upd1[Update Mean: mu = mu_bar + K nu] --> upd2
    kal --> upd2
    upd2[Update Cov: Sigma = I - K C Sigma_bar] --> out
    out[Posterior Belief mu Sigma] --> pred1
    out --> output1[Published Robot Pose]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Mobile Robot KF Localization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensors
        IMU[IMU Gyroscope]
        ODO[Wheel Odometry]
        GPS[GPS Receiver]
        LID[LiDAR Landmark]
    end
    subgraph Motion_Model
        MM[State Transition A B]
        MN[Process Noise R]
    end
    subgraph KF_Engine
        P[Predict Block]
        U[Update Block]
        K[Kalman Gain Computer]
    end
    subgraph Output
        POSE[Robot Pose mu]
        CONF[Confidence Sigma]
    end
    IMU --> MM
    ODO --> MM
    MM --> P
    MN --> P
    P --> K
    GPS --> U
    LID --> U
    K --> U
    P --> U
    U --> POSE
    U --> CONF
    POSE --> P
```

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Notes

- **Loops in the diagram** represent the **recursiveness** of the algorithm ŌĆö the output of step $t$ becomes the input of step $t+1$.
- **Two arrows into the Update block** signify the two information sources: (a) predicted belief from the motion model, and (b) raw sensor measurement.
- **The Kalman Gain block** sits as a *gatekeeper* ŌĆö it adaptively weights the prediction vs. the measurement based on their respective uncertainties $R$ and $Q$.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** schematic ŌĆö covered in subsequent modules ŌĆö the matrices $A$ and $C$ are replaced by **Jacobians** $F_x$ and $H_x$ of the non-linear motion and measurement functions respectively.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

*State the **two phases** of the Kalman Filter localization cycle and write the equation for the **Kalman Gain** $K_t$. Why is $K_t$ called the "fusion weight"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two phases are:

1. **Prediction (Time Update):** The robot's belief is propagated using the motion model:
$$\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t, \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t$$

2. **Correction (Measurement Update):** The belief is refined using the sensor measurement:
$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t), \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$

The Kalman Gain is:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$$

It is called the *fusion weight* because it determines how much the predicted state is corrected by the new measurement ŌĆö it automatically balances trust between the motion model (uncertainty $R_t$) and the sensor (uncertainty $Q_t$). When $Q_t$ is small, $K_t$ is large (high sensor trust); when $R_t$ is small, $K_t$ is small (high motion-model trust).

*(Award 1 mark for phase names, 1 mark for equations, 1 mark for interpretation of fusion weight.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

*What is the **innovation** $\nu_t$ in a Kalman Filter? Show how its covariance $S_t$ is used to validate a measurement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The innovation $\nu_t$ is the difference between the actual sensor reading $z_t$ and the measurement predicted by the robot's belief $\bar{\mu}_t$:

$$\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$$

It is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable with covariance:

$$S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t$$

**Measurement validation:** Under the $\chi^2$ (chi-squared) test, a measurement is accepted as valid if:

$$\nu_t^\top S_t^{-1} \nu_t \le \chi^2_{m, \, \alpha}$$

where $m$ is the measurement dimension and $\alpha$ is the significance level (typically $0.05$). If the test fails, the measurement is treated as an outlier (e.g., wrong landmark association) and discarded.

*(Award 1 mark for $\nu_t$ definition, 1 mark for $S_t$, 1 mark for chi-squared gating.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the **optimal Kalman Gain** $K_t$ by minimizing the trace of the posterior covariance $\Sigma_t$. Clearly state the matrix differentiation identities used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot with state $x_t = [p_x, p_y]^\top$ has prior belief $\mu_{t-1} = [1, 2]^\top$ and $\Sigma_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$. It moves with control $u_t = [0.3, 0.4]^\top$ and $A_t = I_2$, $B_t = I_2$, $R_t = 0.1 I_2$. It then receives a measurement $z_t = [1.5, 2.3]^\top$ with $C_t = I_2$ and $Q_t = 0.2 I_2$. Compute $\mu_t$ and $\Sigma_t$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $K_t$ (7 marks):**

Step 1 ŌĆö Assume the corrected state has the form $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$.

Step 2 ŌĆö Express the posterior error $e_t = x_t - \mu_t$ and compute the posterior covariance $\Sigma_t = E[e_t e_t^\top]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t C_t)^\top + K_t Q_t K_t^\top \\
&= \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top K_t^\top - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t + K_t S_t K_t^\top
\end{aligned}$$

where $S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t$.

Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$ with respect to $K_t$ using:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial X} \text{tr}(XA) = A^\top$
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial X} \text{tr}(X A X^\top B) = B X A + B^\top X A^\top$

Setting the derivative to zero:

$$-2 \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + 2 K_t S_t = 0 \implies K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1}$$

**Award 2 marks for the assumed form, 3 marks for the expanded $\Sigma_t$, 2 marks for the differentiation and final expression.**

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 ŌĆö Predict the mean:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t = [1, 2]^\top + [0.3, 0.4]^\top = [1.3, 2.4]^\top$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Predict the covariance:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t = 0.5 I_2 + 0.1 I_2 = 0.6 I_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the innovation:

$$\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t = [1.5 - 1.3, \; 2.3 - 2.4]^\top = [0.2, -0.1]^\top$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $S_t$:

$$S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t = 0.6 I_2 + 0.2 I_2 = 0.8 I_2$$

Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $K_t$:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1} = 0.6 I_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot (0.8 I_2)^{-1} = 0.6 / 0.8 \cdot I_2 = 0.75 I_2$$

Step 6 ŌĆö Update the mean:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = [1.3, 2.4]^\top + 0.75 \cdot [0.2, -0.1]^\top = [1.45, 2.325]^\top$$

Step 7 ŌĆö Update the covariance:

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t = (1 - 0.75) \cdot 0.6 I_2 = 0.15 I_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.15 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.15 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_t = [1.45, 2.325]^\top$ and $\Sigma_t = 0.15 I_2$.

*(Award 1 mark each for the 7 steps above.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Draw the **schematic block diagram** of a Kalman Filter localization system for a mobile robot. Label all inputs, outputs, and the two operational phases. Explain the function of the **Kalman Gain block** in the diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **chi-squared (Mahalanobis) gating test** for measurement validation in Kalman Filter localization. How is the threshold related to the probability of a false alarm? Show with an example: given $S_t = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ and innovation $\nu_t = [2, 0]^\top$, decide whether to accept the measurement at a 95% confidence level. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and Kalman Gain (7 marks):**

*(The student should reproduce the schematic from Section 4.1 of these notes, showing the Predict block, Update block, and the recursive loop. The Kalman Gain block is shown as receiving $\bar{\Sigma}_t$, $C_t$, $S_t$ as inputs and feeding into the state-update equation.)*

The **Kalman Gain block** performs the following critical functions in the schematic:
1. Computes $S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t$ ŌĆö the predicted measurement covariance.
2. Computes $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1}$ ŌĆö the optimal blending weight.
3. Multiplies $K_t$ with the innovation $\nu_t$ to produce the **correction term** added to the predicted mean.
4. Provides the multiplier $(I - K_t C_t)$ used to shrink the posterior covariance.

It is the **adaptive bridge** between the noisy prediction and the noisy measurement, ensuring the posterior has minimum variance.

*(Award 3 marks for the diagram, 4 marks for the explanation of $K_t$'s role.)*

---

**(b) Chi-squared gating test (7 marks):**

The Mahalanobis distance of the innovation is:

$$d^2 = \nu_t^\top S_t^{-1} \nu_t$$

Under the null hypothesis that the measurement is consistent with the robot's belief, $d^2$ follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $m$ degrees of freedom (where $m$ is the dimension of $z_t$). The measurement is **accepted** if:

$$d^2 \le \chi^2_{m, \, 1 - \alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the false-alarm probability. For $m = 2$ and 95% confidence, $\chi^2_{2, \, 0.95} = 5.991$.

**Example computation:**

$$S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad d^2 = [2, 0] \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2.0$$

Since $d^2 = 2.0 < 5.991$, **the measurement is accepted** at the 95% confidence level.

*(Award 2 marks for theory, 2 marks for threshold definition, 3 marks for the numerical example.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose on $A_t$:** Many students write $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t + R_t$. This is **wrong** ŌĆö the second term must be $A_t^\top$ (transpose). Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_t$ and $Q_t$:** $R_t$ is the **process** (motion) noise, $Q_t$ is the **measurement** (sensor) noise. Swapping them inverts the filter's behavior. Loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 3. **Sign error in innovation:** The innovation is $z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$, **not** $\bar{\mu}_t - z_t$. The Kalman Filter formula is $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$ ŌĆö a sign flip will yield a divergent estimate.
> 4. **Skipping the recursion loop:** In schematic questions, students often draw the Predict and Update blocks but forget to draw the **arrow from Update back to Predict**. This loses the "recursive" marks.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always state units for velocity ($\text{m/s}$), distance ($\text{m}$), angle ($\text{rad}$), and covariance ($\text{m}^2$, $\text{rad}^2$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ The Kalman Filter is a **two-phase recursive estimator**: **Predict** (motion) and **Update** (measurement).
- Ō£ģ Belief is maintained as a **multivariate Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ ŌĆö this is the *fundamental assumption*.
- Ō£ģ The **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ is the optimal *fusion weight* that minimizes posterior variance; it is **not** a tunable parameter ŌĆö it is computed, not chosen.
- Ō£ģ The **innovation** $\nu_t$ measures the *surprise* of the measurement; its covariance $S_t$ is used for outlier rejection via the $\chi^2$ test.
- Ō£ģ Posterior covariance $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$ is **always $\le \bar{\Sigma}_t$** in the matrix sense ŌĆö measurements *always reduce* uncertainty (in the linear-Gaussian case).
- Ō£ģ For **non-linear** systems, the same schematic applies, but $A_t \to F_x$ (Jacobian of motion) and $C_t \to H_x$ (Jacobian of measurement) ŌĆö this is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.
- Ō£ģ **Memorize the 10 formulas** in the cheat sheet (Section 2.2); they appear verbatim in 70% of KTU ESE problems.
- Ō£ģ In schematic diagrams, always show the **loop from Update ŌåÆ Predict** to demonstrate recursiveness.
- Ō£ģ The filter's optimality is **conditional** on Gaussian noise ŌĆö for highly non-Gaussian noise, switch to a **Particle Filter** (Module 3).
- Ō£ģ The **trace of $\Sigma_t$** quantifies the *volume of uncertainty*; minimizing it = minimizing the sum of variances = the filter's optimality criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Position Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Position Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics and the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PERAT741 ŌĆō Robot Navigation**, **Robot Position Prediction** is formally defined as:

> **Robot Position Prediction** is the computational process of estimating a mobile robot's future state (position, orientation, and associated uncertainty) at time step *t* from its prior state at time step *t-1*, by applying a **probabilistic motion model** conditioned on the executed control input and elapsed time, in the absence (or prior to the fusion) of exteroceptive sensor observations.

Mathematically, it computes the predictive prior (or *belief*) of the robot state:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

where:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the robot state (typically $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ in 2-D)
- $u_t$ is the control input applied between *t-1* and *t*
- $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is the **state transition probability** (motion model)
- $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ is the predicted belief **before** incorporating sensor measurements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Position Prediction is the **first half** of the recursive Bayes Filter. The complete filter alternates between **Prediction** (motion update) and **Correction** (measurement update). In Module 2, the focus is exclusively on the **Prediction step**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a large, unfamiliar room** and asked: *"Where will you be in 5 seconds?"* You have only two pieces of information: (a) where you are *right now*, and (b) how fast you are walking and which way you are facing.

You cannot *see* the room, so you **predict** your future position by mentally integrating your walking velocity vector over 5 seconds, rotating it by your current heading. The result is a "best guess" ŌĆö but you are not 100 % certain, because your foot may slip, or you may drift slightly sideways.

> **Robot Position Prediction is exactly this ŌĆö but with rigorous mathematics.**

The robot's **encoders / IMU** provide its velocity and heading (the *control input*). The motion model mathematically integrates these over a small time step $\Delta t$ to predict the new pose. As time progresses without external correction (like a GPS fix or landmark observation), the *spread* (uncertainty) of the robot's belief about its position **grows larger** ŌĆö this is the famous **"uncertainty propagation"** phenomenon.

### 1.3 Core Constituents of a Position Prediction System

| Constituent | Physical / Mathematical Role | KTU Standard Symbol |
|-------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Robot State Vector** | 2-D pose: Cartesian position + heading | $x_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t]^T$ |
| **Control Input** | Commanded velocities or odometric increments | $u_t = [v_t, \omega_t]^T$ |
| **Motion Model** | Probabilistic transition function | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ |
| **Process Noise** | Uncertainty in actuation/slippage | $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ |
| **Belief / Estimate** | Probability distribution over robot pose | $bel(x_t)$ |
| **Covariance Matrix** | Quantifies estimation uncertainty | $P_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| **Sampling Period** | Discrete time step for integration | $\Delta t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Notation:** The hat symbol denotes an estimate (e.g., $\hat{x}_t$), the bar denotes a *predicted* quantity before measurement fusion (e.g., $\bar{x}_t, \bar{P}_t$), and the tilde denotes a noisy/measured value (e.g., $\tilde{v}_t$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition ŌĆö The Four Pillars of Position Prediction

A complete KTU-grade treatment of robot position prediction is built upon four interlocking pillars:

#### Pillar I ŌĆö Kinematic State Propagation (Deterministic Core)

The **ideal (noise-free) motion model** propagates the robot's state through a nonlinear function $g(\cdot)$:

$$x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

For a **differential-drive mobile robot** with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$:

$$x_{t} = x_{t-1} + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega \cdot \Delta t}{2})$$

$$y_{t} = y_{t-1} + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega \cdot \Delta t}{2})$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} + \omega \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition ŌĆö The Half-Angle Trick:** The midpoint angle $\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ is used because the robot's heading changes continuously during the interval, so the *average* direction of travel lies between the start and end headings. This midpoint rule is a **second-order accurate approximation** (Trapezoidal Rule) of the exact integral and is the KTU-preferred form.

#### Pillar II ŌĆö The Two Canonical Motion Models

**Model 1 ŌĆö Velocity-Based Motion Model:**

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + (v + \varepsilon_1) \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_{t-1} + (\omega + \varepsilon_2) \cdot \Delta t)$$

**Model 2 ŌĆö Odometry-Based Motion Model:**

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2})$$

where $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ are obtained from wheel encoder integration over the interval $[t-1, t]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** The **velocity model** uses controls $u = [v, \omega]$, which are *intended* actions ŌĆö they suffer from **actuator mismatch** and **slippage**. The **odometry model** uses $\Delta s, \Delta \theta$, which are *measured* actions ŌĆö they are typically **more accurate in the short term** because they reflect what actually happened (Thrun, Burgard, Fox ŌĆö *Probabilistic Robotics*, KTU reference framing).

#### Pillar III ŌĆö Uncertainty Propagation (Covariance Update)

Because the motion model is **nonlinear**, the covariance update is performed by **linearization** via the Jacobian matrices, yielding the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction equations**:

$$\bar{x}_t = g(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{P}_t = G_x \cdot P_{t-1} \cdot G_x^T + G_u \cdot R_t \cdot G_u^T$$

where $G_x = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg|_{\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t}$ and $G_u = \frac{\partial g}{\partial u}\bigg|_{\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t}$ are the **Jacobians of the motion function** w.r.t. state and control, and $R_t$ is the process-noise covariance.

#### Pillar IV ŌĆö Why the Uncertainty Grows ŌĆö The "Cone of Uncertainty"

A pure-prediction (open-loop, no measurement correction) trajectory exhibits **monotonically growing covariance**. Visually, the robot's belief "smears" into an ever-expanding ellipse, whose area is proportional to the **trace** of $P_t$:

$$\text{Uncertainty Area} \propto \sqrt{\det(P_t)}$$

The rate of growth depends on the magnitude of process noise $R_t$ and the eigenvalues of the linearized dynamics $G_x$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | When to Use |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $x_t = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos\theta$ | Position update, x-axis | meters (m) | Differential-drive kinematics |
| 2 | $y_t = y_{t-1} + v\Delta t \sin\theta$ | Position update, y-axis | m | Differential-drive kinematics |
| 3 | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t$ | Heading update | radians (rad) | All holonomic/non-holonomic bases |
| 4 | $x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2})$ | Odometric x-update | m | Odometry motion model |
| 5 | $y_t = y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2})$ | Odometric y-update | m | Odometry motion model |
| 6 | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta$ | Odometric heading update | rad | Odometry motion model |
| 7 | $\bar{x}_t = g(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Nonlinear state prediction | state units | EKF / Bayes filter |
| 8 | $\bar{P}_t = G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T$ | Covariance prediction | state-units$^2$ | EKF prediction step |
| 9 | $G_x = \partial g / \partial x$ | State Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | EKF linearization |
| 10 | $G_u = \partial g / \partial u$ | Control Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ | EKF linearization |
| 11 | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | State transition probability | $\text{prob. density}$ | Bayes filter / particle filter |
| 12 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Predictive belief integral | dimensionless | Recursive Bayes filter |
| 13 | $\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$ | Average wheel displacement | m | Differential-drive odometry |
| 14 | $\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$ | Differential rotation | rad | Differential-drive odometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table symbol $\vert$ usage note:** For quantities like absolute value $\vert x \vert$ inside text bodies, use $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. All math above is rendered cleanly in display blocks.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Robot position prediction underpins virtually every deployed mobile robot:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs):** The AV's **localization stack** uses EKF prediction to "dead-reckon" between GPS/LiDAR updates, typically at **50ŌĆō100 Hz**.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Amazon Kiva / Locus robots rely on wheel-odometry-based prediction between landmark observations.
- **Drones (UAVs):** The **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** is a continuous position predictor using IMU integration.
- **Underwater ROVs / AUVs:** Where GPS is unavailable, dead-reckoning is the *only* motion-based prediction available.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity):** Use **visual odometry prediction** during long traverses between SLAM-corrected map fixes.

> **Engineering caveat:** Without correction from exteroceptive sensors, position prediction *always* diverges from the true pose ŌĆö this is why the full Bayes filter *fuses* prediction with measurement (Chapter 3 of the KTU syllabus).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Position Update for a Differential-Drive Robot (Midpoint Integration)

**Problem Setup:** A two-wheeled differential-drive robot has state $x_{t-1} = [x_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \theta_{t-1}]^T$ and applies control $u_t = [v, \omega]^T$ (linear and angular velocity in the body frame) for a duration $\Delta t$. Derive the next state $x_t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the body-frame velocity in the world frame.**

The robot's linear velocity vector in the body frame is $[v, 0]^T$. Rotating this into the world frame by the heading $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{x}^{world} &= v \cos\theta \\
v_{y}^{world} &= v \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recognize the time-dependence of $\theta$.**

Because the robot is also rotating with angular velocity $\omega$, its heading is a *function of time* within the interval:

$$\theta(\tau) = \theta_{t-1} + \omega \cdot \tau, \quad \tau \in [0, \Delta t]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the exact integral for $x_t$.**

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \int_0^{\Delta t} v \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \omega \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral analytically.**
