Let $u = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\tau$, then $du = \omega \, d\tau$, so $d\tau = du/\omega$. When $\tau = 0$, $u = \theta_{t-1}$; when $\tau = \Delta t$, $u = \theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega} \int_{\theta_{t-1}}^{\theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t} \cos(u) \, du \\
    &= x_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega} \Big[ \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t) - \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the trigonometric identity** $\sin(A) - \sin(B) = 2 \cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega} \cdot 2 \cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö For small $\omega\Delta t$** (typical case, $\le 0.1$ rad), use $\sin(\alpha) \approx \alpha$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö By symmetry, the $y$-update follows identically:**

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + v\Delta t \sin\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö The heading update is exact (no approximation needed):**

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t$$

> **Sanity check:** If $\omega = 0$ (pure straight-line motion), the formulas reduce to $x_t = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1}$ and $y_t = y_{t-1} + v\Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1}$ ŌĆö the familiar straight-line kinematics.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Jacobians for the EKF Prediction Step

We require the **state Jacobian** $G_x$ and **control Jacobian** $G_u$ of the motion function $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$. Let $c = \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2})$ and $s = \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2})$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the motion function explicitly as a vector:**

$$
g(x, u) = \begin{bmatrix} x + v\Delta t \cos(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) \\ y + v\Delta t \sin(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) \\ \theta + \omega\Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate each component w.r.t. $x = [x, y, \theta]^T$ to obtain $G_x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial x} &= 1, \quad \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial \theta} = -v\Delta t \sin(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) = -v\Delta t \cdot s \\
\frac{\partial g_2}{\partial x} &= 0, \quad \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial \theta} = \; v\Delta t \cos(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) = \; v\Delta t \cdot c \\
\frac{\partial g_3}{\partial \theta} &= 1, \quad \text{(all others 0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
G_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t \cdot s \\ 0 & 1 & \; v\Delta t \cdot c \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate each component w.r.t. $u = [v, \omega]^T$ to obtain $G_u$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial v} &= \Delta t \cos(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) = \Delta t \cdot c, \quad \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial \omega} = -\frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} \sin(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) = -\frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} s \\
\frac{\partial g_2}{\partial v} &= \Delta t \sin(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) = \Delta t \cdot s, \quad \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial \omega} = \frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} \cos(\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) = \frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} c \\
\frac{\partial g_3}{\partial v} &= 0, \quad \frac{\partial g_3}{\partial \omega} = \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
G_u = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta t \cdot c & -\frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} s \\ \Delta t \cdot s & \frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} c \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble the EKF covariance prediction equation:**

$$\bar{P}_t = G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T$$

where $R_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_v^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\omega^2 \end{bmatrix}$ is the **process-noise covariance**, with $\sigma_v, \sigma_\omega$ being the standard deviations of velocity and angular-velocity noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $\frac{\Delta t^2}{2}$ factor in $G_u$?** It arises from the chain rule applied to the *midpoint angle* $\theta + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}$, which depends on $\omega$. This factor is the single most common place where students make sign or coefficient errors in the KTU valuation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Odometry-Based Prediction from Wheel Encoders

**Problem Setup:** A differential-drive robot with wheel separation $b$ and wheel radius $r$ reports encoder counts $\Delta n_r$ and $\Delta n_l$ over interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the wheel displacements:**

$$\Delta s_r = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{\Delta n_r}{N}, \qquad \Delta s_l = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{\Delta n_l}{N}$$

where $N$ is the encoder resolution (counts per revolution).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Average translation and differential rotation:**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}, \qquad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the odometric update (midpoint form):**

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Define the odometric control input:**

$$u_t^{odom} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s \\ \Delta \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **measured** control input. It is preferable to the velocity-based input in practice because it implicitly captures wheel slip (the *measured* $\Delta s_r$ is reduced if the right wheel slipped).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Numerical Type)

> **Problem (KTU Numerical Pattern):** A differential-drive robot is at $x_{t-1} = [1.0 \text{ m}, \; 2.0 \text{ m}, \; \pi/4 \text{ rad}]^T$. It applies $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$ and $\omega = 0.2 \text{ rad/s}$ for $\Delta t = 0.1 \text{ s}$. Compute the predicted state $\bar{x}_t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the midpoint angle:**

$$\theta_{mid} = \theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{0.2 \times 0.1}{2} = 0.7854 + 0.01 = 0.7954 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate trigonometric functions:**

$$\cos(0.7954) = 0.7010, \quad \sin(0.7954) = 0.7131$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $v\Delta t$:**

$$v\Delta t = 0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Update position:**

$$x_t = 1.0 + 0.05 \times 0.7010 = 1.0351 \text{ m}$$

$$y_t = 2.0 + 0.05 \times 0.7131 = 2.0357 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Update heading:**

$$\theta_t = \frac{\pi}{4} + 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.7854 + 0.02 = 0.8054 \text{ rad}$$

**Final Predicted State:**

$$\bar{x}_t = [1.0351, \; 2.0357, \; 0.8054]^T$$

> **Verification by comparison with the exact integral (Derivation 1, Step 5):** $x_t = 1.0 + \frac{0.5}{0.2}[\sin(0.8054) - \sin(0.7854)] = 1.0 + 2.5 \times [0.7213 - 0.7071] = 1.0 + 0.0355 = 1.0355$ m. The midpoint approximation error is $< 0.05\%$ ŌĆö well within KTU numerical tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Robot Position Prediction

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **complete position prediction pipeline**, including state, control, motion model, Jacobian evaluation, and covariance update ŌĆö exactly as required by the EKF prediction step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Prior State Estimate x t minus 1"] --> C["Motion Model g of x and u"]
    B["Control Input u t"] --> C
    C --> D["Predicted State x bar t"]
    A --> E["State Jacobian G x"]
    B --> F["Control Jacobian G u"]
    E --> G["Covariance Update Equation"]
    F --> G
    H["Prior Covariance P t minus 1"] --> G
    I["Process Noise Covariance R t"] --> F
    F --> G
    G --> J["Predicted Covariance P bar t"]
    D --> K["Output to Measurement Update Step"]
    J --> K
    C --> L["Linearization Point"]
    L --> E
    L --> F
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#fff4e1
    style K fill:#e8f5e9
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Inputs (blue) are the prior state and control. The motion model produces the predicted state (orange) and feeds the linearization point. Jacobian evaluation runs in parallel, and the covariance update assembles the predicted covariance. The output (green) is forwarded to the next-stage measurement update.

### 4.2 Concept Map ŌĆö Position Prediction in the Bayes Filter Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Time t minus 1 Belief"] --> P2["Apply Control u t"]
    P2 --> P3["Motion Model p of x t given x t minus 1 and u t"]
    P3 --> P4["Predicted Belief at Time t"]
    P4 --> P5["Measurement Update with z t"]
    P5 --> P6["Posterior Belief at Time t"]
    P6 --> P1
    style P1 fill:#bbdefb
    style P4 fill:#ffe0b2
    style P6 fill:#c8e6c9
```

> **Key insight:** Position prediction (orange) is the **first half** of the recursive loop. The second half ŌĆö measurement update ŌĆö closes the loop and prevents the uncertainty from diverging indefinitely.

### 4.3 Coordinate Frame Geometry ŌĆö The Midpoint Angle Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O["World Frame Origin"] --> X["X axis of World Frame"]
    O --> Y["Y axis of World Frame"]
    R["Robot at time t minus 1"] --> A["Heading vector theta t minus 1"]
    A --> M["Midpoint Angle theta mid"]
    M --> H["Heading vector theta t"]
    R --> D["Displacement vector Delta s"]
    D --> P["New Position x t and y t"]
    style O fill:#f5f5f5
    style R fill:#fff9c4
    style P fill:#c8e6c9
```

> **Interpretation:** The robot begins at pose $x_{t-1}$ with heading $\theta_{t-1}$. After applying the control, the heading changes to $\theta_t$, and the displacement $\Delta s$ is applied *along* the **midpoint angle** $\theta_{mid}$ (not the start or end angle) ŌĆö this is the geometric origin of the midpoint-update formulas.

### 4.4 Uncertainty Growth Schematic ŌĆö The "Cone of Uncertainty"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["t equals 0 tight ellipse"] --> T1["t equals 1 small ellipse"]
    T1 --> T2["t equals 2 larger ellipse"]
    T2 --> T3["t equals 3 elongated ellipse"]
    T3 --> T4["t equals 4 very large smeared ellipse"]
    style T0 fill:#a5d6a7
    style T1 fill:#c5e1a5
    style T2 fill:#fff59d
    style T3 fill:#ffcc80
    style T4 fill:#ef9a9a
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As prediction proceeds without measurement correction, the **covariance ellipse grows** (warmer colors = higher uncertainty). The elongation direction is dictated by the dominant eigenvector of $G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T$. This is the visual essence of "dead reckoning drift."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define "Robot Position Prediction" in the context of mobile robot localization. List the two principal motion models used in KTU Module 2 and state one key difference between them.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Robot Position Prediction is the process of computing the robot's belief about its future state $\bar{x}_t$ by applying a probabilistic motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ to the prior state $x_{t-1}$ and the control $u_t$, *prior* to incorporating exteroceptive sensor measurements.

**Two Motion Models (1 Mark):**
1. **Velocity-Based Motion Model** ŌĆö uses controls $u_t = [v_t, \omega_t]^T$ (intended velocities).
2. **Odometry-Based Motion Model** ŌĆö uses measured motion $u_t = [\Delta s, \Delta \theta]^T$ from wheel encoders.

**Key Difference (1 Mark):** The velocity model reflects *commanded* actions and is therefore susceptible to actuator mismatch and slip, while the odometry model reflects *measured* actions and is typically more accurate over short horizons, but cannot model future motion (odometry is by definition retrospective).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Write the EKF prediction equations for a mobile robot with state $x = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and control $u = [v, \omega]^T$. Briefly justify why the covariance update uses Jacobian matrices instead of the motion function directly.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**State Prediction (1 Mark):**
$$\bar{x}_t = g(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Covariance Prediction (1 Mark):**
$$\bar{P}_t = G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T$$

**Justification (1 Mark):** The motion model $g(\cdot)$ is *nonlinear* in the state and control (it contains $\sin$ and $\cos$ of $\theta$). Gaussian uncertainty propagation through a nonlinear function is analytically intractable; it is performed to **first order** via a Taylor expansion, yielding the Jacobian-based linearized update. The Jacobian is evaluated at the current best estimate $(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$.

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Velocity-Model Derivation & EKF Linearization*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** For a differential-drive mobile robot, derive the **midpoint-integration position update equations** using the velocity motion model with control $u = [v, \omega]^T$ over interval $\Delta t$. Clearly state any trigonometric identity used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the **state Jacobian $G_x$ and control Jacobian $G_u$** for the motion function derived in part (a), and write the complete **EKF covariance prediction update**. Discuss how the structure of $G_x$ contributes to the *elongation* of the uncertainty ellipse along the direction of travel. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

We start from the world-frame velocity of the robot:

$$v_x^{world} = v \cos\theta, \quad v_y^{world} = v \sin\theta$$

Because the robot rotates with angular velocity $\omega$, the heading is time-dependent: $\theta(\tau) = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\tau$.

The exact integral for $x_t$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \int_0^{\Delta t} v \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \omega\tau) \, d\tau$$

Substituting $u = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\tau$ and $du = \omega \, d\tau$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega} \left[ \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t) - \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \right]$$

Applying the identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \frac{2v}{\omega}\cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

For small $\omega\Delta t$, $\sin(\frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}) \approx \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}$:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + v\Delta t \cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

By symmetry:

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + v\Delta t \sin\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)$$

And the exact heading update:

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t \qquad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Jacobians and Covariance Update (7 Marks):**

Let $c = \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2})$ and $s = \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2})$.

**State Jacobian** $G_x = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg|_{\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t}$:

$$G_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t \cdot s \\ 0 & 1 & \; v\Delta t \cdot c \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Control Jacobian** $G_u = \frac{\partial g}{\partial u}\bigg|_{\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t}$:

$$G_u = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta t \cdot c & -\frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} s \\ \Delta t \cdot s & \frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} c \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{bmatrix}$$

**Complete EKF Covariance Update:**

$$\bar{P}_t = G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T, \quad R_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_v^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\omega^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Discussion ŌĆö Ellipse Elongation (3ŌĆō4 sentences):** The off-diagonal terms $-v\Delta t \cdot s$ and $+v\Delta t \cdot c$ in $G_x$ couple the heading uncertainty into the position uncertainty. Specifically, when the robot moves forward, a small $\delta\theta$ in heading projects a **lateral position error** of magnitude $v\Delta t \cdot \delta\theta$. The product $G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T$ therefore amplifies the heading variance $\sigma_\theta^2$ into a position-direction variance of order $(v\Delta t)^2 \sigma_\theta^2$. Consequently, the resulting covariance ellipse is **elongated along the direction of travel**, with the elongation rate proportional to forward speed. This explains the characteristic "banana-shaped" uncertainty of dead-reckoned trajectories. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Odometry Model & Numerical Computation*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **odometry-based position update** for a differential-drive robot from wheel encoder counts. State clearly how the control input is constructed and explain why the midpoint angle $\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}$ is used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A differential-drive robot starts at $x_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$. The right and left wheel encoders report $\Delta n_r = 120$ and $\Delta n_l = 100$ counts. Given wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, encoder resolution $N = 1000$ counts/rev, and wheel base $b = 0.3$ m, compute the predicted pose after one time step. If the prior covariance is $P_0 = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01, 0.001) \text{ m}^2/\text{rad}^2$ and process noise $R = \text{diag}(0.001, 0.0001)$, evaluate the *new* predicted covariance $\bar{P}_1$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Odometry Model Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute wheel displacements from encoder counts:

$$\Delta s_r = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{\Delta n_r}{N}, \quad \Delta s_l = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{\Delta n_l}{N}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Construct the control input $u^{odom} = [\Delta s, \Delta \theta]^T$ where:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}, \quad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the midpoint-angle odometric update:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin\left(\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** The midpoint angle is used because the robot's heading changes **continuously** during the interval from $\theta_{t-1}$ to $\theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta$. The *average* direction of travel is the midpoint, leading to a second-order-accurate (trapezoidal) approximation. Using $\theta_{t-1}$ alone gives a first-order Euler approximation with $O(\Delta t)$ error; using the midpoint gives $O(\Delta t^2)$ error. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Wheel displacements:

$$\Delta s_r = 2\pi(0.05)\frac{120}{1000} = 0.0377 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta s_l = 2\pi(0.05)\frac{100}{1000} = 0.0314 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Control input:

$$\Delta s = \frac{0.0377 + 0.0314}{2} = 0.0346 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta\theta = \frac{0.0377 - 0.0314}{0.3} = 0.0210 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Midpoint angle: $\theta_{mid} = 0 + \frac{0.0210}{2} = 0.0105$ rad.

$\cos(0.0105) = 0.9999$, $\sin(0.0105) = 0.0105$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Predicted pose:

$$\bar{x}_1 = 0 + 0.0346 \times 0.9999 = 0.0346 \text{ m}$$
$$\bar{y}_1 = 0 + 0.0346 \times 0.0105 = 0.000363 \text{ m}$$
$$\bar{\theta}_1 = 0 + 0.0210 = 0.0210 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute Jacobians (using $c \approx 1, s \approx 0.0105$, $\Delta t = 1$ s for notional equivalence):

$$G_x \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.0346 \cdot 0.0105 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.0346 \cdot 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -3.63 \times 10^{-4} \\ 0 & 1 & 0.0346 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G_u = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9999 & -\frac{0.0346 \times 1}{2}(0.0105) \\ 0.0105 & \frac{0.0346 \times 1}{2}(0.9999) \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1.82 \times 10^{-4} \\ 0.0105 & 0.0173 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Apply covariance update $\bar{P}_1 = G_x P_0 G_x^T + G_u R G_u^T$:

$$G_x P_0 G_x^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 & -3.63 \times 10^{-6} \\ 0 & 0.01 + 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.001 & 3.46 \times 10^{-5} \\ -3.63 \times 10^{-6} & 3.46 \times 10^{-5} & 0.001 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G_u R G_u^T \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.001 & 1.05 \times 10^{-5} & -1.82 \times 10^{-4} \\ 1.05 \times 10^{-5} & 1.10 \times 10^{-4} + 2.99 \times 10^{-4} & 0.0173 \\ -1.82 \times 10^{-4} & 0.0173 & 0.0001 \end{bmatrix}$$

Adding both:

$$\bar{P}_1 \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0110 & 1.05 \times 10^{-5} & -1.86 \times 10^{-4} \\ 1.05 \times 10^{-5} & 0.0104 & 0.0173 \\ -1.86 \times 10^{-4} & 0.0173 & 0.0011 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Key observation:** The $\bar{P}_1[2,3] = 0.0173$ off-diagonal term is much larger than the prior $P_0[2,3] = 0$, indicating strong **cross-coupling** between $y$ and $\theta$ ŌĆö i.e., a small heading error induces a significant lateral position error. This is the mathematical signature of dead-reckoning drift. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Position Prediction Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting the midpoint angle:** Using $\theta_{t-1}$ instead of $\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}$ in the trig functions ŌĆö loses **2 marks** for the Euler-vs-trapezoidal distinction.
> 2. **Sign error in the $G_x$ off-diagonal:** The entry $G_x[1,3] = -v\Delta t \sin\theta_{mid}$, NOT $+v\Delta t \sin\theta_{mid}$. This is the **single most common sign error** flagged by KTU examiners.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\frac{\Delta t^2}{2}$ factor in $G_u$:** The control Jacobian's second column has $-\frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} s$ and $+\frac{v\Delta t^2}{2} c$. Omitting the $\frac{1}{2}$ from the chain rule loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $\bar{x}$ (predicted) with $\hat{x}$ (estimated):** The KTU valuation key explicitly checks the bar-vs-hat notation. Use $\bar{x}_t$ for *predicted before measurement* and $\hat{x}_t$ for *estimated after measurement*.
> 5. **Confusing the velocity model with the odometry model:** The two have similar *structure* but different *control inputs*. Students who write $v\Delta t$ when the problem gives $\Delta s$ lose **1 mark** per such instance.
> 6. **Not normalizing the angle:** If $\theta_t$ exceeds $\pi$, it must be wrapped to $[-\pi, \pi]$. Omitting this loses 1 mark in numerical problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Reference Summary ŌĆö Robot Position Prediction (Module 2):**

- **Core equation:** $\bar{x}_t = g(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$ ŌĆö nonlinear motion propagation.
- **Covariance update:** $\bar{P}_t = G_x P_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T$ ŌĆö linearized uncertainty propagation.
- **Velocity model control:** $u = [v, \omega]^T$ ŌĆö intended actions.
- **Odometry model control:** $u = [\Delta s, \Delta \theta]^T$ ŌĆö measured actions (preferred in practice).
- **Midpoint angle is mandatory:** $\theta_{t-1} + \frac{\omega\Delta t}{2}$ ŌĆö never use start-angle only.
- **Wheel-to-odometry conversion:** $\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$, $\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$.
- **EKF linearization point:** Jacobians are evaluated at the current best estimate $(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$.
- **Process noise $R_t$:** Diagonal for independent velocity/angular-velocity noise; tune from encoder statistics.
- **Uncertainty grows monotonically** under pure prediction ŌĆö no upper bound, no convergence without measurement correction.
- **Elongation direction of $P_t$ ellipse** = direction of travel, due to coupling via $G_x[1,3]$ and $G_x[2,3]$.
- **Notation conventions:** bar = predicted, hat = estimated, tilde = measured/noisy, no decoration = true.
- **KTU preferred form:** Midpoint integration (trapezoidal), not Euler; examiners expect this.
- **Bayes filter context:** Prediction is step 1 of 2; measurement update (Module 3) closes the loop.
- **Real-world example:** INS in aircraft, AMRs in warehouses, Mars rovers ŌĆö all rely on the prediction equations derived here.
- **Watch the units:** $v$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $\Delta t$ in s, $\Delta s$ in m, $\Delta\theta$ in rad.
- **Normalize $\theta$ to $(-\pi, \pi]$** after every update to prevent wraparound bugs in numerical code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Observation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Observation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the formal vocabulary of **Probabilistic Robotics** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT741)** curriculum, **Observation** refers to the mathematical and computational process by which a mobile robot acquires, interprets, and mathematically models sensory information from its operating environment. Observation is the *perception* half of the **PerceptionŌĆōAction Loop**, and is formally expressed as the **Observation Model** (also called the **Measurement Model**): $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ This notation represents the **conditional probability** of receiving a sensor measurement $z_t$ at time $t$, given the robot's current state $x_t$ and the known environment map $m$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** > An **observation** in robot navigation is the stochastic (probabilistic) mapping from the continuous physical world into a discrete stream of digital sensor measurements, corrupted by noise, occlusions, and finite sensor field-of-view (FOV). ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Blindfolded Driver" Imagine you are driving a car **blindfolded** but equipped with three senses: 1. **A long stick (Whisker / LiDAR)** ŌĆö pokes the world ahead and tells you the distance to the nearest obstacle. 2. **A rear-view mirror camera (Vision)** ŌĆö sees lane markings, traffic signs, and landmarks. 3. **A small box strapped to your foot (IMU / Odometry)** ŌĆö counts your steps and tracks acceleration. Each of these is an **observation channel**. The "observation" in robotic terms is the *fusion and mathematical processing* of all these noisy, partial, and often conflicting clues to form a coherent belief about the world. Just as a blind driver must reason *probabilistically* (the stick might bend, the camera might be blinded by sun), a robot must also reason with uncertainty ŌĆö this is the essence of the observation model. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** A sensor never tells the robot the "truth." It tells the robot a *noisy hint*, and the robot's brain (the estimator/Kalman/Particle filter) must figure out the truth by combining many such hints using probability theory. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following table lists the **industry-standard sensor specifications** a KTU student is expected to recognize in Part A questions: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Speed of Light | $c$ | $3.00 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s | | Speed of Sound (air, 20┬░C) | $v_s$ | $\mathbf{343}$ | m/s | | Standard Gravity | $g$ | $9.81$ | m/s┬▓ | | LiDAR Wavelength (typical) | $\lambda$ | $905$ or $1550$ | nm | | Camera Frame Rate | $f$ | $30$ to $60$ | fps | ## 1.4 Why Observation Matters in Robot Navigation Without robust observation, navigation collapses into **dead reckoning** ŌĆö the accumulation of integration error from wheel encoders and IMUs makes the robot's pose estimate drift unboundedly (often by **1ŌĆō10% of distance travelled**, and worse on slippery terrain). Observation provides the **external correction** that closes the loop and bounds the localization error. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Robot Observations Robot observations are organized along four orthogonal axes, and KTU questions frequently test these distinctions: 1. **Sensor Location (Proprioceptive vs. Exteroceptive)** - *Proprioceptive:* Measure the robot's *internal* state (wheel encoders, IMU, joint angles). Cannot see the world. - *Exteroceptive:* Measure the *external* world (cameras, LiDAR, GPS, ultrasonic). 2. **Sensor Modality (Active vs. Passive)** - *Active:* Emits energy into the environment (LiDAR laser pulse, sonar ping, radar). - *Passive:* Only receives ambient energy (camera, microphone, GPS receiver is technically active but passive from the robot's perspective). 3. **Measurement Dimensionality** - *1-D:* Single scalar reading (e.g., distance from a single ultrasonic sensor). - *2-D:* Range + Bearing (e.g., a single LiDAR beam or a landmark with $(r, \theta)$). - *3-D:* Range + Bearing + Elevation (3D LiDAR / RGB-D camera). - *Vector:* Full scan (e.g., a 360┬░ LiDAR scan returning 1000 range values). 4. **Information Content** - *Raw observation:* Direct sensor output. - *Feature-based observation:* Landmarks extracted (corners, edges, SIFT/SURF keypoints). ## 2.2 The Mathematical Anatomy of an Observation Model Every observation model in KTU syllabus consists of **three fundamental components** working together: ### Component 1 ŌĆö The Measurement Function $h(x_t, m)$ A (typically nonlinear) deterministic function that, given perfect knowledge of the robot's state $x_t$ and map $m$, computes the *expected* ideal measurement. $$z_t^* = h(x_t, m)$$ For a range-bearing landmark sensor observing landmark $l_i$ at map coordinates $(l_{i,x}, l_{i,y})$ from robot pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$: $$z_t^* = h(x_t, l_i) = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_{i,x}-x)^2 + (l_{i,y}-y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_{i,y}-y,\; l_{i,x}-x) - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Component 2 ŌĆö The Noise Model The measurement $z_t$ is corrupted by additive noise $w_t$, usually drawn from a **zero-mean Gaussian distribution**: $$z_t = h(x_t, m) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$ where $R_t$ is the **measurement noise covariance matrix** that captures the noise power in each dimension. ### Component 3 ŌĆö The Field of View (FOV) Validity The observation is only valid if the landmark lies inside the sensor's cone of vision: $$\vert \phi_t \vert \leq \phi_{\max} \quad \text{and} \quad r_{\min} \leq r_t \leq r_{\max}$$ If the landmark lies outside the FOV, the probability of detecting it is **zero** ŌĆö a hard constraint that all KTU questions test. > [!NOTE] > **Forgetting the FOV constraint is the #1 reason KTU students lose 2 marks in Part B derivations.** Always include it! ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet ŌĆö The "Must-Memorize" Set > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for all KTU Part A and Part B questions on Observation. Every formula is board-tested. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Typical Use | | :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Observation Model (General)** | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Probability of $z_t$ given state and map | Bayes Filter, EKF | | 2 | **Gaussian Noise (1-D)** | $p(z \mid x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\dfrac{(z-h(x))^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $\sigma$ = std deviation | EKF, UKF | | 3 | **Range-Bearing Observation** | $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$, $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$ | $\Delta x = l_x - x$, $\Delta y = l_y - y$ | Landmark-based EKF | | 4 | **Jacobian $H$ (EKF Observation)** | $H = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | Partial derivative of measurement wrt state | EKF Correction Step | | 5 | **Range Measurement Noise (Beam Model)** | $p_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{\text{hit}}\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\dfrac{(z_t^k - z_t^{k*})^2}{2\sigma_{\text{hit}}^2}}$ | $z_t^{k*}$ = true range | LiDAR Localization | | 6 | **Maximum Detection Range** | $z_{\max}$ | Sensor hardware limit | FOV clipping | | 7 | **Total Range Scan Likelihood** | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ | Independence assumption over $K$ beams | Scan Matching | | 8 | **Innovation (Residual)** | $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ | Difference of measured vs predicted | EKF Update | | 9 | **EKF Kalman Gain** | $K_t = P_t^- H^\top (H P_t^- H^\top + R)^{-1}$ | $P_t^-$ = prior covariance | Sensor Fusion | | 10 | **Updated Covariance (EKF)** | $P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^-$ | Posterior covariance after observation | Localization Filter | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Safety Note:** In the above table, all absolute value and magnitude operators are written using the words "value of" or as conditions like $\vert \phi_t \vert$, which is rendered as a stand-alone math expression outside the table syntax. This prevents markdown parser breakage. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Combine LiDAR, camera, and radar observations through EKF / particle filters to localize within **$\pm 10$ cm** accuracy. - **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Use 2D LiDAR observations against a pre-built map to track pose with $\pm 5$ cm precision. - **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Stereo vision + visual odometry observations enable navigation across unmapped craters. - **Drones (Indoor Swarms):** Ultra-wideband (UWB) and onboard cameras are fused for GPS-denied environments. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö The Range-Bearing Observation Jacobian $H$ > This derivation is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU Part B (Module 2) for 7 marks. We will derive it completely, with no skipped steps. ### Setup The robot state is $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$, and a landmark sits at fixed map coordinates $l_i = (l_{i,x}, l_{i,y})^\top$. The ideal measurement is: $$z_t^* = h(x_t, l_i) = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix}$$ with components: $$r = \sqrt{(l_{i,x} - x)^2 + (l_{i,y} - y)^2}$$ $$\phi = \text{atan2}\!\left(l_{i,y} - y,\; l_{i,x} - x\right) - \theta$$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Differences Let us introduce shorthand variables to keep the derivation clean: $$q = (l_{i,x} - x)^2 + (l_{i,y} - y)^2$$ Note that $q = r^2$ by construction, so we can write: $$r = \sqrt{q}$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Partial Derivative of $r$ with Respect to $x$ We use the chain rule. First: $$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \sqrt{q}}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q}{\partial x}$$ Compute each sub-derivative: $$\frac{\partial \sqrt{q}}{\partial q} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{q}} = \frac{1}{2r}$$ $$\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[(l_{i,x} - x)^2 + (l_{i,y} - y)^2\right] = -2(l_{i,x} - x)$$ Multiplying: $$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2r} \cdot \bigl[-2(l_{i,x} - x)\bigr] = -\frac{l_{i,x} - x}{r}$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Partial Derivative of $r$ with Respect to $y$ By symmetry (just swap $x \to y$ and $l_{i,x} \to l_{i,y}$): $$\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = -\frac{l_{i,y} - y}{r}$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Partial Derivative of $r$ with Respect to $\theta$ The expression for $r$ contains **no $\theta$**, so: $$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} = 0$$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Partial Derivative of $\phi$ with Respect to $x$ The function $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$, where $\Delta x = l_{i,x} - x$ and $\Delta y = l_{i,y} - y$. The derivative of $\text{atan2}(y,x)$ with respect to $x$ (the *second* argument) is $\frac{-y}{x^2 + y^2}$. Applying this: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{-(l_{i,y} - y)}{(l_{i,x} - x)^2 + (l_{i,y} - y)^2} = -\frac{l_{i,y} - y}{r^2}$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Partial Derivative of $\phi$ with Respect to $y$ By the analogous formula $\frac{\partial \text{atan2}(y,x)}{\partial y} = \frac{x}{x^2 + y^2}$: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \frac{l_{i,x} - x}{r^2}$$ ### Step 7 ŌĆö Partial Derivative of $\phi$ with Respect to $\theta$ Trivially: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -1$$ ### Step 8 ŌĆö Assemble the Jacobian Matrix The EKF observation Jacobian $H$ is a $2 \times 3$ matrix: $$H = \frac{\partial h(x_t, l_i)}{\partial x_t} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} \\[1.2em] \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix}$$ Substituting all partial derivatives: $$H = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{l_{i,x} - x}{r} & -\dfrac{l_{i,y} - y}{r} & 0 \\[1.2em] \dfrac{l_{i,y} - y}{r^2} & -\dfrac{l_{i,x} - x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{H = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\Delta x}{r} & -\dfrac{\Delta y}{r} & 0 \\[1.2em] \dfrac{\Delta y}{r^2} & -\dfrac{\Delta x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}}$$ This Jacobian is the cornerstone of the EKF localization update step. **Memorize it.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Gaussian Observation Likelihood (1-D Distance Sensor) Starting from the **fundamental definition** of a probability density for a continuous random variable: $$p(z) \, dz = \text{probability that } z \in [z, z+dz]$$ For a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$: $$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ The likelihood of an observed measurement $z_t$ given an expected value $z_t^* = h(x_t, m)$ is: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_t - z_t^*)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ This single expression underlies nearly every sensor model in KTU syllabus ŌĆö from ultrasonic distance to GPS pseudo-range to LiDAR beam return. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Multi-Beam Scan Likelihood (Independence Assumption) A 2D LiDAR produces $K$ simultaneous range measurements per scan: $$z_t = \left\{z_t^1, z_t^2, \dots, z_t^K\right\}$$ **Assumption (commonly tested in KTU):** *Beams are conditionally independent given the robot's pose and the map.* Applying the **chain rule of probability** for independent events: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = p(z_t^1, z_t^2, \dots, z_t^K \mid x_t, m)$$ $$= p(z_t^1 \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(z_t^2 \mid x_t, m) \cdots p(z_t^K \mid x_t, m)$$ $$\boxed{p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)}$$ For computational stability, KTU examiners expect the **log-likelihood** form: $$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why log?** Because raw likelihoods of $10^{-50}$ cannot be represented in floating point. Log-summation is numerically stable and is the industry-standard approach (used in Google Cartographer, ROS amcl, etc.). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Observation Pipeline ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Environment and Map m] --> B{Sensor Array
Hardware Layer} B --> C1[Range Sensor
LiDAR / Ultrasonic] B --> C2[Vision Sensor
Camera / RGB-D] B --> C3[Inertial Sensor
IMU / Odometry] C1 --> D[Raw Measurement
Vector z_t] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Noise Model
w_t ~ N 0 R_t] E --> F[Feature Extraction
Landmarks / Scans] F --> G[Observation Function
h of x_t m] G --> H[Innovation Computation
nu_t = z_t minus hat z_t] H --> I[Kalman Gain
K_t] I --> J[State Update
x_t and P_t] J --> K[Robot Pose Belief
Posterior Estimate] K --> L[Navigation Planner
Path and Control] L --> M[Actuator Commands
u_t] M --> A ``` > **Figure Caption:** The complete PerceptionŌĆōAction Loop. Observation is the block chain from $A \to K$, and the loop closes via the actuator commands $u_t$ that drive the robot's effectors. --- ## 4.2 Observation Model State Diagram ŌĆö Range Sensor Behavior ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> EmitPulse EmitPulse --> WavefrontPropagates: Active Sensing WavefrontPropagates --> FirstObjectHit: t equals r divided by c FirstObjectHit --> PossibleCause: Analyze Return Signal PossibleCause --> CorrectHit: Gaussian noise on range PossibleCause --> UnexpectedObject: Short range outlier PossibleCause --> FailToDetect: No return in window PossibleCause --> RandomMeasurement: Max range reading CorrectHit --> UpdateBelief: Innovation Accepted UnexpectedObject --> UpdateBelief: Weighted Low Likelihood FailToDetect --> UpdateBelief: Miss Probability RandomMeasurement --> UpdateBelief: Mixed with Uniform Prior UpdateBelief --> [*] ``` > **Figure Caption:** The four intrinsic measurement causes of the **Beam Model** of a range finder (Thrun et al., *Probabilistic Robotics*). The EKF uses a *mixture* of these four likelihoods: > $$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \alpha_{\text{hit}}\, p_{\text{hit}} + \alpha_{\text{short}}\, p_{\text{short}} + \alpha_{\max}\, p_{\max} + \alpha_{\text{rand}}\, p_{\text{rand}}$$ --- ## 4.3 EKF Observation Update Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Receive z_t at time t] --> S2[Predict Measurement
hat z_t = h of hat x_t m] S2 --> S3[Compute Jacobian
H = partial h divided by partial x] S3 --> S4[Innovation
nu_t = z_t minus hat z_t] S4 --> S5[Innovation Covariance
S_t = H P_t minus H transposed plus R] S5 --> S6[Kalman Gain
K_t = P_t minus H transposed S_t inverse] S6 --> S7[State Update
x_t = hat x_t plus K_t nu_t] S7 --> S8[Covariance Update
P_t = I minus K_t H P_t minus] S8 --> S9[Output: Posterior Belief
x_t and P_t] S9 --> S10[Stop] ``` > **Figure Caption:** The five-step EKF observation update. This is the *exact sequence* a KTU Part B answer must describe for full marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PERAT741]`** > Define the **observation model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ used in probabilistic robot navigation. List the **three components** that every observation model must specify. #### Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö Board-Standard): The observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **conditional probability density** of receiving a sensor measurement $z_t$ at time $t$, given the robot state $x_t$ and the known environment map $m$. It captures *all* sensor uncertainties ŌĆö noise, occlusions, and field-of-view limits. **Three essential components:** 1. **Measurement Function $h(x_t, m)$:** A deterministic function computing the ideal (noise-free) measurement from the state and map. 2. **Noise Model:** A probability distribution (typically Gaussian with covariance $R_t$) describing the random deviation between true and measured values. 3. **Validity / FOV Constraint:** A hard condition specifying when the sensor can produce a measurement (e.g., range within $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$, bearing within $\pm \phi_{\max}$). > **Marking Key:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correctly listing the three components. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PERAT741]`** > Differentiate between **proprioceptive** and **exteroceptive** sensors. Give **two examples** of each. #### Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö Board-Standard): | Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **What it measures** | Robot's *internal* state | *External* environment | | **World awareness** | Cannot detect obstacles | Detects surroundings | | **Example 1** | Wheel encoder (odometry) | LiDAR | | **Example 2** | Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) | Camera | > **Marking Key:** 1 mark definition each + 0.5 mark per example = 3 marks. --- ## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > KTU Module 2024 Scheme Rule: *Answer **any ONE full question** from the choice. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.* --- ### CHOICE A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PERAT741 ŌĆö Q. 2(a), 2(b)]`** #### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks > Consider a differential-drive robot at state $x_t = (2.0, 3.0, 30┬░)^\top$ observing a landmark at map coordinates $l_i = (8.0, 7.0)^\top$. The sensor returns a range-bearing measurement. **Compute the expected measurement** $z_t^* = (r, \phi)^\top$. ##### Model Solution: The measurement function is: $$r = \sqrt{(l_{i,x} - x)^2 + (l_{i,y} - y)^2}$$ $$\phi = \text{atan2}(l_{i,y} - y,\; l_{i,x} - x) - \theta$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the differences:** $$\Delta x = l_{i,x} - x = 8.0 - 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$ $$\Delta y = l_{i,y} - y = 7.0 - 3.0 = 4.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the range:** $$r = \sqrt{6.0^2 + 4.0^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the bearing (in radians):** $$\psi = \text{atan2}(4.0, 6.0) = 0.5880 \text{ rad} \;\;(\approx 33.69┬░)$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Subtract robot heading $\theta = 30┬░ = 0.5236$ rad:** $$\phi = 0.5880 - 0.5236 = 0.0644 \text{ rad} \;\;(\approx 3.69┬░)$$ ##### Final Answer: $$\boxed{z_t^* = (7.211 \text{ m},\; 0.0644 \text{ rad})^\top}$$ > **Marking Key:** Step 1 (2m), Step 2 (2m), Step 3 (2m), Step 4 (1m) = 7 marks. #### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks > For the scenario in part (a), compute the **observation Jacobian matrix** $H$ evaluated at the given state. ##### Model Solution: Using the formula derived in Section 3.1: $$H = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\Delta x}{r} & -\dfrac{\Delta y}{r} & 0 \\[1.2em] \dfrac{\Delta y}{r^2} & -\dfrac{\Delta x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $\Delta x = 6.0$, $\Delta y = 4.0$, $r = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211$:** $$-\frac{\Delta x}{r} = -\frac{6.0}{7.211} \approx -0.8321$$ $$-\frac{\Delta y}{r} = -\frac{4.0}{7.211} \approx -0.5547$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute second row entries:** $$r^2 = 52.0$$ $$\frac{\Delta y}{r^2} = \frac{4.0}{52.0} \approx 0.0769$$ $$-\frac{\Delta x}{r^2} = -\frac{6.0}{52.0} \approx -0.1154$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Final entry (constant):** $-1$. ##### Final Answer: $$\boxed{H = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8321 & -0.5547 & 0 \\ 0.0769 & -0.1154 & -1 \end{bmatrix}}$$ > **Marking Key:** Correct formula (3m), correct numerical evaluation (3m), correct shape $2 \times 3$ (1m) = 7 marks. --- ### CHOICE B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PERAT741 ŌĆö Q. 3(a), 3(b)]`** #### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks > Explain the **Beam Model of a Range Finder** with its **four intrinsic measurement causes**. Write the mathematical mixture equation. ##### Model Solution: The beam model (Thrun et al., *Probabilistic Robotics*) decomposes a single LiDAR beam's measurement into **four possible causes**, each with its own probability density: | # | Cause | Physical Meaning | Density | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Hit** | Beam correctly hit an obstacle | $p_{\text{hit}} = \mathcal{N}(z; z^*, \sigma_{\text{hit}}^2)$ | | 2 | **Short** | Beam hit an *unexpected* object (person, dust) | Exponential decay, $p_{\text{short}} = \lambda e^{-\lambda z}$ | | 3 | **Max** | Beam reflected off nothing ŌĆö sensor returned $z_{\max}$ | $p_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{z_{\max}} \mathbf{1}_{z = z_{\max}}$ | | 4 | **Random** | Beam caused by random environmental noise | $p_{\text{rand}} = \dfrac{1}{z_{\max}}$ uniform | The **total measurement likelihood** is a weighted mixture: $$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \alpha_{\text{hit}}\, p_{\text{hit}} + \alpha_{\text{short}}\, p_{\text{short}} + \alpha_{\max}\, p_{\max} + \alpha_{\text{rand}}\, p_{\text{rand}}$$ with the weights satisfying $\alpha_{\text{hit}} + \alpha_{\text{short}} + \alpha_{\max} + \alpha_{\text{rand}} = 1$. > **Marking Key:** Naming the 4 causes (4m) + writing the mixture equation correctly (3m) = 7 marks. #### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks > A 2D LiDAR produces a scan of $K = 360$ beams, each with a range value $z_t^k$ in meters. State the **assumption required** to compute the **total scan likelihood** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$, and write the resulting **log-likelihood expression**. Justify why the log-form is preferred. ##### Model Solution: **Assumption:** The $K$ beams are **conditionally independent** given the robot's pose $x_t$ and the map $m$. **Derivation** (using the chain rule for independent events): $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$ **Log-likelihood form** (taking natural log on both sides): $$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$ **Why log-form is preferred:** 1. **Numerical stability:** Raw product of 360 small probabilities (e.g., $10^{-5}$ each) yields $10^{-1800}$, far below floating-point underflow limit. 2. **Computational efficiency:** Log transforms expensive multiplications into cheap additions. 3. **Optimization friendliness:** Gradient-based pose optimizers (Gauss-Newton, Levenberg-Marquardt) operate on log-likelihoods directly. > **Marking Key:** Stating the assumption (2m) + writing the product (2m) + log form + 2 justifications (3m) = 7 marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Observation Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the FOV constraint ($-2$ to $-3$ marks).** The Jacobian $H$ and measurement function $h$ are *only valid* when the landmark lies within the sensor's field of view. Always state the FOV validity condition $\vert \phi \vert \leq \phi_{\max}$. > 2. **Using $\arctan$ instead of $\text{atan2}$ ($-2$ marks).** Standard $\arctan(y/x)$ fails in the second and third quadrants. KTU strictly expects the four-quadrant $\text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x)$ form. > 3. **Missing the $-1$ in $H[2,3]$ ($-1$ mark).** The bearing partial derivative with respect to heading $\theta$ is **always exactly $-1$**. Examiners *love* to test this. > 4. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $\sigma^2$ ($-1$ mark).** Standard deviation $\sigma$ goes in $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$'s denominator's square root. Variance $\sigma^2$ goes inside the squared term's denominator. Mixing them is a classic error. > 5. **No units in final numerical answers ($-0.5$ mark).** Always write "m" for range and "rad" (or "deg") for bearing. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam.** - [x] **Observation =** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ ŌĆö probability of measurement given state and map. - [x] **Three Components:** Measurement function $h$, noise model $\mathcal{N}(0, R)$, FOV constraint. - [x] **Range-Bearing Function:** $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$, $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$. - [x] **Jacobian $H$ (Memorize):** $\begin{bmatrix} -\Delta x/r & -\Delta y/r & 0 \\ \Delta y/r^2 & -\Delta x/r^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$. - [x] **Proprioceptive Sensors:** Internal state only (encoders, IMU). - [x] **Exteroceptive Sensors:** External world (LiDAR, camera, GPS). - [x] **Active Sensors:** Emit energy (LiDAR, sonar, radar). - [x] **Passive Sensors:** Receive ambient energy (camera, microphone). - [x] **Beam Model Mixture:** Hit + Short + Max + Random, weighted by $\alpha_i$ summing to 1. - [x] **Scan Likelihood:** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ (independence assumption). - [x] **Log-Likelihood:** $\log p = \sum \log p$ (numerical stability). - [x] **EKF Innovation:** $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$, where $\hat{z}_t = h(\hat{x}_t, m)$. - [x] **EKF Kalman Gain:** $K_t = P_t^- H^\top (H P_t^- H^\top + R)^{-1}$. - [x] **EKF Covariance Update:** $P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^-$. - [x] **Speed of Sound:** **343 m/s** (use for sonar time-of-flight calculations). - [x] **Speed of Light:** $3 \times 10^8$ m/s (use for LiDAR time-of-flight). - [x] **FOV Constraint:** $\vert \phi \vert \leq \phi_{\max}$ and $r_{\min} \leq r \leq r_{\max}$. > **End of KTU Module 2 ŌĆö Observation Notes. Best of luck for your examination!**

topics.title: Measurement Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Measurement Prediction** is the process of estimating the expected sensor reading (measurement) that a robot's sensor should produce at the next time step, based on the robot's current predicted state estimate. Mathematically, it is the deterministic projection of the a priori state estimate $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ through the measurement (observation) model $H(k+1)$, before the actual noisy measurement $z(k+1)$ arrives.

In state-space estimation theory (particularly within the **Kalman Filter** framework), the **predicted measurement** (also called the *innovation mean* or *expected observation*) is formally expressed as:

$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$$

where:
- $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ is the predicted measurement vector at time $k+1$, conditioned on all measurements up to time $k$.
- $H(k+1)$ is the **measurement (observation) Jacobian matrix** that linearly maps the state space to the measurement space.
- $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ is the a priori state estimate obtained from the time-update (prediction) step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Measurement Prediction is the **bridge between the Prediction Step and the Measurement Update Step** of the Kalman Filter. It answers the question: *"Given where I think I am, what do I expect my sensors to read?"* ŌĆö before the actual sensor data corrupts this expectation with noise.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a familiar room** and you are told: *"You are standing 3 meters from the north wall."* Based only on this belief (your state estimate), you can mentally *predict* that if you stretch your hand forward, you should feel the wall in 3 meters. This mental prediction of "the wall should be at 3 meters" is your **measurement prediction**.

Now, when you actually stretch your hand (the real sensor reading), your fingers might touch the wall at 2.9 meters or 3.1 meters due to:
- Slight errors in your belief of position (state uncertainty),
- Hand tremor or sensor noise.

The **difference** between what you predicted (3 m) and what you actually felt (3.1 m) is called the **Innovation** or **Measurement Residual**. This residual is the *new information* the Kalman Filter uses to correct its belief about position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:**
> - *Belief of position* ŌåÆ State estimate $\hat{x}$
> - *Mental prediction of wall distance* ŌåÆ Predicted measurement $\hat{z}$
> - *Actual hand reach* ŌåÆ Real measurement $z$
> - *Discrepancy* ŌåÆ Innovation $\nu = z - \hat{z}$
> - *Hand tremor + belief error* ŌåÆ Innovation covariance $S$

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude (Robot Nav) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R(k)$ | Measurement noise covariance | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{0}$ (range-bearing), $\sim 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ (GPS-like) | Unit of measurement squared |
| $H(k)$ | Observation matrix | Dimension $m \times n$ (m measurements, n states) | Dimensionless |
| $S(k+1)$ | Innovation covariance | Tracks uncertainty in predicted measurement | Unit of measurement squared |
| $\nu(k+1)$ | Innovation vector | Range: $|\nu_i| \approx 2\sqrt{S_{ii}}$ for healthy filter | Unit of measurement |

> [!TIP]
> A well-tuned Kalman Filter produces innovations whose **Mahalanobis distance squared** $\nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ follows a **chi-squared ($\chi^2$) distribution** with $m$ degrees of freedom ŌĆö this is the standard for filter consistency checks in KTU exam problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö The Logic Chain

The Measurement Prediction phase sits inside the recursive **PredictŌĆōCorrect** loop of the Kalman Filter. It is the **third sub-step** in the prediction half of the cycle:

1. **State Prediction (Time Update):**
   $$\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) = F(k)\,\hat{x}(k \mid k) + B(k)\,u(k)$$
   *Why:* Propagates the state forward using the motion model $F$.

2. **Covariance Prediction (Time Update):**
   $$P(k+1 \mid k) = F(k)\,P(k \mid k)\,F(k)^T + Q(k)$$
   *Why:* Propagates the uncertainty forward; $Q(k)$ is process noise.

3. **Measurement Prediction (Observation Forecast):**
   $$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$$
   *Why:* Projects the predicted state into the measurement space ŌĆö *"What should the sensor see?"*

4. **Innovation Covariance (Observation Uncertainty):**
   $$S(k+1) = H(k+1)\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T + R(k+1)$$
   *Why:* Quantifies how uncertain we are about the predicted measurement; $R$ is sensor noise.

5. **Optimal Kalman Gain:**
   $$K(k+1) = P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T\,S(k+1)^{-1}$$
   *Why:* Weighs belief vs. measurement ŌĆö large $K$ means trust the sensor more.

6. **Innovation (Residual):**
   $$\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$$
   *Why:* The new information from the sensor.

### 2.2 Why Measurement Prediction Matters

- **Filter Consistency:** It provides the *expected* observation needed to validate the filter against real data (e.g., $\chi^2$ gating in data association).
- **Outlier Rejection:** Innovations lying outside $\pm 3\sqrt{S_{ii}}$ are flagged as outliers ŌĆö crucial in SLAM and feature tracking.
- **Data Association in SLAM:** In landmark-based navigation, the predicted measurement tells the robot *where to look* for a known feature in its sensor field-of-view.
- **Information Form:** $\hat{z}$ defines the linearization point for the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** and **Iterated EKF**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ | Predicted Measurement | Forecast sensor reading |
| 2 | $\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ | Innovation / Residual | New information content |
| 3 | $S(k+1) = H(k+1)\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T + R(k+1)$ | Innovation Covariance | Confidence in prediction |
| 4 | $K(k+1) = P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T\,S(k+1)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | Optimal belief blending |
| 5 | $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k+1) = \hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) + K(k+1)\,\nu(k+1)$ | State Update | Corrected estimate |
| 6 | $P(k+1 \mid k+1) = \left[I - K(k+1)\,H(k+1)\right] P(k+1 \mid k)$ | Covariance Update | Reduced uncertainty |
| 7 | $d^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ | Mahalanobis Distance | Filter consistency / gating |
| 8 | $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = h(\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k))$ (nonlinear) | EKF Predicted Measurement | General observation model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** $S(k+1)$ has units of $(\text{measurement units})^2$. For range-bearing sensors, $S$ is $2 \times 2$ with diagonal elements $[\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\theta^2]$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Predicts where a radar/lidar return *should* appear for an obstacle tracked in a Kalman tracker.
- **GPS-INS Integration:** Predicts pseudoranges from the predicted position to detect satellite measurement anomalies.
- **Robotic SLAM:** Measurement prediction enables **predictive data association** ŌĆö matching predicted landmark observations to actual sensor detections.
- **Spacecraft Navigation:** NASA's onboard navigation filters use measurement prediction to schedule sensor pointing (predictive slew).
- **Drone Swarms:** Each drone predicts the relative bearing its neighbor *should* report, enabling consistency-based fault detection.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Predicted Measurement Equation

We begin from the **linear discrete-time measurement model**:

$$z(k+1) = H(k+1)\,x(k+1) + v(k+1)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Take the conditional expectation of both sides** with respect to all measurements up to time $k$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\,z(k+1) \,\Big|\, z^{k}\,\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\,H(k+1)\,x(k+1) + v(k+1) \,\Big|\, z^{k}\,\right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply linearity of expectation:**

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\,z(k+1) \,\Big|\, z^{k}\,\right] = H(k+1)\,\mathbb{E}\left[\,x(k+1) \,\Big|\, z^{k}\,\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[\,v(k+1) \,\Big|\, z^{k}\,\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply standard assumptions:**
- $\mathbb{E}\left[x(k+1) \mid z^{k}\right] = \hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ (definition of a priori state estimate)
- $\mathbb{E}\left[v(k+1) \mid z^{k}\right] = 0$ (zero-mean measurement noise, independent of past)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute and define:**

$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) \;=\; H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Innovation Covariance $S(k+1)$

The innovation is $\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute the measurement model:**

$$\nu(k+1) = H(k+1)\,x(k+1) + v(k+1) - H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the state estimation error** $\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k) = x(k+1) - \hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$:

$$\nu(k+1) = H(k+1)\,\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k) + v(k+1)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take the outer product and apply expectation** (definition of covariance, $\text{Cov}(\cdot) = \mathbb{E}[\cdot\, \cdot^T]$):

$$S(k+1) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nu(k+1)\,\nu(k+1)^T\right]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand using independence of $\tilde{x}$ and $v$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(k+1) &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(H\tilde{x} + v\right)\left(H\tilde{x} + v\right)^T\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[H\tilde{x}\tilde{x}^T H^T + H\tilde{x}\,v^T + v\tilde{x}^T H^T + v v^T\right] \\
&= H\,\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}\tilde{x}^T]\,H^T + H\,\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}\,v^T] + \mathbb{E}[v\tilde{x}^T]\,H^T + \mathbb{E}[v v^T]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply orthogonality:** $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k)\,v(k+1)^T] = 0$ because measurement noise is independent of past estimation error. Also define:

- $P(k+1 \mid k) = \mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k)\,\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k)^T]$
- $R(k+1) = \mathbb{E}[v(k+1)\,v(k+1)^T]$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{S(k+1) = H(k+1)\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T + R(k+1)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** $S$ is symmetric and positive-definite (for non-degenerate $H$ and $R \succ 0$), guaranteeing its inverse $S^{-1}$ exists ŌĆö a prerequisite for the Kalman Gain to be well-defined.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö 2D Range-Bearing Robot

**Problem Setup:** A robot with state $x = [x_r, y_r, \phi]^T$ observes a stationary landmark at known position $(x_L, y_L)$ via a range-bearing sensor. Derive the predicted measurement and innovation covariance.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the true (nonlinear) measurement model:**

$$z = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_L - x_r)^2 + (y_L - y_r)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_L - y_r,\; x_L - x_r) - \phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the predicted measurement as the model evaluated at $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$:**

$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_L - \hat{x}_r)^2 + (y_L - \hat{y}_r)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_L - \hat{y}_r,\; x_L - \hat{x}_r) - \hat{\phi} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Jacobian $H$ (linearization required for EKF):**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\hat{x}_r - x_L}{\hat{r}} & \dfrac{\hat{y}_r - y_L}{\hat{r}} & 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{y_L - \hat{y}_r}{\hat{r}^2} & \dfrac{\hat{x}_r - x_L}{\hat{r}^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\hat{r} = \sqrt{(x_L - \hat{x}_r)^2 + (y_L - \hat{y}_r)^2}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Innovation covariance (given $P(k+1 \mid k)$ and $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\theta^2)$):**

$$S(k+1) = H\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H^T + \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_r^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\theta^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical demonstration** (let $x_L = 5, y_L = 5, \hat{x}_r = 2, \hat{y}_r = 1, \hat{\phi} = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{r} &= \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \\
\hat{\theta} &= \text{atan2}(5-1,\; 5-2) - 0 = \text{atan2}(4, 3) = 0.9273 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 0.9273 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\hat{H} = \begin{bmatrix} (2-5)/5 & (1-5)/5 & 0 \\ (5-1)/25 & (2-5)/25 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \\ 0.16 & -0.12 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Jacobian row 1 of $H$ is the **unit vector from landmark to robot**, pointing in the negative range-gradient direction. Always derive $H$ for full marks.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Inputs Needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Predict State | $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ | $F\hat{x}(k \mid k) + Bu$ | $F, B, u$ |
| Predict Covariance | $P(k+1 \mid k)$ | $F P F^T + Q$ | $F, P(k \mid k), Q$ |
| Predict Measurement | $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ | $H \hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ | $H, \hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ |
| Innovation Covariance | $S(k+1)$ | $H P(k+1 \mid k) H^T + R$ | $H, P(k+1 \mid k), R$ |
| Kalman Gain | $K(k+1)$ | $P H^T S^{-1}$ | $P(k+1 \mid k), H, S$ |
| Innovation | $\nu(k+1)$ | $z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ | $z, \hat{z}$ |
| State Update | $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k+1)$ | $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) + K\nu$ | All above |
| Covariance Update | $P(k+1 \mid k+1)$ | $(I - KH)P(k+1 \mid k)$ | $K, H, P(k+1 \mid k)$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Kalman Filter with Measurement Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input u_k and measurement z_k+1"] --> B["State Predict x_hat_k+1_given_k"]
    B --> C["Covariance Predict P_k+1_given_k"]
    C --> D["Measurement Predict z_hat_k+1_given_k = H x_hat"]
    C --> E["Innovation Covariance S_k+1 = H P H_T plus R"]
    D --> F["Innovation nu_k+1 = z_k+1 minus z_hat"]
    E --> G["Kalman Gain K_k+1 = P H_T S_inv"]
    F --> H["State Update x_hat_k+1_given_k+1"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Covariance Update P_k+1_given_k+1"]
    I --> J["Output corrected state and covariance"]
    J --> K["Loop back to next time step"]
    D --> L["Consistency Check using Mahalanobis"]
    E --> L
    F --> L
    L --> M["Gate or reject outlier measurement"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Measurement Prediction in a Robotic Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception["Perception Layer"]
        P1["Sensor Frontend"]
        P2["Feature Extractor"]
        P3["Raw Measurement z_k+1"]
    end

    subgraph Estimation["Estimation Layer - Kalman Core"]
        E1["Motion Model F"]
        E2["Predicted State x_hat_k+1_given_k"]
        E3["Predicted Covariance P_k+1_given_k"]
        E4["Measurement Predict z_hat = H x_hat"]
        E5["Innovation Covariance S = H P H_T plus R"]
        E6["Kalman Gain K = P H_T S_inv"]
        E7["Innovation nu = z minus z_hat"]
        E8["State Update x_hat_corrected"]
    end

    subgraph Decision["Decision Layer"]
        D1["Gating by Mahalanobis distance"]
        D2["Data Association"]
        D3["Outlier Rejection Flag"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> E7
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E5
    E3 --> E6
    E4 --> E7
    E4 --> D1
    E5 --> D1
    E7 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    E7 --> E8
    E6 --> E8
    E8 --> E2
```

### 4.3 Data Flow Diagram ŌĆö Innovation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z["Actual Measurement z_k+1"] --> N["Innovation nu_k+1"]
    ZP["Predicted Measurement z_hat_k+1_given_k"] --> N
    N --> C1["Chi squared Test"]
    S["Innovation Covariance S_k+1"] --> C1
    C1 --> Q{"In Gate?"}
    Q -->|Yes| ACC["Accept and Apply Kalman Update"]
    Q -->|No| REJ["Reject as Outlier"]
    ACC --> NE["New Estimate x_hat_k+1_given_k+1"]
    REJ --> NE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice how $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ is *derived* (not directly sensed) and is the *only* bridge between the predicted state and the real world of sensor data. This is the conceptual heart of Measurement Prediction.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "predicted measurement" in the context of a Kalman Filter. State its mathematical expression for a linear measurement model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The predicted measurement is the expected sensor reading at the next time step, computed from the a priori state estimate through the observation model. It represents what the sensor *should* report if the state estimate were perfect and the world behaved exactly as modeled.

For a linear measurement model $z(k+1) = H(k+1)\,x(k+1) + v(k+1)$, the predicted measurement is:
$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$$

It is the conditional expectation of the measurement given all past measurements, and serves as the reference value against which the actual measurement is differenced to form the innovation. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the "innovation" in a Kalman Filter, and why is its covariance $S(k+1)$ important in robot navigation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The innovation (or measurement residual) is the difference between the actual measurement $z(k+1)$ and the predicted measurement $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$:
$$\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$$

Its covariance, $S(k+1) = H\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H^T + R(k+1)$, quantifies the *expected uncertainty* of the innovation under the filter's assumptions.

Importance in robot navigation:
1. **Gating:** It enables the Mahalanobis test $\nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ to validate or reject sensor measurements (critical for data association in SLAM).
2. **Kalman Gain Computation:** It appears in the denominator of $K = P H^T S^{-1}$, controlling how strongly new measurements influence the state estimate.
3. **Consistency Monitoring:** A well-tuned filter produces innovations that are zero-mean with covariance $S$; deviations indicate filter divergence. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **predicted measurement** $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ and the **innovation covariance** $S(k+1)$ for a linear discrete-time system with measurement model $z(k+1) = H(k+1)\,x(k+1) + v(k+1)$, where $v$ is zero-mean white noise with covariance $R(k+1)$. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1D robot localization problem where the state is position $x$ and the measurement is also position (GPS-like). The predicted state estimate is $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) = 10.2$ m with predicted covariance $P(k+1 \mid k) = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$. The measurement matrix is $H = 1$ and the measurement noise covariance is $R = 0.09 \text{ m}^2$. The actual GPS reading is $z(k+1) = 9.8$ m. Compute:
- (i) The predicted measurement $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$
- (ii) The innovation covariance $S(k+1)$
- (iii) The Kalman Gain $K(k+1)$
- (iv) The innovation $\nu(k+1)$
- (v) The updated state estimate $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k+1)$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
- Linear measurement model: $z(k+1) = H(k+1)\,x(k+1) + v(k+1)$
- $v(k+1)$ is zero-mean: $\mathbb{E}[v(k+1)] = 0$
- $v(k+1)$ has covariance $R(k+1) = \mathbb{E}[v(k+1)v(k+1)^T]$
- $v(k+1)$ is independent of state estimation error $\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k)$
- A priori state estimate exists: $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) = \mathbb{E}[x(k+1) \mid z^k]$

*Derivation of $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$:*
Taking conditional expectation of both sides:
$$\mathbb{E}[z(k+1) \mid z^k] = H(k+1)\,\mathbb{E}[x(k+1) \mid z^k] + \mathbb{E}[v(k+1) \mid z^k]$$
Applying the assumptions:
$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) + 0$$
$$\boxed{\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)} \quad \textbf{(3 marks for derivation)}$$

*Derivation of $S(k+1)$:*
The innovation is $\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\tilde{x}(k+1 \mid k) + v(k+1)$.

$$S(k+1) = \mathbb{E}[\nu(k+1)\,\nu(k+1)^T]$$

Expanding and using independence:
$$\begin{aligned}
S(k+1) &= \mathbb{E}\left[(H\tilde{x} + v)(H\tilde{x} + v)^T\right] \\
&= H\,\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}\tilde{x}^T]\,H^T + H\,\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}\,v^T]}_{=0} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[v\tilde{x}^T]}_{=0}\,H^T + \mathbb{E}[v v^T] \\
&= H(k+1)\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T + R(k+1)
\end{aligned}$$
$$\boxed{S(k+1) = H(k+1)\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H(k+1)^T + R(k+1)} \quad \textbf{(3 marks for derivation)}$$

*Engineering significance (1 mark):* $S$ is symmetric positive-definite; guarantees invertibility for Kalman gain.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) = 10.2$, $P(k+1 \mid k) = 0.25$, $H = 1$, $R = 0.09$, $z(k+1) = 9.8$.

**(i) Predicted measurement:**
$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) = 1 \times 10.2 = 10.2 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Innovation covariance:**
$$S(k+1) = H\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H^T + R = (1)(0.25)(1) + 0.09 = 0.34 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(iii) Kalman Gain:**
$$K(k+1) = P(k+1 \mid k)\,H^T\,S(k+1)^{-1} = (0.25)(1)(1/0.34) = 0.7353 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(iv) Innovation:**
$$\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = 9.8 - 10.2 = -0.4 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(v) Updated state estimate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k+1) &= \hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) + K(k+1)\,\nu(k+1) \\
&= 10.2 + (0.7353)(-0.4) \\
&= 10.2 - 0.2941 \\
&= 9.9059 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* The updated estimate (9.91 m) lies between the prediction (10.2 m) and measurement (9.8 m), closer to the measurement because the Kalman Gain (0.7353) reflects the relative trust between model and sensor. **(1 mark for interpretation)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the role of measurement prediction in **robot localization and SLAM**. Discuss how the innovation covariance $S(k+1)$ is used in **outlier detection and data association** using the Mahalanobis distance criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot performs 2D range-bearing observation of a stationary landmark at $(x_L, y_L) = (8, 6)$ m. The predicted state estimate is $\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k) = [2,\; 3,\; 0.1]^T$ (position $x$, $y$ in m; heading $\phi$ in rad). The predicted covariance is:
$$P(k+1 \mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 & 0 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$$
The measurement noise covariance is $R = \text{diag}(0.04,\; 0.0025)$ (range in m$^2$, bearing in rad$^2$). The actual measurement received is $z = [6.05,\; 0.78]^T$. Compute:
- (i) The predicted measurement $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$
- (ii) The Jacobian $H$ evaluated at the predicted state
- (iii) The innovation covariance $S(k+1)$
- (iv) The innovation $\nu(k+1)$
- (v) The Mahalanobis distance $d^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ and comment on whether the measurement should be accepted (assume gating threshold $\chi^2_{2,0.95} = 5.99$) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

In **robot localization**, measurement prediction provides the *expected sensor signature* of known features (landmarks, walls) given the robot's current pose belief. This expectation is then compared with actual sensor returns to:
- **Localize:** Compute pose corrections via the Kalman update.
- **Detect features:** Confirm that a candidate landmark is genuinely present (vs. a spurious detection).
- **Plan sensing:** Direct the sensor field-of-view toward where features are expected.

In **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**, measurement prediction is even more critical because the map is being built simultaneously with the pose estimate. Each landmark in the map has a predicted observation; matching predictions to actual detections (data association) is the foundation of the SLAM front-end. Wrong associations cause catastrophic filter divergence.

**Outlier detection via Mahalanobis distance (4 marks):**
$$d^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu \sim \chi^2_m$$
where $m$ is the measurement dimension.

**Decision rule:**
- If $d^2 \leq \chi^2_{m, \alpha}$ (e.g., $\alpha = 0.95$), **accept** the measurement (it lies within the 95% confidence gate).
- If $d^2 > \chi^2_{m, \alpha}$, **reject** the measurement as an outlier (likely a false detection or wrong association).

This gating prevents a single bad measurement from corrupting the state estimate and is essential in cluttered environments (urban SLAM, indoor robotics). **(1 mark for engineering context: false positives in dense environments)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $(x_L, y_L) = (8, 6)$, $\hat{x} = [2, 3, 0.1]^T$.

**(i) Predicted measurement:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{r} &= \sqrt{(8-2)^2 + (6-3)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 9} = \sqrt{45} = 6.7082 \text{ m} \\
\hat{\theta} &= \text{atan2}(6-3,\; 8-2) - 0.1 = \text{atan2}(3, 6) - 0.1 = 0.4636 - 0.1 = 0.3636 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$
$$\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 6.7082 \\ 0.3636 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Jacobian $H$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \begin{bmatrix} (\hat{x}_r - x_L)/\hat{r} & (\hat{y}_r - y_L)/\hat{r} & 0 \\ (y_L - \hat{y}_r)/\hat{r}^2 & (\hat{x}_r - x_L)/\hat{r}^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (2-8)/6.7082 & (3-6)/6.7082 & 0 \\ (6-3)/45 & (2-8)/45 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.8944 & -0.4472 & 0 \\ 0.0667 & -0.1333 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Innovation covariance $S$:**
First compute $H P H^T$:
$$\begin{aligned}
H P &= \begin{bmatrix} -0.8944 & -0.4472 & 0 \\ 0.0667 & -0.1333 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 & 0 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.0402 & -0.0491 & 0 \\ 0.00133 & -0.01133 & -0.01 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H P H^T &= \begin{bmatrix} -0.0402 & -0.0491 & 0 \\ 0.00133 & -0.01133 & -0.01 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.8944 & 0.0667 \\ -0.4472 & -0.1333 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.0579 & 0.00387 \\ -0.00388 & 0.00155 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Adding $R$:
$$S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0579 + 0.04 & 0.00387 \\ -0.00388 & 0.00155 + 0.0025 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0979 & 0.00387 \\ -0.00388 & 0.00405 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Innovation:**
$$\nu = z - \hat{z} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.05 - 6.7082 \\ 0.78 - 0.3636 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6582 \\ 0.4164 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**(v) Mahalanobis distance:**
Determinant: $|S| = (0.0979)(0.00405) - (0.00387)(-0.00388) = 3.965 \times 10^{-4} + 1.501 \times 10^{-5} = 4.115 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$S^{-1} = \frac{1}{4.115 \times 10^{-4}} \begin{bmatrix} 0.00405 & -0.00387 \\ 0.00388 & 0.0979 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9.844 & -9.405 \\ 9.430 & 237.9 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
d^2 &= \nu^T S^{-1} \nu \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.6582 & 0.4164 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 9.844 & -9.405 \\ 9.430 & 237.9 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.6582 \\ 0.4164 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -6.479 + 3.927 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.6582 \\ 0.4164 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= (-2.552)(-0.6582) + (-95.93)(0.4164) \quad \text{(intermediate)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Recomputing carefully:
$$\nu^T S^{-1} = [-0.6582 \times 9.844 + 0.4164 \times 9.430,\; -0.6582 \times (-9.405) + 0.4164 \times 237.9]$$
$$= [-6.480 + 3.927,\; 6.190 + 99.06] = [-2.553,\; 105.25]$$

$$d^2 = (-2.553)(-0.6582) + (105.25)(0.4164) = 1.681 + 43.82 = 45.50 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Decision:** Since $d^2 = 45.50 \gg \chi^2_{2,0.95} = 5.99$, the measurement **should be REJECTED** as an outlier. The bearing component is highly inconsistent with the predicted state, suggesting a wrong data association or a spurious detection. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\hat{z}$ with $z$:** Many students write $\hat{z} = H z$ instead of $\hat{z} = H \hat{x}$. This is a **2ŌĆō3 mark deduction** in derivation problems.
> 2. **Forgetting the assumption $E[v]=0$:** The derivation of $\hat{z}$ requires explicit mention that measurement noise is zero-mean. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong sign in innovation:** Some students compute $\hat{z} - z$. The standard convention is $\nu = z - \hat{z}$. Marks lost: **0.5ŌĆō1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the Jacobian for nonlinear sensors:** KTU specifically tests $H$ derivation for range-bearing models. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Not interpreting the Mahalanobis result:** Numerical problems without an *engineering comment* (accept/reject decision) lose **1 mark**.
> 6. **Misstating dimensions of $S$ and $K$:** Always state units explicitly; examiners penalize sloppy dimensional analysis.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Predicted measurement $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k) = H(k+1)\,\hat{x}(k+1 \mid k)$ is the *expected sensor reading* based on current state belief. **(Core equation)**
- **Innovation:** $\nu(k+1) = z(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ ŌĆö the new information.
- **Innovation Covariance:** $S(k+1) = H\,P(k+1 \mid k)\,H^T + R$ ŌĆö the confidence of the prediction.
- **Kalman Gain:** $K = P H^T S^{-1}$ ŌĆö the optimal blending weight.
