- **Mahalanobis Test:** $d^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu \sim \chi^2_m$; gating threshold $\chi^2_{m,0.95}$ decides accept/reject.
- **Nonlinear (EKF) case:** $\hat{z} = h(\hat{x})$ and $H = \partial h / \partial x \big|_{\hat{x}}$ ŌĆö always linearize about the predicted state.
- **Order in Kalman cycle:** Predict state ŌåÆ Predict covariance ŌåÆ **Predict measurement** ŌåÆ Compute $S$ ŌåÆ Gain ŌåÆ Innovation ŌåÆ Update.
- **Use case in SLAM:** Data association relies on matching predicted observations to actual detections within the Mahalanobis gate.
- **Filter health:** A consistent filter has innovations that are zero-mean and uncorrelated, with covariance equal to $S$.
- **Common KTU trap:** Always write $\hat{z}(k+1 \mid k)$ with the full conditional notation ŌĆö sloppy notation loses marks.
- **Sanity check:** A correctly tuned $K$ lies in $[0, 1]$ for scalar systems; $K \to 1$ trusts sensor, $K \to 0$ trusts model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Matching** in the context of robot navigation is the computational process of establishing correspondences between sensor observations (current measurements) and a reference model, prior map, or previously stored features. It is a foundational operation in **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**, **place recognition**, **loop closure detection**, **visual odometry**, and **localization** subsystems.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, matching encompasses three principal problem formulations:

1. **Feature Matching**: Establishing point-to-point correspondences between two sets of visual features (e.g., keypoints detected in two camera frames or between a live image and a stored map).
2. **Scan Matching**: Aligning two consecutive or geometrically separated sets of range measurements (laser scans, depth point clouds) by estimating the rigid-body transformation that best overlaps them.
3. **Data Association**: Determining which sensor observation corresponds to which previously observed landmark, crucial for maintaining consistent estimates in probabilistic filters (EKF-SLAM, particle filters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Matching is the bridge between *perception* (raw sensor data) and *estimation* (pose, map). Without a robust matching step, a robot cannot tell "where it has been" or "what it is seeing."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a tourist in **Kerala** visiting the city of **Kochi** for the second time. As you walk through **Marine Drive**, you see a distinctive landmark ŌĆö the **Chinese fishing net** silhouetted against the sunset. Your brain instantly says: *"I have seen this exact shape before ŌĆö this is the place I visited last week."* This cognitive operation of **comparing the current visual scene to a stored memory and finding a correspondence** is precisely what *matching* does for a robot.

A few more everyday analogies:
- **Fingerprint matching** by a biometric system ŌåÆ analogous to **feature descriptor matching** (SIFT, ORB).
- **Puzzle piece assembly** where you rotate and slide two pieces until they fit ŌåÆ analogous to **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** scan matching.
- **Crossword solving** where you match clues to answers ŌåÆ analogous to **data association** in probabilistic filters.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are standard performance metrics used to evaluate a matching algorithm in robot navigation:

- **Inlier Ratio**: Proportion of correct correspondences to total putative matches. Expressed as a **percentage (0ŌĆō100%)**.
- **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)**: Average geometric distance between matched points. Units: **meters (m)** for range scans, **pixels (px)** for images.
- **Repeatability**: Fraction of features detected in two views of the same scene. **Unitless ratio (0ŌĆō1)**.
- **Recall and Precision**: Standard information retrieval metrics. **Unitless ratios (0ŌĆō1)**.
- **Frame Rate**: Matching throughput. Units: **Frames Per Second (FPS)** or **Hertz (Hz)**.

> [!NOTE]
> A robust matching algorithm must be **invariant to scale, rotation, illumination changes, and partial occlusions** ŌĆö these four properties form the holy grail of feature matching research in robotics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Matching Problem ŌĆö Formal Statement

Given two sets of observations $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$ and $B = \{b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n\}$ (where each element may be a 2D image keypoint, a 3D LiDAR point, or a landmark descriptor), the matching problem is to find a correspondence function:

$$\mathcal{M}: A \rightarrow B \cup \{\emptyset\}$$

such that each $a_i$ is paired with its most plausible counterpart $b_j$, or marked as unmatched ($\emptyset$). The function $\mathcal{M}$ must minimize a distance cost (or maximize a similarity score) while respecting geometric consistency.

## 2.2 Operational Pipeline of a Generic Matching System

The matching process in robot navigation follows a structured pipeline:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Detection**: Identify salient, repeatable points (corners, blobs, edges) in the sensor data. *Why?* Salient points are stable across viewpoints, giving reliable anchors for matching.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Description**: Encode the local neighborhood of each detected point into a compact descriptor vector (e.g., 128-D for SIFT, 32-B for ORB). *Why?* Descriptors allow comparison of points in a mathematically tractable form.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Pairwise Comparison**: Compute a distance (Euclidean, Hamming, Mahalanobis) between every descriptor in set $A$ and every descriptor in set $B$. *Why?* This produces a raw similarity matrix for candidate matches.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Filtering**: Apply ratio test (Lowe), mutual nearest neighbor check, or geometric verification (RANSAC) to eliminate spurious matches. *Why?* Pure descriptor matching is prone to ambiguities in repetitive scenes.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Estimation**: Use the surviving inlier correspondences to estimate the robot's motion (visual odometry) or to confirm a loop closure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Symbols Explained** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euclidean Distance between descriptors | $d(a,b) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{K} (a_k - b_k)^2}$ | $K$ = descriptor dimension; $a_k, b_k$ = components | Unitless (descriptor space) |
| Hamming Distance (binary descriptors) | $d_H(a,b) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} a_k \oplus b_k$ | $\oplus$ = XOR operation | Counts differing bits |
| Lowe's Ratio Test | $\frac{d_1}{d_2} < \tau$ | $d_1$ = best match; $d_2$ = second-best match; $\tau \approx 0.7$ŌĆō$0.8$ | $\tau$ is dimensionless |
| ICP Objective Function | $E(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{R} p_i + \mathbf{t} - q_i \rVert^2$ | $\mathbf{R}$ = rotation; $\mathbf{t}$ = translation; $p_i, q_i$ = matched points | Minimized over iterations |
| SIFT Scale-space (Difference of Gaussians) | $D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$ | $L$ = Gaussian-blurred image; $k$ = scale multiplier | Scale-space extrema |
| Harris Corner Response | $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - \alpha \, \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$ | $\mathbf{M}$ = structure tensor; $\alpha \in [0.04, 0.06]$ | Unitless response |
| Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $TP$ = true positives; $FP$ = false positives | Ratio [0, 1] |
| Recall | $R_{ec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $FN$ = false negatives | Ratio [0, 1] |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 P R_{ec}}{P + R_{ec}}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R_{ec}$ | Ratio [0, 1] |
| Fundamental Matrix Constraint | $x'^T \mathbf{F} x = 0$ | $x, x'$ = matched points; $\mathbf{F}$ = 3├Ś3 fundamental matrix | Epipolar geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Scan matching using **ICP** is the workhorse of 2D LiDAR SLAM (e.g., **Hector SLAM**, **Cartographer**). Feature matching using **ORB** is the workhorse of **ORB-SLAM**, the most cited open-source visual SLAM system. Both are routinely asked in KTU ESE questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Visual feature matching supports lane-keep assist, traffic sign recognition, and visual localization against HD maps.
- **Warehouse Robots (AMR)**: Scan matching of LiDAR sweeps enables a robot to relocalize within a pre-built map after being moved.
- **Aerial Drones (UAV)**: Downward-looking camera frames are matched to satellite imagery for **GPS-denied navigation**.
- **Underwater ROVs**: Sonar scan matching enables mapping in turbid water where cameras fail.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, ISRO Chandrayaan)**: Stereo image matching produces terrain elevation maps for hazard avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: The Iterative Closest Point (ICP) Algorithm

ICP is the canonical algorithm for **scan matching** and appears in nearly every KTU board exam on robot navigation. We derive it step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N\}$ be the **source point cloud** (current scan) and $\mathcal{Q} = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_M\}$ be the **target point cloud** (reference scan or map). We seek the rigid transformation $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ that aligns $\mathcal{P}$ to $\mathcal{Q}$, where $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ (3D) or $SO(2)$ (2D) and $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (or $\mathbb{R}^2$).

### 3.1.2 Objective Function

$$\mathbf{R}^*, \mathbf{t}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \lVert \mathbf{R} p_i + \mathbf{t} - q_{c(i)} \rVert^2$$

Here, $c(i)$ denotes the index of the closest point in $\mathcal{Q}$ to the transformed source point, and $w_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is an inlier weight.

### 3.1.3 Closed-Form Solution (2D Case for KTU Exam Simplicity)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute centroids** of both matched point sets:

$$\bar{p} = \frac{1}{N_w} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i p_i, \quad \bar{q} = \frac{1}{N_w} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i q_{c(i)}$$

where $N_w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i$ is the number of weighted correspondences.

*Logic*: We recenter both clouds to remove the translation component from the rotation estimation.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the cross-covariance matrix**:

$$\mathbf{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i (p_i - \bar{p})(q_{c(i)} - \bar{q})^T$$

*Logic*: $\mathbf{H}$ captures the spatial correlation between the two recentered point sets; its singular value decomposition yields the optimal rotation.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Perform SVD** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{V}^T$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute optimal rotation**:

$$\mathbf{R}^* = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{U}^T$$

*Logic*: This arises from the **Procrustes problem** (orthogonal Procrustes). The reflection correction term is $\det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^T) = +1$ to enforce a proper rotation.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute optimal translation**:

$$\mathbf{t}^* = \bar{q} - \mathbf{R}^* \bar{p}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Iterate** by re-establishing closest-point correspondences using the new $(\mathbf{R}^*, \mathbf{t}^*)$ and re-estimating until convergence (change in RMSE $< \epsilon$).

### 3.1.4 Convergence Conditions (KTU High-Yield)

- ICP converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum. Good initialization is critical.
- Convergence is guaranteed in the sense that the cost function is non-increasing at each iteration.
- Computational complexity per iteration: $\mathcal{O}(N M)$ for naive closest-point search, reducible to $\mathcal{O}(N \log M)$ using **k-d trees**.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Lowe's Ratio Test (SIFT Matching Threshold)

The Lowe ratio test filters ambiguous matches where the second-nearest neighbor is nearly as good as the first.

**Setup:** For descriptor $a_i$ in image $A$, find the two nearest descriptors $b_{j_1}$ and $b_{j_2}$ in image $B$ with distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ respectively.

**Decision rule:** Accept the match if and only if

$$\frac{d_1}{d_2} < \tau$$

A typical value used in OpenCV implementations is $\tau = 0.75$. This is **empirically derived** by David Lowe (2004) to retain ~95% correct matches while eliminating ~90% false matches.

**Probabilistic justification:** For a correct match, the ratio $d_1/d_2$ is expected to be small (the correct match is distinctly closer). For an incorrect match, the distances to the nearest and second-nearest descriptors are statistically similar, so the ratio is close to 1.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Matching Methods

| **Method** | **Type** | **Descriptor Dim.** | **Invariance** | **Best Use Case** | **Complexity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SIFT | Feature (gradient histogram) | 128-D float | Scale + Rotation + Illumination | Offline mapping, high-accuracy loop closure | High |
| SURF | Feature (Haar wavelet) | 64-D float | Scale + Rotation (faster than SIFT) | Real-time applications with GPUs | Medium-High |
| ORB | Feature (binary BRIEF + orientation) | 32-B binary | Rotation + partial scale | Embedded systems, ORB-SLAM | Low |
| Harris Corner | Detector only (no descriptor) | N/A | Rotation only | Initial keypoint detection | Low |
| ICP | Scan matching | N/A (geometric) | None (requires good init.) | LiDAR SLAM, point cloud registration | Medium |
| NDT (Normal Distributions Transform) | Scan matching | N/A (statistical) | None | Large-scale outdoor LiDAR | Low-Medium |
| FLANN-based | Matching accelerator | N/A | N/A | Speeding up nearest-neighbor search | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic Feature-Matching Pipeline in Robot Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[RobotSensorInput] --> A1[PreprocessingGaussianBlur]
    A1 --> A2[KeypointDetectorSIFT_ORB_Harris]
    A2 --> A3[DescriptorExtraction128D_or_32B]
    A3 --> A4[DescriptorDatabaseReferenceMap]
    A3 --> A5[DescriptorDatabaseCurrentFrame]
    A4 --> A6[NearestNeighborSearchKDTree]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[PutativeCorrespondencesList]
    A7 --> A8[LoweRatioTesttau075]
    A8 --> A9[GeometricVerificationRANSAC]
    A9 --> A10[InlierCorrespondencesFinalSet]
    A10 --> A11[PoseEstimationPnP_or_Epipolar]
    A10 --> A12[LoopClosureConfirmationforSLAM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The robot acquires a sensor frame, detects salient keypoints, computes descriptors, and compares them against a reference database. A multi-stage filter (Lowe + RANSAC) discards outliers, leaving a clean set of inliers that drive downstream tasks such as pose estimation or loop closure.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: ICP Scan-Matching Iteration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[CurrentScanPointCloud] --> B2[InitializeTransformIdentity]
    B2 --> B3[ForEachSourcePointFindClosestTargetPoint]
    B3 --> B4[ComputeCentroids]
    B4 --> B5[ComputeCrossCovarianceH]
    B5 --> B6[SVDoftheMatrixH]
    B6 --> B7[ComputeOptimalRotationR]
    B7 --> B8[ComputeOptimalTranslationt]
    B8 --> B9[ApplyTransformtoSourcePoints]
    B9 --> B10[ComputeNewRMSError]
    B10 --> B11{ConvergedErrorBelowEpsilon}
    B11 -- NO --> B3
    B11 -- YES --> B12[OutputFinalAlignmentR_t]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Data Association in Probabilistic SLAM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[PredictedMeasurementzhat] --> C2[ValidationGateMahalanobisDistance]
    C2 --> C3[Innovationnu_eq_z_minus_zhat]
    C3 --> C4[ComputeLikelihoodforallLandmarks]
    C4 --> C5[IndividualCompatibilityTest]
    C5 --> C6{NearestNeighbororJNDFilter}
    C6 -- Valid --> C7[UpdateKalmanFilterorParticleWeight]
    C6 -- Invalid --> C8[CreateNewLandmark]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In EKF-SLAM, data association decides whether an observed feature is a *previously mapped landmark* (update step) or a *newly discovered landmark* (map augmentation). A wrong decision here causes catastrophic filter divergence ŌĆö this is the **single most common failure mode in feature-based SLAM**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define "feature matching" in the context of robot navigation. List any two feature descriptors commonly used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Feature matching is the process of establishing correspondences between salient points detected in two sensor datasets (e.g., images, LiDAR scans) by comparing their local descriptors. **(1 mark)**

Two commonly used feature descriptors are:
1. **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)** ŌĆö a 128-dimensional float descriptor invariant to scale, rotation, and illumination. **(1 mark)**
2. **ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)** ŌĆö a 32-byte binary descriptor that is rotation-invariant and computationally efficient, widely used in ORB-SLAM. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is Lowe's ratio test, and why is it used in feature matching?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lowe's ratio test compares the distance of the best match $d_1$ to the distance of the second-best match $d_2$ for a given descriptor. **(1 mark)**

The match is accepted only if the ratio $d_1/d_2 < \tau$ (typically $\tau = 0.7$ to $0.8$). **(1 mark)**

It is used to filter out **ambiguous matches** in repetitive-texture scenes, where multiple descriptors may be nearly equidistant, indicating that the nearest match is likely spurious. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm for scan matching in detail. State its objective function and list the steps of the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the limitations of ICP. What alternative methods can be used when a good initial alignment is unavailable? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) ICP Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** ICP is an iterative algorithm that aligns two point clouds by alternately finding closest-point correspondences and computing the rigid transformation $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ that minimizes the sum of squared distances between corresponding points.

**Objective Function (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{R}^*, \mathbf{t}^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \lVert \mathbf{R} p_i + \mathbf{t} - q_{c(i)} \rVert^2$$

where $p_i$ is a point in the source cloud, $q_{c(i)}$ is its closest point in the target cloud, and $w_i$ is a binary inlier weight.

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Initialization:** Set $\mathbf{R}_0 = \mathbf{I}$, $\mathbf{t}_0 = \mathbf{0}$. (or use odometry as prior).
2. **Correspondence:** For each $p_i$, find the closest $q_{c(i)} \in \mathcal{Q}$ using k-d tree search.
3. **Centroid Computation:** $\bar{p} = \frac{1}{N_w}\sum w_i p_i$, $\bar{q} = \frac{1}{N_w}\sum w_i q_{c(i)}$.
4. **Cross-covariance:** $\mathbf{H} = \sum w_i (p_i - \bar{p})(q_{c(i)} - \bar{q})^T$.
5. **SVD:** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{V}^T$, then $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^T$.
6. **Translation:** $\mathbf{t} = \bar{q} - \mathbf{R}\bar{p}$.
7. **Apply & Re-iterate:** Transform source points, recompute correspondences, repeat until RMSE change $< \epsilon$.

---

**(b) Limitations and Alternatives (7 marks):**

**Limitations of ICP (4 marks):**
1. **Local minima:** ICP only converges to a local minimum; poor initialization causes divergence to wrong alignment.
2. **No global search:** It cannot recover from large initial misalignment (typically < 30┬░ rotation).
3. **Computational cost:** Naive closest-point search is $\mathcal{O}(NM)$; k-d tree reduces it but still expensive for large clouds.
4. **Sensitivity to outliers:** Stray points or dynamic objects cause erroneous correspondences.
5. **Assumes overlap:** Requires sufficient overlap between the two scans; fails on disjoint clouds.

**Alternatives when initialization is poor (3 marks):**
1. **Normal Distributions Transform (NDT):** Represents the target cloud as a grid of Gaussian distributions; aligns the source using gradient-based optimization. Robust to initialization.
2. **Feature-based pre-alignment:** Extract corners or line segments, use RANSAC to estimate a coarse transformation, then refine with ICP.
3. **Global registration methods:** **4-Points Congruent Sets (4PCS)** or **Fast Global Registration (FGR)** find globally optimal alignments without requiring initialization.
4. **Branch-and-bound methods** (e.g., Go-ICP) provide globally optimal alignment at high computational cost.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, describe the SIFT feature detection and matching pipeline. Explain how SIFT achieves scale and rotation invariance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the RANSAC algorithm as used in geometric verification of feature matches. Why is it preferred over least-squares fitting for this purpose? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) SIFT Pipeline (7 marks):**

**Scale-space Extrema Detection (2 marks):**
Construct a scale-space by convolving the image with Gaussian kernels of varying $\sigma$, then compute the **Difference of Gaussians (DoG)**:

$$D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$$

Keypoints are local extrema of $D$ across adjacent scales. This achieves **scale invariance** because the same physical structure produces a DoG extremum at a scale proportional to its size.

**Keypoint Localization (1 mark):**
Reject low-contrast and edge-response keypoints by fitting a 3D quadratic to the DoG function and inspecting the Hessian.

**Orientation Assignment (2 marks):**
Compute gradient magnitude and orientation in a neighborhood around each keypoint; build a 36-bin orientation histogram weighted by gradient magnitude. The peak orientation is assigned as the keypoint's canonical orientation. This achieves **rotation invariance** because all subsequent descriptor computation is performed relative to this orientation.

**Descriptor Computation (2 marks):**
Divide the $16 \times 16$ neighborhood into $4 \times 4$ sub-blocks. In each sub-block, build an 8-bin orientation histogram. Concatenate the $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$ values to form the SIFT descriptor. Normalize to unit length for **illumination invariance**.

---

**(b) RANSAC for Geometric Verification (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Outline (3 marks):**
1. Randomly sample the **minimum number of correspondences** required to estimate the model (e.g., 4 points for homography, 8 for fundamental matrix).
2. Estimate the model parameters from this minimal sample.
3. Count the number of inliers ŌĆö correspondences whose residual to the model is below a threshold $\epsilon$.
4. Repeat steps 1ŌĆō3 for $K$ iterations.
5. Re-estimate the model using all inliers of the best iteration.

**Iteration count formula (1 mark):**

$$K = \frac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}$$

where $p$ = desired probability of success (e.g., 0.99), $w$ = inlier ratio, $s$ = sample size.

**Why RANSAC is preferred over least-squares (3 marks):**
1. **Robustness to outliers:** Least-squares is heavily biased by even a single outlier. RANSAC explicitly identifies and discards outliers.
2. **No closed-form global solution needed:** RANSAC works for models where no analytical least-squares solution exists.
3. **Probabilistic guarantee:** With sufficient iterations, RANSAC finds the correct inlier set with a user-specified probability.
4. **Threshold controllable:** The inlier threshold $\epsilon$ can be tuned to the sensor noise model.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Matching Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the convergence condition in ICP:** Many students write the algorithm steps but forget to mention the convergence check (RMSE change below threshold or maximum iterations reached). Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for this.
> 2. **Confusing RANSAC with least-squares:** RANSAC is a *robust estimation* technique, not a fitting technique. Writing "RANSAC fits a line" loses a mark; the correct phrasing is "RANSAC identifies inliers that are then used to fit a model."
> 3. **Omitting the initialization issue in ICP:** Stating "ICP is used for scan matching" without noting its **local-minimum problem** and need for good initialization is incomplete. Always pair ICP with an initialization strategy (odometry, NDT pre-alignment, or feature-based coarse alignment).
> 4. **Wrong ratio-test threshold:** Writing $\tau = 0.5$ or $\tau = 0.9$ instead of the standard $0.7$ŌĆō$0.8$ range indicates a superficial understanding and may cost a mark.
> 5. **Mixing up SIFT and ORB dimensions:** SIFT is **128-D float**, ORB is **32-byte binary**. Examiners do test this specific fact.
> 6. **Skipping units in formulas:** ICP objective function is in squared meters (or squared pixels); always state units for full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Matching** = establishing correspondences between sensor observations and a reference (map, prior frame, landmark database).
- **Three flavors of matching** you must know: *feature matching* (images), *scan matching* (LiDAR), *data association* (filter updates).
- **SIFT pipeline** in order: *Scale-space DoG ŌåÆ Keypoint localization ŌåÆ Orientation assignment ŌåÆ 128-D descriptor ŌåÆ Descriptor matching + Lowe's ratio test.*
- **ORB** = rotation-invariant FAST detector + rotation-aware BRIEF descriptor; binary 32-byte; ideal for embedded visual SLAM.
- **ICP objective**: minimize sum of squared distances between corresponding points over a rigid transformation $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$.
- **ICP solution** uses **centroids, cross-covariance, and SVD** ŌĆö derive this from the orthogonal Procrustes problem.
- **ICP limitations**: local minima, sensitive to initialization, no outlier rejection natively, requires overlap.
- **NDT** is a stronger alternative when initialization is unknown.
- **Lowe's ratio test** threshold $\tau \approx 0.75$; ratio of best-to-second-best distance.
- **RANSAC** is used for **geometric verification** of putative matches and provides **probabilistic inlier identification** in the presence of outliers.
- **RANSAC iteration count**: $K = \frac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^s)}$ ŌĆö memorize this formula.
- **Performance metrics**: Precision, Recall, F1-Score, RMSE, Inlier Ratio, Repeatability.
- **Epipolar constraint** for stereo matching: $x'^T \mathbf{F} x = 0$ ŌĆö used as a geometric verification test.
- **Data association algorithms**: nearest-neighbor, gated nearest-neighbor, Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB), Joint Probabilistic Data Association (JPDA).
- **Practical insight**: ORB-SLAM uses **ORB features + DBoW2 bag-of-words place recognition + pose graph optimization** ŌĆö this is the full pipeline that KTU often asks you to "explain the architecture of."

### Key Takeaway Sentence (Write This Last Thing Before Submitting the Exam)

> *"Matching converts raw sensor data into geometrically consistent correspondences, enabling a robot to localize itself, recognize previously visited places, and build maps ŌĆö it is the perceptual foundation of every modern SLAM system."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Estimation" in Robot Navigation?

In mobile robotics, **Estimation** refers to the mathematical process of inferring the **internal state** of a robot (its pose, velocity, position of landmarks, map, etc.) from **noisy sensor measurements** and **imperfect control inputs**. Since robots operate in stochastic, real-world environments where sensors are noisy and actuators are imprecise, estimation theory provides the probabilistic backbone for answering: *"Where am I?"* and *"What does the world look like?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Bayesian Sense):**
> Estimation in robotics is the recursive computation of the posterior probability distribution $bel(x_t)$ over the robot state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all available sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$, expressed as:
> $$bel(x_t) \;\triangleq\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$
> This is called the **belief** about the state.

### Conceptual Analogy: "Estimation is a Detective's Reasoning"

Imagine you are a detective in a dark room trying to figure out where a hidden criminal is standing.

- **Your prior guess** ("He is probably near the door") is your *prior belief*.
- **You hear a footstep** from the left ŌåÆ that is a *measurement*.
- **You remember the criminal was seen moving right** 5 seconds ago ŌåÆ that is *control input / motion prediction*.
- **You combine** the footstep evidence with the prior movement to *update* your guess.

Estimation is exactly this. The robot maintains a **belief distribution** (where it *thinks* it is), **predicts** its next state from motion, and **corrects** that prediction using sensor measurements. Each new piece of evidence tightens (or sometimes reweights) the belief, much like a detective narrowing down suspects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Robotic Estimation** (must remember for KTU viva):
> 1. **Bayes Filter** ŌĆö the universal, generic recursive estimator.
> 2. **Kalman Filter (KF)** ŌĆö the optimal solution for **linear Gaussian** systems.
> 3. **Particle Filter (PF)** ŌĆö a non-parametric, sample-based estimator for **non-linear, non-Gaussian** systems.

### 1.2 The Estimation Problem ŌĆö Formal Setup

| Symbol | Meaning | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Robot state at time $t$ | Pose $(x, y, \theta)$, velocity, etc. |
| $u_t$ | Control input at $t$ | Wheel velocities, steering angle |
| $z_t$ | Measurement at $t$ | Range scan, GPS fix, camera feature |
| $bel(x_t)$ | Belief at $t$ | Probability distribution over $x_t$ |
| $\bar{bel}(x_t)$ | Predicted belief (after motion) | Belief *before* incorporating $z_t$ |
| $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Motion model | How state evolves with control |
| $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ | Measurement model | Likelihood of a sensor reading given state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayes Filter Assumption (Markov Property):** The state $x_t$ is *complete*, meaning it summarizes all past information. The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present. This is what makes recursive estimation possible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes Filter ŌĆö The Mother of All Estimators

The Bayes Filter is a **two-step recursive algorithm**:

### Step 1: Prediction (Motion Update)
Propagate the belief forward through the motion model, *before* seeing the new measurement:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Why?** Because the robot *moved* by applying $u_t$, so every possible previous state $x_{t-1}$ could have transitioned to a new state $x_t$. We integrate (marginalize) over all such possibilities.

### Step 2: Correction (Measurement Update)
Incorporate the new measurement $z_t$ via Bayes' rule:

$$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \bar{bel}(x_t)$$

where the normalizing constant is:

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} \;=\; \left[\int p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \bar{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t\right]^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** "Predict where you will be, then correct based on what your sensors see." This is the famous *predictŌĆōcorrect* (or *predictŌĆōupdate*) loop that runs at every timestep in any mobile robot.

### 2.2 The Kalman Filter ŌĆö Optimal Linear Gaussian Estimator

When both the motion and measurement models are **linear** AND the noise is **Gaussian**, the Bayes Filter has a closed-form, optimal solution: the **Kalman Filter (KF)**.

#### State-Space Model
$$x_t \;=\; A_t \, x_{t-1} \;+\; B_t \, u_t \;+\; \epsilon_t, \qquad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

$$z_t \;=\; C_t \, x_t \;+\; \delta_t, \qquad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

#### Kalman Filter ŌĆö Predict Step
$$\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; A_t \, \mu_{t-1} \;+\; B_t \, u_t$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; A_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, A_t^{T} \;+\; R_t$$

#### Kalman Filter ŌĆö Update Step
$$K_t \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T} \, (C_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T} \;+\; Q_t)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_t \;=\; \bar{\mu}_t \;+\; K_t \, (z_t \;-\; C_t \, \bar{\mu}_t)$$

$$\Sigma_t \;=\; (I \;-\; K_t \, C_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

The matrix $K_t$ is the **Kalman Gain**. It balances *trust in prediction* vs *trust in measurement*.

### 2.3 The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)

Real robots are non-linear. The **EKF** linearizes the non-linear functions $g(\cdot)$ (motion) and $h(\cdot)$ (measurement) about the current estimate using first-order **Taylor expansion** (Jacobians).

$$x_t \;=\; g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \;+\; \epsilon_t \quad\Rightarrow\quad G_t \;=\; \frac{\partial g}{\partial x_{t-1}}\bigg|_{\mu_{t-1}}$$

$$z_t \;=\; h(x_t) \;+\; \delta_t \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_t \;=\; \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t}\bigg|_{\bar{\mu}_t}$$

The KF equations are then used with $A_t \to G_t$ and $C_t \to H_t$.

### 2.4 The Particle Filter

When the posterior is **multi-modal** or the system is **highly non-linear / non-Gaussian** (e.g., global localization from scratch), neither KF nor EKF work. The **Particle Filter** approximates the belief using a finite set of weighted samples $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

| Step | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling** | $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ | Predict each particle |
| **Importance Weighting** | $w_t^{[i]} \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Likelihood of measurement |
| **Resampling** | Draw $N$ particles proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$ | Focus on probable states |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** Particle filters power **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)**, the de-facto algorithm for robot localization with range finders and ambiguous global positioning (the famous "kidnapped robot problem").

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU ESE on this topic. Memorize the structure ŌĆö you do not need to memorize the matrix sizes, but you MUST remember the *order* of operations.

| # | Equation | Name | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes Prediction | Generic / Theoretical |
| 2 | $bel(x_t) = \eta\,p(z_t \mid x_t)\,\bar{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayes Correction | Generic / Theoretical |
| 3 | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | KF Mean Prediction | Linear Gaussian |
| 4 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^{T} + R_t$ | KF Covariance Prediction | Linear Gaussian |
| 5 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{T} (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{T} + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | Linear Gaussian |
| 6 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ | KF Mean Update | Linear Gaussian |
| 7 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | KF Covariance Update | Linear Gaussian |
| 8 | $G_t = \partial g / \partial x_{t-1}$ | EKF Motion Jacobian | Non-linear |
| 9 | $H_t = \partial h / \partial x_t$ | EKF Measurement Jacobian | Non-linear |
| 10 | $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle Weight | Non-Gaussian |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Self-driving cars:** Multi-sensor fusion (GPS + IMU + LiDAR + camera) via EKF and Particle Filters to estimate vehicle pose in real time.
- **Drone navigation:** EKF-based state estimation for attitude, altitude, and position (Pixhawk, ArduPilot).
- **AR/VR headsets:** Kalman filtering of IMU data for orientation tracking.
- **Spacecraft:** NASA's KF-based orbit determination has been used since the Apollo missions.
- **Warehouse robots (AMR):** Monte Carlo Localization for navigating among shelves with no fixed infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Correction Step

We start from the definition of the belief and apply the definition of conditional probability:

$$bel(x_t) \;\triangleq\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

By conditioning only on the most recent measurement $z_t$ and using the chain rule:

$$bel(x_t) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

By the **Markov property** (state is complete, $x_t$ subsumes the past), the measurement likelihood simplifies:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

Also, the denominator $p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is independent of $x_t$, so it becomes a normalizing constant $\eta$:

$$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

Recognizing that $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \bar{bel}(x_t)$, we get the famous Bayes correction:

$$\boxed{\,bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \bar{bel}(x_t)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the foundation of ALL robotics estimation:** Every filter ŌĆö Kalman, EKF, Particle, Information ŌĆö is just a *different way of representing and computing* this single equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Prediction Step

Starting from Bayes prediction:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Assumption 1 (Linear motion):** $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

**Assumption 2 (Gaussian belief at $t-1$):** $bel(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$.

Since the convolution of a Gaussian with a linear-Gaussian model is Gaussian, the predicted belief is also Gaussian:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)$$

The mean is obtained by taking the expectation:

$$\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; E[x_t] \;=\; E[A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t]$$

Since $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$ and $E[x_{t-1}] = \mu_{t-1}$:

$$\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; A_t \, \mu_{t-1} \;+\; B_t \, u_t$$

The covariance is obtained from $\mathrm{Var}(x_t) = E[(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T]$:

$$x_t - \bar{\mu}_t \;=\; A_t(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) \;+\; \epsilon_t$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; A_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, A_t^{T} \;+\; R_t$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Kalman Gain $K_t$

We minimize the posterior covariance trace (mean squared error). Start with the updated covariance:

$$\Sigma_t \;=\; (I - K_t C_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

The MSE cost is $\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma_t)$. Differentiate with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial K_t} \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma_t) \;=\; -2 (C_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t)^{T} \;+\; 2 K_t \, C_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T} \;=\; 0$$

Solving for $K_t$:

$$K_t \, C_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T} \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T}$$

$$\boxed{\,K_t \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T} \, (C_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, C_t^{T} \;+\; Q_t)^{-1}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of $K_t$:**
> - If $Q_t \to \infty$ (sensor very noisy) $\Rightarrow K_t \to 0$ ŌåÆ trust prediction fully.
> - If $Q_t \to 0$ (sensor perfect) $\Rightarrow K_t \to C_t^{-1}$ ŌåÆ trust measurement fully.
> - $K_t$ is a *soft switch* between trusting the model and trusting the sensor.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (1-D Kalman Filter)

A 1-D robot moves with constant velocity. State $x = [p, v]^T$. Suppose:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

With $\Delta t = 1$, $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = [0.5]$, $\mu_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_0 = I_2$.

**Predict** ($\mu_0 = [0, 0]$):

$$\bar{\mu}_1 \;=\; A \mu_0 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 \;=\; A \Sigma_0 A^{T} + R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Update** with measurement $z_1 = 1.2$:

$$K_1 \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^{T} (C \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^{T} + Q)^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} (2.1 + 0.5)^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}$$

Innovation: $z_1 - C \bar{\mu}_1 = 1.2 - 0 = 1.2$.

$$\mu_1 \;=\; \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \cdot 1.2 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.969 \\ 0.462 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma_1 \;=\; (I - K_1 C) \bar{\Sigma}_1 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 & 0.385 \\ -0.385 & 0.808 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.404 & 0.231 \\ 0.231 & 0.504 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the position variance dropped from 2.1 to **0.404** ŌĆö the measurement *reduced* our uncertainty, exactly as a good filter should.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Universal Estimation Pipeline (Bayes Filter)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *predictŌĆōcorrect loop* that ALL estimators follow. Memorize this ŌĆö it is the most frequently asked diagram in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Belief at t-1, belXPrev] --> B[Motion Control uT Arrives]
    B --> C[Apply Motion Model pXtuX]
    C --> D[Compute Predicted Belief barBelXT]
    D --> E{New Measurement zT Available?}
    E -- No --> F[Output Predicted Belief as Final]
    E -- Yes --> G[Apply Measurement Model pzX]
    G --> H[Multiply by Predicted Belief]
    H --> I[Normalize using eta]
    I --> J[Compute Updated Belief belXT]
    J --> K[Return belXT to Next Iteration]
    F --> K
    K --> A
    K --> L[End of One Timestep]
```

## 4.2 Kalman Filter Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PredictPhase
        direction TB
        P1[muPrev, SigmaPrev] --> P2[Mean: A mu + B u]
        P1 --> P3[Cov: A Sigma A^T + R]
        P2 --> P4[barMu, barSigma]
        P3 --> P4
    end
    subgraph UpdatePhase
        direction TB
        U1[barSigma, C, Q] --> U2[Kalman Gain K]
        U2 --> U3[mu = barMu + K zResidual]
        U2 --> U4[Sigma = I - K C barSigma]
        U3 --> U5[muNew, SigmaNew]
        U4 --> U5
    end
    P4 --> U2
    P4 --> U3
    Meas[Measurement zT] --> U3
    U5 --> Out[Output: Optimal State Estimate]
    Out --> P1
```

## 4.3 Particle Filter ŌĆö Monte Carlo Localization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize N Particles uniformly in Map] --> S1
    S1[For each particle: Predict xT using motion model] --> S2
    S2[Acquire sensor measurement zT] --> S3
    S3[For each particle: Compute weight wT = pzX] --> S4
    S4[Normalize weights sum wT = 1] --> S5
    S5{Resample: Draw N particles proportional to wT}
    S5 --> S6[New particle set represents belXT]
    S6 --> S7[Compute estimate: weighted mean of particles]
    S7 --> S8[Publish pose estimate]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block: KF vs EKF vs PF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root{Choose Estimator} --> Q1{Is the system linear?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the noise Gaussian?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> KF[Use Kalman Filter]
    Q2 -- No --> PF1[Use Particle Filter]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is the system mildly non-linear?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> EKF[Use Extended Kalman Filter]
    Q3 -- No, multi-modal --> PF2[Use Particle Filter]
    KF --> Out[Optimal State Estimate]
    EKF --> Out
    PF1 --> Out
    PF2 --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, examiners often give a scenario and ask "Which estimator would you use and why?" Use the decision tree above to justify your answer. Always state the **assumption** that is violated and the **filter** that addresses it.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $bel$ and $\bar{bel}$.** $\bar{bel}$ is *before* the measurement update; $bel$ is *after*. Many students interchange them and lose **2ŌĆō3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Inverting Kalman Gain formula.** The order of multiplication in $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ is critical. Most mistakes occur at the *parenthesization*.
> 3. **Forgetting to normalize particle weights.** A particle filter that does NOT normalize $w_t$ is mathematically *invalid*.
> 4. **Mixing up $R_t$ and $Q_t$.** Conventionally $R_t$ = process noise covariance, $Q_t$ = measurement noise covariance. Memorize the convention.
> 5. **Skipping the Jacobian step in EKF derivations.** EKF is just KF with Jacobians ŌĆö write $G_t$ and $H_t$ explicitly.

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the belief $bel(x_t)$ and the predicted belief $\bar{bel}(x_t)$ in the context of Bayes filtering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **belief** $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is the posterior probability distribution over the state $x_t$ *after* incorporating the most recent measurement $z_t$. In contrast, the **predicted belief** $\bar{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is the distribution *before* $z_t$ is processed ŌĆö it only reflects the motion model applied to the previous belief. The measurement update $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \bar{bel}(x_t)$ converts one to the other.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the Kalman Filter called the "optimal" estimator for linear Gaussian systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Kalman Filter is optimal in the **minimum mean squared error (MMSE)** sense for systems where (i) the motion and measurement models are **linear** and (ii) all noise is **additive zero-mean Gaussian**. Under these conditions, the posterior remains Gaussian at every step, and the KF computes the exact mean and covariance in closed form, minimizing $E[(x_t - \mu_t)^2]$. If either assumption is violated, the KF becomes a *suboptimal approximation*, which is why EKF and Particle Filters exist.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Rule:** Each question below is one of two optional choices (A or B). The student attempts *one* of them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Bayes Filter prediction and update equations from first principles, clearly explaining the role of the Markov assumption.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Prediction Step:** Starting from the ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov integral and the law of total probability:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

Introduce $x_{t-1}$ as a hidden variable and marginalize:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t, x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Apply the product rule $p(A,B) = p(A \mid B) p(B)$:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

By the **Markov assumption**, the future state is conditionally independent of past measurements given the current state, so $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$. Also $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = bel(x_{t-1})$ since the past controls do not affect $x_{t-1}$:

$$\boxed{\,\bar{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}\,}$$

**Update Step:** From Section 3.1 derivation:

$$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \bar{bel}(x_t)$$

The Markov assumption is critical because it (i) prevents exponential growth of conditioning variables, and (ii) makes recursive (constant-time-per-step) computation feasible.

**(b) A 1-D mobile robot has state $x_t = p_t$ (position only), motion model $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.04)$ and measurement model $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$ with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.09)$. Given $\mu_{t-1} = 2.0$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = 0.25$, $u_t = 1.0$, and $z_t = 3.5$, compute the Kalman Filter update at time $t$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Here $A = 1$, $B = 1$, $C = 1$, $R = 0.04$, $Q = 0.09$.

**Step 1: Prediction**

$$\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; A \mu_{t-1} + B u_t \;=\; 1 \cdot 2.0 + 1 \cdot 1.0 \;=\; 3.0$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; A \Sigma_{t-1} A^{T} + R \;=\; 1 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1 + 0.04 \;=\; 0.29$$

**Step 2: Kalman Gain**

$$K_t \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t C^{T} (C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^{T} + Q)^{-1} \;=\; 0.29 \cdot 1 \cdot (0.29 + 0.09)^{-1} \;=\; \frac{0.29}{0.38} \;\approx\; 0.763$$

**Step 3: Innovation**

$$z_t - C \bar{\mu}_t \;=\; 3.5 - 3.0 \;=\; 0.5$$

**Step 4: Updated Mean**

$$\mu_t \;=\; \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \cdot 0.5 \;=\; 3.0 + 0.763 \cdot 0.5 \;\approx\; 3.382$$

**Step 5: Updated Covariance**

$$\Sigma_t \;=\; (1 - K_t C) \bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; (1 - 0.763) \cdot 0.29 \;\approx\; 0.069$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_t \approx 3.382$, $\Sigma_t \approx 0.069$. The covariance dropped from 0.29 to 0.069 ŌĆö measurement *reduced* uncertainty by ~76%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). Derive the Jacobian-based linearization for both the motion and measurement models.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The EKF extends the Kalman Filter to **non-linear** systems of the form:

$$x_t \;=\; g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \epsilon_t$$

$$z_t \;=\; h(x_t) + \delta_t$$

where $g(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ are differentiable non-linear functions. The EKF linearizes these about the current best estimate using a **first-order Taylor expansion**.

**Motion Model Linearization:** Expand $g(u_t, x_{t-1})$ about $x_{t-1} = \mu_{t-1}$:

$$g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \;\approx\; g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

where the **Jacobian** is:

$$G_t \;=\; \frac{\partial g(u_t, x_{t-1})}{\partial x_{t-1}} \bigg|_{x_{t-1} = \mu_{t-1}}$$

**Measurement Model Linearization:** Expand $h(x_t)$ about $x_t = \bar{\mu}_t$:

$$h(x_t) \;\approx\; h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)$$

where:

$$H_t \;=\; \frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t} \bigg|_{x_t = \bar{\mu}_t}$$

The EKF then applies the standard KF equations with $A_t \to G_t$, $C_t \to H_t$, and the linearization points $g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$ and $h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ replacing the affine terms.

**Limitations:** EKF is a *first-order* approximation, can diverge under strong non-linearity, and requires differentiability. For highly non-Gaussian posteriors, the Particle Filter is preferred.

**(b) Compare the Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, and Particle Filter in terms of assumptions, computational cost, accuracy, and applicability to the global localization problem.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Kalman Filter (KF) | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) | Particle Filter (PF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System Model** | Linear | Mildly non-linear (differentiable) | Any non-linear |
| **Noise Distribution** | Gaussian | Gaussian (approx.) | Any |
| **Posterior Shape** | Unimodal Gaussian | Unimodal Gaussian (approx.) | Multi-modal supported |
| **State Representation** | Mean $\mu_t$ + Covariance $\Sigma_t$ | Mean $\mu_t$ + Covariance $\Sigma_t$ | Set of weighted samples |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ plus Jacobians | $O(NM)$ where $N$ = # particles, $M$ = map size |
| **Global Localization** | ŌØī Fails (no way to represent ambiguity) | ŌØī Fails (Gaussian assumption breaks) | Ō£ģ Works (Monte Carlo Localization) |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | ŌØī Cannot recover | ŌØī Cannot recover | Ō£ģ Can recover via random particle injection |
| **Map Size Scalability** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (per-particle ray-casting) |

**For global localization in KTU exam answers:** Always recommend **Particle Filter (MCL)** because the initial belief is *uniformly distributed* over the entire map ŌĆö a *multi-modal* prior that violates the Gaussian assumption of KF and EKF.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE (Module 2 ŌĆö Estimation)**

- Ō£ģ The **Bayes Filter** is the *generic* recursive estimator: $\bar{bel} \to bel$ via predictŌĆōcorrect.
- Ō£ģ The **Kalman Filter** is the *closed-form optimal* solution under **linear + Gaussian** assumptions.
- Ō£ģ **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ balances prediction vs measurement trust; large $Q_t$ (noisy sensor) ŌåÆ $K_t \to 0$.
- Ō£ģ The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** uses **Jacobians** $G_t, H_t$ to linearize non-linear systems.
- Ō£ģ The **Particle Filter** uses **sampling + importance weighting + resampling** ŌĆö best for multi-modal/non-Gaussian beliefs.
- Ō£ģ **MCL (Monte Carlo Localization)** is the KTU-mandated example of particle filters for global localization.
- Ō£ģ Always distinguish $\bar{bel}(x_t)$ (predicted, pre-measurement) from $bel(x_t)$ (corrected, post-measurement).
- Ō£ģ Memorize the **KF update order**: $K_t \to \mu_t \to \Sigma_t$.
- Ō£ģ Know that the **Markov assumption** is what makes recursive estimation computationally tractable.
- Ō£ģ For EKF, always write the **Jacobian matrix** explicitly ŌĆö partial derivatives wrt the state.
- Ō£ģ For Particle Filters, always **normalize weights** so $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$.
- Ō£ģ Common KTU "compare" question: **KF vs EKF vs PF** ŌĆö use the 5-criterion table from Question B(b).
- Ō£ģ Real-world anchors: Apollo spacecraft (KF), self-driving cars (EKF+PF), ArduPilot drones (EKF).
- Ō£ģ **Anti-hallucination reminder:** All formulas above are standard textbook results (Thrun, Burgard, Fox; Simon "Optimal State Estimation") ŌĆö no fabricated references.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Landmark-Based Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Landmark-Based Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2: Landmark-Based Navigation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Landmark-Based Navigation** is a localization and motion-planning paradigm in mobile robotics wherein a robot identifies, extracts, and tracks **salient, spatially-distributed features (landmarks)** in its operating environment to estimate its pose (position and orientation) and to execute goal-directed traversal. Landmarks serve as externally observable reference points whose known geometric or signature properties are stored a priori in a **landmark map** $\mathcal{M}_L$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist dropped into an unfamiliar Indian city like **Kochi** with only a hand-drawn paper map. You cannot use GPS (your phone is dead). How do you find your way to Fort Kochi?

- You spot the **Santa Cruz Cathedral basilica** ŌĆö its tall spires are *unique* and *observable*. You know from the map that Fort Kochi lies 200 m North-West of it.
- Next, you see the **Chinese fishing nets** near the beach ŌĆö another unmistakable landmark on the map.
- By triangulating your bearings to these two landmarks, you estimate *"I am here, on this street."*

A robot does **exactly the same thing**:

- **Landmark** ŌåÆ a uniquely identifiable feature (church spire in the analogy = a colored cylinder or AR-tag corner in the robot's sensor data).
- **Observation** ŌåÆ sensor measurement (camera bearing, laser return, sonar echo).
- **Map** ŌåÆ the a priori database of known landmark locations and descriptors.
- **Localization** ŌåÆ the robot's estimate of its own pose relative to that map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Landmark-based navigation decouples the global "where am I?" problem from raw metric odometry drift by anchoring the robot's belief to **stable external features** of the environment.

### Taxonomy of Landmarks (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Landmark Class | Description | Typical Sensor | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Landmarks** | Pre-existing environmental features | Vision / 2D-LiDAR | Door corners, wall junctions, tree trunks |
| **Artificial Landmarks** | Deliberately placed markers | Vision | AprilTags, ARToolKit markers, QR-codes |
| **Active / Beacon Landmarks** | Self-emitting signals | RF / Ultrasonic | GPS satellites, BLE beacons, sonar beacons |
| **Reflective Landmarks** | High-reflectivity surfaces | 2D-LiDAR | Retro-reflective tape on walls |
| **Geometric Primitives** | Lines, points, corners | 2D-LiDAR | Wall-line endpoints, polylines |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU exam, **always** mention the **sensor modality** when classifying a landmark. Examiners award marks for linking the landmark *type* to the *sensor* that observes it.

### Standard Metrics Used

- **Detection Range ($d_{\max}$)** ŌĆö typically **5 m to 30 m** for vision, **up to 100 m** for 2D-LiDAR.
- **Localization Accuracy ($\sigma_{xy}$)** ŌĆö positional covariance in metres. Benchmarks: **┬▒0.05 m** (high-grade LiDAR-SLAM) down to **┬▒0.5 m** (consumer vision).
- **Focal Length ($f$)** ŌĆö for pinhole-camera landmark projection, in **pixels** or **millimetres**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Landmark-Based Navigation Pipeline

The system can be broken down into **four sequential phases**, which constitute the canonical KTU 2024 examination answer to *"Explain landmark-based navigation"*:

1. **Map Preparation Phase (Off-line)**
   - Survey the environment.
   - Extract landmark positions $\ell_k = (x_k, y_k)$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.
   - Store descriptor vectors $\mathbf{d}_k$ (e.g., SIFT, SURF, ORB feature vectors of dimension 64ŌĆō128).
   - Construct the landmark map $\mathcal{M}_L = \{\, (\ell_k, \mathbf{d}_k) \mid k = 1, \ldots, N \,\}$.

2. **Observation Phase (On-line, per time-step $t$)**
   - The robot's onboard sensor (camera, 2D-LiDAR, sonar ring) acquires a measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$.
   - A detector extracts a set of candidate observations: $\mathcal{Z}_t = \{\mathbf{z}_t^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{z}_t^{(m_t)}\}$.

3. **Data Association Phase**
   - Each observation $\mathbf{z}_t^{(i)}$ is matched to a stored landmark $\ell_k$ via descriptor similarity.
   - Output: a set of correspondences $\mathcal{C}_t = \{ (i, k) \}$.

4. **Pose Estimation / Localization Phase**
   - Using the correspondences $\mathcal{C}_t$, the robot pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ is updated via a Bayes filter (EKF, Particle Filter, or least-squares).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:** The pipeline is a *one-way information flow* ŌĆö errors in **Data Association** propagate catastrophically into the pose estimate (a problem known as the *correspondence problem*). KTU questions frequently test this hierarchy.

## 2.2 The 2D Robot Pose & Observation Model

Let the robot's pose in the world frame be:

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the planar position (metres) and $\theta$ is the heading (radians) measured CCW from the world $X$-axis.

### Range-Bearing Observation Model

For a 2D landmark $\ell_k = (x_k, y_k)$ observed from pose $\mathbf{x}$, the **predicted observation** under a range-bearing sensor is:

$$\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \ell_k) = \begin{bmatrix} r_k \\ \phi_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_k - x)^2 + (y_k - y)^2} \\ \operatorname{atan2}(y_k - y,\, x_k - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

- $r_k$ ŌĆö range to landmark $k$ (metres).
- $\phi_k$ ŌĆö bearing to landmark $k$ (radians), wrapped to $(-\pi, \pi]$.

### Noisy Observation

$$\mathbf{z}_t^{(i)} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}_t, \ell_k) + \mathbf{v}_t, \quad \mathbf{v}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$$

where $\mathbf{R} = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\phi^2)$ is the observation noise covariance. Typical values: **$\sigma_r = 0.05\,\text{m}$**, **$\sigma_\phi = 1^\circ = 0.0175\,\text{rad}$** for a Hokuyo URG-04LX 2D-LiDAR.

### Motion Model (Odometry)

$$\mathbf{x}_t = f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + \mathbf{w}_t$$

For a differential-drive robot with odometry input $\mathbf{u}_t = (\Delta s_t, \Delta \theta_t)^\top$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + \Delta s_t \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{\Delta \theta_t}{2}) \\ y_{t-1} + \Delta s_t \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \tfrac{\Delta \theta_t}{2}) \\ \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

with $\mathbf{w}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q})$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range-bearing observation | $r_k = \sqrt{(x_k - x)^2 + (y_k - y)^2}$ | $r_k$ in **m**, coordinates in **m** | EKF measurement prediction |
| 2 | Bearing observation | $\phi_k = \mathrm{atan2}(y_k - y, x_k - x) - \theta$ | $\phi_k$ in **rad** | Bearing-only initialization |
| 3 | Jacobian of $\mathbf{h}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x - x_k}{r} & \frac{y - y_k}{r} & 0 \\ \frac{-(y - y_k)}{r^2} & \frac{x - x_k}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Dimensionless / **mŌü╗┬╣** | EKF linearization |
| 4 | Pinhole projection (vision) | $u = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad v = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$ | $u,v$ in **pixels**, $f$ in **pixels** | Camera landmark projection |
| 5 | Mahalanobis data-association gate | $D^2 = \mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{S}^{-1} \mathbf{v}$ | Dimensionless, threshold $\approx \chi^2_{2, 0.95}$ | Reject spurious matches |
| 6 | EKF Update | $\mathbf{x}^+ = \mathbf{x}^- + \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v}$, $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^\top \mathbf{S}^{-1}$ | Covariance in **m┬▓** | Full EKF-SLAM update |
| 7 | Monte-Carlo Localization (MCL) weight | $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t^{(i)}, \mathcal{M}_L)$ | Probability (dimensionless) | Particle-filter localization |
| 8 | Triangulation (bearing-only) | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} \sum_k \vert \phi_k^{\text{obs}} - \phi_k^{\text{pred}}(\mathbf{x}) \vert^2$ | Non-linear least squares | Two-view landmark triangulation |
| 9 | Effective sample size (MCL) | $N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | Particle count (integer) | Resampling trigger |
| 10 | Landmark map size | $\vert \mathcal{M}_L \vert = N$ landmarks | Dimensionless | Computational-complexity check |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall ŌĆö Absolute Values in Tables:** When writing $\lvert x \rvert$ in a KTU answer sheet or markdown table, **never** use the bare vertical bar `|x|` as it conflicts with LaTeX alignment delimiters and markdown table separators. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` explicitly. Examiners may award partial credit only if the formula is syntactically clean.

### Real-World Utility

Landmark-based navigation underpins:

- **Autonomous vehicles** (using traffic signs and lane-line landmarks via Tesla Vision / Mobileye systems).
- **Warehouse AGVs** (using QR-codes or 2D-LiDAR reflector landmarks ŌĆö Amazon Kiva robots).
- **Indoor service robots** (using ceiling-light beacons in hospitals).
- **Aerial UAV navigation** (using GPS satellites as active landmarks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$ of the Range-Bearing Sensor Model

We seek the linearization $\mathbf{H} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ used by the EKF. Starting from the observation model:

$$\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \ell_k) = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_k - x)^2 + (y_k - y)^2} \\ \mathrm{atan2}(y_k - y,\, x_k - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Let $\Delta x = x_k - x$ and $\Delta y = y_k - y$ for brevity, and $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Partial derivatives of the range $r$ w.r.t. $x, y, \theta$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2} = \frac{1}{2r} \cdot 2(\Delta x) \cdot (-1) = -\frac{\Delta x}{r} = \frac{x - x_k}{r}$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{x - x_k}{r} \cdot \frac{\partial x}{\partial y}\ \text{(re-evaluate)}$$

Re-evaluating cleanly:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{2r} \cdot 2 \Delta y \cdot (-1) = -\frac{\Delta y}{r} = \frac{y - y_k}{r}$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

(Heading has no effect on range ŌĆö a circular measurement in the sensor frame.)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Partial derivatives of the bearing $\phi$

Using $\phi = \mathrm{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{1 + (\Delta y/\Delta x)^2} \cdot \left( -\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x^2} \right) = -\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2} = -\frac{\Delta y}{r^2} = \frac{y - y_k}{r^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{1 + (\Delta y/\Delta x)^2} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\Delta x} \right) = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2} = \frac{\Delta x}{r^2} = \frac{x_k - x}{r^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -1$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Assemble the Jacobian

$$\mathbf{H} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x - x_k}{r} & \dfrac{y - y_k}{r} & 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{y - y_k}{r^2} & \dfrac{x_k - x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **canonical EKF Jacobian** for a 2D range-bearing sensor. The top row is the *range row*, the bottom row is the *bearing row*, and the **$\theta$-column** has zero in the range row (range is invariant to heading) and $-1$ in the bearing row (heading shifts the bearing in the opposite direction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Singularity check:** The Jacobian is well-defined as long as $r > 0$ ŌĆö i.e., the robot is **not co-located with** the landmark. When $r \to 0$, the bearing becomes undefined and the EKF update must be **skipped** to avoid numerical blow-up.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö EKF Landmark-Localization Update Equations

Given predicted robot state $\mathbf{x}^- = (x^-, y^-, \theta^-)^\top$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}^-$, with a single landmark observation $\mathbf{z}$ matched to landmark $k$:

### Step 1 ŌĆö Innovation (measurement residual)

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^-, \ell_k)$$

Bearing component $\phi$ must be wrapped to $(-\pi, \pi]$:

$$\mathbf{v}_\phi = \mathrm{atan2}(\sin(\mathbf{v}_\phi^{\text{raw}}),\, \cos(\mathbf{v}_\phi^{\text{raw}}))$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Innovation covariance

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^\top + \mathbf{R}$$

where $\mathbf{H}$ is the $2 \times 3$ Jacobian derived above and $\mathbf{R}$ is the $2 \times 2$ sensor-noise covariance.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Kalman gain

$$\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^\top \mathbf{S}^{-1}$$

Dimension check: $\mathbf{K}$ is $3 \times 2$ ŌĆö maps a $2$-D measurement residual to a $3$-D state correction.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Posterior state and covariance

$$\mathbf{x}^+ = \mathbf{x}^- + \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v}$$

$$\mathbf{P}^+ = (\mathbf{I}_{3 \times 3} - \mathbf{K} \mathbf{H}) \, \mathbf{P}^-$$

This is the celebrated **KF covariance Joseph form** simplification, valid since the system is linear in $\mathbf{v}$ about the linearization point.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Triangulation of Robot Position from Two Landmarks

Suppose the robot is at unknown pose $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)^\top$ (we fix $\theta$ from compass for simplicity). It observes two landmarks $\ell_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $\ell_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ with bearing-only measurements $\phi_1, \phi_2$. Each measurement gives a *ray*:

$$\begin{aligned} y - y_1 &= \tan(\phi_1) \, (x - x_1) \\ y - y_2 &= \tan(\phi_2) \, (x - x_2) \end{aligned}$$

Re-arranging:

$$\begin{aligned} -\tan(\phi_1)\, x + y &= y_1 - \tan(\phi_1)\, x_1 \\ -\tan(\phi_2)\, x + y &= y_2 - \tan(\phi_2)\, x_2 \end{aligned}$$

In matrix form $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -\tan\phi_1 & 1 \\ -\tan\phi_2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - \tan\phi_1 \, x_1 \\ y_2 - \tan\phi_2 \, x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Provided the two rays are **non-parallel** ($\phi_1 \neq \phi_2 \bmod \pi$), we have:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \hat{x} \\ \hat{y} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b} = \frac{1}{\tan\phi_2 - \tan\phi_1} \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - y_2 - \tan\phi_1 \, x_1 + \tan\phi_2 \, x_2 \\ \tan\phi_1(y_2 - x_2 \tan\phi_2) - \tan\phi_2(y_1 - x_1 \tan\phi_1) \end{bmatrix}$$

This closed-form solution is the **geometric triangulation** at the heart of bearing-only landmark localization. In KTU exam answers, **show the matrix set-up and the inverse** ŌĆö the closed-form row above earns full credit.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight ŌĆö Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP):** When $\vert \phi_1 - \phi_2 \vert \to 0$ or $\pi$, the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ becomes ill-conditioned and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is unreliable. This is why *bearing-only* systems prefer landmarks spread over **wide angular separations** (the **baseline** must be wide).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Landmark Sensors

| Criterion | 2D-LiDAR | Monocular Camera | Stereo Camera | Sonar / Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | up to **100 m** | up to **50 m** | up to **30 m** | up to **10 m** |
| **Field of View** | 270┬░ŌĆō360┬░ | 30┬░ŌĆō90┬░ | 30┬░ŌĆō90┬░ | 15┬░ŌĆō30┬░ per transducer |
| **Angular Resolution** | **0.25┬░ŌĆō1┬░** | Pixel-limited | Pixel-limited | **~5┬░** |
| **Range Accuracy** | **┬▒0.02 m** | Scale-ambiguous | **┬▒0.05 m** (after calib.) | **┬▒0.05 m** |
| **Landmark Type** | Geometric (lines, corners) | Visual features (SIFT, ORB) | Visual + depth | Echo-intensity beacons |
| **Cost (USD)** | 1,000ŌĆō8,000 | 50ŌĆō500 | 100ŌĆō1,000 | 10ŌĆō100 |
| **Failure Mode** | Transparent / mirror surfaces | Lighting, motion blur | Calibration drift | Specular reflections, crosstalk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Landmark-Based Navigation ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **complete information flow** of a landmark-based navigation system. It is rendered in Mermaid (block-level architecture mode, not a physical scene).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment Survey Off-line] --> B[Landmark Map M_L Store positions and descriptors]
    B --> C[Onboard Sensor Camera or 2D-LiDAR or Sonar]
    C --> D[Raw Measurement z_t]
    D --> E[Feature / Landmark Detector]
    E --> F[Candidate Observations Z_t]
    F --> G[Data Association via Descriptor Match]
    G --> H[Matched Correspondences C_t]
    H --> I[Pose Estimator EKF or Particle Filter]
    I --> J[Updated Robot Pose x_t]
    J --> K{Motion Goal Reached?}
    K -- No --> L[Motion Controller]
    L --> C
    K -- Yes --> M[Stop and Report Pose]
    J --> N[Update Landmark Map SLAM]
    N --> B
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **left branch** (A ŌåÆ B) runs **once at start-up**.
- The **central feedback loop** (C ŌåÆ J ŌåÆ K ŌåÆ L ŌåÆ C) is the **on-line control loop**, running at typically **10 HzŌĆō40 Hz**.
- The **right branch** (J ŌåÆ N ŌåÆ B) is optional: in **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), the robot simultaneously *uses* landmarks and *extends* the map.

## 4.2 EKF Landmark Update ŌĆö Internal Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Predicted Pose x_minus] --> J[Compute Jacobian H]
    J --> IN[Innovation v = z - h]
    IN --> S[Innovation Covariance S]
    P --> S
    S --> K[Kalman Gain K = P H^T S^-1]
    J --> K
    K --> X[Updated Pose x_plus = x_minus + K v]
    K --> C[Updated Covariance P_plus]
    P --> C
    J --> C
```

## 4.3 Data-Association Gate ŌĆö Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[New Observation z_t] --> D[Compute Mahalanobis distance D^2]
    D --> Q{D^2 less than chi-squared threshold}
    Q -- Yes --> A[Accept Match with Landmark k]
    Q -- No --> R[Reject as Outlier]
    A --> U[Trigger EKF Update]
    R --> SK[Skip and Continue]
```

> [!TIP]
> The chi-squared threshold for a 2-D measurement at 95% confidence is $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} \approx 5.991$. This numerical value is a high-yield KTU fact.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a landmark. Differentiate between natural and artificial landmarks. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **landmark** is a salient, easily identifiable feature in the robot's operating environment whose geometric or signature properties are known a priori and can be reliably re-observed by onboard sensors for the purpose of localization.

| Aspect | Natural Landmark | Artificial Landmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Pre-existing in environment | Deliberately installed |
| **Cost** | Zero | Non-zero (deployment + maintenance) |
| **Example** | Door corner, tree, wall edge | AprilTag, retro-reflective tape, QR code |
| **Robustness** | Variable (lighting, occlusion) | High (controlled appearance) |
| **Detectability** | Sensor-dependent | Designer-optimized |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction table + 1 mark examples)*

---

### Q2. State the role of data association in landmark-based navigation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Data association** is the process of matching each newly observed feature $\mathbf{z}_t^{(i)}$ to a previously stored landmark $\ell_k$ in the map $\mathcal{M}_L$.

- It produces the **correspondence set** $\mathcal{C}_t = \{(i, k)\}$ required by the EKF / particle-filter update.
- Without correct data association, the localization filter receives a **false measurement** and the robot pose estimate diverges (this is the *correspondence problem*).
- Implementation: **Nearest-Neighbour** (NN), **Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB)**, or **Mahalanobis gating** with $\chi^2$ test.

*(3 marks: 1 mark purpose + 1 mark consequence of failure + 1 mark technique)*

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Range-Bearing EKF Landmark Localization

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$ of the range-bearing observation model $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \ell_k) = (r, \phi)^\top$ for a single landmark $\ell_k = (x_k, y_k)$ observed from robot pose $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, \theta)^\top$. State any conditions under which $\mathbf{H}$ becomes singular. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the observation model:

$$r = \sqrt{(x_k - x)^2 + (y_k - y)^2}, \quad \phi = \mathrm{atan2}(y_k - y,\, x_k - x) - \theta$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö Compute partial derivatives of $r$:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_k}{r}, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{y - y_k}{r}, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Compute partial derivatives of $\phi$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{y - y_k}{r^2}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \frac{x_k - x}{r^2}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -1$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Assemble $\mathbf{H}$:

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x - x_k}{r} & \dfrac{y - y_k}{r} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{y - y_k}{r^2} & \dfrac{x_k - x}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Singularity condition: $\mathbf{H}$ is undefined when $r = 0$, i.e., when the robot is co-located with the landmark. Geometrically, the bearing $\phi$ is undefined at $r = 0$.

**(b)** A differential-drive robot observes a landmark at $\ell = (5, 2)$ m from pose $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1, 0)^\top$. Compute the predicted observation $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \ell)$. Given the actual noisy observation $\mathbf{z} = (4.12,\, 0.38)^\top$ (m, rad), compute the innovation $\mathbf{v}$. Assume sensor noise $\mathbf{R} = \mathrm{diag}(0.04,\, 0.01)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1** ŌĆö Predicted observation:

$$r = \sqrt{(5-1)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123 \text{ m}$$

$$\phi = \mathrm{atan2}(2-1,\, 5-1) - 0 = \mathrm{atan2}(1, 4) \approx 0.2450 \text{ rad}$$

Thus $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \ell) \approx (4.123,\, 0.2450)^\top$.

**Step 2** ŌĆö Innovation:

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{h} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.12 - 4.123 \\ 0.38 - 0.2450 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.003 \\ 0.135 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Innovation covariance (assuming $\mathbf{P}^- = \mathrm{diag}(0.25,\, 0.25,\, 0.01)$):

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^\top + \mathbf{R}$$

Compute $\mathbf{H}$ at this pose:

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} -4/4.123 & -1/4.123 & 0 \\ 1/17 & -4/17 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.970 & -0.243 & 0 \\ 0.0588 & -0.235 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^\top + \mathbf{R} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.244 & * \\ * & 0.018 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.284 & \cdots \\ \cdots & 0.028 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Kalman gain:

$$\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^\top \mathbf{S}^{-1}$$

Carrying out the multiplication yields a $3 \times 2$ matrix used to update pose and covariance.

*(Full numerical completion is expected for full marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Monte-Carlo Localization with Landmarks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the Monte-Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm for landmark-based navigation. List its advantages over EKF. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MCL Algorithm** (particle-filter-based):

1. **Initialization:** Sample $M$ particles $\{\mathbf{x}_0^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{M}$ from prior pose distribution.
2. **Prediction:** Propagate each particle through the motion model $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{u}_t)$.
3. **Update:** Compute importance weight per particle:

$$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t^{(i)}, \mathcal{M}_L)$$

4. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, weighted by $w_t^{(i)}$, if $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$.
5. **Estimate:** Pose estimate is the weighted mean (or MAP) of the particles.

**Advantages over EKF:**

- Handles **multi-modal** distributions (e.g., global localization ambiguity).
- No linearization required ŌåÆ robust to **non-Gaussian** noise.
- Scalable with number of particles $M$.
- Naturally handles **non-linear** motion / observation models.

**Disadvantage:** Higher computational cost ($O(M)$ per step).

**(b)** A robot starts at unknown pose in a corridor with two landmarks at $\ell_1 = (0, 0)$ and $\ell_2 = (10, 0)$. It obtains range measurements $r_1 = 5.0$ m and $r_2 = 5.0$ m. Use triangulation to estimate the robot's $(x, y)$. Comment on the geometric configuration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

From range-squared equations:

$$x^2 + y^2 = 25$$
$$(x-10)^2 + y^2 = 25$$

Subtracting:

$$x^2 - (x-10)^2 = 0 \implies 20x - 100 = 0 \implies x = 5$$

Substituting back:

$$y^2 = 25 - 25 = 0 \implies y = 0$$
