\tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \tfrac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$ | points away from obstacle | | 8 | Total Potential | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$ | Superposition | | 9 | Total Artificial Force | $F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = F_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$ | Applied to robot | | 10 | Robot Velocity Command | $\dot{q} = v_{max} \cdot \dfrac{F(q)}{\vert\vert F(q) \vert\vert}$ | Normalized direction | > [!CAUTION] > **Table Formatting Note:** All absolute value / magnitude notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert$ (LaTeX) ŌĆö never raw `|` ŌĆö to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. --- ### ­¤īŹ Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | How PFM is Used | |---|---| | **Industrial Manipulators (KUKA, ABB)** | Real-time collision avoidance between robot arm links and workpiece fixtures | | **AGVs in Warehouses (Amazon Kiva)** | Reactive obstacle avoidance on dynamic floors with moving workers | | **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla)** | Lane-keeping and pedestrian avoidance as a low-level reactive layer | | **Drone Swarms** | Inter-agent collision avoidance using pairwise repulsion potentials | | **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)** | Tool-tip protection from anatomical obstacles using localized repulsion fields | | **Underwater ROVs** | Reef and pipeline avoidance in unstructured marine environments | > [!NOTE] > PFM is **almost never used alone** in modern systems ŌĆö it is combined with **global planners** (RRT*, A*) as a local reactive controller. This hybrid architecture overcomes its biggest weakness: **local minima**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### ­¤ö¼ Derivation 1 ŌĆö Attractive Potential from a Quadratic Function We seek a potential whose gradient produces a force proportional to the displacement from $q_{goal}$, so that the force vanishes at the goal and grows linearly with distance. **Step 1:** Let the Euclidean distance be $$\rho(q, q_{goal}) = \sqrt{(x - x_{goal})^2 + (y - y_{goal})^2}$$ **Step 2:** Choose the parabolic (quadratic) candidate potential: $$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, \rho^2(q, q_{goal})$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $x$ using the chain rule: $$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = k_{att} \, \rho(q, q_{goal}) \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the partial derivative of $\rho$: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{goal}}{\rho(q, q_{goal})}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back: $$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = k_{att} \, \rho(q, q_{goal}) \cdot \frac{x - x_{goal}}{\rho(q, q_{goal})} = k_{att} \, (x - x_{goal})$$ **Step 6:** The attractive force is the negative gradient: $$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \, (q - q_{goal})$$ **Step 7 (Interpretation):** At $q = q_{goal}$, $F_{att} = 0$ (equilibrium). For every other configuration, the force is a vector pointing directly toward $q_{goal}$ with magnitude proportional to distance ŌĆö exactly what we want for a smooth converging trajectory. --- ### ­¤ö¼ Derivation 2 ŌĆö Repulsive Potential & Force We require a potential that (a) is zero outside the influence region, (b) approaches infinity as the robot touches the obstacle, and (c) is continuously differentiable. **Step 1:** Define the influence distance threshold $\rho_0$. For an obstacle at $q_{obs}$: $$\rho(q, q_{obs}) = \sqrt{(x - x_{obs})^2 + (y - y_{obs})^2}$$ **Step 2:** Construct the candidate repulsive potential: $$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q, q_{obs})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{\!2} & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\[2mm] 0 & \text{if } \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$ **Step 3:** Verify the boundary conditions: - As $\rho \to \rho_0$, the bracket $\to 0$, so $U_{rep} \to 0$ ŌĆö continuous match. - As $\rho \to 0$ (collision), $\tfrac{1}{\rho} \to \infty$, so $U_{rep} \to \infty$ ŌĆö guarantees safety. **Step 4:** Compute the gradient with respect to $x$. Let $u = \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} u^2$, so: $$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep} \, u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$ **Step 5:** Compute $\partial u / \partial x$: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}$$ **Step 6:** Substitute: $$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \right) \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}$$ **Step 7:** The repulsive force is the negative gradient: $$F_{rep}(q) = -k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$$ **Step 8 (Interpretation):** Note the unit vector $\dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$ points **away from the obstacle**. The scalar coefficient is positive when $\rho < \rho_0$, ensuring the force vector pushes the robot outward ŌĆö collision avoidance achieved. --- ### ­¤ö¼ Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem Setup:** - Robot at $q = (2, 3)$ - Goal at $q_{goal} = (10, 8)$ - Obstacle at $q_{obs} = (4, 4)$ - $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 100$, $\rho_0 = 3$ **Step A ŌĆö Compute Distance to Goal:** $$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(10-2)^2 + (8-3)^2} = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43$$ **Step B ŌĆö Compute Distance to Obstacle:** $$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$ **Step C ŌĆö Attractive Force Vector:** $$F_{att} = -1 \cdot (q - q_{goal}) = -(2 - 10, \, 3 - 8) = (8, 5)$$ **Step D ŌĆö Repulsive Force (since $\rho_{obs} = 2.24 < 3 = \rho_0$):** $$F_{rep,x} = 100 \left( \frac{1}{2.24} - \frac{1}{3} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{(2.24)^2} \cdot \frac{2 - 4}{2.24}$$ Computing each term numerically: $$\frac{1}{2.24} \approx 0.4464, \quad \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333, \quad \frac{1}{(2.24)^2} \approx 0.1993, \quad \frac{2 - 4}{2.24} \approx -0.8929$$ Therefore: $$F_{rep,x} = 100 \times (0.4464 - 0.3333) \times 0.1993 \times (-0.8929)$$ $$F_{rep,x} = 100 \times 0.1131 \times 0.1993 \times (-0.8929) \approx -2.01$$ **Step E ŌĆö Repulsive Force (y-component):** $$F_{rep,y} = 100 \times 0.1131 \times 0.1993 \times \frac{3 - 4}{2.24} \approx -1.01$$ **Step F ŌĆö Total Artificial Force:** $$F_{total} = F_{att} + F_{rep} = (8 - 2.01, \, 5 - 1.01) = (5.99, \, 3.99)$$ **Step G ŌĆö Robot's Next Velocity Command (unit direction):** $$\vert\vert F_{total} \vert\vert = \sqrt{5.99^2 + 3.99^2} = \sqrt{35.88 + 15.92} = \sqrt{51.80} \approx 7.20$$ $$v = v_{max} \cdot \frac{(5.99, 3.99)}{7.20} = v_{max} \cdot (0.832, \, 0.554)$$ > [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** Notice how $F_{rep}$ acts **perpendicularly outward** from the obstacle ŌĆö this is precisely why PFM trajectories curve smoothly around obstacles instead of bouncing off them. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### ­¤ōŖ Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Potential Field Controller ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Robot Current Pose q] --> B[Perception Module
Sensor Fusion LIDAR Camera] B --> C[Compute Distances
rho_goal rho_obs] C --> D[Attractive Potential
U_att Computation] C --> E[Repulsive Potential
U_rep Computation] D --> F[Gradient Descent
F_att equals negative grad U_att] E --> G[Gradient Descent
F_rep equals negative grad U_rep] F --> H[Force Superposition
F_total equals F_att plus sum F_rep] G --> H H --> I[Normalize and Scale
v equals v_max times F over norm F] I --> J[Low Level Motor Controller] J --> K[Robot Actuator Update] K --> L[New Pose q new] L --> A ``` ### ­¤ōŖ Visualization of the Combined Potential Landscape ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GoalAttraction A1[Goal q_goal] -->|Forms Paraboloid Bowl| A2[U_att negative gradient pulls inward] end subgraph ObstacleRepulsion B1[Obstacle q_obs] -->|Forms Infinite Cone| B2[U_rep negative gradient pushes outward] end subgraph CombinedField A2 --> C[Total U equals U_att plus U_rep] B2 --> C C --> D[Robot moves along minus grad U
Gradient Descent Trajectory] end ``` ### ­¤ōŖ Force Decomposition Diagram (Robot Perspective) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Robot at q] --> F1[Attractive Force F_att
Direction Toward Goal] Q --> F2[Repulsive Force F_rep_1
Direction Away from Obstacle 1] Q --> F3[Repulsive Force F_rep_2
Direction Away from Obstacle 2] Q --> F4[Repulsive Force F_rep_n
Direction Away from Obstacle n] F1 --> SUM[Vector Summation
F_total equals F_att plus F_rep] F2 --> SUM F3 --> SUM F4 --> SUM SUM --> RESULT[Resultant Velocity Vector
Robot Motion Direction] ``` ### ­¤ōŖ Local Minima Problem Illustration ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Robot at q_start] --> Path1[Follows Gradient Downhill] Path1 --> Trap[Local Minimum
F_att equals F_rep
Robot Stuck] Trap --> Solution[Solution Hybrid Planning
Global Planner plus PFM Reactive Layer] Solution --> Goal[Reaches q_goal Successfully] ``` > [!WARNING] > **Local Minima Pitfall:** A classic failure mode occurs when $F_{att}$ and $F_{rep}$ become exactly equal and opposite ŌĆö the robot halts in a non-goal location. This is the most cited limitation in KTU exam answers. Always mention the **hybrid solution** (PFM + global planner) to earn full marks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** *Define Artificial Potential Field Method for robot navigation. List any two advantages and one limitation.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Artificial Potential Field Method (APFM)** is a reactive local path planning technique in which the robot moves through a scalar potential field $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$. The attractive potential $U_{att}$ pulls the robot toward the goal, while the repulsive potential $U_{rep}$ pushes it away from obstacles. The robot's motion follows the negative gradient: $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$. **Advantages (any 2):** 1. Real-time computation ŌĆö only gradient evaluation per cycle ($\mathcal{O}(n)$). 2. Generates smooth, continuous trajectories without explicit path post-processing. 3. Mathematically elegant and easy to implement on embedded hardware. **Limitation (1):** - Susceptible to **local minima** where attractive and repulsive forces cancel, trapping the robot in non-goal locations (1 mark). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** *Write the mathematical expression for the repulsive potential $U_{rep}(q)$ used in the Potential Field Method and explain the role of $\rho_0$.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** $$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q, q_{obs})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{\!2} & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\[2mm] 0 & \text{if } \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$ **Role of $\rho_0$ (2 marks):** - $\rho_0$ is the **influence distance** that defines the region around an obstacle where the repulsive field is active. - For $\rho > \rho_0$, the obstacle has **no effect** on the robot (zero potential) ŌĆö this limits computational cost and avoids unnecessary perturbations. - For $\rho \le \rho_0$, the repulsion grows rapidly as $\rho \to 0$, guaranteeing collision avoidance. - Choice of $\rho_0$ is a **trade-off**: small values may cause late obstacle reactions, large values slow the robot unnecessarily. --- ### ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the attractive potential function $U_{att}(q)$ for a robot with configuration $q$ and goal $q_{goal}$. Explain the difference between the parabolic and conic forms of $U_{att}$ and justify when each is preferred. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A mobile robot at position $q = (1, 2)$ must navigate to goal $q_{goal} = (8, 6)$ in the presence of one obstacle at $q_{obs} = (4, 3)$. Given $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 50$, and $\rho_0 = 2.5$, compute the total artificial force $F_{total}$ and the unit velocity command. **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) \[7 Marks\] **Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of distance:** Let $\rho(q, q_{goal}) = \sqrt{(x - x_{goal})^2 + (y - y_{goal})^2}$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Parabolic form (1 mark):** $$U_{att}^{para}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, \rho^2(q, q_{goal})$$ The corresponding attractive force is $F_{att}^{para} = -k_{att} (q - q_{goal})$, which grows linearly with distance. **Step 3 ŌĆö Conic form (1 mark):** For a bounded attractive force magnitude: $$U_{att}^{conic}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, \rho^2 & \rho \le d^* \\[1mm] d^* \, k_{att} \, \rho - \dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, (d^*)^2 & \rho > d^* \end{cases}$$ The conic form produces a **constant-magnitude** force for $\rho > d^*$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Comparison table (3 marks):** | Property | Parabolic | Conic | |---|---|---| | Force at far distances | Grows unboundedly | Saturated at $d^* k_{att}$ | | Behavior near goal | Smooth, gentle | Smooth, gentle (parabolic inside $d^*$) | | Stability near obstacles | Large forces may dominate | More stable, predictable | | Use case | Short-range, indoor | Long-range, large environments | **Step 5 ŌĆö Justification (2 marks):** The conic form is preferred when the robot is far from the goal because it prevents the attractive force from becoming too large and overshadowing repulsive forces. The parabolic form is simpler and is typically used when the workspace is compact. --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) \[7 Marks\] **Given:** - $q = (1, 2)$, $q_{goal} = (8, 6)$, $q_{obs} = (4, 3)$ - $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 50$, $\rho_0 = 2.5$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Distance to goal (1 mark):** $$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(8-1)^2 + (6-2)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Attractive force (1 mark):** $$F_{att} = -k_{att} (q - q_{goal}) = -1 \cdot ((1-8), (2-6)) = (7, 4)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Distance to obstacle (1 mark):** $$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Check influence region (1 mark):** Since $\rho_{obs} = 3.16 > \rho_0 = 2.5$, the robot is **outside** the influence region. **Step 5 ŌĆö Repulsive force (1 mark):** $$F_{rep} = 0$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Total force (1 mark):** $$F_{total} = F_{att} + F_{rep} = (7, 4) + (0, 0) = (7, 4)$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Unit velocity command (1 mark):** $$\vert\vert F_{total} \vert\vert = \sqrt{49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$$ $$v = v_{max} \cdot \frac{(7, 4)}{8.06} = v_{max} \cdot (0.868, \, 0.496)$$ The robot moves in the direction $(0.868, 0.496)$, heading directly toward the goal since no obstacle influence is active. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the concept of local minima in the Potential Field Method. Illustrate with a diagram and discuss two approaches to overcome this limitation. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Two obstacles are at $q_{obs,1} = (3, 4)$ and $q_{obs,2} = (7, 2)$. The robot is at $q = (5, 5)$ and the goal is at $q_{goal} = (10, 8)$. Given $k_{att} = 1$, $k_{rep} = 200$, and $\rho_0 = 2$, compute the resultant force $F_{total}$ assuming both obstacles are within influence range. **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) \[7 Marks\] **Step 1 ŌĆö Definition (2 marks):** A **local minimum** in the potential field is a configuration $q^*$ where the total force $F(q^*) = -\nabla U(q^*) = 0$ but $q^* \ne q_{goal}$. At such a point, attractive and repulsive forces exactly cancel, and the robot becomes **trapped**, unable to reach the goal. **Step 2 ŌĆö Diagram (2 marks):** [Student should sketch: an obstacle near the goal, with the robot trapped in a pocket between the goal's attractive pull and the obstacle's repulsion. Equivalent to the marble stuck in a small depression.] **Step 3 ŌĆö Approach 1: Hybrid Planning (1.5 marks):** Combine PFM with a **global planner** (e.g., A*, RRT*). The global planner provides sub-goals (waypoints) that guide the robot out of local minima, while PFM handles local reactive control. **Step 4 ŌĆö Approach 2: Random Walk / Virtual Force (1.5 marks):** When $F_{total} \approx 0$ for several iterations, inject a **random perturbation force** or use the *virtual force field (VFF)* method (Borenstein & Koren, 1989) that uses a histogram grid to escape traps. --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) \[7 Marks\] **Step 1 ŌĆö Distances (2 marks):** $$\rho_{obs,1} = \sqrt{(3-5)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$ $$\rho_{obs,2} = \sqrt{(7-5)^2 + (2-5)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Influence check (1 mark):** $\rho_{obs,1} = 2.24 > \rho_0 = 2$ ŌåÆ **outside influence**. $\rho_{obs,2} = 3.61 > 2$ ŌåÆ **outside influence**. **Step 3 ŌĆö Repulsive forces are zero (1 mark):** Since both obstacles are outside $\rho_0$, $F_{rep,1} = 0$ and $F_{rep,2} = 0$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Attractive force (1 mark):** $$F_{att} = -k_{att} (q - q_{goal}) = -(5-10, 5-8) = (5, 3)$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Total force (1 mark):** $$F_{total} = (5, 3) + (0, 0) = (5, 3)$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Unit velocity (1 mark):** $$\vert\vert F_{total} \vert\vert = \sqrt{25 + 9} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.83$$ $$v = v_{max} \cdot (0.857, \, 0.514)$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤öź KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error in Force:** Many students write $F_{att} = +k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$ instead of $-k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$. The negative gradient is **mandatory** ŌĆö mark deduction of 2 points. > 2. **Forgetting Influence Boundary:** Students often compute $U_{rep}$ even when $\rho > \rho_0$. Always verify the influence condition first. > 3. **Local Minima Omission:** When asked about limitations, simply stating "it gets stuck" is insufficient. You must explain **why** (force cancellation) and **how to overcome** (hybrid planner). > 4. **Units in Numerical Problems:** Always declare units (meters for distance, N for force). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. > 5. **Normalization of Velocity:** Final answer should always be expressed as a unit vector scaled by $v_{max}$, not raw force. --- ### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **PFM is a reactive local planner**, not a global planner ŌĆö always clarify this distinction. - [x] **Total potential** = $U_{att} + \sum U_{rep}$ (linear superposition). - [x] **Total force** = $-\nabla U$ (gradient descent). - [x] **Attractive force** always points toward $q_{goal}$. - [x] **Repulsive force** is zero outside $\rho_0$ and infinite at the obstacle boundary. - [x] **Conic $U_{att}$** is bounded; **Parabolic $U_{att}$** is unbounded ŌĆö choose based on environment scale. - [x] **Local minima** are the #1 failure mode ŌĆö mitigation = hybrid global + local planning. - [x] **GNRON problem** (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby) is an advanced extension of local minima ŌĆö be ready to name it. - [x] **Computational complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ per step where $n$ = number of obstacles ŌĆö extremely efficient. - [x] **Always normalize** the final force vector before applying as a velocity command. - [x] Mention **Khatib (1986)** as the original proposer ŌĆö examiners reward historical accuracy. - [x] Real-world extensions: **VFF** (Virtual Force Field), **VFH** (Vector Field Histogram), **Harmonic Potential Fields** ŌĆö be aware of these names for higher marks.

topics.title: Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Navigation Function (NF)** is a real-valued, smooth (at least $\mathcal{C}^2$) function

$$\varphi : \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

defined on the robot's free configuration space $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ such that it satisfies the following three classical KoditschekŌĆōRimon conditions:

1. **Analytic / Smooth**: $\varphi$ is a Morse function ŌĆö its critical points are isolated and non-degenerate.
2. **Admissible**: $\varphi(q) \rightarrow 1$ as $q$ approaches the boundary of $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ (i.e., $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ or near obstacles).
3. **Polar**: $\varphi$ possesses a unique global minimum at the goal configuration $q_{\text{goal}}$, and **no other local minima** exist anywhere in $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In a *Static Deterministic Environment*, the obstacle positions are **fixed in time** and **known with certainty** to the robot. This allows the navigation function to be computed offline or updated only when the map changes, simplifying the analysis of its critical points.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large, dimly lit circular valley. Somewhere at the very bottom of the valley lies a beautiful glowing treasure (the **goal**). Scattered around the valley are hot volcanic vents (the **obstacles**) that you must avoid.

Now imagine that the floor of the valley is replaced by a single smooth rubber sheet that:
- **Slopes gently downward** toward the treasure (the *attractive* component).
- **Rises steeply** near every volcanic vent, like a high safety mound (the *repulsive* component).

Because the rubber sheet has only one true valley bottom (the treasure) and every other slope leads *toward* it, if you simply follow the gradient of the sheet's height (sliding downhill), you are guaranteed to reach the treasure without ever falling into a vent. This rubber-sheet height map **is** the navigation function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A navigation function converts a *geometric* path-planning problem into a *numerical optimization* problem ŌĆö the robot just performs gradient descent on $\varphi(q)$ until it reaches the global minimum.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $q$ | Robot configuration | $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $q_{\text{goal}}$ | Goal configuration | $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $q_i$ | Center of $i$-th obstacle | $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $r_i$ | Radius of $i$-th obstacle | $> 0$ |
| $k$ | Tuning exponent | $\geq 2$ integer |
| $\lambda$ | Normalization gain | $> 0$ |
| $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ | Free configuration space | Subset of $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ | Boundary (obstacle surfaces) | Topological boundary |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Navigation Function

A navigation function for a static, deterministic sphere world is composed of two superimposed scalar fields:

### 2.1.1 Attractive Component (Goal-Seeking)

The attractive field models the "pull" of the goal:

$$\gamma(q) = \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^2$$

This is a paraboloid centered at $q_{\text{goal}}$. Its gradient $-\nabla \gamma(q)$ always points toward the goal.

### 2.1.2 Obstacle (Repulsive) Component

For spherical obstacles $O_i$ with center $q_i$ and radius $r_i$, define the obstacle function:

$$b_i(q) = \parallel q - q_i \parallel^2 - r_i^2$$

This is **negative inside** the obstacle and **positive outside**. The combined obstacle field is:

$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_i(q)$$

### 2.1.3 Combined Navigation Function (KoditschekŌĆōRimon)

The classical sphere-world navigation function is:

$$\varphi(q) = \frac{\parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2}}{\left( \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2k} + \beta(q) \right)^{1/k}}$$

where $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $k \geq 1$, is a tuning parameter that controls the *sharpness* of the repulsive wells.

## 2.2 Why This Form?

- **Numerator** $\parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2}$: This vanishes only at the goal, providing the *unique* global minimum.
- **Denominator** $(\cdot)^{1/k}$: This grows large as $q$ approaches any obstacle, providing the *admissibility* property.
- **Exponent $k$**: As $k \to \infty$, the function approximates an idealized "bang-bang" behavior ŌĆö the robot hugs the medial axis between obstacles.

## 2.3 Why "Static Deterministic" Matters

| Property | Static | Deterministic |
|---|---|---|
| **Obstacle motion** | Zero velocity: $\dot{q}_i = 0$ | No stochastic noise: $q_i$ is exact, not a distribution |
| **Planning horizon** | Time-invariant NF: $\varphi(q, t) = \varphi(q)$ | Expected gradient equals actual gradient |
| **Critical points** | Fixed under workspace changes | Stability of equilibria guaranteed |

> [!NOTE]
> In a **static deterministic** setting, the navigation function is a true Morse function and its set of critical points (excluding the goal) ŌĆö typically saddle points ŌĆö can be rigorously catalogued using Morse theory.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma(q) = \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2}$ | Attractive potential | $\text{length}^2$ |
| $b_i(q) = \parallel q - q_i \parallel^{2} - r_i^{2}$ | $i$-th obstacle function | $\text{length}^2$ |
| $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_i(q)$ | Combined obstacle potential | $\text{length}^{2n}$ |
| $\varphi(q) = \dfrac{\gamma(q)}{(\gamma(q)^{k} + \beta(q))^{1/k}}$ | KoditschekŌĆōRimon NF | dimensionless in $[0,1]$ |
| $\nabla \varphi(q)$ | Gradient descent direction | $\text{length}^{-1}$ |
| $\nabla^2 \varphi(q) \succ 0$ | Local minimum condition (Hessian PD) | $\text{length}^{-2}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs**: Pre-compute $\varphi$ once, update only when shelf positions change.
- **Surgical robots in CT-scanned anatomy**: Static deterministic bones and organs allow NF-based smooth motion.
- **Autonomous CNC tool paths**: Obstacles (fixtures) are static and known a priori.
- **Spacecraft formation flying**: Once orbital geometry is locked, deterministic NF avoids collisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient $\nabla \varphi(q)$

Starting from the KoditschekŌĆōRimon form:

$$\varphi(q) = \frac{\gamma(q)}{\left(\gamma(q)^{k} + \beta(q)\right)^{1/k}}$$

Let us denote $D(q) = \left(\gamma(q)^{k} + \beta(q)\right)^{1/k}$ for compactness.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Quotient Rule:**

$$\nabla \varphi = \frac{\nabla \gamma \cdot D - \gamma \cdot \nabla D}{D^{2}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $\nabla D$:**

$$\nabla D = \frac{1}{k}\left(\gamma^{k} + \beta\right)^{\frac{1}{k}-1} \cdot \left(k\,\gamma^{k-1}\nabla\gamma + \nabla\beta\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the exponent $\frac{1}{k}-1 = \frac{1-k}{k}$:**

$$\nabla D = \left(\gamma^{k} + \beta\right)^{\frac{1-k}{k}} \cdot \left(\gamma^{k-1}\nabla\gamma + \frac{\nabla\beta}{k}\right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $\nabla D$ back:**

$$\nabla \varphi = \frac{\nabla\gamma}{D} - \frac{\gamma}{D^{2}}\left(\gamma^{k} + \beta\right)^{\frac{1-k}{k}} \left(\gamma^{k-1}\nabla\gamma + \frac{\nabla\beta}{k}\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Use $D = (\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k}$ to obtain the clean closed form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi(q) \;=\;& \frac{1}{\gamma^{k}+\beta}\Bigg[\bigl(\gamma^{k}+\beta\bigr)\,\frac{\nabla\gamma}{\gamma} \;-\; \bigl(\gamma^{k-1}\nabla\gamma + \tfrac{1}{k}\nabla\beta\bigr)\Bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compact vector form used in KTU board papers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi(q) \;=\; \frac{1}{\gamma^{k}+\beta}\left[\,\beta\,\frac{\nabla\gamma}{\gamma} \;-\; \frac{1}{k}\,\nabla\beta\,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **exact gradient** the robot follows via the control law $\dot{q} = -K\,\nabla \varphi(q)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Obstacle Function $\beta(q)$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define a single spherical obstacle $O_i$:**

The signed distance squared from a point $q$ to the surface of obstacle $O_i$:

$$b_i(q) = (q - q_i)^{\top}(q - q_i) - r_i^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take the gradient:**

$$\nabla b_i = 2(q - q_i)$$

This is a vector pointing **radially outward** from the obstacle center ŌĆö physically, it is the direction along which the repulsive force pushes the robot.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combine $n$ obstacles multiplicatively:**

$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_i(q) = b_1 \cdot b_2 \cdots b_n$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the product rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \beta(q) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[\nabla b_j \cdot \prod_{i \neq j} b_i\right] \;=\; 2\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[(q-q_j)\,\prod_{i \neq j} b_i(q)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Proof of Admissibility (Limit Behavior at Boundary)

We must show $\varphi(q) \to 1$ as $q \to \partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ (i.e., $b_j(q) \to 0$ for some $j$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö When $q \to O_j$, $b_j(q) \to 0^+$, hence $\beta(q) \to 0$.**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Under the assumption that the goal is *strictly outside* every obstacle, $\gamma(q)$ remains bounded away from $0$ near $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Then:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{q \to \partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}}\varphi(q) \;=\; \lim_{\beta \to 0}\frac{\gamma(q)}{\left(\gamma(q)^{k} + \beta\right)^{1/k}} \;=\; \frac{\gamma(q)}{\gamma(q)} \;=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies the admissibility condition $\square$.

## 3.4 Critical Points Analysis (Local Minima Suppression)

The critical points of $\varphi$ satisfy $\nabla \varphi(q) = 0$. From Step 6 of ┬¦3.1:

$$\beta\,\nabla\gamma \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{k}\,\nabla\beta$$

Using $\nabla\gamma = 2(q - q_{\text{goal}})$ and $\nabla\beta = 2\sum_{j}(q-q_j)\prod_{i\neq j}b_i$, the above reduces to a polynomial equation whose only solutions in $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ are:

- The goal $q_{\text{goal}}$ (Hessian positive definite ŌåÆ **local minimum**).
- A finite number of saddle points on the medial axis (Hessian indefinite).

No additional local minima exist ŌĆö hence $\varphi$ is **polar**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** 2-D plane, goal at $q_{\text{goal}} = (5, 5)$, one obstacle $O_1$ centered at $q_1 = (2, 2)$ with radius $r_1 = 1$. Compute $\varphi$ at $q = (3, 3)$ for $k = 2$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Attractive term:**

$$\gamma(q) = (3-5)^{2} + (3-5)^{2} = 4 + 4 = 8$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Single obstacle term:**

$$b_1 = (3-2)^{2} + (3-2)^{2} - 1^{2} = 1 + 1 - 1 = 1$$

$$\beta(q) = 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Navigation function value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(3,3) \;=\; \frac{8}{\sqrt{8^{2} + 1}} \;=\; \frac{8}{\sqrt{65}} \;\approx\; 0.9923
\end{aligned}
$$

The value is close to 1 because $(3,3)$ is near the obstacle boundary, confirming the repulsive lift.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Navigation Function Computation

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how a static deterministic environment feeds into the navigation function pipeline and ultimately produces the gradient descent control signal:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Static Workspace Map"]:::input --> B["Workspace to Configuration Space Transform"]
    B --> C["Extract Obstacle Centers and Radii"]
    C --> D["Compute Single Obstacle Function b_i_q"]
    D --> E["Multiply to Form Combined Beta_q"]
    F["Goal Configuration q_goal"]:::input --> G["Compute Attractive Field Gamma_q"]
    E --> H["Combine Gamma and Beta in Koditschek Rimon NF"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Compute Gradient Del phi"]
    I --> J["Apply Control Law u equals minus K times Del phi"]
    J --> K["Robot Actuator Command"]
    K --> L["Robot Configuration Update q dot equals u"]
    L --> M{"Norm Del phi less than epsilon"}
    M -- NO --> I
    M -- YES --> N["Goal Reached Output"]

    classDef input fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Decomposition Flowchart of $\varphi(q)$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Configuration q"]:::cfg --> Q["Attractive Branch"]
    P --> R["Repulsive Branch"]
    Q --> S["Gamma_q equals distance to goal squared"]
    R --> T["Loop over n obstacles"]
    T --> U["b_i_q equals distance squared minus r_i squared"]
    U --> V["Beta_q equals product of all b_i"]
    S --> W["phi_q equals gamma over gamma to k plus beta to one over k"]
    V --> W
    W --> X["Gradient Descent Direction"]:::out

    classDef cfg fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Critical Points Topology on $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z["Free Space F_free"]:::space --> A1["Goal q_goal - unique global minimum"]
    Z --> A2["Saddle Point 1 on medial axis"]
    Z --> A3["Saddle Point 2 on medial axis"]
    Z --> A4["Saddle Point M on medial axis"]
    Z --> A5["Boundary Limit phi approaches 1"]

    A1 -.->|gradient descent attracts| R["Robot Trajectory"]
    A2 -.->|unstable equilibrium| R
    A3 -.->|unstable equilibrium| R
    A4 -.->|unstable equilibrium| R
    A5 -.->|admissibility wall| R

    classDef space fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> All saddle points are **non-attracting** under the gradient flow ŌĆö perturbations in any direction push the trajectory either toward the goal or toward the boundary. This topological property is what guarantees completeness of the navigation function approach.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Navigation Function. State its three essential properties.

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A *Navigation Function* $\varphi: \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow [0,1]$ is a smooth real-valued map on the robot's free configuration space that converts a path-planning problem into gradient-descent optimization.

Its three essential properties are:

1. **Analytic / Morse**: $\varphi$ is at least $\mathcal{C}^2$-smooth, and all critical points are isolated and non-degenerate.
2. **Admissible**: $\varphi(q) \to 1$ as $q$ approaches the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.
3. **Polar**: $\varphi$ has a **unique global minimum** at $q_{\text{goal}}$ and no other local minima in $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$.

---

### Q2. What is meant by a "Static Deterministic Environment" in the context of robot navigation?

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **static** environment is one in which all obstacles are stationary ŌĆö their positions $q_i$ do not vary with time ($\dot{q}_i = 0$, $\forall i$). A **deterministic** environment is one in which the robot possesses complete and exact knowledge of all obstacle positions, sizes, and geometries ŌĆö there is no sensor noise or probabilistic uncertainty.

Combined, a **static deterministic environment** allows the navigation function $\varphi(q)$ to be **time-invariant** ($\partial \varphi / \partial t = 0$), enabling offline pre-computation and rigorous Morse-theoretic guarantees that every gradient-descent trajectory reaches $q_{\text{goal}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the KoditschekŌĆōRimon navigation function for a sphere world with $n$ spherical obstacles. Clearly define the attractive field $\gamma(q)$, the obstacle field $\beta(q)$, and explain the role of the tuning exponent $k$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Prove that the derived navigation function satisfies the **admissibility** condition, and explain why it has **no local minima** other than the goal configuration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Attractive Field:**
The attractive field captures the Euclidean proximity to the goal:

$$\gamma(q) = \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2} = (q - q_{\text{goal}})^{\top}(q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

Its gradient is $\nabla \gamma = 2(q - q_{\text{goal}})$, which always points from $q$ toward $q_{\text{goal}}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Single Obstacle Function:**
For obstacle $O_i$ with center $q_i$ and radius $r_i$:

$$b_i(q) = \parallel q - q_i \parallel^{2} - r_i^{2} = (q - q_i)^{\top}(q - q_i) - r_i^{2}$$

$b_i(q) > 0$ outside the obstacle, $b_i(q) = 0$ on the boundary, $b_i(q) < 0$ inside.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combined Obstacle Field:**
Multiply all obstacle functions:

$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_i(q)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Navigation Function:**

$$\varphi(q) = \frac{\parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2}}{\left( \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2k} + \beta(q) \right)^{1/k}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Role of $k$:**
The exponent $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $k \geq 1$, governs how sharply the repulsive wells rise near obstacles. Larger $k$ produces a tighter, more medial-axis-following trajectory. When $k = 1$, the function simplifies to a smooth quasi-potential; as $k \to \infty$, the trajectory asymptotically follows the Voronoi diagram.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Admissibility Proof:**
As $q \to \partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$, at least one $b_j(q) \to 0^+$, causing $\beta(q) \to 0$. Since the goal lies strictly outside every obstacle, $\gamma(q)$ remains finite and non-zero. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{q \to \partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}} \varphi(q) \;=\; \lim_{\beta \to 0} \frac{\gamma}{(\gamma^{k}+\beta)^{1/k}} \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{\gamma} \;=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the navigation function is bounded and attains its supremum of $1$ on the boundary ŌĆö satisfying admissibility.

**No Spurious Local Minima Proof:**
Critical points satisfy $\nabla \varphi = 0$, which from ┬¦3.4 reduces to:

$$\beta\,\nabla\gamma = \frac{\gamma}{k}\,\nabla\beta$$

Substituting $\nabla\gamma = 2(q - q_{\text{goal}})$ and $\nabla\beta$ gives a polynomial equation whose roots are:
- $q_{\text{goal}}$ (the unique global minimum with $\nabla^2 \varphi \succ 0$),
- A finite set of saddle points where the Hessian is indefinite.

By Morse theory on a compact domain, no additional minima can exist, hence $\varphi$ is **polar**.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the structure of the gradient $\nabla \varphi(q)$ for the KoditschekŌĆōRimon navigation function. Derive the closed-form expression. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 2-D mobile robot operates in a workspace containing a single circular obstacle centered at $q_1 = (2, 3)$ with radius $r_1 = 1$. The goal is at $q_{\text{goal}} = (8, 8)$. Compute the navigation function value $\varphi$ and its gradient $\nabla \varphi$ at $q = (4, 5)$, using $k = 2$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

Starting from $\varphi = \gamma / D$ where $D = (\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k}$, applying the quotient rule and the chain rule gives the closed form (see ┬¦3.1 Step 6):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi(q) \;=\; \frac{1}{\gamma^{k} + \beta}\left[\,\beta\,\frac{\nabla\gamma}{\gamma} \;-\; \frac{1}{k}\,\nabla\beta\,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term $\beta (\nabla\gamma/\gamma)$ is the **attractive-repulsive trade-off**: large near the goal (where $\gamma$ small, gradient amplified) and small near obstacles. The second term $-\frac{1}{k}\nabla\beta$ is the **pure repulsive push** away from obstacle boundaries.

The gradient vector $\nabla\varphi$ is the **direction of steepest increase** of $\varphi$. Hence the control law for the robot is:

$$\dot{q} = -K\,\nabla\varphi(q), \qquad K > 0$$

which produces a smooth descent toward $q_{\text{goal}}$ while maintaining a safety margin from obstacles.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\gamma$ at $q = (4, 5)$:**

$$\gamma = (4-8)^{2} + (5-8)^{2} = 16 + 9 = 25$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $b_1$ (single obstacle):**

$$b_1 = (4-2)^{2} + (5-3)^{2} - 1^{2} = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$$

Hence $\beta = 7$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Navigation Function value with $k = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(4,5) \;=\; \frac{25}{\sqrt{25^{2} + 7}} \;=\; \frac{25}{\sqrt{632}} \;\approx\; 0.9944
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\nabla\gamma$ and $\nabla\beta$:**

$$\nabla\gamma = 2(q - q_{\text{goal}}) = 2(-4, -3) = (-8, -6)$$

$$\nabla b_1 = 2(q - q_1) = 2(2, 2) = (4, 4)$$

Since $n = 1$, $\nabla\beta = \nabla b_1 = (4, 4)$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute into gradient formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi \;=\; \frac{1}{25^{2} + 7}\left[\,7 \cdot \frac{(-8,-6)}{25} \;-\; \frac{1}{2}(4,4)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi \;=\; \frac{1}{632}\left[\,(-2.24, -1.68) \;-\; (2, 2)\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{632}(-4.24, -3.68)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi \;\approx\; (-0.00671,\; -0.00582)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Interpretation:**
The negative gradient direction $(\approx 0.00671, \approx 0.00582)$ points toward the goal quadrant. The robot at $(4, 5)$ moves smoothly toward $(8, 8)$, avoiding the obstacle centered at $(2, 3)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent $\frac{1}{k}$**: Many students write the denominator as $(\gamma^{k} + \beta)$ instead of $(\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k}$. This breaks the bound $\varphi \in [0,1]$ and is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\gamma(q)$ with $\parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel$**: $\gamma$ is the **square** of the distance, not the distance itself. Writing $\gamma = \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel$ will cause dimensional errors.
>
> 3. **Omitting the Morse / non-degeneracy condition**: A navigation function must be Morse. Stating only "admissible and polar" is **incomplete** for full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing static vs. dynamic NF**: In static environments, $\varphi$ is **time-invariant**. Writing $\varphi(q, t)$ with $t$-dependence is incorrect.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify the goal lies outside all obstacles**: This is essential for the admissibility proof. Without this assumption, the limit $\varphi \to 1$ on the boundary fails.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Navigation Function** is a smooth, bounded, polar function $\varphi: \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow [0,1]$.
- Three properties: **Morse (analytic), Admissible, Polar**.
- **KoditschekŌĆōRimon form**: $\varphi = \gamma / (\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k}$.
- **Attractive term**: $\gamma(q) = \parallel q - q_{\text{goal}} \parallel^{2}$.
- **Obstacle term**: $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} b_i(q)$, where $b_i(q) = \parallel q - q_i \parallel^{2} - r_i^{2}$.
- **Tuning exponent $k \geq 1$**: larger $k$ ŌåÆ medial-axis trajectories.
- **Gradient formula**: $\nabla \varphi = \frac{1}{\gamma^{k}+\beta}\big[\beta \frac{\nabla\gamma}{\gamma} - \frac{1}{k}\nabla\beta\big]$.
- **Static deterministic** environments yield **time-invariant** NFs with rigorous Morse guarantees.
- Critical points are either the **goal (global min)** or **saddles (unstable)** ŌĆö **no local minima** other than the goal.
- Control law: $\dot{q} = -K\,\nabla\varphi$, $K > 0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: In Static Uncertain Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** In Static Uncertain Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation in a Static Uncertain Environment** is defined as the process by which a mobile robot determines a collision-free trajectory from a start configuration to a goal configuration in a workspace whose geometric structure is time-invariant (static), but where at least one of the following sources of information is imperfect or noisy: (a) the robot's own pose, (b) the map of obstacles, or (c) the sensor measurements.

In formal mathematical notation, the problem is defined as a tuple:
$$\Pi = \langle C_{space}, q_{start}, q_{goal}, \Sigma, O \rangle$$
where:
- $C_{space}$ is the Configuration Space of the robot
- $q_{start}$ is the initial configuration
- $q_{goal}$ is the target configuration
- $\Sigma$ represents the source of uncertainty (sensor, motion, or map)
- $O$ is the set of static obstacles

The static nature implies that for any time $t_1 \neq t_2$, the obstacle set $O(t_1) = O(t_2)$, while "uncertain" means the robot's belief state $bel(q_t)$ is a probability distribution rather than a point estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module distinguishes between *known* environments (where a map is given) and *unknown* environments (where the robot must sense obstacles online). The uncertainty arises in BOTH cases through noisy odometry, imperfect sensors, and partial observability.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Dark Warehouse" Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded inside a large, frozen warehouse (the environment is static ŌĆö nothing moves). You hold a flashlight (your sensor) with a narrow beam that only reveals a small circular patch of the floor at any time. You also have a slightly broken pedometer that drifts a few centimeters every ten steps (noisy odometry). Your task is to walk from the entrance (start) to the manager's office (goal).

This analogy captures the three fundamental challenges of static uncertain navigation:
1. **Static Obstacles:** The boxes and pillars in the warehouse don't move ŌĆö your mental map, once built, remains valid.
2. **Partial Observability:** Your flashlight is your only window to the world ŌĆö you must decide *where to point it*.
3. **Uncertainty:** Your pedometer drifts, so you must constantly reconcile what you *think* you know (dead reckoning) with what you *see* (sensor feedback).

### 1.3 Sources of Uncertainty in Static Environments

| Source of Uncertainty | Description | Standard Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Odometric Uncertainty** | Error accumulated from wheel slippage, encoder quantization | Additive Gaussian: $\hat{x} = x + \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$ |
| **Sensor Noise** | Random fluctuation in range/bearing measurements | Gaussian / Rayleigh distribution |
| **Map Uncertainty** | Imprecise obstacle boundaries in prior map | Bounded polyhedra with tolerance $\delta$ |
| **Localization Uncertainty** | Pose belief spreads over time without correction | Bayesian posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Uncertainty in robot pose is typically quantified by a **3├Ś3 covariance matrix** $\Sigma_t$ for 2D planar navigation, with units of $\text{m}^2$ for $(x, y)$ and $\text{rad}^2$ for orientation $\theta$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Static Uncertain Navigation

The core challenge is decomposed by the robotics community into three inter-locking sub-problems:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Perception Under Uncertainty:**
- **Why:** Sensors (LIDAR, sonar, cameras) return noisy distance/bearing estimates. A single reading is unreliable.
- **How:** Fuse multiple sensor readings using probabilistic filters to build a *belief* about the world.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Localization:**
- **Why:** Without knowing *where* it is, the robot cannot plan a useful path.
- **How:** Maintain a probability distribution $bel(x_t)$ over all possible poses, updated by a *prediction step* (motion model) and a *correction step* (measurement model).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Path Planning with Re-planning:**
- **Why:** In unknown or partially known static maps, the initially planned path may be invalidated by newly discovered obstacles.
- **How:** Use reactive planners (Bug algorithms, Potential Fields) or sampling-based planners (PRM, RRT) that can adapt to new information.

### 2.2 Core Algorithmic Strategies

#### A. Bug Algorithms (Tactile / Range Sensor Based)
The Bug family are the simplest reactive navigators for static uncertain environments.

**Bug 0 Algorithm Logic:**
1. Head directly toward the goal.
2. On contact with an obstacle, follow its boundary.
3. Resume motion to goal after circumnavigation.

**Bug 1 Algorithm (Optimal Contact Point):**
1. Move from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ in a straight line.
2. If an obstacle is hit, circumnavigate it completely and return to the **point of departure** (called the *hit point*).
3. From all *leave points* encountered, choose the one **closest to the goal**, and continue toward the goal.

**Bug 2 Algorithm (M-line Following):**
1. Define the **M-line** as the straight line from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
2. Move along the M-line.
3. On hitting an obstacle, follow its boundary in a predefined direction (say, clockwise).
4. Leave the boundary the moment the robot **re-encounters** the M-line at a point **closer to the goal** than the hit point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Comparison:** For a path of length $L$ and obstacle perimeter $P_i$, Bug 1 gives $L \leq d_{path} \leq L + 1.5 \cdot \sum P_i$, while Bug 2 is tighter but assumes the M-line intersection is a sufficient leave condition.

#### B. Configuration Space (C-Space) Obstacles

A robot of physical shape $\mathcal{A}$ translated by configuration $q$ in workspace $\mathcal{W}$ is said to be in collision if:
$$\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset$$
The C-space obstacle is the set of all "forbidden" configurations:
$$C_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$$
The free space is:
$$C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$$

For a circular robot of radius $r$ and a point obstacle, the C-obstacle is the obstacle "grown" by $r$:
$$C_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(q, p) \leq r \}$$
This reduction allows the robot to be treated as a point in C-space.

#### C. Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)

The robot experiences an **attractive** potential toward the goal and a **repulsive** potential from obstacles.

**Attractive Potential:**
$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot k_{att} \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$$

**Repulsive Potential:**
$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot k_{rep} \cdot \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Total Force (Gradient Descent):**
$$F(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Potential field methods suffer from **local minima** ŌĆö the robot can get trapped where attractive and repulsive forces cancel. This is a frequently asked concept in KTU examinations.

#### D. Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) ŌĆö Multi-Query Planning

For static environments with high uncertainty in map completion, PRMs sample the C-space and build a roadmap:

1. **Sampling:** Draw $N$ random configurations $q_i \in C_{free}$.
2. **Connection:** For each $q_i$, find $k$ nearest neighbors and connect with a straight line if the edge lies in $C_{free}$.
3. **Query:** Connect $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ to the roadmap, then run graph search (e.g., A$^*$).

#### E. Bayesian Localization Filter

The recursive Bayesian filter is the backbone of handling uncertainty. It alternates between prediction and update:

**Prediction (using motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$):**
$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Update (using measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$):**
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$
where $\eta$ is a normalizer:
$$\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x_t') \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t' \right]^{-1}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö Module 3**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Bayesian prediction | Localization update |
| 2 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayesian measurement update | Sensor fusion |
| 3 | $F(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$ | Potential field force | Reactive navigation |
| 4 | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$ | Attractive potential | Path attraction |
| 5 | $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ | Repulsive potential | Obstacle avoidance |
| 6 | $C_{obs} = \{ q \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$ | C-space obstacle definition | Path planning |
| 7 | $d_{path}^{Bug1} \leq L + 1.5 \sum_i P_i$ | Bug 1 worst-case path | Performance bound |
| 8 | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) p(x_{t-1} \mid \cdot) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Full recursive posterior | Markov localization |
| 9 | $H(z) = -\sum p(z_i) \log p(z_i)$ | Entropy (uncertainty measure) | Information gain |
| 10 | $\Sigma_t = A \Sigma_{t-1} A^T + R$ | Covariance propagation (EKF) | Uncertainty evolution |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Static uncertain navigation is foundational to:
- **Autonomous Warehouse Robots** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) navigating among fixed shelving.
- **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** (Roomba) operating in unmapped homes.
- **Underwater Mapping AUVs** exploring the seabed with noisy sonar.
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers) navigating rock-strewn terrain with hours of communication delay.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Recursive Posterior from First Principles

We begin with the *desired* posterior over the current state $x_t$ given all measurements $z_{1:t} = z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t$ and all control inputs $u_{1:t} = u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the posterior using Bayes' Rule:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Markov Assumption (state is complete summary):**
Under the Markov property, the current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$:
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and introduce the normalizer $\eta$:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the prediction term using the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation and Markov assumption:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize that the second factor is just the previous belief:**
$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1})$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Combine all steps into the canonical two-stage update:**

$$\boxed{ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} }$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Interpretation of the derivation:**
- The *integral* (prediction) propagates the previous belief through the noisy motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.
- The *multiplication* (correction) re-weights the predicted belief by the likelihood of the new sensor reading $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.
- The *normalizer* $\eta$ ensures the result is a valid probability distribution summing/integrating to 1.

### 3.2 Derivation: Potential Field Gradient (Force Computation)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the total potential:**
$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{rep,i}(q)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the gradient of the attractive potential:**
$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} (q - q_{goal})^T (q - q_{goal})$$

Taking the partial derivative with respect to $q$:
$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = k_{att} (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the gradient of the repulsive potential for one obstacle:**
$$U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, \quad \rho(q) \leq \rho_0$$

Using the chain rule with $\rho(q) = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert$:
$$\nabla U_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \cdot (q - q_{obs})$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The total force on the robot is the negative gradient:**
$$\boxed{ F(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal}) - \sum_{i} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{(q - q_{obs,i})}{\rho_i^2(q)} }$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Robot motion law (gradient descent in configuration space):**
$$\dot{q} = F(q) \quad \Rightarrow \quad q(t + \Delta t) = q(t) + \Delta t \cdot F(q(t))$$

### 3.3 Bug 1 Path Length Bound ŌĆö Analytical Walkthrough

Given a path of perimeter $L$ (length of straight-line trajectory) and total obstacle perimeter $P = \sum_i P_i$ encountered:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Straight-line segments traversed:**
The robot traverses the M-line, broken into segments of total length $L$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Circumnavigation overhead:**
Each obstacle is fully circumnavigated. The worst case is when the goal lies diametrically opposite the hit point on a convex obstacle, requiring traversal of half the perimeter before being able to leave. Summing worst-cases:
$$\text{Extra distance} = \sum_i \tfrac{1}{2} P_i + \sum_i P_i = 1.5 \sum_i P_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combine into upper bound:**
$$d_{path}^{Bug1} \leq L + 1.5 \sum_i P_i$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bug 2 Algorithm ŌĆö Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at q_start] --> B[Compute M-line to q_goal]
    B --> C[Move along M-line]
    C --> D{Obstacle Detected?}
    D -- No --> E{Goal Reached?}
    D -- Yes --> F[Record hit point H]
    F --> G[Follow obstacle boundary clockwise]
    G --> H{Re-encounter M-line?}
    H -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> I{Closer to goal than H?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Record leave point L]
    J --> C
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> K[STOP at q_goal]
```

### 4.2 Probabilistic Navigation System ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS[Sensor Layer]
        S1[LIDAR Scan]
        S2[Wheel Odometry]
        S3[IMU]
    end
    subgraph FILT[Bayesian Filter Core]
        P1[Prediction Step - Motion Model]
        P2[Update Step - Measurement Model]
        P3[Belief bel x t]
    end
    subgraph PLAN[Planning Layer]
        Q1[PRM / Visibility Graph]
        Q2[Potential Field]
        Q3[Bug Reactive]
    end
    subgraph ACT[Actuator Layer]
        R1[Velocity Controller]
        R2[Motor Drivers]
    end
    S1 --> P2
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> SENS
```

### 4.3 Configuration Space Reduction ŌĆö Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Workspace W with point obstacles] --> M[Minkowski Sum: O plus robot shape A]
    M --> C[C-obstacle: grown obstacles]
    C --> T[Treat robot as point in C-space]
    T --> P[Plan path through C-free]
    P --> E[Execute path by inverse transform]
```

### 4.4 Uncertainty Propagation Over Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0[Initial Pose x_0 - low uncertainty] --> M1[Motion u_1 adds noise]
    M1 --> X1[Pose x_1 - growing covariance]
    X1 --> M2[Motion u_2 adds noise]
    M2 --> X2[Pose x_2 - larger covariance]
    X2 --> Z[Sensor z_3 reduces covariance]
    Z --> X3[Corrected pose x_3 - shrunken covariance]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
Differentiate between the Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms for robot navigation in a static unknown environment. State the worst-case path-length bound for Bug 1.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Bug 1 | Bug 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Traversal** | Full circumnavigation of each obstacle | Partial ŌĆö leaves at first M-line re-encounter closer to goal |
| **Leave Criterion** | Minimum-distance leave point to goal | Closest M-line point to goal |
| **Optimality** | More robust, longer path | Shorter path, less robust to non-convex obstacles |
| **Complexity** | $O(n \cdot P)$ where $P$ is total perimeter | $O(n \cdot P)$ but with smaller constant |

**Worst-case bound for Bug 1:** $d_{path} \leq L + 1.5 \sum_i P_i$ where $L$ is the start-to-goal straight-line distance and $P_i$ is the perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
Define Configuration Space (C-space) and Configuration Space Obstacle (C-obstacle). Why is this transformation important for path planning under uncertainty?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**C-space:** The set of all possible configurations (poses) $q$ that a robot can attain. For a planar rigid robot, $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (i.e., $x, y, \theta$). **(1 mark)**

**C-obstacle:** $C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$ ŌĆö the set of configurations that cause a collision. **(1 mark)**

**Importance under uncertainty:** Reducing the robot to a point in C-space eliminates the need to reason about its shape during planning. The planner can compute collision-free paths in a single representation, and uncertainty in pose can be modeled as a probability distribution over $C_{free}$. This separation of *where the robot is* from *what shape it is* is the foundation of modern probabilistic planners. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B ŌĆö Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Module 3:**
> Students frequently lose 3ŌĆō4 marks by:
> 1. **Confusing Bug 1's leave rule** with Bug 2's M-line rule ŌĆö Bug 1 finds the *minimum-distance-to-goal* leave point; Bug 2 leaves on first M-line encounter closer to the goal.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization constant $\eta$** in the Bayesian update ŌĆö without it, the posterior is not a valid probability.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption of static environment** explicitly ŌĆö the static assumption is what allows us to *reuse* the map and avoid re-planning the same obstacles.
> 4. **Confusing potential field local minima** with global minima ŌĆö the issue is local minima where $F(q) = 0$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a clear functional block diagram, describe the architecture of a probabilistic navigation system for a mobile robot operating in a static environment with sensor and odometric uncertainty. Explain the roles of the *prediction step* and *correction step* of the Bayesian filter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the definition of belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, derive the recursive Bayesian update equations step-by-step. State clearly the role of the Markov assumption in your derivation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A

**(a) Probabilistic Navigation Architecture (7 Marks):**

The system comprises four interconnected layers: **Sensing**, **Belief Maintenance**, **Planning**, and **Actuation**.

1. **Sensing Layer:** LIDAR and wheel encoders provide range and odometric data respectively. Both are corrupted by additive Gaussian noise characterized by covariance matrices $Q$ (process) and $R$ (measurement). **(1 mark)**

2. **Prediction Step:** Uses the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ to propagate the previous belief $bel(x_{t-1})$ forward in time, *before* the new measurement arrives. This yields the predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Correction Step:** Upon receiving a new sensor reading $z_t$, the predicted belief is multiplied by the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ and normalized. This *corrects* the pose estimate and shrinks the uncertainty. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Planning Layer:** With an updated belief and known (or partially known) static map, a global planner (e.g., A$^*$ on a PRM) generates a coarse path. A local planner (e.g., Dynamic Window Approach or Potential Field) refines the path using real-time sensor data. **(1.5 marks)**

5. **Actuation Layer:** Velocity commands $(v, \omega)$ are sent to the motor controllers. Feedback closes the loop. **(1 mark)**

6. **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above for the Mermaid block).** **(0.5 mark)**

**(b) Derivation of Recursive Bayesian Update (7 Marks):**

Begin with the desired posterior:
$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Bayes' Rule:
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply Markov Assumption ŌĆö the current measurement depends only on the current state:
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Introduce the normalizer:
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Apply the law of total probability to the prediction:
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Recognize the second factor as $bel(x_{t-1})$:
$$\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Role of Markov Assumption:** It guarantees that the entire history of measurements and controls is summarized by the current state $x_t$, making the recursion tractable. Without it, the integral would involve the entire trajectory, which is computationally intractable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the Potential Field method for robot navigation in a static uncertain environment. Derive the expressions for the attractive and repulsive potentials, and show that the total force on the robot is the negative gradient of the total potential. Mention one major limitation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms in detail with suitable state-transition diagrams. For an environment with a single circular obstacle of radius $R$ placed such that the start and goal are on the M-line, compute the worst-case path length for Bug 1. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B

**(a) Potential Field Method (7 Marks):**

The robot is treated as a point in a fictitious potential field. **(0.5 mark)**

**Attractive Potential (2 marks):**
$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$$

This potential is **zero at the goal** and grows quadratically with distance, ensuring the robot is "pulled" toward the goal. The corresponding attractive force is:
$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Repulsive Potential (2 marks):**
$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

This potential blows up as the robot approaches an obstacle ($\rho \to 0$) and vanishes beyond a distance of influence $\rho_0$. The repulsive force is:
$$F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2(q)} (q - q_{obs})$$

**Total Force (1.5 marks):**
$$F(q) = -\nabla[U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)] = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q)$$

The robot executes a gradient descent: $q_{t+1} = q_t + \alpha F(q_t)$ where $\alpha$ is the step size.

**Limitation (1 mark):** **Local Minima Problem** ŌĆö in narrow corridors or symmetric obstacle configurations, the attractive and repulsive forces can cancel at a non-goal point, trapping the robot permanently.

**(b) Bug Algorithms (7 Marks):**

**Bug 1 Algorithm (2.5 marks):**
1. Move toward the goal along the M-line until an obstacle is encountered.
2. Mark the *hit point* $H$ ŌĆö the location where the boundary was first touched.
3. Circumnavigate the obstacle completely, recording every *leave point* $L_i$ where the M-line is re-encountered.
4. At the start point of the obstacle, compute the distance $\Vert L_i - q_{goal} \Vert$ for every leave point.
5. Choose the leave point $L^*$ with the **minimum** distance to the goal, and resume straight-line motion.

**Bug 2 Algorithm (1.5 marks):**
1. Move along the M-line until an obstacle is hit at hit point $H$.
2. Follow the boundary in a fixed direction (e.g., clockwise).
3. Leave the boundary *immediately* upon re-encountering the M-line at a point $L$ such that $\Vert L - q_{goal} \Vert < \Vert H - q_{goal} \Vert$.

**State Diagrams** (refer to Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 ŌĆö Bug 2; for Bug 1, the "leave condition" is replaced with the minimum-distance comparison). **(1 mark)**

**Worst-Case Path Length for Bug 1 with Single Circular Obstacle (2 marks):**
- $L$ = straight-line distance from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ (this is the M-line length).
- Perimeter of the circular obstacle: $P = 2\pi R$.
- Bug 1 worst-case bound: $d_{path} \leq L + 1.5 P$
- Substituting: $d_{path} \leq L + 1.5 \cdot 2\pi R = L + 3\pi R$

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 3**

- **Static Uncertain Environment:** Time-invariant obstacles, but noisy sensors, odometry, or maps.
- **Three Pillars of the Module:** (i) **Planning** (Bug, C-space, PRM, Potential Field), (ii) **Localization under uncertainty** (Bayesian filter, EKF, Particle Filter), (iii) **Probabilistic integration** of both.
- **Bug 1 vs Bug 2:** Bug 1 = full circumnavigation + best leave point; Bug 2 = M-line re-encounter leave rule.
- **Bug 1 Worst-Case Path:** $L + 1.5 \sum P_i$.
- **C-Space Reduction:** Treat the robot as a point by *growing* obstacles by the robot's radius (Minkowski sum).
- **Potential Field Limitation:** Local minima ŌĆö the robot can get stuck.
- **Attractive Potential:** Quadratic, $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} d^2$.
- **Repulsive Potential:** Inverse-square blow-up near obstacles, zero beyond $\rho_0$.
- **Bayesian Filter:** Prediction (motion model integral) + Update (measurement likelihood, normalized).
- **Markov Assumption:** The current state summarizes all history ŌĆö this is what makes the filter *recursive*.
- **PRM:** Sampling-based, multi-query, suitable for static high-dimensional C-spaces.
- **Uncertainty Quantification:** Maintained as a **covariance matrix** $\Sigma_t$ in EKF or a set of weighted particles in a Particle Filter.
- **Static Property Justifies Map Reuse:** Once an obstacle is mapped, the robot need not re-sense it; this drastically reduces computational cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** (static environment, Markov property, Gaussian noise) explicitly at the start of your answer. KTU examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks for "clear problem formulation" ŌĆö do not skip it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Function and Potential Fields in Dynamic Environment - Estimation
Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation** and **Bayesian Localization**, **Prediction** refers to the *prior update* step of the recursive Bayes Filter. It computes the robot's belief about its state at time $t$ given all control inputs received up to time $t-1$, *before* incorporating the latest sensor measurement. Formally, the prediction step computes:

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot state (pose: position + orientation) at time $t$
- $u_t$ = control input (motion command) executed at time $t$
- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ = **state transition probability** (motion model)
- $\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$ = posterior belief at previous time step
- $\bar{bel}(x_t)$ = predicted belief (prior) at current time step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Prediction step is the *first half* of the Bayes Filter cycle. It uses the **Motion Model** only ŌĆö NO sensor measurement is used. This is a high-frequency exam topic because it is the foundation of Kalman Filters, Extended Kalman Filters (EKF), and Particle Filters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a familiar room and asked, *"Where are you right now?"* You remember where you were 10 seconds ago, and you have a rough idea that you took 5 steps forward. Your **Prediction** is your *guess* of your current position *before* you take off the blindfold and actually see (sense) the room.

The moment you look around and match what you see with your memory of furniture ŌĆö that is the **Correction** (Update) step. Prediction is therefore *"where do I think I am, just by tracking my footsteps?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In a self-driving car, prediction answers the question: *"Given my previous GPS fix, my wheel encoder readings, and my steering angle ŌĆö where am I likely to be 0.1 seconds from now?"* Notice the time horizon is short because prediction without measurement drift is inherently uncertain.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $p$ | Position $(x, y)$ | meters (m) |
| $\theta$ | Orientation / Heading | radians (rad) |
| $\mu_t$ | Mean state estimate at $t$ | $3 \times 1$ vector |
| $\Sigma_t$ | State covariance matrix at $t$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| $R_t$ | Motion noise covariance | $3 \times 3$ positive semi-definite |
| $\Delta t$ | Sampling period | **0.1 s** (typical 10 Hz loop) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Prediction Step

The prediction step is executed in three logical phases:

1. **Retrieve Previous Belief:** Pull $\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = \{\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1}\}$ (or particle set for Particle Filters) from memory.
2. **Apply Motion Model:** Propagate each hypothesis forward through $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$. This *adds* uncertainty because real-world motion is noisy.
3. **Marginalize (Integrate) Out the Previous State:** Compute the convolution integral to obtain the new prior $\bar{bel}(x_t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does uncertainty grow during prediction?** Because we are applying a probabilistic function (motion model) to a probabilistic input. Convolution of Gaussians is a Gaussian whose variance is the **sum** of variances ŌĆö never decreases. Without measurement correction, the robot's belief would diverge to infinity.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of the Motion Model

### A. Velocity-Based Motion Model
Suitable for robots with wheel encoder odometry reported as linear/angular velocity.

$$x_t = \begin{pmatrix} x_{t-1} \\ y_{t-1} \\ \theta_{t-1} \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} -v_t \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \\ v_t \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \\ \omega_t \Delta t \end{pmatrix} + \epsilon_t$$

where $v_t$ = linear velocity, $\omega_t$ = angular velocity, $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

### B. Odometry-Based Motion Model
Suitable for robots reporting odometry as $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta$ (e.g., from wheel encoders).

$$x_t = x_{t-1} \oplus \begin{pmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta \theta \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} x_{t-1} + \Delta x \cos\theta - \Delta y \sin\theta \\ y_{t-1} + \Delta x \sin\theta + \Delta y \cos\theta \\ \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta \end{pmatrix}$$

### C. Gaussian Noise Assumption
The motion noise $R_t$ is the covariance of the additive Gaussian noise injected at every control step. The standard choice is:

$$R_t = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_1 v_t^2 + \alpha_2 \omega_t^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \alpha_3 v_t^2 + \alpha_4 \omega_t^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \alpha_5 v_t^2 + \alpha_6 \omega_t^2 \end{pmatrix}$$

where $\alpha_1 \ldots \alpha_6$ are robot-specific calibration constants.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Table ŌĆö Memorize This for KTU Exams**

| Equation | Name | Use Case | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes Filter Prediction | General derivation / short answer | dimensionless probability |
| $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | Kalman Mean Prediction | Linear systems | state units |
| $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | Kalman Covariance Prediction | Linear systems | state$^2$ |
| $V_t = \frac{\partial g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})}{\partial x_{t-1}}$ | Jacobian (EKF Prediction) | Nonlinear systems | unitless $\times$ state units |
| $\bar{\Sigma}_t = V_t \Sigma_{t-1} V_t^T + R_t$ | EKF Covariance Prediction | Nonlinear systems | state$^2$ |
| $\bar{x}_t^{[m]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})$ | Particle Propagation | Monte Carlo | state units |
| $G_t = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v_t \Delta t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & v_t \Delta t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Velocity Model Jacobian | EKF with velocity input | unitless |
| $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \Delta t$ | Heading Update | Pure rotation | rad |
| $\dot{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_t + \Sigma_t F_t^T + Q_t$ | Continuous-time Riccati | Continuous EKF | state$^2$/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Prediction Step |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | 100 Hz prediction of ego-trajectory for collision avoidance |
| **Aerial Drones (UAV)** | IMU-based prediction in GPS-denied environments |
| **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles)** | Warehouse robot dead-reckoning between fiducial marker sightings |
| **Planetary Rovers (NASA)** | Visual odometry prediction when communication is delayed |
| **AR/VR Headsets** | 90 Hz pose prediction to prevent motion sickness from rendering lag |
| **Underwater AUVs** | Doppler velocity log (DVL) based prediction between sonar fixes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Bayes Rule to the Bayes Filter Prediction Equation

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form prediction equation $\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition of belief as a posterior:**
The belief over the state at time $t$, *before* measurement $z_t$, is the prior.

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) \triangleq p(x_t \mid u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Condition on the previous state $x_{t-1}$ via the Law of Total Probability:**

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t, x_{t-1} \mid u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the product rule of probability ($p(A,B) = p(A \mid B)p(B)$):**

$$\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1})\, p(x_{t-1} \mid u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Markov Assumption (state $x_{t-1}$ is a sufficient statistic):**
The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present state. Therefore, $x_t$ does not depend on $u_{1:t-1}$ or $z_{1:t-1}$ once $x_{t-1}$ is known.

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize the second factor as the previous belief:**

$$p(x_{t-1} \mid u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) = p(x_{t-1} \mid u_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) = \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Assemble the final result:**

$$\boxed{\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This derivation appears verbatim in 2-mark short-answer questions. Be able to name the **Markov Assumption** and the **Law of Total Probability** explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kalman Filter Linear Prediction

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form Kalman mean and covariance prediction from the general Bayes prediction, assuming linear Gaussian models.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume linear motion model:**

$$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$
