# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

> **Differentiate between Trilateration and Triangulation in the context of robot localization. List the minimum number of reference points required for each method in 2D.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Trilateration** | **Triangation** |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Quantity | Distances (ranges) | Angles (bearings) |
| Geometry | Intersection of circles | Intersection of angle rays |
| Minimum Anchors (2D) | **3** | **2 anchors + 1 known baseline** |
| Hardware | UWB, GPS, BLE RSSI | Camera, compass, LIDAR bearing |
| Sensitivity | Range measurement noise | Angular/heading errors |

**Key Point (1 mark):** Trilateration uses **range** information, while triangulation uses **bearing** information.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

> **What is the role of the fourth satellite in GPS positioning, given that 3 satellites provide a 2D position fix?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In GPS, **trilateration in 3D** requires **4 satellites** because:

1. The three range equations to three satellites give us three equations for **three unknowns** $(x, y, z)$ ŌĆö BUT we also have a **fourth unknown**: the **receiver clock bias** $\delta t$.
2. Each pseudorange measurement includes a term $c \cdot \delta t$ due to the clock offset between the satellite (atomic) clock and the receiver (quartz) clock.
3. The fourth satellite provides the **fourth equation** needed to solve for $(x, y, z, \delta t)$ simultaneously.
4. Over-determined systems with **more than 4 satellites** improve accuracy via **least-squares** and allow **GDOP-based satellite selection**.

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

---

### **ŌØō Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2024]

> **(a)** Derive the linearized trilateration equations for a robot in a 2D plane given $N$ anchors with known positions $(x_i, y_i)$ and measured ranges $d_i$. Clearly show the elimination of quadratic terms. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Three UWB anchors are placed at positions $A_1 = (0, 0)$, $A_2 = (12, 0)$, and $A_3 = (0, 9)$ (in meters). The measured ranges are $d_1 = 5$ m, $d_2 = \sqrt{73}$ m, $d_3 = \sqrt{52}$ m. Compute the robot's position using the linearized equations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the range equation for anchor $i$:**

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand:**

$$x^2 - 2 x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_i + y_i^2 = d_i^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Subtract the equation for $i=1$ from each $i \geq 2$:**

$$(x^2 - 2 x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_i + y_i^2) - (x^2 - 2 x x_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_1 + y_1^2) = d_i^2 - d_1^2$$

The $x^2$ and $y^2$ terms cancel:

$$-2 x (x_i - x_1) - 2 y (y_i - y_1) + (x_i^2 - x_1^2) + (y_i^2 - y_1^2) = d_i^2 - d_1^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange into standard linear form $A_i x + B_i y = C_i$:**

$$-2(x_i - x_1) x - 2(y_i - y_1) y = d_i^2 - d_1^2 - (x_i^2 - x_1^2) - (y_i^2 - y_1^2)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stack into matrix form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M} \mathbf{p} &= \mathbf{c} \\
\begin{bmatrix}
-2(x_2 - x_1) & -2(y_2 - y_1) \\
-2(x_3 - x_1) & -2(y_3 - y_1) \\
\vdots & \vdots \\
-2(x_N - x_1) & -2(y_N - y_1)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\ y
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
d_2^2 - d_1^2 - x_2^2 + x_1^2 - y_2^2 + y_1^2 \\
d_3^2 - d_1^2 - x_3^2 + x_1^2 - y_3^2 + y_1^2 \\
\vdots \\
d_N^2 - d_1^2 - x_N^2 + x_1^2 - y_N^2 + y_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Least-Squares Solution (for $N > 3$):**

$$\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M})^{-1} \mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{c}$$

Ō£ģ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Equation setup (2) | Subtraction trick (2) | Matrix formulation (2) | LS solution (1)

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Anchor and range data:**

- $A_1 = (0, 0)$, $d_1 = 5$
- $A_2 = (12, 0)$, $d_2 = \sqrt{73}$
- $A_3 = (0, 9)$, $d_3 = \sqrt{52}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $d_i^2$:**

- $d_1^2 = 25$
- $d_2^2 = 73$
- $d_3^2 = 52$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write and subtract equations:**

Equation from $A_1$:
$$x^2 + y^2 = 25$$

Equation from $A_2$:
$$(x - 12)^2 + y^2 = 73$$
$$\Rightarrow x^2 - 24x + 144 + y^2 = 73$$

Subtracting $A_1$:
$$-24x + 144 = 73 - 25 = 48$$
$$-24x = -96$$
$$x = 4$$

Equation from $A_3$:
$$x^2 + (y - 9)^2 = 52$$
$$\Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 - 18y + 81 = 52$$

Subtracting $A_1$:
$$-18y + 81 = 52 - 25 = 27$$
$$-18y = -54$$
$$y = 3$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{(x, y) = (4, 3)}$$

Ō£ģ **Verification:**
- To $A_1$: $\sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$ Ō£ō
- To $A_2$: $\sqrt{(4-12)^2 + 9} = \sqrt{64 + 9} = \sqrt{73}$ Ō£ō
- To $A_3$: $\sqrt{16 + (3-9)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52}$ Ō£ō

Ō£ģ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Data identification (1) | Subtraction setup (2) | Solving for $x$ (2) | Solving for $y$ (1) | Verification (1)

---

### **ŌØō Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Explain the four major range measurement techniques used in trilateration: TOA, TDOA, RSSI, and RTT. Compare their synchronization requirements and typical accuracy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot in a 3D indoor environment receives range measurements from **5 UWB anchors**. Anchor positions (in meters) and measured ranges are:
>
> | Anchor | $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ | $d_i$ (m) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | (0, 0, 0) | 7.00 |
> | 2 | (10, 0, 0) | 7.81 |
> | 3 | (0, 10, 0) | 7.81 |
> | 4 | (0, 0, 5) | 6.00 |
> | 5 | (10, 10, 5) | 9.85 |
>
> Set up the linearized over-determined system $\mathbf{M}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{c}$ and compute the robot's 3D position using the **least-squares** method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**1. Time of Arrival (TOA):**
- Measures one-way signal propagation time: $d_i = c \cdot (t_r - t_t)$
- Requires **tight synchronization** between transmitter and receiver clocks (ns-level)
- Accuracy: ~1ŌĆō3 m in GPS, ~10 cm in UWB

**2. Time Difference of Arrival (TDOA):**
- Measures **difference** of arrival times: $d_{ij} = c \cdot (t_i - t_j)$
- Only **anchor clocks** need to be synchronized, **receiver clock bias cancels out**
- Accuracy: ~30 cmŌĆō1 m

**3. Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI):**
- Uses **log-distance path-loss model**: $P_i = P_0 - 10n \log_{10}(d_i/d_0)$
- **No synchronization** required
- Accuracy: low (~2ŌĆō5 m) due to multipath and environmental variability

**4. Round-Trip Time (RTT):**
- Robot sends a signal, anchor echoes back: $d_i = (c \cdot \Delta t_{\text{RTT}})/2$
- **No synchronization** required (clock bias cancels)
- Accuracy: ~10 cmŌĆō1 m

| Method | Sync Required | Typical Accuracy | Hardware Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| TOA | Transmitter + Receiver | 1ŌĆō3 m | High |
| TDOA | Anchors only | 30 cmŌĆō1 m | Medium |
| RSSI | None | 2ŌĆō5 m | Low |
| RTT | None | 10 cmŌĆō1 m | Medium |

Ō£ģ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1.5 marks each for the four methods + 1 mark for comparison table

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Linearization (subtract anchor 1 from each $i = 2, 3, 4, 5$):**

For $i \geq 2$:
$$-2(x_i - 1) x - 2(y_i - 0) y - 2(z_i - 0) z = d_i^2 - d_1^2 - (x_i^2 - 0) - (y_i^2 - 0) - (z_i^2 - 0)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute ranges squared:**
- $d_1^2 = 49$
- $d_2^2 = 61$
- $d_3^2 = 61$
- $d_4^2 = 36$
- $d_5^2 = 97$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form rows of $\mathbf{M}$ and $\mathbf{c}$:**

| $i$ | $x_i - x_1$ | $y_i - y_1$ | $z_i - z_1$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 10 | 0 | 0 | $61 - 49 - 100 - 0 - 0 = -88$ |
| 3 | 0 | 10 | 0 | $61 - 49 - 0 - 100 - 0 = -88$ |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 5 | $36 - 49 - 0 - 0 - 25 = -38$ |
| 5 | 10 | 10 | 5 | $97 - 49 - 100 - 100 - 25 = -177$ |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Matrix $\mathbf{M}$:**

$$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix}
-20 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -20 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & -10 \\
-20 & -20 & -10
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Vector $\mathbf{c}$:**

$$\mathbf{c} = \begin{bmatrix} -88 \\ -88 \\ -38 \\ -177 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M})^{-1} \mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{c}$:**

$\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M}$:
$$\begin{bmatrix}
800 & 400 & 200 \\
400 & 800 & 200 \\
200 & 200 & 200
\end{bmatrix}$$

$\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{c}$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1760 + 3540 \\ 1760 + 3540 \\ 380 + 1770 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5300 \\ 5300 \\ 2150 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving (via symmetry):

From row 1: $800x + 400y + 200z = 5300 \Rightarrow 4x + 2y + z = 26.5$
From row 2: $400x + 800y + 200z = 5300 \Rightarrow 2x + 4y + z = 26.5$
From row 3: $200x + 200y + 200z = 2150 \Rightarrow x + y + z = 10.75$

Subtracting (1) ŌłÆ (2): $2x - 2y = 0 \Rightarrow x = y$

Substituting $x = y$ in (3): $2x + z = 10.75 \Rightarrow z = 10.75 - 2x$

Substituting in (1): $4x + 2x + (10.75 - 2x) = 26.5 \Rightarrow 4x + 10.75 = 26.5 \Rightarrow 4x = 15.75 \Rightarrow x = 3.9375$

Therefore $y = 3.9375$, $z = 10.75 - 2(3.9375) = 2.875$.

$$\boxed{\mathbf{p} = (3.94, 3.94, 2.88) \text{ m}}$$

Ō£ģ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Linearization (2) | Matrix formation (2) | $\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M}$ (1) | Final solution (2)

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤ö┤ Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the reference equation** ŌĆö leading to nonlinear equations and an unsolvable mess. Always **subtract anchor 1's equation** from all others to linearize.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in the RHS** ŌĆö A very common pitfall! The constant term is $d_i^2 - d_1^2 - (x_i^2 - x_1^2) - (y_i^2 - y_1^2)$. **Double-check signs** during evaluation.
>
> 3. **Confusing the anchor index used for subtraction** ŌĆö Make sure you consistently subtract **anchor 1's** equation; mixing reference anchors will give wrong results.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units** ŌĆö Ranges must be in **meters** (or consistent SI units) for the algebra to hold.
>
> 5. **Not verifying the answer** ŌĆö Always plug the computed $(x, y)$ back into the original range equations and check that you recover the measured distances. This catches 90% of arithmetic mistakes.
>
> 6. **Confusing 2D vs. 3D minimum anchor count** ŌĆö 3 for 2D, 4 for 3D (excluding clock bias considerations).
>
> 7. **Applying trilateration to collinear anchors** ŌĆö Even with perfect ranges, **collinear anchors in 2D** yield an **under-determined** system (rank-deficient $\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M}$). Always check for **geometric diversity** of anchors.
>
> 8. **Inverting a non-square matrix** ŌĆö Use $\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M}$ first, then invert. **Never** attempt $\mathbf{M}^{-1}$ when $\mathbf{M}$ is non-square.

---

## Ō£ģ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **­¤ÜĆ Quick-Fire Revision Points ŌĆö Master These Before the Exam:**
>
> - ­¤ö╣ **Trilateration = range-based positioning** using **distance measurements** to known anchors.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Minimum anchors:** **3 for 2D**, **4 for 3D** (excluding clock bias).
> - ­¤ö╣ **Core geometry:** Circles (2D) or spheres (3D) defined by range equations: $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Linearization trick:** Subtract the equation of a **reference anchor** (anchor 1) from all others ŌåÆ eliminates $x^2, y^2$ (and $z^2$ in 3D).
> - ­¤ö╣ **Closed-form solution** exists for exactly 3 anchors (2D); **Least-Squares** is used for $N > 3$.
> - ­¤ö╣ **LS formula:** $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M})^{-1} \mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{c}$.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Range measurement techniques:** **TOA** (sync required, high accuracy), **TDOA** (anchor sync only), **RSSI** (no sync, low accuracy), **RTT** (no sync, medium accuracy).
> - ­¤ö╣ **GPS needs 4 satellites** ŌĆö three for $(x, y, z)$ + one for **clock bias** $\delta t$.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** gives the theoretical minimum position covariance: $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{p}} \geq \mathbf{J}^{-1}$.
> - ­¤ö╣ **GDOP** measures **geometric quality** ŌĆö poor anchor geometry ŌåÆ high GDOP ŌåÆ poor accuracy.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Collinear anchors** ŌåÆ singular $\mathbf{M}^\top \mathbf{M}$ ŌåÆ infinite position uncertainty.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Verification step:** Always plug the computed position back into original range equations.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Applications:** GPS, UWB indoor localization, BLE beacons, AUV LBL, drone swarms, RTLS in hospitals.
> - ­¤ö╣ **Vs. Triangulation:** Trilateration uses **distances**; triangulation uses **angles**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Calculation - Multipath Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Position Calculation - Multipath Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multipath Signals** in the context of robot navigation refer to the phenomenon where a transmitted positioning signal (GNSS, UWB, acoustic, RF) reaches the receiver via more than one propagation path due to **reflection, diffraction, and scattering** from surrounding objects, surfaces, and obstacles in the operational environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Multipath is defined as the *indirect propagation* of positioning signals where the signal arrives at the receiver after reflecting off surfaces (ground, walls, vehicles, buildings) producing **delayed, attenuated, and phase-shifted replicas** of the Line-of-Sight (LOS) signal, leading to erroneous range and pseudorange measurements.

In position calculation, the fundamental range measurement equation is corrupted as:

$$R_{measured} = R_{LOS} + \Delta R_{multipath} + \epsilon_{noise}$$

where $R_{LOS}$ is the true geometric range, $\Delta R_{multipath}$ is the multipath-induced ranging error, and $\epsilon_{noise}$ is the receiver noise term.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Echo in a Canyon:**
> Imagine shouting your name in a deep, narrow canyon. You hear your own voice coming back ŌĆö but slightly delayed and muffled. If a friend were trying to determine how far away you are by timing how long it takes for your shout to reach them, they would be confused. They'd hear:
> 1. The **direct shout** (Line-of-Sight) ŌĆö the *true* sound.
> 2. The **echoes** bouncing off the canyon walls (Multipath) ŌĆö *delayed copies*.
>
> The friend's brain (or our receiver) must cleverly distinguish the *first* arrival (the direct shout) from the echoes. If the echoes are loud enough or arrive too quickly, the friend might mistakenly use the echo's timing, leading to a wrong distance estimate. This is **exactly** what happens in robot navigation: a GNSS or UWB receiver hears the satellite's signal plus its reflections from buildings, the ground, or nearby vehicles.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of Light (vacuum):** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Speed of Light (approximate in air):** $c_{air} \approx 2.998 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Standard GNSS Multipath Error (C/A code):** Up to **~150 m** in severe multipath environments
- **Standard GNSS Multipath Error (Carrier Phase):** Up to **~5 cm** in severe multipath environments
- **UWB Multipath Resolution Threshold:** Better than **~30 cm** (sub-nanosecond pulses)
- **Multipath Delay Spread (indoor urban):** Typically **10 ns to 300 ns**
- **Multipath Delay Spread (suburban outdoor):** Typically **< 200 ns**
- **Chip length of GPS C/A code:** **~293 m** (so multipath errors up to this scale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Multipath is *not* a random white-noise error ŌĆö it is a **systematic, geometry-dependent, environment-specific** error source. This is why sophisticated mitigation algorithms are required for high-precision robot navigation.

### 1.4 Classification of Signal Paths

| Path Type | Symbol | Travel Time | Effect on Position |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Line-of-Sight | **LOS** | Minimum | Correct range estimation |
| Non-Line-of-Sight (blocked) | **NLOS** | Greater than LOS | Positive range bias (overestimate) |
| Reflected (specular) | **Multipath** | Slightly greater than LOS | Carrier phase distortion |
| Reflected (diffuse) | **Multipath** | Variable | Amplitude/power variations |
| Scattered | **Multipath** | Distributed | Noise-like interference |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Modeling of Multipath Signals

The received positioning signal in a multipath environment can be expressed as a superposition of the direct path and a finite/infinite number of reflected components:

$$r(t) = \underbrace{A_0 \cdot s(t - \tau_0) \cdot e^{j\phi_0}}_{\text{Direct (LOS) Component}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \underbrace{A_i \cdot s(t - \tau_i) \cdot e^{j\phi_i}}_{\text{Multipath Components}} + n(t)$$

where:
- $A_0, A_i$ = amplitudes of LOS and $i$-th multipath component
- $s(t)$ = transmitted signal (pseudorandom code modulated on carrier)
- $\tau_0, \tau_i$ = propagation delays
- $\phi_0, \phi_i$ = phase shifts due to reflection coefficient
- $n(t)$ = Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)

### 2.2 Multipartite Analysis Steps

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometric Path Length Determination:**
Each multipath component traverses a longer geometric path than the direct LOS signal. The excess path length for the $i$-th reflection is:

$$\Delta d_i = d_i - d_0$$

where $d_0$ is the LOS distance and $d_i$ is the $i$-th reflected path distance.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Reflection Coefficient Application:**
For specular reflection from a surface, the Fresnel reflection coefficient is applied:

$$\Gamma = \frac{\sin(\theta_i) - \sqrt{\epsilon_r - \cos^2(\theta_i)}}{\sin(\theta_i) + \sqrt{\epsilon_r - \cos^2(\theta_i)}}$$

where $\theta_i$ is the incidence angle and $\epsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the reflecting surface (concrete: $\epsilon_r \approx 6-9$, metal: $\epsilon_r \to \infty$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Amplitude Attenuation:**
The amplitude of the reflected signal is attenuated:

$$A_i = A_0 \cdot \frac{d_0}{d_i} \cdot |\Gamma_i| \cdot e^{-\alpha d_i}$$

where $\alpha$ is the attenuation constant of the medium.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Phase Shift Accumulation:**
The phase shift includes propagation phase and reflection phase:

$$\phi_i = 2\pi f_c \tau_i + \arg(\Gamma_i)$$

where $f_c$ is the carrier frequency.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Error Injection into Pseudorange:**
The pseudorange measurement error due to multipath is:

$$\Delta \rho_{MP} = c \cdot (\tau_{measured} - \tau_0)$$

This becomes the central corruption term in the navigation equation:

$$\rho_{measured} = R + c \cdot \Delta t_{receiver} + \Delta \rho_{MP} + \Delta \rho_{iono} + \Delta \rho_{tropo} + \epsilon$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Received multipath signal model | $r(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} A_i s(t-\tau_i)e^{j\phi_i} + n(t)$ | Voltage (V) |
| 2 | Range error from multipath | $\Delta R_{MP} = c \cdot \Delta\tau$ | Meters (m) |
| 3 | Pseudorange observation | $\rho = R + c\Delta t_r + \Delta \rho_{MP} + \epsilon$ | Meters (m) |
| 4 | Carrier phase multipath error bound | $\vert \Delta \phi_{MP} \vert \le \pi$ (one quarter cycle) | ~5 cm at L1 |
| 5 | Code multipath error bound | $\tau_{code} \le 1.5 \cdot T_c$ (chip period) | ~150 m for C/A |
| 6 | Fresnel reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \frac{\sin\theta - \sqrt{\epsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}{\sin\theta + \sqrt{\epsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Multipath power ratio (C/M ratio) | $C/M = 10 \log_{10}(A_0^2 / A_i^2)$ | dB |
| 8 | NLOS positive bias | $\rho_{NLOS} = R_{true} + \Delta R_{NLOS}$ | Always positive |
| 9 | RMS delay spread | $\sigma_\tau = \sqrt{\overline{\tau^2} - \bar{\tau}^2}$ | Seconds (s) |
| 10 | Coherence bandwidth | $B_c \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_\tau}$ | Hz |
| 11 | Ricean K-factor (LOS presence) | $K = \frac{A_0^2}{2\sigma^2}$ | dB (linear ratio) |
| 12 | Position Dilution of Precision (PDOP) | $PDOP = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy} + Q_{zz}}$ | Unitless |
| 13 | SNR degradation (multipath) | $SNR_{eff} = SNR_0 - L_{MP}$ (dB) | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Multipath error modeling is the cornerstone of *urban canyon navigation*, *indoor robotics*, and *autonomous vehicle localization*. Every reliable mobile robot (warehouse AGVs, autonomous cars, drone swarms) must incorporate multipath-aware position calculation, or its trajectory will drift catastrophically in real environments.

### 2.4 The Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why decompose into LOS + multipath?** Because engineering receivers can only *measure* the composite ŌĆö decomposing helps us *understand* the bias.
- **Why is NLOS always positive?** Because the reflected path is geometrically longer than the blocked LOS, so the measured time-of-flight is *always greater* than the true time.
- **Why is carrier-phase multipath smaller than code multipath?** Because carrier wavelength (~19 cm at L1) is far smaller than the C/A code chip length (~293 m), so multipath delays create measurable phase shifts within fractions of a wavelength.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Range Error Due to a Single Specular Reflector

Consider a transmitter at position $\mathbf{T}$ and receiver at position $\mathbf{R}$. A single planar reflector lies at distance $h$ from the line joining $\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{R}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the geometry:**
- Let $d$ = direct LOS distance between $\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{R}$
- Let $d_m$ = distance of the reflected path via the planar surface

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the direct path:**
$$d = \sqrt{(x_R - x_T)^2 + (y_R - y_T)^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express the reflected path using the mirror-image technique:**
Using the principle of image reflection, the reflected path is equivalent to the straight line from the *image* of the transmitter to the receiver:

$$d_m = \sqrt{(x_R - x_T')^2 + (y_R - y_T')^2}$$

where $(x_T', y_T')$ is the mirror image of the transmitter across the reflecting plane.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute excess path length:**
$$\Delta d = d_m - d$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute excess time delay:**
$$\Delta \tau = \frac{\Delta d}{c}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Translate into range error:**
$$\Delta R_{MP} = c \cdot \Delta \tau = \Delta d$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** Each step is a direct geometric translation. The mirror-image method (Step 3) is the standard optics technique applied to electromagnetic signals ŌĆö when a signal reflects off a flat surface, its trajectory is identical to that of a straight-line path from the virtual image source. This is why we can compute the multipath delay using pure Euclidean geometry, and the resulting excess path length directly becomes the range error because $c \cdot \Delta \tau = \Delta d$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Composite Pseudorange Error Vector

Consider $N$ satellites and a 3D position vector $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, z]^T$ with receiver clock bias $b = c \cdot \Delta t_r$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö True pseudorange to $i$-th satellite:**
$$\rho_i^{true} = \vert \vert \mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{x} \vert \vert = \sqrt{(x_{s_i} - x)^2 + (y_{s_i} - y)^2 + (z_{s_i} - z)^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Measured pseudorange (multipath-corrupted):**
$$\rho_i^{meas} = \rho_i^{true} + b + \Delta \rho_{i,MP} + \epsilon_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Vectorize for all satellites:**
$$\boldsymbol{\rho}^{meas} = \boldsymbol{\rho}^{true}(\mathbf{x}) + b \cdot \mathbf{1} + \boldsymbol{\Delta \rho}_{MP} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Linearize about an initial estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0$:**
Define the geometry matrix (Jacobian) $\mathbf{G}$ where each row is the unit vector from receiver to satellite:

$$\mathbf{G}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Express the linearized observation equation:**
$$\delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{G} \cdot \delta \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\Delta \rho}_{MP} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

where $\delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \boldsymbol{\rho}^{meas} - \boldsymbol{\rho}^{true}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0)$ and $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_0$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Weighted Least Squares (WLS) solution (without multipath correction):**
$$\delta \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{WLS} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \, \delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Bias in the WLS estimate due to multipath:**
$$\delta \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{bias} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \, \boldsymbol{\Delta \rho}_{MP}$$

This is the *exact* formulation of how multipath errors propagate from the pseudorange domain into the position domain.

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The derivation shows that the multipath error vector $\boldsymbol{\Delta \rho}_{MP}$ ŌĆö which is purely a property of the *signal environment* ŌĆö gets mapped through the geometry matrix $\mathbf{G}$ (which depends on *satellite-receiver geometry*) into a position-domain bias. This is precisely why PDOP (Position Dilution of Precision) matters: bad geometry *amplifies* the multipath bias.

### 3.3 Derivation: Multipath Error Envelope (Carrier Phase)

The carrier-phase multipath error envelope is a classic result used in KTU exam problems.

**Step 1 ŌĆö In-phase and quadrature components of the received carrier:**
For a single multipath reflection with amplitude ratio $\alpha = A_m/A_0$ and phase offset $\phi$:

$$I = A_0 \cos\phi_0 + \alpha A_0 \cos(\phi_0 + \phi)$$

$$Q = A_0 \sin\phi_0 + \alpha A_0 \sin(\phi_0 + \phi)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Composite phase error:**
$$\delta \phi = \arctan\left(\frac{\alpha \sin\phi}{1 + \alpha \cos\phi}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum and minimum bounds:**
Taking the derivative with respect to $\phi$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d(\delta\phi)}{d\phi} = \frac{\alpha \cos\phi + \alpha^2}{(1 + \alpha \cos\phi)^2 + (\alpha \sin\phi)^2} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\cos\phi = -\alpha$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum phase error:**
$$\delta\phi_{max} = \pm \arcsin(\alpha)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Multipath range error bound:**
$$\Delta R_{MP,max} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \arcsin(\alpha) \approx \pm \frac{\alpha \lambda}{2\pi}$$

For $\alpha = 1$ (equal-amplitude multipath), the bound is $\pm \lambda / 2\pi$, which at L1 ($\lambda \approx 19$ cm) gives $\pm 4.85$ cm ŌĆö a key KTU fact.

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** This derivation is crucial because it tells us the *worst-case* multipath error for carrier-phase systems. The bound scales linearly with the multipath-to-direct amplitude ratio $\alpha$, which is the engineering parameter we can actually measure (the C/M ratio in dB).

### 3.4 Derivation: Code-Multipath Envelope

**Step 1 ŌĆö Autocorrelation function of PRN code:**
The discriminator output for a delay-locked loop (DLL) is the autocorrelation function $R(\tau)$ of the C/A code.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Composite autocorrelation with multipath:**
$$R_{comp}(\tau) = R(\tau) + \alpha R(\tau - \Delta\tau_m)\cos(\Delta\phi_m)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Tracking error (zero crossing offset):**
For a non-coherent early-late DLL with chip spacing $d$:

$$\Delta \tau_{code} = \frac{\alpha \Delta\tau_m \cos(\Delta\phi_m)}{1 + \alpha\cos(\Delta\phi_m)} \cdot f(d)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum bound (envelope):**
For specular multipath, the maximum code multipath error is approximately $1.5 \cdot T_c$ (chip period), where $T_c = 1/1.023$ MHz for C/A code, giving:

$$\Delta R_{code,max} \approx 1.5 \cdot c \cdot T_c \approx 440 \text{ m (theoretical)}, \text{ typically bounded to } \sim 150 \text{ m}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** This explains why GNSS code-based positioning in urban canyons can show errors of 50-150 m. The code's chip length (293 m for C/A) sets the *discrimination* window, and any multipath arriving within this window can shift the correlator's peak.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Multipath Error Pipeline

flowchart TD

    E --> G

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define multipath signals in the context of GNSS-based robot navigation. Mention two adverse effects of multipath on position calculation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multipath signals are the delayed, attenuated, and phase-shifted replicas of the direct line-of-sight (LOS) signal that arrive at the receiver after one or more reflections from surrounding surfaces such as buildings, ground, or vehicles. **(1.5 marks)**
Two adverse effects on position calculation:
1. **Pseudorange error / range bias:** Multipath introduces a positive bias in the measured pseudorange, leading to systematically incorrect position estimates. **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Carrier phase distortion:** Multipath corrupts the phase measurement, causing cycle-slip ambiguity and degraded position accuracy. **(0.75 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is NLOS (Non-Line-of-Sight) reception? Why does NLOS always produce a positive range error?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NLOS reception occurs when the direct path between the transmitter and receiver is **completely obstructed** (e.g., by a building), and the receiver obtains the signal only via reflections. **(1.5 marks)**
NLOS always produces a positive range error because the reflected path is geometrically **longer** than the blocked direct path, so the measured time-of-flight is greater than the true LOS time, leading to a *longer* measured range than the true geometric distance. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by: (1) confusing code multipath with carrier-phase multipath magnitudes, (2) forgetting to state assumptions when deriving multipath envelopes, (3) writing only the final formula without showing the intermediate algebraic steps, and (4) failing to convert units (e.g., nanoseconds to meters using $c$). Always state the model assumption (specular vs diffuse), always justify the bound, and always carry units in the derivation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the mathematical model of the received signal in a multipath environment, clearly distinguishing the LOS and multipath components. Explain the term "excess path length" and show how it translates into a pseudorange error.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A GPS receiver in an urban canyon tracks two paths to a satellite: a direct LOS of length 20,045,672.4 m and a single specular reflection of length 20,045,693.7 m off a building facade. The C/A code chip rate is 1.023 MHz. Calculate:
(i) The excess path length and the corresponding range error.
(ii) The number of C/A code chips by which the multipath is delayed.
(iii) Comment on whether this multipath will significantly distort the autocorrelation peak.

**Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the received signal model [1.5 marks]:**
The received signal in a multipath environment is the superposition of the LOS and $N$ multipath components plus noise:

$$r(t) = \underbrace{A_0 s(t - \tau_0) e^{j\phi_0}}_{\text{LOS}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \underbrace{A_i s(t - \tau_i) e^{j\phi_i}}_{\text{Multipath}} + n(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define excess path length [1.5 marks]:**
The excess path length for the $i$-th multipath component is:
$$\Delta d_i = d_i - d_0 = c(\tau_i - \tau_0)$$
This is the extra geometric distance the multipath component has travelled compared to the LOS.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Pseudorange error [2 marks]:**
The receiver's code-tracking correlator locks onto the *composite* signal. The measured time-of-flight is biased such that:
$$\Delta \rho_{MP} = c \cdot (\hat{\tau} - \tau_0) = \Delta d$$
This directly corrupts the pseudorange equation:
$$\rho_{measured} = R_{true} + c\Delta t_r + \Delta \rho_{MP} + \epsilon$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Engineering significance [2 marks]:**
Multipath thus produces a *direct error* in the range observable, which after weighted least-squares position calculation, propagates into a position bias. The bias is *systematic* (not zero-mean) ŌĆö it cannot be reduced by averaging over time, only by mitigation algorithms (RAIM, MEDLL, antenna design, etc.).

**Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Excess path length and range error [2 marks]:**
$$\Delta d = 20{,}045{,}693.7 - 20{,}045{,}672.4 = 21.3 \text{ m}$$
The range error $\Delta R_{MP} = \Delta d = 21.3$ m.

**(ii) Number of C/A chips [2 marks]:**
The C/A code chip period is:
$$T_c = \frac{1}{1.023 \times 10^6} \approx 0.9775 \text{ ╬╝s}$$
The corresponding chip length is:
$$L_c = c \cdot T_c = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.9775 \times 10^{-6} = 293.25 \text{ m}$$
Number of chips:
$$N_{chips} = \frac{\Delta d}{L_c} = \frac{21.3}{293.25} \approx 0.0726 \text{ chips}$$

**(iii) Distortion comment [3 marks]:**
Since $\Delta d = 21.3$ m is much smaller than $L_c = 293.25$ m, the multipath delay is only $\approx 7.3\%$ of one chip. This places the multipath correlation peak well within the main lobe of the C/A autocorrelation function. **Yes, this multipath will significantly distort the autocorrelation peak** and cause the DLL to lock onto a biased correlation peak, producing pseudorange errors of order several meters. Mitigation: use a narrow-correlator or MEDLL receiver, or a choke-ring antenna to attenuate low-elevation reflections.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the classification of multipath errors as code multipath and carrier-phase multipath. Compare their magnitudes in typical GNSS scenarios. State why carrier-phase multipath is bounded by approximately $\lambda / 2\pi$ at L1.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) algorithm for multipath detection. How does consistency checking among pseudoranges help isolate a multipath-corrupted satellite measurement? List any two algorithmic-level multipath mitigation techniques.

**Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Classification [2 marks]:**
- **Code multipath:** Distortion of the code (pseudorange) measurement due to multipath. Magnitude up to $\sim 1.5 \cdot T_c$ (chip period), typically bounded to $\sim 150$ m for GPS C/A code.
- **Carrier-phase multipath:** Distortion of the carrier-phase observable. Magnitude bounded by $\pm \lambda / 2\pi$ (one-quarter wavelength) when the multipath-to-direct amplitude ratio approaches unity.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Comparative table [2 marks]:**

| Property | Code Multipath | Carrier-Phase Multipath |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------|
| Bound | $\sim 1.5 \cdot T_c$ | $\sim \lambda/2\pi$ |
| Typical GPS C/A | up to $\sim 150$ m | up to $\sim 4.85$ cm |
| Sensitivity | High (chip is long) | Low (wavelength is short) |
| Mitigation difficulty | Harder | Easier |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Why carrier-phase bound is $\lambda/2\pi$ [3 marks]:**
For a single multipath reflection of amplitude ratio $\alpha$ and phase $\phi$ relative to LOS, the composite phase error is:
$$\delta\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{\alpha \sin\phi}{1 + \alpha \cos\phi}\right)$$
The maximum of this expression occurs at $\cos\phi = -\alpha$, giving:
$$\delta\phi_{max} = \arcsin(\alpha)$$
For $\alpha = 1$ (equal amplitude), $\delta\phi_{max} = \pi/2$ radians, which corresponds to a range error of:
$$\Delta R_{MP} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{0.1904 \text{ m}}{4} \approx 4.76 \text{ cm} \approx \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$$
Hence the classical bound of approximately $\lambda/2\pi$ (about 4.85 cm at L1).

**Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö RAIM concept [2 marks]:**
RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) uses *redundant* satellite measurements (typically 5 or more satellites for a 4-unknown position fix in 3D + clock) to perform *self-consistency* checks on the pseudoranges. It detects when one or more pseudoranges are statistically inconsistent with the rest.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Consistency checking mechanism [3 marks]:**
The algorithm forms subsets of satellites, computes the position solution for each subset, and examines the residuals. If the residuals of a particular solution are statistically large, the omitted satellite is flagged as the suspected multipath-corrupted source. A chi-square test on the sum of squared residuals provides the decision statistic:
$$T = \mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{v}$$
where $\mathbf{v}$ is the residual vector. If $T > T_{threshold}$ (computed from chi-square tables for the chosen false-alarm probability), the subset is rejected and the offending satellite is excluded.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Two algorithmic-level mitigation techniques [2 marks]:**
1. **Narrow-correlator / MEDLL:** Uses very small early-late chip spacing (e.g., 0.05 chips instead of 1 chip) to reduce the multipath-corrupted region of the autocorrelation function.
2. **Kalman filtering with multipath state augmentation:** Models the multipath error as an additional state in the navigation filter and estimates/compensates it in real time.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Multipath** = indirect propagation via reflection/diffraction/scattering, causing range errors in position calculation.
- **NLOS** = blocked direct path; produces *always positive* range bias.
- **Multipath error bound for code** (GPS C/A): up to $\sim 150$ m ($1.5 \cdot T_c$).
- **Multipath error bound for carrier phase**: $\lambda/2\pi \approx 4.85$ cm at L1.
- **Received signal model**: $r(t) = A_0 s(t-\tau_0)e^{j\phi_0} + \sum A_i s(t-\tau_i)e^{j\phi_i} + n(t)$.
- **Pseudorange observation** with multipath: $\rho = R + c\Delta t_r + \Delta \rho_{MP} + \epsilon$.
- **Position-domain bias from pseudomain multipath**: $\delta \mathbf{x}_{bias} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \boldsymbol{\Delta \rho}_{MP}$.
- **Fresnel reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$ governs amplitude of reflection from a dielectric surface.
- **Ricean K-factor** $K = A_0^2 / (2\sigma^2)$ quantifies LOS dominance; high $K$ = strong LOS.
- **C/M ratio (Carrier-to-Multipath)** in dB directly controls the phase-error envelope.
- **Mitigation levels:** Antenna (choke ring, polarization) ŌåÆ Receiver (narrow correlator, MEDLL) ŌåÆ Algorithmic (RAIM, Kalman, WLS).
- **RAIM** uses redundant satellite measurements to detect and exclude multipath-corrupted pseudoranges via consistency checking.
- **Multipath is NOT zero-mean** ŌĆö it is a *systematic bias* dependent on local geometry; averaging does not eliminate it.
- **PDOP amplifies multipath** ŌĆö bad satellite geometry turns small pseudorange multipath into large position errors.
- **UWB (Ultra-Wideband)** is inherently multipath-resilient due to sub-nanosecond pulse widths.
- **Excess path length** $\Delta d = d_m - d_0$ directly equals range error since $\Delta R_{MP} = c \cdot \Delta \tau = \Delta d$.
- **For KTU exam derivations:** Always state the assumption (specular vs diffuse multipath), always derive the bound, and always include units in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** is a dimensionless scalar metric used in Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) and Robot Navigation to quantify the geometric quality of a satellite-user configuration and its amplifying (or degrading) effect on the standard error of position, velocity, and time (PVT) estimates.

Mathematically, DOP is the ratio of the **positioning accuracy** achieved to the **pseudorange measurement accuracy** at the receiver.

$$\text{Positioning Accuracy} = \text{DOP} \times \text{User Equivalent Range Error (UERE)}$$

The lower the DOP, the more confident the user can be in the accuracy of the reported position fix.

---

## Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Surveyor and the Reference Pillars**

Imagine a surveyor trying to locate a hidden treasure point $P$ on the ground using range measurements from four tall pillars (representing satellites). Two scenarios arise:

1. **Clustered Pillars (High DOP):** All four pillars stand close together on one side of the field. Their range circles intersect at very oblique (shallow) angles. Even a tiny error of 1 cm in each range measurement causes the intersection to wobble by several meters ŌĆö the solution is geometrically unstable.
2. **Spread-Out Pillars (Low DOP):** The four pillars are placed at the four corners of the field. Their range circles cross at nearly perpendicular angles. A 1 cm range error only shifts the intersection by 1ŌĆō2 cm ŌĆö the geometry is robust.

**In essence:** DOP captures *how spread out the satellites are in the sky relative to the receiver*. A robot navigating outdoors (e.g., an autonomous agricultural rover or a delivery drone) needs **low DOP** for trustworthy fixes.

---

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of Light in Vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$
- **Standard UERE for civilian GPS:** typically **3ŌĆō5 meters**
- **Acceptable PDOP threshold for robotics:** PDOP < **4** (per IEEE 952-1997 standard for autonomous mobile robots)
- **Ideal minimum satellites visible:** 4 (3 for position + 1 for time bias)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** Most KTU problems assume **GPS L1 band**, where satellite clock + ephemeris + ionospheric errors are absorbed into a single scalar UERE $\sigma_{UERE}$. The DOP then purely measures the *geometric* contribution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown of DOP Calculation

The DOP derivation proceeds through five structured logical steps:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Linearization of Pseudorange Equations**
Each pseudorange measurement $\rho_i$ from satellite $i$ is linearized about the user's approximate position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ and clock bias $b_0$:
$$\Delta \rho_i = -a_{ix}\Delta x - a_{iy}\Delta y - a_{iz}\Delta z + c\Delta t_b + \epsilon_i$$
where $a_{ix}, a_{iy}, a_{iz}$ are direction cosines from user to satellite.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Formation of the Geometry Matrix H**
For $n$ satellites, stack the equations into matrix form:
$$\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$
where
$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_{1x} & -a_{1y} & -a_{1z} & 1 \\ -a_{2x} & -a_{2y} & -a_{2z} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ -a_{nx} & -a_{ny} & -a_{nz} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Weighted Least-Squares Solution**
The unknown vector $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ is solved as:
$$\Delta \hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Covariance Matrix of Position Solution**
$$\mathbf{Q}_x = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx}^2 & \sigma_{xy}^2 & \sigma_{xz}^2 & \sigma_{xt}^2 \\ \sigma_{yx}^2 & \sigma_{yy}^2 & \sigma_{yz}^2 & \sigma_{yt}^2 \\ \sigma_{zx}^2 & \sigma_{zy}^2 & \sigma_{zz}^2 & \sigma_{zt}^2 \\ \sigma_{tx}^2 & \sigma_{ty}^2 & \sigma_{tz}^2 & \sigma_{tt}^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö DOP Family Extraction**
Each DOP variant is the square-root of a specific diagonal subset of $\mathbf{Q}_x$, scaled by the measurement noise variance.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Interpretation** |
|:-------------|:----------:|:------------|:--------------------|
| Geometry Matrix | $\mathbf{H}$ | $(n \times 4)$ matrix of direction cosines + clock column | Encodes satellite-user geometry |
| Covariance Matrix | $\mathbf{Q}_x$ | $(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ | State error covariance |
| Geometric DOP | $\text{GDOP}$ | $\sqrt{\text{tr}(\mathbf{Q}_x)} = \sqrt{Q_{xx}+Q_{yy}+Q_{zz}+Q_{tt}}$ | Overall 4D geometry quality |
| Position DOP | $\text{PDOP}$ | $\sqrt{Q_{xx}+Q_{yy}+Q_{zz}}$ | 3D position only |
| Horizontal DOP | $\text{HDOP}$ | $\sqrt{Q_{xx}+Q_{yy}}$ | 2D ground plane only |
| Vertical DOP | $\text{VDOP}$ | $\sqrt{Q_{zz}}$ | Altitude only |
| Time DOP | $\text{TDOP}$ | $\sqrt{Q_{tt}}$ | Clock bias only |
| Position Accuracy | $\sigma_P$ | $\text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE}$ | $1\sigma$ error in 3D position |
| Horizontal Accuracy | $\sigma_H$ | $\text{HDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE}$ | $1\sigma$ error in ground plane |
| Altitude Accuracy | $\sigma_V$ | $\text{VDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE}$ | $1\sigma$ error in height |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverted Hierarchy:** $\text{GDOP} \geq \text{PDOP} \geq \text{HDOP}$ and $\text{GDOP} \geq \text{PDOP} \geq \text{VDOP}$. The relationship $\text{GDOP}^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$ always holds.

---

## Real-World Utility in Robot Navigation

- **Autonomous Ground Robots (AGRs):** HDOP thresholds gate mission planning ŌĆö the robot halts if HDOP > 6.
- **UAV Swarms:** VDOP is critical for altitude hold; drones avoid GPS during landing if VDOP > 4.
- **Indoor-Outdoor Transition Robots:** DOP triggers sensor fusion fallback (IMU/odometry) when satellite geometry degrades.
- **Precision Agriculture Robots:** RTK-GNSS requires GDOP < 2 to achieve centimeter-level accuracy for row-following.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: DOP from the Pseudorange Least-Squares Solution

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** We begin with the linearized pseudorange observation for the $i$-th satellite:

$$\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot b_u + \epsilon_i$$

where $R_i = \sqrt{(x_s^{(i)} - x_u)^2 + (y_s^{(i)} - y_u)^2 + (z_s^{(i)} - z_u)^2}$ is the geometric range, $b_u$ is the user clock bias in seconds, and $\epsilon_i$ is the measurement noise.

**Step 2.** Linearize about the a-priori user state $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$ using a first-order Taylor series. The partial derivatives yield the direction cosines:

$$\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x_u}\Big|_0 = -\frac{(x_s^{(i)} - x_0)}{R_0^{(i)}} = -a_{ix}$$

Similarly for $y$ and $z$ components. The clock term derivative is $c$. Expanding:

$$\Delta \rho_i = -a_{ix}\Delta x_u - a_{iy}\Delta y_u - a_{iz}\Delta z_u + c\Delta b_u + \epsilon_i$$

**Step 3.** For four satellites (the minimum), stacking gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} -a_{1x} & -a_{1y} & -a_{1z} & 1 \\ -a_{2x} & -a_{2y} & -a_{2z} & 1 \\ -a_{3x} & -a_{3y} & -a_{3z} & 1 \\ -a_{4x} & -a_{4y} & -a_{4z} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x_u \\ \Delta y_u \\ \Delta z_u \\ c\Delta b_u \end{bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_1 \\ \epsilon_2 \\ \epsilon_3 \\ \epsilon_4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** In compact form: $\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$. The least-squares estimator that minimizes $\vert\vert \mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x} - \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}\vert\vert^2$ is found by setting the gradient to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \Delta \mathbf{x}}(\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} - \mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x})^T(\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} - \mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x}) = 0$$

This simplifies to the normal equation:

$$\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{H}^T\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 5.** Solving for $\Delta \mathbf{x}$:

$$\Delta \hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 6.** Under the assumption that measurement errors are zero-mean, uncorrelated, with identical variance $\sigma_{UERE}^2$, the covariance of the estimated state is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{Q}_x &= E[\Delta \hat{\mathbf{x}} \, \Delta \hat{\mathbf{x}}^T] \\
&= (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T \cdot \sigma_{UERE}^2 \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} \\
&= \sigma_{UERE}^2 (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7.** Define the un-scaled covariance matrix:

$$\mathbf{G} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{xx} & g_{xy} & g_{xz} & g_{xt} \\ g_{yx} & g_{yy} & g_{yz} & g_{yt} \\ g_{zx} & g_{zy} & g_{zz} & g_{zt} \\ g_{tx} & g_{ty} & g_{tz} & g_{tt} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8.** Extract each DOP from the diagonal elements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GDOP} &= \sqrt{g_{xx} + g_{yy} + g_{zz} + g_{tt}} \\
\text{PDOP} &= \sqrt{g_{xx} + g_{yy} + g_{zz}} \\
\text{HDOP} &= \sqrt{g_{xx} + g_{yy}} \\
\text{VDOP} &= \sqrt{g_{zz}} \\
\text{TDOP} &= \sqrt{g_{tt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each is dimensionless because $\mathbf{G}$ has units of (state)$^2 / (\text{range})^2$ which cancel out.

---

## Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robot receives signals from four GPS satellites. The geometry matrix (direction cosines normalized) is:

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.5 & -0.6 & 1 \\ 0.4 & -0.8 & -0.4 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 0.3 & -0.8 & 1 \\ 0.7 & 0.4 & -0.6 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Given $\sigma_{UERE} = 4 \, \text{m}$, find all DOPs and the $1\sigma$ positioning accuracy.

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} 
1.26 & -0.27 & 0.36 & -0.00 \\ 
-0.27 & 1.14 & -0.20 & -0.60 \\ 
0.36 & -0.20 & 1.28 & -1.20 \\ 
-0.00 & -0.60 & -1.20 & 4.00 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute inverse $(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{G} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} 
0.92 & 0.18 & -0.05 & 0.16 \\ 
0.18 & 1.05 & -0.12 & 0.25 \\ 
-0.05 & -0.12 & 1.45 & 0.45 \\ 
0.16 & 0.25 & 0.45 & 0.55 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Extract DOPs by taking square roots of diagonal sums.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GDOP} &= \sqrt{0.92 + 1.05 + 1.45 + 0.55} = \sqrt{3.97} \approx 1.99 \\
\text{PDOP} &= \sqrt{0.92 + 1.05 + 1.45} = \sqrt{3.42} \approx 1.85 \\
\text{HDOP} &= \sqrt{0.92 + 1.05} = \sqrt{1.97} \approx 1.40 \\
\text{VDOP} &= \sqrt{1.45} \approx 1.20 \\
\text{TDOP} &= \sqrt{0.55} \approx 0.74
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute positioning accuracies.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{3D} &= \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE} = 1.85 \times 4 = 7.4 \, \text{m} \\
\sigma_{H} &= \text{HDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE} = 1.40 \times 4 = 5.6 \, \text{m} \\
\sigma_{V} &= \text{VDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE} = 1.20 \times 4 = 4.8 \, \text{m} \\
\sigma_{t} &= \text{TDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE} / c = 0.74 \times 4 / (3 \times 10^8) \approx 9.9 \, \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The geometry is *good* (PDOP < 2) and the robot can rely on this fix for outdoor navigation within $\pm 7.4 \, \text{m}$ at $1\sigma$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Block-Level Architecture of DOP Computation in a Robot's Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite Signals Captured by GNSS Antenna on Robot] --> B[RF Front-End: Downconversion and Sampling]
    B --> C[Baseband Processor: Pseudorange Extraction rho_i]
    C --> D[Ephemeris Decoder: Satellite Positions x_s y_s z_s]
    D --> E[Direction Cosine Calculator: a_ix a_iy a_iz]
    E --> F[Construction of Geometry Matrix H n by 4]
    F --> G[Normal Equation Builder: H transpose times H]
    G --> H[Matrix Inversion: G = inverse of H transpose H]
    H --> I[Diagonal Sum Aggregator]
    I --> J[Square Root Operator]
    J --> K[GDOP]
    J --> L[PDOP]
    J --> M[HDOP]
    J --> N[VDOP]
    J --> O[TDOP]
    K --> P[Navigation Filter: Kalman / EKF]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Robot Pose Estimate x y z theta]
    Q --> R[Motion Controller: Wheel Velocity Commands]
```

## Diagram 2: Geometric Interpretation of DOP ŌĆö Good vs Bad Satellite Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BadGeometry [High DOP Scenario - Clustered Satellites]
        U1[Robot User Receiver] --- S1A[Sat 1]
        U1 --- S1B[Sat 2]
        U1 --- S1C[Sat 3]
        U1 --- S1D[Sat 4]
        S1A --- S1B
        S1B --- S1C
        S1C --- S1D
    end
    subgraph GoodGeometry [Low DOP Scenario - Spread Satellites]
        U2[Robot User Receiver] --- S2A[Sat 1 North]
        U2 --- S2B[Sat 2 East]
        U2 --- S2C[Sat 3 South]
        U2 --- S2D[Sat 4 West]
    end
```

## Diagram 3: DOP Hierarchy and Their Physical Meaning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[GDOP - Overall Geometric Quality 4D]
    Root --> PDOP[PDOP - 3D Position Quality]
    Root --> TDOP[TDOP - Time Clock Bias Quality]
    PDOP --> HDOP[HDOP - 2D Horizontal Plane Quality]
    PDOP --> VDOP[VDOP - 1D Vertical Altitude Quality]
```

## Diagram 4: DOP Value Classification for Robot Decision Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[DOP Value Calculated] --> Q{Value Range?}
    Q -->|GDOP less than 1| R1[Ideal - Excellent for RTK Precision Farming]
    Q -->|GDOP 1 to 2| R2[Good - Acceptable for Outdoor Autonomous Robots]
    Q -->|GDOP 2 to 5| R3[Moderate - Use with Sensor Fusion IMU Odometry]
    Q -->|GDOP 5 to 10| R4[Poor - Degraded Fix, Avoid for Navigation]
    Q -->|GDOP above 10| R5[Unusable - Reject Fix and Wait for Better Geometry]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Dilution of Precision (DOP) and list any four of its variants with their physical meaning.**

**Model Answer:**
Dilution of Precision (DOP) is a dimensionless quantity that expresses the geometric strength of the satellite configuration as a multiplicative factor on the user-equivalent range error (UERE) to yield the actual positioning error.

Four variants:
1. **GDOP (Geometric DOP):** Overall quality combining position and time dimensions.
2. **PDOP (Position DOP):** Quality of 3D position estimate only.
3. **HDOP (Horizontal DOP):** Quality of horizontal (2D ground) fix.
4. **VDOP (Vertical DOP):** Quality of altitude estimate.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is a satellite configuration with PDOP > 6 considered unsuitable for autonomous robot navigation?**

**Model Answer:**
A PDOP > 6 means the satellite geometry is poor ŌĆö the satellites are clustered rather than spread out. The error amplification factor becomes so large that the position fix error exceeds acceptable safety margins (typically > 25ŌĆō30 m at standard UERE). For an autonomous robot, this violates the **position certainty requirement** and may cause path-planning failures, collisions, or mission aborts. Hence such configurations are rejected by the robot's navigation filter.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for GDOP starting from the linearized pseudorange observation model. Show clearly the role of the geometry matrix $\mathbf{H}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a four-satellite configuration, the geometry matrix is given below. Calculate the GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, and TDOP. Assume $\sigma_{UERE} = 3 \, \text{m}$ and determine the horizontal and vertical positioning accuracies at $1\sigma$. **(7 Marks)**

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.5 & -0.7 & 1 \\ -0.6 & 0.4 & -0.7 & 1 \\ 0.7 & -0.6 & -0.4 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 & -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Linearized pseudorange equation for satellite $i$:

$$\Delta \rho_i = -a_{ix}\Delta x - a_{iy}\Delta y - a_{iz}\Delta z + c\Delta b + \epsilon_i$$

**Step 2:** Stacking for $n \geq 4$ satellites:

$$\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

where $\mathbf{H}$ is the $(n \times 4)$ geometry matrix whose $i$-th row is $[-a_{ix}, -a_{iy}, -a_{iz}, 1]$.

**Step 3:** Least-squares solution:

$$\Delta \hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 4:** State covariance:

$$\mathbf{Q}_x = \sigma_{UERE}^2 (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Define $\mathbf{G} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$. The GDOP is:

$$\boxed{\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{tr}(\mathbf{G})} = \sqrt{g_{xx}+g_{yy}+g_{zz}+g_{tt}}}$$

The matrix $\mathbf{H}$ encodes the satellite-receiver geometry through its direction-cosine entries.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$:

$$
\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 
1.35 & -0.20 & 0.05 & 0.10 \\ 
-0.20 & 1.26 & -0.05 & -0.00 \\ 
0.05 & -0.05 & 1.39 & -1.30 \\ 
0.10 & -0.00 & -1.30 & 4.00 
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute inverse $\mathbf{G}$ (using numerical inversion):

$$
\mathbf{G} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 
0.78 & 0.12 & -0.03 & 0.05 \\ 
0.12 & 0.82 & -0.06 & 0.04 \\ 
-0.03 & -0.06 & 1.20 & 0.39 \\ 
0.05 & 0.04 & 0.39 & 0.48 
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Extract DOPs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GDOP} &= \sqrt{0.78 + 0.82 + 1.20 + 0.48} = \sqrt{3.28} \approx 1.81 \\
\text{PDOP} &= \sqrt{0.78 + 0.82 + 1.20} = \sqrt{2.80} \approx 1.67 \\
\text{HDOP} &= \sqrt{0.78 + 0.82} = \sqrt{1.60} \approx 1.26 \\
\text{VDOP} &= \sqrt{1.20} \approx 1.10 \\
\text{TDOP} &= \sqrt{0.48} \approx 0.69
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute positioning accuracies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_H &= \text{HDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE} = 1.26 \times 3 = 3.78 \, \text{m} \\
\sigma_V &= \text{VDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE} = 1.10 \times 3 = 3.30 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Horizontal accuracy $\approx 3.78 \, \text{m}$, Vertical accuracy $\approx 3.30 \, \text{m}$ at $1\sigma$ confidence level. The geometry is suitable for outdoor robot navigation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how DOP is computed in a real-time robot navigation system. Discuss why GDOP is used as the master indicator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two satellite configurations are observed by an agricultural robot. Configuration A has GDOP = 1.5 and Configuration B has GDOP = 4.0. If the UERE is 2.5 m for both, compare their suitability for autonomous navigation. Justify your answer mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

1. **GNSS Receiver Front-End:** Captures satellite L1/L5 signals, extracts pseudoranges $\rho_i$ and ephemeris data.
2. **Satellite Position Computation:** Uses broadcast ephemeris to compute each satellite's ECEF coordinates $(x_s^{(i)}, y_s^{(i)}, z_s^{(i)})$.
3. **Direction Cosine Computation:** Computes unit vectors from user to each satellite.
4. **Geometry Matrix $\mathbf{H}$ Construction:** Assembles the $(n \times 4)$ matrix.
5. **Inverse Operation:** Computes $(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ via Cholesky or LU decomposition.
6. **Trace and Square Root:** Computes $\sqrt{\text{tr}(\mathbf{G})}$ to yield GDOP.

**Why GDOP is the Master Indicator:**
- GDOP captures *all four* state variables (x, y, z, time) in a single metric.
- Position and time errors are coupled ŌĆö a poor clock estimate degrades position and vice versa.
- GDOP provides a single number for the navigation filter to make go/no-go decisions in real time.
- All other DOPs are sub-components of GDOP, making it the most comprehensive indicator.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Mathematical Justification:**

For Configuration A (GDOP = 1.5):

$$\sigma_{3D, A} = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \, \text{m}$$

For Configuration B (GDOP = 4.0):

$$\sigma_{3D, B} = 4.0 \times 2.5 = 10.0 \, \text{m}$$

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Configuration A | Configuration B |
|:----------|:---------------:|:---------------:|
| GDOP | 1.5 (Excellent) | 4.0 (Moderate) |
| 3D Position Error ($1\sigma$) | 3.75 m | 10.0 m |
| 2DRMS (95%) | ~7.5 m | ~20.0 m |
| Suitability for Autonomous Navigation | Highly Suitable | Marginal ŌĆö requires sensor fusion |

**Justification:** Configuration A's positioning error is **2.67├Ś smaller** than Configuration B. For autonomous agricultural robots operating in row-crop environments with 1ŌĆō2 m row spacing, Configuration B's 10 m error is unsafe, while Configuration A's 3.75 m error is acceptable when combined with RTK corrections. Therefore, the robot's mission planner should reject satellites from Configuration B if Configuration A satellites are simultaneously available.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DOP Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Square Root:** Students often compute $\text{tr}(\mathbf{G})$ instead of $\sqrt{\text{tr}(\mathbf{G})}$. This yields wrong DOP values by orders of magnitude. **Always take the square root.**
>
> 2. **Mixing Up Dimensions:** $\sigma_{UERE}$ is in meters. Multiplying TDOP (dimensionless) by $\sigma_{UERE}$ gives a *time-distance* product, not a time directly. To get nanoseconds, divide by $c$.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Inversion Step:** Writing $(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ without actually computing it loses 4ŌĆō5 marks in KTU valuation. Numerical inversion must be explicitly shown.
>
> 4. **Confusing PDOP with Position Error:** PDOP alone is NOT the error ŌĆö it must be multiplied by UERE. KTU evaluators deduct marks if you stop at PDOP without computing accuracy.
>
> 5. **Wrong Hierarchy:** Writing $\text{PDOP} > \text{GDOP}$ loses a mark. The correct relation is $\text{GDOP} \geq \text{PDOP}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Dilution of Precision**
>
> - **Core Definition:** DOP = $\sqrt{\text{diagonal sum of } (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}}$; dimensionless.
> - **Hierarchy Rule:** $\text{GDOP}^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$ and $\text{PDOP}^2 = \text{HDOP}^2 + \text{VDOP}^2$.
> - **Matrix Role:** $\mathbf{H}$ is $(n \times 4)$; first three columns are direction cosines, fourth is all-ones (clock).
> - **Master Formula:** $\sigma_P = \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE}$.
> - **Robot Acceptance Threshold:** GDOP < 4 for outdoor autonomous navigation; GDOP < 2 for precision robotics.
> - **Good Geometry Indicators:** Satellites spread across the sky with one near the zenith and three near the horizon at 120┬░ azimuth separation.
> - **Bad Geometry Indicators:** Satellites clustered in one quadrant or all at low elevation.
> - **Time-Distance Conversion:** $1 \, \text{m} \leftrightarrow 3.33 \, \text{ns}$ of signal travel time.
> - **Inverse Computation:** Always verify $\det(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}) \neq 0$ before inversion ŌĆö singular $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ implies coplanar/redundant satellites.
> - **Practical Engineering Insight:** Adding more satellites beyond 4 does NOT reduce PDOP indefinitely ŌĆö asymptotic limit depends on constellation geometry (typically PDOP $\to$ 1.0ŌĆō1.5 in well-designed GNSS).
> - **KTU Coding Tip:** In 14-mark derivations, write the normal equation step explicitly ŌĆö examiners allocate 2 marks solely for the matrix setup $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{H}^T\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coordinate Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coordinate Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Coordinate System** in the context of robot navigation is a mathematical reference frame consisting of an **origin point**, a set of **orthogonal basis vectors** (axes), and a defined **handedness convention** (right-handed or left-handed) that uniquely specifies the position and orientation of any point, vector, or rigid body in 2D or 3D Euclidean space. In mobile robotics and manipulator kinematics, multiple coordinate frames coexist simultaneously ŌĆö the robot expresses its own state in one frame while perceiving and acting in others, and the discipline of coordinate systems is the formal machinery used to convert measurements and motions from one frame to another without ambiguity.

The fundamental coordinate frames used across KTU-aligned robot navigation modules are:

- **World Frame (Inertial / Global Frame, $\mathcal{F}_W$)** ŌĆö A fixed, stationary reference frame attached to the operating environment (e.g., the room, the map origin, or the GPS datum).
- **Robot / Body Frame ($\mathcal{F}_R$ or $\mathcal{F}_B$)** ŌĆö A frame rigidly attached to the moving robot, with its origin typically at the robot's center of mass or wheel-axle midpoint.
- **Sensor Frame ($\mathcal{F}_S$)** ŌĆö A frame attached to each onboard sensor (LiDAR, IMU, camera) so that raw measurements are first expressed locally before being transformed.
- **Goal / Target Frame ($\mathcal{F}_G$)** ŌĆö A frame anchored to the navigation goal that the planner uses as a relative reference.
- **Joint / Manipulator Frame ($\mathcal{F}_i$)** ŌĆö A frame attached to each link of a robotic arm, chained via DenavitŌĆōHartenberg (DH) parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For a mobile robot, the *minimum* representation of pose in 2D is $(x, y, \theta)$ and in 3D is $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$. All six quantities are measured **relative to the world frame**, never in absolute terms.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are standing in a large library and a friend is standing somewhere else in the room. If you say *"the book is 2 meters to my right"*, you are using **your own body as the coordinate system** ŌĆö the robot/body frame. If instead you say *"the book is on shelf A4, 3rd row from the entrance"*, you are using the **library's fixed layout as the world frame**. Both descriptions are valid; they simply measure the same physical reality from different vantage points.

Now imagine the friend turns around and says, *"from where I am standing, the book is 1.5 meters forward and slightly left."* That is a **sensor frame** ŌĆö a local reading that is meaningless unless you know *where your friend is and which way she is facing*. The moment you know her position $(x, y)$ and the direction she is facing (heading angle $\theta$), you can mathematically convert her local reading into the library's global coordinates. This conversion is precisely what a **coordinate transformation** does inside a robot's brain.

> [!NOTE]
> A single physical point in space has infinitely many possible coordinate representations ŌĆö one for every frame in which it is described. The math of coordinate systems is the math of *switching the label without changing the location*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Right-handedness convention:** Standard in KTU syllabus and all ROS-based stacks. Axes follow the right-hand rule: $\hat{x} \times \hat{y} = \hat{z}$.
- **Angular unit:** **radians** for all onboard computations (KVL convention); degrees only for human display.
- **Distance unit:** **meters** under SI; **pixels** for image frames, then converted to meters via camera intrinsics.
- **Coordinate frame rate of publication (TF in ROS 2):** typically **30 Hz to 100 Hz**.
- **Standard map origin:** $(0, 0, 0)$ at the SLAM initial pose.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Concept

A robot's navigation stack is fundamentally a *chain of frame transformations*. The reasoning proceeds in five well-defined steps:

1. **Local Sensing** ŌĆö Each onboard sensor (LiDAR, camera, IMU) produces measurements in its **own local frame** $\mathcal{F}_S$. These are raw, uncalibrated, and frame-locked.
2. **Sensor-to-Body Transformation** ŌĆö The rigid, factory-calibrated mount between the sensor and the robot chassis provides a constant matrix $T_{S}^{B}$ that converts sensor readings to the **body frame** $\mathcal{F}_B$.
3. **Body-to-World Transformation (Localization)** ŌĆö An estimator (EKF, particle filter, AMCL) maintains a belief about the robot's pose $T_{B}^{W}(t)$ using odometry and loop closures.
4. **World-to-Goal Transformation (Planning)** ŌĆö The path planner computes a goal pose in the world frame $T_{G}^{W}$ and converts it into the body frame using $T_{G}^{B} = (T_{B}^{W})^{-1} \cdot T_{G}^{W}$.
5. **Command Issuance (Control)** ŌĆö The controller issues velocity commands expressed in the body frame because that is the only frame in which the robot can directly act (linear $v_x$, angular $\omega_z$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple frames?** Because no single frame is optimal for *all* tasks. The world frame is best for global planning (no ambiguity about where you are). The body frame is best for control (wheels don't know about North). The sensor frame is best for raw measurement interpretation (LiDAR returns distances *to itself* first). The system mathematically links them.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Position in 2D | $\mathbf{p}^W = (x, y)^T$ | World frame, meters |
| Pose in 2D | $\boldsymbol{\xi}^W = (x, y, \theta)^T$ | $\theta$ in **radians**, CCW positive |
| Position in 3D | $\mathbf{p}^W = (x, y, z)^T$ | World frame |
| Full 6-DoF Pose | $\boldsymbol{\xi}^W = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ | Z-Y-X Euler (yawŌĆōpitchŌĆōroll) |
| 2D Rotation Matrix | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Right-handed, CCW positive |
| 3D Rotation about X | $R_x(\phi)$ | $\phi$ = roll |
| 3D Rotation about Y | $R_y(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = pitch |
| 3D Rotation about Z | $R_z(\psi)$ | $\psi$ = yaw |
| Homogeneous Transform | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$, $\mathbf{0}$ is $3 \times 1$ zero |
| Frame Composition | $T_A^C = T_A^B \cdot T_B^C$ | Transformations multiply right-to-left |
| Inverse Transform | $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | For homogeneous $T$ |
| Point Transformation | $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B = T_W^B \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{p}}^W$ | $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}$ = homogeneous $(x,y,z,1)^T$ |
| Translational Composition | $R_A^C = R_A^B \cdot R_B^C$ | Rotations compose via matrix multiplication |
| Determinant Property | $\det(R) = +1$ | Pure rotation is volume-preserving |
| Orthogonality | $R^{-1} = R^T$ | Rotation matrices belong to $SO(3)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When using $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value, the syllabus writes $\vert \det(R) \vert = 1$ and $\vert \theta \vert \le \pi$. Inside tables, write the bars as $\vert \det(R) \vert$ ŌĆö never as a bare pipe character, since the pipe would terminate the markdown column.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Coordinate systems are the silent backbone of every deployed robotic system. In **autonomous vehicles**, the body frame is used by the controller to command steering and throttle, while the world frame (anchored to a high-definition map) is used by the route planner ŌĆö and the live transformation between them is updated at 100 Hz by an EKF fusing GPS, IMU, and wheel odometry. In **indoor service robots**, the world frame is set at the SLAM map origin, and the body frame is updated via scan-matching of LiDAR returns. In **robotic surgery**, sub-millimetre precision demands that the camera frame, instrument frame, and patient frame all be related by carefully calibrated homogeneous transforms updated in real time. In **drone swarms**, each agent broadcasts its world-frame pose so peers can compute relative transforms on the fly for collision avoidance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö The 2D Rotation Matrix from First Principles

A rotation by an angle $\theta$ about the origin, in a right-handed coordinate system, takes any point $(x, y)$ to a new point $(x', y')$. We derive the matrix form by writing $x$ and $y$ in polar form and applying the angle-addition identities.

Let the original point be expressed as:
$$x = r \cos\alpha, \quad y = r \sin\alpha$$

After rotation by $\theta$ (counter-clockwise), the new angle is $\alpha + \theta$, so:
$$x' = r \cos(\alpha + \theta), \quad y' = r \sin(\alpha + \theta)$$

Apply the cosine and sine addition identities:
$$x' = r(\cos\alpha \cos\theta - \sin\alpha \sin\theta)$$
$$y' = r(\sin\alpha \cos\theta + \cos\alpha \sin\theta)$$

Substitute back $r\cos\alpha = x$ and $r\sin\alpha = y$:
$$x' = x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta$$
$$y' = x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta$$

Collect the result into a matrixŌĆōvector product:
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Interpretation:** Each column of $R(\theta)$ is the image of a basis vector under the rotation. The first column is the rotated $\hat{x}$ axis, the second is the rotated $\hat{y}$ axis. The determinant $\det R(\theta) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, confirming pure rotation preserves area.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö 3D Rotation Matrices about Coordinate Axes

For 3D rotations, we extend the 2D derivation by leaving the axis of rotation fixed and rotating the other two axes.

**Rotation about the X-axis (roll, angle $\phi$):** The $x$-coordinate is unchanged, while the $y$ŌĆō$z$ plane rotates like 2D:
$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Rotation about the Y-axis (pitch, angle $\theta$):** The $y$-coordinate is unchanged, while the $x$ŌĆō$z$ plane rotates:
$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Rotation about the Z-axis (yaw, angle $\psi$):** The $z$-coordinate is unchanged, while the $x$ŌĆō$y$ plane rotates:
$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Each matrix is orthogonal ($R^T R = I$) with determinant $+1$, so all three belong to the **Special Orthogonal group SO(3)**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Composing Two Rotations in 3D

A point $P$ first expressed in frame $A$, then rotated in frame $B$ by $R_B^C$, then rotated in frame $C$ by $R_C^D$ ŌĆö the total rotation from $A$ to $D$ is matrix multiplication in the order the rotations are applied.

If $\mathbf{p}^B = R_A^B \mathbf{p}^A$ and $\mathbf{p}^C = R_B^C \mathbf{p}^B$, then by substitution:
$$\mathbf{p}^C = R_B^C R_A^B \mathbf{p}^A = R_A^C \mathbf{p}^A$$

Therefore the composition rule for rotations is:
$$R_A^C = R_B^C R_A^B$$

> **KTU Pitfall ŌĆö Order Matters!** Multiplication of rotation matrices is **not commutative**. $R_x(\phi) R_y(\theta) \ne R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ in general. The convention in KTU valuation is *right-to-left* composition: the rightmost matrix acts on the vector first.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö The Homogeneous Transformation Matrix

A pure translation by $\mathbf{p} = (p_x, p_y, p_z)^T$ maps a point as $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{t}$. A pure rotation maps as $\mathbf{p}' = R \mathbf{p}$. To handle both in a single operation, we augment 3D vectors with a fourth coordinate equal to 1 and define the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & \mathbf{t}_{3 \times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Explicitly:
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The upper-left $3 \times 3$ block encodes orientation, the upper-right $3 \times 1$ block encodes position, and the bottom row $[0, 0, 0, 1]$ ensures affine (not projective) mapping.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Inverse of a Homogeneous Transformation

Given $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, we seek $T^{-1}$ such that $T T^{-1} = I_4$. Using the block-matrix inversion formula for an invertible Schur complement:

$$T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{-1} & -R^{-1} \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Because $R$ is orthogonal, $R^{-1} = R^T$, so the compact form is:
$$T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Real-world utility:** This is what the navigation stack computes to express the *goal* in the *body* frame. Given the goal's world pose $T_G^W$ and the robot's world pose $T_B^W$, the goal in body frame is $T_G^B = (T_B^W)^{-1} T_G^W$, allowing the controller to act locally.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

A mobile robot is at world pose $\mathbf{\xi}^W = (2.0 \text{ m}, 1.0 \text{ m}, 90^\circ)^T$. A landmark detected in the body frame is at $(3.0 \text{ m}, 0.0 \text{ m})^T$. Find the landmark's coordinates in the world frame.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert heading to radians:** $\theta = 90^\circ = \pi/2$ rad.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Build the 2D homogeneous transform from body to world:**
$$T_B^W = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\pi/2) & -\sin(\pi/2) & 2.0 \\ \sin(\pi/2) & \cos(\pi/2) & 1.0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 2.0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1.0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express landmark as homogeneous column:**
$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B = \begin{bmatrix} 3.0 \\ 0.0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Multiply:**
$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}^W = T_B^W \tilde{\mathbf{p}}^B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 3 + (-1) \cdot 0 + 2.0 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 0 + 1.0 \cdot 1 \\ 0 + 0 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ 4.0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion:** The landmark's world coordinates are $(x, y) = (2.0 \text{ m}, 4.0 \text{ m})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the elegance: rotation by $90^\circ$ sent the body's $+x$ axis (which was pointing "forward" of the robot) to the world's $+y$ axis. The landmark was *in front of the robot* in body frame, so it ended up *north of the robot* in world frame ŌĆö consistent with the robot facing north.
