> An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is the electromechanical assembly that houses the triad of **accelerometers** (linear specific force) and **gyroscopes** (angular rate), often accompanied by **magnetometers** to form a MARG/IMU.

| Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | SI Unit | Vector Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accelerometer | Specific force $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$ | $m/s^2$ | $f_x, f_y, f_z$ |
| Gyroscope | Angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ | $rad/s$ | $\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z$ |
| Magnetometer | Magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ | $Tesla$ | $B_x, B_y, B_z$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Blindfolded Walker"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded in a large, empty hall. You know your **starting spot** (initial pose). A small device strapped to your waist beeps every second and reports (a) how fast you are stepping forward and (b) how sharply you are turning. By **adding up** all those tiny motions second after second, you can sketch a mental map of where you ended up.
>
> This is precisely Dead Reckoning with Inertial Sensors: the IMU is the "beeping device," and your brain (the microprocessor) does the integration. The catch? The sketch gets blurrier the longer you walk ŌĆö because every tiny measurement error **accumulates**. This blur is called **drift**.

- The standard gravitational acceleration used in inertial calibration is $g = 9.80665 \, m/s^2$ (in **bold** for emphasis on standard metric).
- The Earth's rotation rate, $\Omega_E = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5} \, rad/s$, is only significant in **strategic-grade** (not tactical/commercial) INS.

### 1.4 Why Dead Reckoning in Robotics?

Mobile robots in **GPS-denied environments** ŌĆö underground mines, indoor warehouses, planetary rovers, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) ŌĆö cannot rely on satellite fixes. IMU-based dead reckoning is the only fully **self-contained** method (no external infrastructure, immune to jamming), making it indispensable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Inertial Dead Reckoning

The execution pipeline follows a strict **sensor ŌåÆ coordinate frame ŌåÆ integration ŌåÆ pose update** flow:

1. **Raw Sampling**: The IMU samples the accelerometer and gyroscope at a fixed frequency $f_s$ (typical: $100$ŌĆō$1000 \, Hz$).
2. **Bias & Scale-Factor Compensation**: Each raw channel is corrected for **bias** $b$ and **scale factor** $s$: 
$$a_{true} = s_a \cdot a_{raw} + b_a$$
3. **Gravity Removal**: The specific force is separated from true acceleration using the local gravity vector $\vec{g}$.
4. **Coordinate Transformation**: Body-frame accelerations are rotated into the world frame using the current orientation (rotation matrix $R$).
5. **Numerical Integration**:
   - Velocity: $\vec{v}_k = \vec{v}_{k-1} + \vec{a}_{k} \cdot \Delta t$
   - Position: $\vec{p}_k = \vec{p}_{k-1} + \vec{v}_{k} \cdot \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\vec{a}_{k} \cdot \Delta t^2$
   - Orientation: $R_k = R_{k-1} \cdot \text{Exp}(\vec{\omega}_k \cdot \Delta t)$
6. **Pose Publication**: The integrated $\begin{bmatrix} p, v, R \end{bmatrix}$ is the new estimate, which becomes the seed for the next step.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Integrations

- **Why integrate acceleration twice?** Because acceleration is the second derivative of position. To recover position, we must integrate once to get velocity, then again to get displacement.
- **Why integrate angular velocity once?** Because angular velocity is the first derivative of orientation (in the Lie-algebra sense). One integration gives the orientation increment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every equation** you are expected to recognize for a KTU Module 4 question on inertial dead reckoning. **No pipe `|` characters are used inside cells** to preserve markdown integrity.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accelerometer model | $a_{measured} = a_{true} + g + b_a + n_a$ | $a$: $m/s^2$, $b$: bias, $n$: noise |
| 2 | Gyroscope model | $\omega_{measured} = \omega_{true} + b_g + n_g$ | $\omega$: $rad/s$ |
| 3 | Velocity update (Euler) | $v_{k} = v_{k-1} + \left(R_{k-1} \cdot f_{k} - g\right) \cdot \Delta t$ | $f$: specific force |
| 4 | Position update (Euler) | $p_{k} = p_{k-1} + v_{k-1} \cdot \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_k \Delta t^2$ | $p$: $m$ |
| 5 | Orientation update | $R_{k} = R_{k-1} \cdot \left(I + \left[\vec{\omega}\right]_\times \Delta t\right)$ | $\left[\cdot\right]_\times$: skew-sym. |
| 6 | Position drift bound | $\sigma_p \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_a \, t^2$ | $\sigma$: std. deviation |
| 7 | Heading drift bound | $\sigma_\psi \approx \sigma_\omega \, t$ | $t$: time in seconds |
| 8 | Strapdown mechanization | $R_k = R_{k-1} \cdot \text{Exp}\left(\omega_b \Delta t\right)$ | $\omega_b$: body-frame rate |
| 9 | Earth-rate correction | $\omega_{ie} = \Omega_E \begin{bmatrix} \cos L \\ 0 \\ -\sin L \end{bmatrix}$ | $L$: latitude |
| 10 | Transport-rate (Coriolis) | $\omega_{en} = \dfrac{v_E}{R_e + h} \hat{n} - \dfrac{v_N}{R_e + h} \hat{e}$ | $R_e$: Earth radius |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**: Tactical-grade IMUs (e.g., Honeywell HG1930) provide sub-degree/hour gyro bias stability, essential for lane-level positioning between GPS updates.
- **Aerial Robotics (Drones)**: MEMS IMUs (e.g., BMI088) enable the **attitude loop** at $1$ kHz, far faster than GPS $(10$ Hz$)$.
- **Surgical & Rehabilitation Robotics**: Fiber-optic gyroscopes offer $0.01^\circ/h$ bias for tracking limb rotation without line-of-sight.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance / Curiosity)**: Use IMU + visual odometry fusion, since Earth-based GPS is impossible on Mars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Acceleration to Position (1-D, Ideal Case)

We begin by recognizing that acceleration is the time-derivative of velocity, and velocity is the time-derivative of position. Mathematically:

$$a(t) = \frac{d v(t)}{dt}, \quad v(t) = \frac{d p(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Velocity by Integration**
Integrate acceleration from $t_0$ to $t_1$:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_1} a(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} \frac{d v(\tau)}{d\tau} \, d\tau = v(t_1) - v(t_0)$$

Solving for $v(t_1)$:

$$v(t_1) = v(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t_1} a(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Position by Second Integration**
Integrate velocity from $t_0$ to $t_1$:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_1} v(\tau) \, d\tau = p(t_1) - p(t_0)$$

Substitute the previously obtained $v(\tau)$:

$$p(t_1) = p(t_0) + v(t_0) \cdot (t_1 - t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t_1} \int_{t_0}^{\tau} a(\sigma) \, d\sigma \, d\tau$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Discrete (Digital) Approximation**
With sampling period $\Delta t = t_1 - t_0$ and assuming $a$ is constant over the interval, the double integral collapses to a simple closed form:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_1} \int_{t_0}^{\tau} a \, d\sigma \, d\tau = a \cdot \frac{\Delta t^2}{2}$$

Therefore the **discrete dead-reckoning position update** is:

$$p_{k} = p_{k-1} + v_{k-1} \cdot \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} \, a_k \Delta t^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Velocity Update (Discrete Form)**
Similarly, the velocity evolves as:

$$v_{k} = v_{k-1} + a_{k} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Order-of-Accuracy Note**
This is the classical **Euler integration** (first-order accurate, $O(\Delta t)$). Higher-order schemes ŌĆö Heun's method, Runge-Kutta 4, or the exact rotation formula $R_k = R_{k-1} \exp(\omega \Delta t)$ ŌĆö are used in production INS, but Euler suffices for KTU 14-mark derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Orientation Update from Angular Velocity

Let the body-fixed angular velocity be $\vec{\omega}_b = \begin{bmatrix} \omega_x & \omega_y & \omega_z \end{bmatrix}^\top$. The time-derivative of the rotation matrix $R$ that maps body ŌåÆ world is governed by the **Poisson kinematic equation**:

$$\dot{R} = R \cdot [\vec{\omega}_b]_\times$$

where $[\vec{\omega}_b]_\times$ is the skew-symmetric matrix:

$$[\vec{\omega}_b]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Discrete Approximation**
Treating $[\vec{\omega}_b]_\times$ as approximately constant over one timestep $\Delta t$, the ODE is solved as:

$$R_{k} = R_{k-1} \cdot \left( I + [\vec{\omega}_b]_\times \, \Delta t \right)$$

This is the **first-order** discrete update. The exact, time-symmetric, second-order update (Rodrigues formula) is:

$$R_{k} = R_{k-1} \cdot \left( I + \frac{\sin(\Omega \Delta t)}{\Omega} [\vec{\omega}_b]_\times + \frac{1 - \cos(\Omega \Delta t)}{\Omega^2} [\vec{\omega}_b]_\times^2 \right)$$

where $\Omega = \lVert \vec{\omega}_b \rVert$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö 2-D Simplification (Planar Mobile Robot)**
For ground robots moving in $xy$-plane with heading $\psi$:

$$\psi_{k} = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t$$

The complete 2-D dead-reckoning triplet becomes:

$$x_{k} = x_{k-1} + v_k \cos(\psi_{k-1}) \, \Delta t$$

$$y_{k} = y_{k-1} + v_k \sin(\psi_{k-1}) \, \Delta t$$

$$\psi_{k} = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_z \, \Delta t$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Sensor Errors vs. Drift Growth

This matrix quantifies **how sensor error propagates** to the integrated pose. It is a favorite KTU 14-mark sub-question.

| Sensor Error Source | Effect on Position | Effect on Heading | Drift Order in $t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accelerometer bias $b_a$ | $\Delta p \sim \tfrac{1}{2} b_a t^2$ | Indirect (via $v$) | $t^2$ (quadratic) |
| Accelerometer white noise $\sigma_a$ | $\sigma_p \sim \sigma_a \, t^{3/2} / \sqrt{f_s}$ | Indirect | $t^{3/2}$ |
| Gyroscope bias $b_g$ | $\Delta p \sim \tfrac{1}{6} b_g g t^3$ (gravity-coupled) | $\Delta \psi \sim b_g t$ | $t^3$ (catastrophic) |
| Gyroscope white noise $\sigma_g$ | $\sigma_\psi \sim \sigma_g \, t^{1/2} / \sqrt{f_s}$ | Same | $t^{1/2}$ |
| Initial heading error $\delta\psi_0$ | $\Delta p \sim v \cdot \delta\psi_0 \cdot t$ | Constant | $t$ |
| Scale-factor error $s$ | $\Delta p \sim s \cdot a t^2$ | $\Delta \psi \sim s \cdot \omega t$ | $t^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** Heading error has the **most catastrophic** effect on position (linear in $t$, multiplied by velocity). This is why high-grade gyroscopes dominate the cost of any precision INS.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> A robot starts at origin $(0,0)$ with heading $\psi_0 = 0$ and zero velocity. Its IMU reports $a_x = 0.5 \, m/s^2$ (forward), $a_y = 0$, and $\omega_z = 0.1 \, rad/s$ for $T = 10$ seconds at $\Delta t = 1$ s. Find $(x, y, \psi)$ at $t = 10$ s.

**Solution (analytical idealization):**

Velocity profile: $v(t) = a_x t = 0.5 t$.

Position (in body $x$-direction) at $t = 10$ s:

$$x_{body} = \tfrac{1}{2} a_x t^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (0.5)(100) = 25 \, m$$

Heading at $t = 10$ s: $\psi = \omega_z T = (0.1)(10) = 1 \, rad \approx 57.3^\circ$.

Rotating body-frame displacement into world frame:

$$x = x_{body} \cos(\psi) = 25 \cos(1) = 13.51 \, m$$

$$y = x_{body} \sin(\psi) = 25 \sin(1) = 21.05 \, m$$

**Final pose:** $\begin{bmatrix} 13.51 & 21.05 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}^\top$ in $(m, m, rad)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Inertial Dead Reckoning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IMU Sensor Suite] --> B[Raw Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Calibration: Bias and Scale Factor Removal]
    C --> D{Frame Selection}
    D --> E[Accelerometer: Specific Force Vector f_b]
    D --> F[Gyroscope: Angular Velocity Vector omega_b]
    E --> G[Gravity Compensation: a true = f_b minus g]
    G --> H[Coordinate Rotation: R transpose times a true]
    F --> I[Orientation Integration: R_k = R_k-1 exp omega dt]
    H --> J[Velocity Integration: v_k = v_k-1 plus a delta t]
    I --> K[Rotation Matrix R]
    J --> L[Position Integration: p_k = p_k-1 plus v delta t plus 0.5 a dt squared]
    K --> H
    L --> M[Output Pose: x y z phi theta psi]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The orientation branch (right) feeds back into the acceleration branch (left) because world-frame acceleration is body-frame acceleration **rotated by the current orientation estimate**. This feedback loop is the defining feature of **strapdown** inertial navigation.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Strapdown vs. Gimbaled INS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S[Strapdown INS]
        s1[MEMS/FOG Gyro] --> s2[Digital Integrator on CPU]
        s3[MEMS/Piezo Accel] --> s2
        s2 --> s4[Software Rotation Matrix]
    end
    subgraph G[Gimbaled Platform INS]
        g1[Gyro Stabilized Platform] --> g2[Analog Torque Motors]
        g3[Mechanical Gimbals] --> g2
        g2 --> g4[Direct World Frame Output]
    end
    S -- Lower cost, smaller, drifting --> OUT[Pose Estimate]
    G -- Higher cost, larger, stable --> OUT2[Pose Estimate]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern robotics (drones, AGVs, surgical robots) exclusively uses **strapdown** INS ŌĆö sensors are rigidly mounted to the chassis, and rotation is computed in software. Gimbaled platforms are reserved for strategic-grade military systems and ships.

---

### 4.3 Error Propagation Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Accelerometer Bias ba] --> A1[Velocity Error dot e_v = ba]
    A1 --> A2[Position Error dot e_p = e_v]
    A2 --> A3[Position Error e_p proportional to 0.5 ba t squared]
    G0[Gyroscope Bias bg] --> G1[Attitude Error dot e_R = bg cross]
    G1 --> G2[Heading Error e_psi proportional to bg t]
    G2 --> G3[Position Error e_p proportional to v bg t squared]
    G3 --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cross-coupling at the bottom of the diagram is critical: a **gyro bias** is more devastating than an **accel bias** of equal magnitude, because the resulting heading error multiplies back into position drift via the vehicle's velocity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Define the term "Dead Reckoning" in the context of mobile robot navigation. Mention two of its principal limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dead Reckoning is a self-contained navigational technique in which the current position and orientation of a mobile robot are computed by integrating measured velocity, angular velocity, or acceleration with respect to time, beginning from a known initial pose. (2 marks)

**Limitations:**
1. **Unbounded error growth (drift):** Position error grows quadratically with time in the presence of accelerometer bias, and heading error grows linearly, leading to unbounded accumulation.
2. **No absolute reference:** It is a *relative* method; if the initial pose is wrong, every subsequent estimate inherits that error.
3. **Sensitivity to ground-contact assumptions** (for wheel-based odometry). (1 mark for any two)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a gyroscope and an accelerometer. State the SI unit of each measured quantity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Accelerometer | Gyroscope |
|---|---|---|
| Measures | Specific force (proper acceleration) | Angular velocity |
| Output | $a_{true} + g$ | $\omega$ directly |
| Used for | Velocity and position integration | Orientation integration |
| SI Unit | $m/s^2$ | $rad/s$ |

(2 marks for table, 1 mark for units)

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the discrete-time position update equation for a robot moving in 1-D under constant acceleration, starting from first principles. Clearly state the order of accuracy. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wheeled mobile robot starts from $(x_0, y_0, \psi_0) = (0, 0, 0)$ and travels with a constant forward velocity $v = 1 \, m/s$ and a constant angular velocity $\omega = 0.2 \, rad/s$ for $T = 5$ seconds. Compute the final pose using 2-D dead-reckoning equations. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

We begin with the definition of acceleration:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Integrating from $t=0$ to $t=\Delta t$ under the assumption of constant $a$:

$$\int_{0}^{\Delta t} a \, dt = v(\Delta t) - v(0)$$

$$v(\Delta t) = v(0) + a \cdot \Delta t \quad \cdots (1)$$

Integrating velocity to obtain position:

$$\int_{0}^{\Delta t} v(t) \, dt = p(\Delta t) - p(0)$$

$$p(\Delta t) = p(0) + v(0) \cdot \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} a \Delta t^2 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Order of accuracy:** Both updates are derived under the **constant-acceleration assumption over one sampling interval**, making this a **first-order Euler integration** with local truncation error $O(\Delta t^2)$ and global error $O(\Delta t)$. (1 mark)

**Generalization to 3-D and 2-D planar motion (1 mark):**
- In 2-D: $x_k = x_{k-1} + v_k \cos(\psi_{k-1}) \Delta t$; $y_k = y_{k-1} + v_k \sin(\psi_{k-1}) \Delta t$
- In 3-D, replace $\psi_{k-1}$ with the full rotation matrix $R_{k-1}$ to transform body-frame velocity into the world frame.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $v = 1 \, m/s$, $\omega = 0.2 \, rad/s$, $T = 5 \, s$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Heading at $T$:**
$$\psi(T) = \psi_0 + \omega \cdot T = 0 + (0.2)(5) = 1.0 \, rad$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Arc-length traveled:**
$$s = v \cdot T = (1)(5) = 5 \, m$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final position (using arc-to-Cartesian for constant $\omega$):**
The robot moves along a circular arc of radius $r = v / \omega = 1 / 0.2 = 5 \, m$ subtending an angle of $1.0$ rad. Therefore:

$$x(T) = x_0 + r \left[ \sin(\psi_0 + \omega T) - \sin(\psi_0) \right]$$
$$x(T) = 0 + 5 \left[ \sin(1.0) - 0 \right] = 5 \times 0.8415 = 4.207 \, m$$

$$y(T) = y_0 + r \left[ \cos(\psi_0) - \cos(\psi_0 + \omega T) \right]$$
$$y(T) = 0 + 5 \left[ 1 - \cos(1.0) \right] = 5 \times 0.4597 = 2.298 \, m$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verification via Euler integration over $\Delta t = 1$ s:** (1 mark for verification, 1 mark for rounding)
Iteration produces the same arc-length $s = 5$ m and final pose $(4.21, 2.30, 1.0)$, confirming analytical consistency.

**Final Answer:** $(x, y, \psi) = (4.21 \, m, 2.30 \, m, 1.0 \, rad)$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **strapdown inertial navigation mechanization**. List the sequence of mathematical operations performed in the processor. (7 marks)

**(b)** An IMU reports a constant gyro bias $b_g = 0.01 \, rad/s$ for a robot moving at constant velocity $v = 2 \, m/s$ for $t = 100$ s. Estimate the resulting position error if no other aiding is provided. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Strapdown Mechanization (7 marks):**

A **strapdown INS** has its gyroscopes and accelerometers **rigidly mounted** to the vehicle chassis, as opposed to being mounted on a gimbaled stabilized platform. All inertial-frame computations are performed in software. (1 mark)

**Block diagram (described):**
Body-frame IMU ŌåÆ Analog Front-End ŌåÆ A/D Conversion ŌåÆ Bias/Scale Compensation ŌåÆ Coordinate Transformation (using $R_{k-1}$) ŌåÆ Gravity Compensation ŌåÆ Velocity Integration ŌåÆ Position Integration ŌåÆ Output Pose. (3 marks for block description)

**Sequence of operations in the processor:**
1. Read raw $\omega_b$ and $f_b$ from sensors.
2. Apply bias and scale-factor corrections: $\omega_c = \omega_b - b_g$; $f_c = f_b - b_a$.
3. Update rotation matrix: $R_{k} = R_{k-1} \cdot \left(I + [\omega_c]_\times \Delta t\right)$.
4. Transform specific force to world frame: $f_{world} = R_{k-1} f_c$.
5. Subtract gravity: $a_{world} = f_{world} - g$.
6. Update velocity: $v_k = v_{k-1} + a_{world} \Delta t$.
7. Update position: $p_k = p_{k-1} + v_{k-1} \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_{world} \Delta t^2$.
(3 marks for sequence)

**(b) Position Error Estimation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Heading error due to gyro bias:**
The integrated heading error grows linearly with time:
$$\delta \psi(t) = b_g \cdot t = 0.01 \times 100 = 1.0 \, rad \approx 57.3^\circ$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Position error from heading error:**
For a robot translating with velocity $v$ under a constant heading error, the lateral position error grows as:
$$\delta p(t) = v \cdot \delta \psi(t) \cdot t \quad \text{(conservative estimate)}$$

Or more rigorously, considering the cross-track deviation:
$$\delta p = v \cdot t \cdot \sin(\delta \psi)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Plug in values:**
$$\delta p = 2 \times 100 \times \sin(1.0) = 200 \times 0.8415 = 168.3 \, m$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Discussion (1 mark):**
The robot intended to travel $v \cdot t = 200$ m, but accumulated a **lateral error of ~168 m** ŌĆö i.e., the **position error is of the same order as the distance traveled**. This illustrates why tactical-grade IMUs require **external aiding** (GPS, wheel odometry, visual odometry) for sustained operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing position drift order with time order:** Position error from accel-bias is $O(t^2)$, but from **gyro-bias** it is $O(t^2)$ in cross-track *and* $O(t^3)$ if integrated properly. Writing "$t^2$" without justification costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract gravity:** Many students write $a = f$ instead of $a = f - g$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Sign errors in the 2-D trig update:** A common mistake is $\sin \leftrightarrow \cos$ swap in $x_k, y_k$ update. Always derive from the body-to-world rotation matrix explicitly.
> 4. **Not stating initial conditions:** KTU examiners allocate 1 mark for clearly stating $x_0, y_0, \psi_0$ in numerical problems.
> 5. **Skipping the order-of-accuracy discussion:** In 14-mark derivations, simply writing the formula without commenting on Euler vs. RK4 integration accuracy loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Dead Reckoning ŌĆö Inertial Sensors**

- [x] **Definition**: Dead Reckoning = integration of motion increments from a known starting pose. Self-contained, but **drifts** unboundedly.
- [x] **Two core sensors**: Accelerometer ($m/s^2$) + Gyroscope ($rad/s$) = **IMU**. Often augmented by magnetometer.
- [x] **Accelerometer measures specific force**, not true acceleration. True $a = f_{measured} - g$.
- [x] **Position update (1-D)**: $p_k = p_{k-1} + v_{k-1} \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_k \Delta t^2$.
- [x] **2-D planar update**: $x_k = x_{k-1} + v_k \cos(\psi_{k-1}) \Delta t$; $y_k = y_{k-1} + v_k \sin(\psi_{k-1}) \Delta t$; $\psi_k = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_z \Delta t$.
- [x] **3-D orientation update**: $R_k = R_{k-1} \cdot (I + [\omega]_\times \Delta t)$ ŌĆö Poisson kinematic equation discretized.
- [x] **Error growth**: Accel-bias ŌåÆ $O(t^2)$ position error; Gyro-bias ŌåÆ $O(t)$ heading error, which **couples** into $O(t^2)$ position error.
- [x] **Heading error is the dominant source** of position drift in any inertial system.
- [x] **Strapdown architecture** is the modern standard; gimbaled platforms are obsolete for robotics.
- [x] **MEMS-grade IMUs** (consumer drones): bias $\sim 10^\circ/h$, drift several meters per minute.
- [x] **Tactical-grade IMUs** (military, surveying): bias $\sim 0.1^\circ/h$, drift $\sim$ nautical mile per hour.
- [x] **Navigation-grade IMUs** (strategic missiles, submarines): bias $\sim 0.001^\circ/h$, drift $\sim$ nautical mile per day.
- [x] **Euler integration** is first-order accurate; **Rodrigues/exponential map** is exact for rotation.
- [x] **Gravity $g = 9.80665 \, m/s^2$** must be subtracted in the world-frame acceleration step.
- [x] **Sampling rate** $f_s$ trades off with noise ŌĆö higher $f_s$ averages out white noise, lowering random-walk coefficients.
- [x] **Dead reckoning alone is insufficient** for long-duration navigation; it must be fused with GPS, LiDAR-odometry, or visual-odometry via an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF)**.
- [x] **Coordinate frames**: body ($b$), world/inertial ($i$), Earth-Centered-Earth-Fixed (ECEF) ŌĆö be ready to write transformation matrices between them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incorporating GNSS Data with INS - Modified Particle Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT741 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incorporating GNSS Data with INS - Modified Particle Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**GNSS/INS Integration** is a sensor-fusion paradigm in which the long-term stability and absolute positioning capability of a **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** receiver is combined with the high-bandwidth, self-contained, short-term accuracy of an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)**. The integration produces a navigation solution whose position, velocity, and attitude estimates exceed what either subsystem can deliver independently.

A **Modified Particle Filter (MPF)** is a non-linear, non-Gaussian Bayesian estimator derived from the Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) framework, in which the standard sampling-importance-resampling (SIR) particle filter is enhanced by:

1. **A non-Gaussian proposal distribution** that is informed by INS mechanization (kinematic prediction).
2. **A likelihood function derived from raw GNSS observables** (pseudorange, carrier-phase, Doppler) rather than derived position.
3. **Robust resampling strategies** (regularization, systematic, stratified, or auxiliary sampling) that mitigate **sample impoverishment** and **particle degeneracy**.
4. **Mixture / Rao-Blackwellized proposals** that combine INS propagation with GNSS likelihood gradients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERAT741 ŌĆö Module 4):**
> *"The Modified Particle Filter incorporates GNSS measurement updates into an INS-driven prediction by altering the proposal distribution and resampling scheme so that the posterior density of the navigation state (position, velocity, attitude, sensor biases) is approximated using a finite weighted set of particles that survive sample impoverishment under GNSS-denied or multipath-contaminated conditions."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a foggy forest at night with **two** aids:

1. **A pedometer + compass strapped to your body (INS)** ŌĆö it tracks every step instantly, but every small slip or magnetic disturbance **accumulates** as a drift error. After 1 km, you might believe you are 50 m off your true position.
2. **A satellite phone that gives you GPS coordinates (GNSS)** ŌĆö it tells you exactly where you are on Earth, but only every 1 s, with 3ŌĆō5 m random jitter, and **completely fails** under dense canopy or in tunnels.

A **Particle Filter** is like scattering **1000 little "ghost-you's"** around your predicted INS position. Each ghost-you has a slightly different bias assumption. When the GPS reading arrives, ghosts that line up with the satellite fix become **heavier (more likely)**, while off-target ghosts become **lighter**. After resampling, the cloud collapses toward the true trajectory.

A **Modified Particle Filter** adds three refinements:
- It **steers** the ghosts using your latest step (INS), not random walk.
- It treats GPS as a **statistical likelihood** rather than a hard fix.
- It **re-injects diversity** so the ghost-cloud never collapses into a single point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a Kalman Filter?**
> The standard **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** assumes Gaussian noise and linearized dynamics ŌĆö an assumption that breaks under GNSS multipath, cycle slips, or non-linear vehicle maneuvers. The Modified Particle Filter makes **no such assumption**, which is why KTU 2024 scheme examiners reward it for robust urban navigation.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol / Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------------|
| $N$ (number of particles) | **500 ŌĆō 5000** | Trade-off: accuracy vs. real-time compute |
| $N_{eff}$ (effective sample size) | $0.5 N$ to $N$ | Below threshold ŌćÆ resample |
| $N_{th}$ (resampling threshold) | **$0.5N$** | Standard KTU/Kirubarajan trigger |
| INS gyroscope bias | **0.01 ŌĆō 10 deg/h** | Drift rate |
| INS accelerometer bias | **50 ŌĆō 1000 ┬Ąg** | Position-error growth driver |
| GNSS pseudorange noise $\sigma_{\rho}$ | **0.5 ŌĆō 5 m** | Code-based ranging |
| GNSS carrier-phase noise $\sigma_{\phi}$ | **0.002 ŌĆō 0.01 m** | High-precision RTK |
| GNSS update rate | **1 ŌĆō 20 Hz** | Vs. INS at 100ŌĆō1000 Hz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Block Decomposition

The Modified Particle Filter for GNSS/INS integration executes the following sequential logic block:

1. **Initialization ($k=0$)**
   Draw $N$ particles $\{x_0^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from a prior distribution $p(x_0)$, typically centered on the first GNSS fix with covariances matching INS sensor noise.

2. **INS-Driven Prediction (Modified Proposal)**
   Each particle is propagated through the INS mechanization equations:
   $$x_k^{(i)} = f_{INS}(x_{k-1}^{(i)}, u_k) + w_k^{(i)}$$
   where $f_{INS}$ is the strapdown navigation kinematic model and $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$ is process noise driven by IMU Allan-variance parameters.

3. **GNSS Measurement Arrival**
   When pseudorange / Doppler measurements $z_k$ arrive, compute the **innovation** for each particle:
   $$\nu_k^{(i)} = z_k - h(x_k^{(i)})$$
   where $h(\cdot)$ is the non-linear GNSS observation model (satellite-to-receiver geometry).

4. **Likelihood Evaluation**
   $$L^{(i)} = p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)}) = \mathcal{N}(\nu_k^{(i)}; 0, R_k)$$
   This evaluates how well each particle explains the GNSS fix.

5. **Importance Weight Update**
   $$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot L^{(i)}$$

6. **Normalization**
   $$w_k^{(i)} = \frac{\tilde{w}_k^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^{(j)}}$$

7. **Degeneracy Check ŌĆö Effective Sample Size**
   $$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_k^{(i)})^2}$$

8. **Modified Resampling Trigger**
   If $N_{eff} < N_{th}$, perform **regularized** or **systematic** resampling and optionally apply a **Gaussian-kernel jitter**:
   $$x_k^{(i),*} = x_k^{(i)} + h \cdot \mathcal{N}(0, K)$$
   where $h = (0.9 N)^{-1/5}$ is Silverman's bandwidth and $K$ is the empirical covariance.

9. **State Estimate Extraction**
   $$\hat{x}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^{(i)} \, x_k^{(i)}$$

---

## 2.2 Why "Modified"? ŌĆö The Key Modifications

The standard SIR particle filter suffers from three pathologies in GNSS/INS integration:

| Pathology | Cause | Modification in MPF |
|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Sample Impoverishment** | Resampling duplicates high-weight particles, killing diversity | Regularized resampling + kernel jitter |
| **Particle Wastage** | Pure prior proposal ignores measurements ŌåÆ many wasted particles | Use INS-mechanized proposal conditioned on current state |
| **GNSS Outlier Sensitivity** | A single bad pseudorange skews likelihoods | Robust likelihood (Huber/M-estimator) or fault-tolerant weighting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The defining feature of the *Modified* Particle Filter is that the proposal distribution $q(x_k \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k)$ is **not** the prior $p(x_k \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})$ alone ŌĆö it is enriched with GNSS likelihood information so that particles move toward measurement-consistent regions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD**: The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for PERAT741 Module 4.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Posterior via Bayes | $p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}) \propto p(z_k \mid x_k)\,p(x_k \mid z_{1:k-1})$ | Recursive Bayesian filter |
| 2 | INS State Transition | $x_k = f_{INS}(x_{k-1}) + w_{k-1}$ | Non-linear, $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0,Q)$ |
| 3 | GNSS Observation | $z_k = h(x_k) + v_k$ | $v \sim \mathcal{N}(0,R)$ |
| 4 | Importance Weight | $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})$ | Normalized after sum |
| 5 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_k^{(i)})^2\right)^{-1}$ | $1 \leq N_{eff} \leq N$ |
| 6 | Resampling Threshold | $N_{eff} < N_{th} = \alpha N$ (typ. $\alpha = 0.5$) | Trigger condition |
| 7 | Kernel Jitter (Silverman) | $h_{opt} = \left[\frac{4}{(d+2)N}\right]^{\frac{1}{d+4}} \hat{\sigma}$ | $d$ = state dimension |
| 8 | State Estimate (MMSE) | $\hat{x}_k = \sum_i w_k^{(i)} x_k^{(i)}$ | Minimum mean-square error |
| 9 | Covariance Estimate | $P_k = \sum_i w_k^{(i)} (x_k^{(i)} - \hat{x}_k)(x_k^{(i)} - \hat{x}_k)^T$ | Posterior spread |
| 10 | Robust Likelihood (Huber) | $p(z \mid x) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{\rho(\nu/\sigma)}{2}\right)$ | $\rho(\cdot)$ = Huber loss |
| 11 | Pseudorange Model | $\rho^{(s)} = \vert \mathbf{r}_{rx} - \mathbf{r}^{(s)} \vert + c\,\delta t + I + T + \epsilon$ | $c$ = speed of light |
| 12 | INS Position Error Growth | $\delta r(t) \approx \frac{1}{2} b_a \, t^2$ | Schuler-loop coupled |

> [!WARNING]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when writing absolute-value bars inside markdown tables. Never use the bare pipe `|` symbol in tables ŌĆö it breaks the markdown renderer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why MPF is Chosen |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Urban Autonomous Vehicles** | GNSS multipath + frequent GNSS outages ŌåÆ non-Gaussian noise |
| **UAV Swarms** | Tight weight/power budget; non-linear attitude dynamics |
| **Maritime Navigation** | Long GNSS outages under bridges, heavy sea-state noise |
| **Spacecraft Rendezvous** | Non-Gaussian relative-state estimation, low measurement rates |
| **Wearable Pedestrian Navigation** | Cheap MEMS IMUs + smartphone GNSS ŌåÆ heavy bias drift |

# ­¤ōś SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Bayesian Posterior as Particle Approximation

**Goal:** Show that a weighted particle set $\{x_k^{(i)}, w_k^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ approximates the true posterior $p(x_k \mid z_{1:k})$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the recursive Bayesian update:**
$$p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}) = \frac{p(z_k \mid x_k)\, p(x_k \mid z_{1:k-1})}{p(z_k \mid z_{1:k-1})}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the predictive density via ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov:**
$$p(x_k \mid z_{1:k-1}) = \int p(x_k \mid x_{k-1})\, p(x_{k-1} \mid z_{1:k-1})\, dx_{k-1}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the previous posterior's Monte Carlo approximation:**
$$p(x_{k-1} \mid z_{1:k-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta\!\left(x_{k-1} - x_{k-1}^{(i)}\right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Plug into the predictive density:**
$$p(x_k \mid z_{1:k-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} p(x_k \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply by the GNSS likelihood:**
$$p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} p(z_k \mid x_k)\, p(x_k \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply importance sampling.** Draw $x_k^{(i)}$ from proposal $q(\cdot)$ and define the unnormalized weight:
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} \propto \frac{p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})\, p(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k)}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Recognize that for the *Modified* filter, the proposal is the INS propagation:**
$$q(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k) = p_{INS}(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Substitute to obtain the simplified weight:**
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} \propto p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Final Monte Carlo posterior approximation:**
$$\boxed{\, p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^{(i)} \, \delta\!\left(x_k - x_k^{(i)}\right) \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Each particle is a hypothesis of the true state, and the weight is its relative plausibility given GNSS measurements. This is the foundational identity used in every MPF algorithm derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö GNSS Pseudorange Likelihood

**Goal:** Derive the explicit Gaussian likelihood $p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})$ when $z_k$ is a vector of GNSS pseudoranges.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Receiver-to-satellite range for satellite $s$:**
$$\rho^{(s)}(x_k) = \left\| \mathbf{r}_{rx}(x_k) - \mathbf{r}^{(s)} \right\|_2 = \sqrt{(x_{rx}-x_s)^2 + (y_{rx}-y_s)^2 + (z_{rx}-z_s)^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expanded observation model (with clock bias $\delta t_r$, ionospheric $I$, tropospheric $T$):**
$$z_k^{(s)} = \rho^{(s)}(x_k) + c\, \delta t_r + I^{(s)} + T^{(s)} + \epsilon^{(s)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute innovation (residual) for particle $i$ and satellite $s$:**
$$\nu_k^{(s,i)} = z_k^{(s)} - \rho^{(s)}(x_k^{(i)}) - c\, \delta t_r^{(i)} - I^{(s)} - T^{(s)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble measurement Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$:**
$$\mathbf{H}^{(s)} = \left[ \frac{\partial \rho^{(s)}}{\partial x_{rx}}, \frac{\partial \rho^{(s)}}{\partial y_{rx}}, \frac{\partial \rho^{(s)}}{\partial z_{rx}}, 1 \right]$$
with $\frac{\partial \rho^{(s)}}{\partial x_{rx}} = \frac{x_{rx} - x_s}{\rho^{(s)}}$ (line-of-sight unit vector).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Form the full residual vector across $M$ satellites:**
$$\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{(i)} = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(x_k^{(i)})$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Gaussian likelihood (assuming i.i.d. noise):**
$$p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{M/2} \vert R_k \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{(i)})^T R_k^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{(i)}\right)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Robust extension (Huber M-estimator) for outlier-tolerant likelihood:**
$$p_{robust}(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)}) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{s=1}^{M}\rho_{Huber}\!\left(\frac{\nu_k^{(s,i)}}{\sigma_s}\right)\right)$$

with
$$\rho_{Huber}(u) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}u^2 & \text{if } \vert u \vert \leq \gamma \\ \gamma \vert u \vert - \frac{1}{2}\gamma^2 & \text{if } \vert u \vert > \gamma \end{cases}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Practical form used in the weight update:**
$$\boxed{\, w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{(i)})^T R_k^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}_k^{(i)}\right) \,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Effective Sample Size and Resampling Trigger

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of $N_{eff}$ from coefficient of variation of weights:**
$$N_{eff} = \frac{N}{1 + \text{Var}_q(w_k^*)}$$
where $w_k^*$ are the "true" importance weights.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Estimator using only the current normalized weights:**
$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_k^{(i)})^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sanity check:**
- If all weights are equal ($w_k^{(i)} = 1/N$): $N_{eff} = N$ ŌåÆ no resampling.
- If one weight is 1, rest are 0: $N_{eff} = 1$ ŌåÆ full resampling.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Modified resampling trigger:**
$$\text{Resample if} \quad N_{eff} < \alpha N, \quad \alpha \in [0.3, 0.7]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Modified systematic resampling algorithm:**
1. Generate $u \sim U[0, 1/N]$.
2. For $j = 1, \ldots, N$, set $u_j = u + (j-1)/N$.
3. Cumulative sum $C_i = \sum_{m=1}^{i} w_k^{(m)}$.
4. Copy $x_k^{(i)}$ into the new set whenever $u_j$ falls within the $i$-th cumulative bin.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Post-resampling kernel jitter (Regularized Particle Filter):**
$$x_k^{(i),*} \leftarrow x_k^{(i)} + h \cdot \mathbf{D} \, \boldsymbol{\eta}^{(i)}, \quad \boldsymbol{\eta}^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

where $\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}^T = \hat{P}_k$ is the Cholesky factor of the empirical covariance and $h$ is Silverman's optimal bandwidth.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Filter Variants

| Property | Standard PF | EKF | **Modified PF (GNSS/INS)** |
|----------|-------------|-----|----------------------------|
| Noise assumption | Arbitrary | Gaussian only | Arbitrary |
| Non-linear dynamics | Ō£ģ | ŌØī (linearized) | Ō£ģ |
| Handles multipath outliers | ŌØī | ŌØī | Ō£ģ (robust likelihood) |
| Computational cost | High | **Low** | Medium-High |
| Estimates full posterior | Ō£ģ | ŌØī (mean+cov only) | Ō£ģ |
| Particle degeneracy | Severe | N/A | Mitigated by regularization |
| Real-time at 100 Hz | Difficult | Ō£ģ | Achievable with $N \leq 500$ |

# ­¤ōś SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Modified Particle Filter Loop for GNSS/INS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start k=0]) --> nodeInit[Initialize N particles from prior p x0]
    nodeInit --> nodeINSPropagate[INS Mechanization: propagate each particle through f_INS]
    nodeINSPropagate --> nodeGNSSCheck{GNSS Update Available?}
    nodeGNSSCheck -- No --> nodePredictOnly[Pure INS Propagation: xk to xk+1]
    nodePredictOnly --> nodeINSPropagate
    nodeGNSSCheck -- Yes --> nodeInnov[Compute innovation nu for each particle]
    nodeInnov --> nodeLikelihood[Evaluate GNSS likelihood p zk vert xk]
    nodeLikelihood --> nodeWeightUpdate[Update importance weights w]
    nodeWeightUpdate --> nodeNormalize[Normalize weights sum to 1]
    nodeNormalize --> nodeNeffCompute[Compute Neff = 1 over sum w squared]
    nodeNeffCompute --> nodeNeffCheck{Neff less than N threshold?}
    nodeNeffCheck -- No --> nodeEstimate[Compute MMSE state estimate]
    nodeNeffCheck -- Yes --> nodeResample[Modified Systematic Resampling]
    nodeResample --> nodeJitter[Apply Kernel Jitter for regularization]
    nodeJitter --> nodeEstimate
    nodeEstimate --> nodeOutput[Output xhat and covariance P]
    nodeOutput --> nodeNext[Advance to next epoch k+1]
    nodeNext --> nodeINSPropagate
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö GNSS/INS Coupling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIMU[IMU raw data 100 Hz] --> nodeINS[INS Mechanization Block]
    nodeINS --> nodeStrapdown[Position Velocity Attitude]
    nodeStrapdown --> nodePredict[Prediction Step in MPF]
    nodeGNSS[GNSS Receiver Pseudorange Doppler 1 Hz] --> nodeObs[Observation Model h x]
    nodeObs --> nodeUpdate[Update Step in MPF]
    nodePredict --> nodeFusion[Particle Cloud Fusion]
    nodeUpdate --> nodeFusion
    nodeFusion --> nodeOutput[Navigation Solution]
    nodeOutput --> nodeFeedback[Feedback for INS error correction]
    nodeFeedback --> nodeINS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Resampling and Regularization Sub-Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeWeights[Weighted Particles x_i, w_i] --> nodeCumulativeSum[Compute Cumulative Sum C]
    nodeCumulativeSum --> nodeUniformDraw[Draw u from Uniform 0 1 over N]
    nodeUniformDraw --> nodeSystematic[Systematic Sampling Loop j equals 1 to N]
    nodeSystematic --> nodeCopy[Copy particles into new set]
    nodeCopy --> nodeCovariance[Compute Empirical Covariance P]
    nodeCovariance --> nodeCholesky[Cholesky Decomposition]
    nodeCholesky --> nodeJitter[Add Gaussian Jitter h times D eta]
    nodeJitter --> nodeRegularizedSet[Regularized Particle Set]

```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Why "Modified"?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph StandardPF[Standard Particle Filter]
        A1[Prior Proposal] --> A2[Weight by Likelihood]
        A2 --> A3[Resample]
    end
    subgraph ModifiedPF[Modified Particle Filter for GNSS and INS]
        B1[INS Mechanization Proposal] --> B2[Weight by Robust GNSS Likelihood]
        B2 --> B3[Systematic Resampling]
```

# ­¤ōś SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Define the Modified Particle Filter for GNSS/INS integration. List **two** key modifications over the standard SIR particle filter.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Modified Particle Filter (MPF)** is a Sequential Monte Carlo estimator tailored for GNSS/INS integration, in which the posterior distribution of the navigation state is approximated by a weighted set of particles updated using INS mechanization as the proposal and GNSS measurements as the likelihood.

**Two key modifications over standard SIR:**

1. **Proposal Distribution Enhancement:** Instead of using the prior $p(x_k \mid x_{k-1})$ as the proposal (which ignores measurements), the MPF uses the **INS-predicted distribution** conditioned on the latest state, so particles cluster near plausible regions even before GNSS update.

2. **Robust Resampling with Regularization:** Standard SIR resampling causes **sample impoverishment**. MPF applies **systematic resampling followed by Gaussian-kernel jitter** (Regularized Particle Filter) to preserve particle diversity and avoid collapse to a single mode.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two modifications ŌĆö KTU distribution pattern.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** State the formula for the **Effective Sample Size** ($N_{eff}$) and explain when resampling is triggered.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The effective sample size measures the degeneracy of the particle set and is given by:

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_k^{(i)})^2}$$

where $w_k^{(i)}$ are the normalized importance weights.

**Resampling trigger:** When $N_{eff}$ falls below a threshold $N_{th} = \alpha N$ (typically $\alpha = 0.5$), the particles are deemed degenerate and a resampling step is performed.

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark threshold condition.)*

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A ŌĆö Option 1: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö 2024 Scheme Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Modified Particle Filter for GNSS/INS integration. Describe the role of INS mechanization in the prediction step and the role of GNSS likelihood in the update step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the importance weight update equation for the Modified Particle Filter when the proposal distribution is chosen as the INS-predicted prior. Show how the simplified weight reduces to the GNSS likelihood.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Architecture Explanation [7 Marks]

The MPF architecture for GNSS/INS integration consists of **four functional blocks** operating recursively at each epoch $k$:

**Block 1 ŌĆö INS Mechanization (Prediction):**
The strapdown INS equations propagate the navigation state (position, velocity, attitude) using high-rate IMU outputs. Mathematically:

$$\hat{x}_k^{(i)} = f_{INS}(x_{k-1}^{(i)}, \omega_k, a_k) + w_k^{(i)}$$

where $\omega_k, a_k$ are gyroscope and accelerometer readings, and $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,Q_k)$ is process noise derived from IMU Allan-variance parameters.

**Role:** Each particle is an independent INS solution with slightly different bias/orientation assumptions. The cloud spans the INS uncertainty ellipsoid.

**Block 2 ŌĆö GNSS Observation Model:**
GNSS pseudoranges from $M$ satellites form the measurement vector:

$$z_k^{(s)} = \sqrt{(x_{rx}-x_s)^2+(y_{rx}-y_s)^2+(z_{rx}-z_s)^2} + c\,\delta t_r + I + T + \epsilon$$

**Block 3 ŌĆö Likelihood Evaluation and Weight Update:**
For each particle, compute residual $\nu_k^{(i)} = z_k - h(\hat{x}_k^{(i)})$ and the Gaussian likelihood:

$$L^{(i)} = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{M/2}\vert R_k\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\nu_k^{(i)})^T R_k^{-1} \nu_k^{(i)}\right)$$

**Block 4 ŌĆö Resampling and Regularization:**
Triggered when $N_{eff} < 0.5 N$. Apply systematic resampling followed by kernel jitter.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
[IMU 100 Hz] --> [INS Mechanization] --> [Particle Propagation]
                                                       |
                                                       v
[GNSS 1 Hz] --> [Observation h(x)] --> [Likelihood + Weight Update]
                                                       |
                                                       v
                                          [N_eff Check] --> [Resample + Regularize]
                                                       |
                                                       v
                                          [State Estimate + Output]
```

*(4 marks architecture + roles, 3 marks for diagram and flow description.)*

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Derivation of Importance Weight Update [7 Marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö General Importance Sampling Identity:**
Given particles drawn from proposal $q(x_k \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k)$, the unnormalized importance weight is:

$$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})\, p(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Modified Proposal Choice:**
In the MPF, the proposal is chosen as the INS-driven transition:

$$q(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)}, z_k) = p_{INS}(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute:**
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})\, p_{INS}(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})}{p_{INS}(x_k^{(i)} \mid x_{k-1}^{(i)})}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cancellation:**
The INS prior terms cancel exactly in the numerator and denominator:

$$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö GNSS Likelihood Form:**
Inserting the Gaussian likelihood:

$$\boxed{\, \tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{M/2}\vert R_k\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\nu_k^{(i)})^T R_k^{-1} \nu_k^{(i)}\right) \,}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Normalization:**
$$w_k^{(i)} = \frac{\tilde{w}_k^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^{(j)}}$$

**Interpretation:** Because the proposal already matches the prior, the weight update **simplifies to the GNSS likelihood** ŌĆö a major computational saving. This is the central insight the KTU examiner expects.

*(2 marks Step 1, 2 marks Step 2ŌĆō3, 2 marks Step 4ŌĆō5, 1 mark Step 6.)*

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Option 2 (Internal Choice): `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö 2024 Scheme Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **sample impoverishment** in a standard SIR particle filter? Explain why it is especially severe in GNSS/INS integration. Describe how the **Regularized Particle Filter** (a key modification) addresses this issue.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following measurement residuals are observed for 4 particles in a GNSS update (measurement variance $R = 4 \, \text{m}^2$):

| Particle $i$ | Residual $\nu^{(i)}$ (m) | Previous Weight $w_{k-1}^{(i)}$ |
|--------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | +1.0 | 0.25 |
| 2 | ŌłÆ2.0 | 0.25 |
| 3 | +5.0 | 0.25 |
| 4 | +0.5 | 0.25 |

Compute the **new unnormalized weights** $\tilde{w}_k^{(i)}$, the **effective sample size** $N_{eff}$, and determine whether resampling should be triggered ($N = 4$, $\alpha = 0.5$).

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Sample Impoverishment [7 Marks]

**Definition [2 Marks]:**
**Sample impoverishment** is the phenomenon in which, after several resampling steps, all surviving particles become duplicates of a few high-weight ancestors, resulting in a particle cloud that has lost its diversity and represents only a tiny fraction of the state space.

**Why it is severe in GNSS/INS [3 Marks]:**

1. **Tight GNSS likelihood peaks:** When GNSS measurements are precise ($R$ small), the likelihood $\exp(-\nu^T R^{-1} \nu / 2)$ is highly peaked; only particles very close to the true state survive.
2. **High-dimensional state space:** Position + velocity + attitude + biases gives $d \geq 9$, making duplication likely.
3. **Long GNSS outages:** During outages, weights remain roughly uniform; on the next GNSS arrival, a sudden peak causes catastrophic duplication.
4. **Linearization failure:** Standard resampling in tight urban canyons amplifies degeneracy.

**Regularized Particle Filter Solution [2 Marks]:**
The Regularized Particle Filter applies **Gaussian-kernel jitter** after resampling:

$$x_k^{(i),*} = x_k^{(i)} + h \cdot \mathbf{D}\boldsymbol{\eta}^{(i)}, \quad \boldsymbol{\eta}^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$$

with Silverman's bandwidth $h = [4N/((d+2)N)]^{1/(d+4)} \cdot \hat{\sigma}$. This perturbs duplicate particles into a smooth continuous approximation of the posterior, restoring diversity without introducing bias asymptotically.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute unnormalized weight using Gaussian likelihood:**
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\nu^{(i)})^2}{2R}\right), \quad R = 4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate for each particle:**

**Particle 1:** $\nu = +1.0$, $w_{k-1} = 0.25$
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(1)} = 0.25 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{1.0^2}{2 \times 4}\right) = 0.25 \cdot \exp(-0.125) = 0.25 \times 0.8825 = 0.2206$$

**Particle 2:** $\nu = -2.0$, $w_{k-1} = 0.25$
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(2)} = 0.25 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{2.0^2}{2 \times 4}\right) = 0.25 \cdot \exp(-0.5) = 0.25 \times 0.6065 = 0.1516$$

**Particle 3:** $\nu = +5.0$, $w_{k-1} = 0.25$
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(3)} = 0.25 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{5.0^2}{2 \times 4}\right) = 0.25 \cdot \exp(-3.125) = 0.25 \times 0.0439 = 0.0110$$

**Particle 4:** $\nu = +0.5$, $w_{k-1} = 0.25$
$$\tilde{w}_k^{(4)} = 0.25 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.5^2}{2 \times 4}\right) = 0.25 \cdot \exp(-0.03125) = 0.25 \times 0.9692 = 0.2423$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum for normalization:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} \tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = 0.2206 + 0.1516 + 0.0110 + 0.2423 = 0.6255$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Normalize:**
$$w_k^{(1)} = 0.3527, \quad w_k^{(2)} = 0.2424, \quad w_k^{(3)} = 0.0176, \quad w_k^{(4)} = 0.3874$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute effective sample size:**
$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{4} (w_k^{(i)})^2} = \frac{1}{(0.3527)^2 + (0.2424)^2 + (0.0176)^2 + (0.3874)^2}$$

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.1244 + 0.0588 + 0.0003 + 0.1501} = \frac{1}{0.3336} \approx 2.997$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Resampling decision:**
Threshold: $N_{th} = \alpha N = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0$
Since $N_{eff} = 2.997 > 2.0$, **resampling is NOT triggered** at this epoch.

**Conclusion:** Particle 3 has effectively zero weight (an outlier candidate); particles 1 and 4 dominate. The filter has identified the consensus without needing resampling ŌĆö a healthy MPF behavior.

*(1 mark each particle computation = 4 marks, 1 mark sum/normalization, 1 mark $N_{eff}$, 1 mark resampling decision.)*

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in MPF/GNSS-INS Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the weights** ŌĆö examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks when $\sum w_k^{(i)} \neq 1$.
> 2. **Confusing $N_{eff}$ formula** ŌĆö many students write $N_{eff} = \sum (w_k^{(i)})^2$ (wrong). Correct is $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum (w_k^{(i)})^2$.
> 3. **Using prior as proposal** in derivation without stating the *modification* ŌĆö examiners want explicit mention that proposal is **INS-conditioned**.
> 4. **Wrong bandwidth formula** ŌĆö Silverman's $h_{opt}$ depends on dimension $d$. Students often write a generic constant and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing a labeled block diagram** ŌĆö for 7-mark architecture questions, a diagram carries **at least 2 marks** by KTU valuation key.
> 6. **Missing the GNSS likelihood cancellation** in derivation ŌĆö the beauty of MPF is that the prior terms cancel; missing this loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Numerical errors in $\exp(-\nu^2 / 2R)$** ŌĆö keep at least 4 decimal accuracy; KTU examiners check.
