## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Jammer Effective Range (J/S Link Budget)

We derive the radius around a jammer at which the **Jammer-to-Signal ratio (J/S)** equals the receiver's **jamming margin** ($J/S_{req}$). Beyond this radius, the GNSS signal is recoverable.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the received signal power at the victim receiver from the satellite:**

$$P_{r,sat} = \dfrac{P_t G_{st} G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 R_s^2}$$

where $P_t$ is satellite EIRP, $G_{st}$ is satellite antenna gain, $G_r$ is victim receiver antenna gain, and $R_s$ is the slant range (Ōēł 20,200 km for MEO).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the received jammer power at the victim receiver:**

$$P_{r,jam} = \dfrac{P_j G_{jt} G_{rj} \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 R_j^2}$$

where $P_j$ is jammer transmit power, $G_{jt}$ is jammer antenna gain, $G_{rj}$ is victim antenna gain *toward the jammer*, and $R_j$ is the jammer-to-receiver distance.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the J/S ratio by dividing Step 2 by Step 1:**

$$\dfrac{J}{S} = \dfrac{P_j G_{jt} G_{rj}}{P_t G_{st} G_r}\cdot\left(\dfrac{R_s}{R_j}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to logarithmic (dB) form for engineering use:**

$$(J/S)_{dB} = P_j + G_{jt} + G_{rj} - P_t - G_{st} - G_r + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{R_s}{R_j}\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for the *critical* $R_j$ at which $(J/S)_{dB} = (J/S)_{req}$:**

$$R_j = R_s \sqrt{\dfrac{P_j G_{jt} G_{rj}}{P_t G_{st} G_r}\cdot 10^{-\,(J/S)_{req}/10}}$$

**Conversion logic:** When the J/S in dB equals the receiver's required jamming margin (typically 25ŌĆō40 dB for civilian GPS with spread-spectrum processing gain), the signal collapses into noise.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Jammer Effective Range

**Problem:** A ground robot receives GPS L1 from a satellite at $R_s = 20{,}200$ km. Satellite EIRP $P_t G_{st} = 26.8$ dBW. The jammer transmits $P_j = 5$ W (= 7 dBW) with $G_{jt} = 6$ dBi. Receiver gain toward satellite $G_r = 0$ dBi, toward jammer $G_{rj} = 0$ dBi. Required J/S margin = 30 dB. Find the jammer's effective radius.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert all values to linear/dB consistently:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t G_{st} &= 26.8 \text{ dBW} \\
P_j G_{jt} &= 7 + 6 = 13 \text{ dBW} \\
G_r = G_{rj} &= 0 \text{ dBi} \\
R_s &= 2.02 \times 10^7 \text{ m} \\
(J/S)_{req} &= 30 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the derived formula:**

$$R_j = R_s \cdot 10^{\frac{(P_j+G_{jt}+G_{rj}) - (P_t+G_{st}+G_r) - (J/S)_{req}}{20}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the exponent:**

$$\dfrac{13 - 26.8 - 0 - 30}{20} = \dfrac{-43.8}{20} = -2.19$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the radius:**

$$R_j = 2.02 \times 10^7 \times 10^{-2.19} = 2.02 \times 10^7 \times 6.46 \times 10^{-3} \approx 130{,}500 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** A mere **5 W jammer can deny GPS lock to a robot within ~130 km** ŌĆö a sobering figure for navigation security.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Ionospheric Range Error and Dual-Frequency Correction

The ionospheric group delay for a signal at carrier frequency $f$ is:

$$\Delta R_{iono}(f) = \dfrac{40.3}{f^2}\int N_e \, ds = \dfrac{K}{f^2}$$

where $K$ is the **Total Electron Content (TEC)** along the ray path times 40.3. For a single-frequency receiver, this is the dominant error source.

For **dual-frequency** receivers (e.g., L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz), the ionosphere-free linear combination is:

$$R_{iono-free} = \dfrac{f_1^2 R_1 - f_2^2 R_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$$

**Step-by-step logic:** The L1 pseudo-range $R_1$ contains delay $K/f_1^2$, and $R_2$ contains $K/f_2^2$. Subtracting eliminates $K$ exactly.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Threat Severity for a Mobile Robot

| Threat | Frequency of Occurrence | Severity (1ŌĆō5) | Detectability | Best Counter-Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Multipath | Very High | 2 | Easy (SNR monitoring) | Choke-ring antenna, RAIM |
| Ionospheric Delay | Medium | 3 | Moderate (Klobuchar model) | Dual-frequency GNSS |
| Signal Blockage | High (urban) | 4 | Trivial (no satellites) | IMU/LiDAR/Visual SLAM fusion |
| Narrowband Jamming | Medium | 4 | Easy (spectrum scan) | Adaptive notch filter |
| Broadband Jamming | Low | 5 | Easy | CRPA antenna, INS aiding |
| Spoofing (Simple) | Low | 5 | Moderate (power check) | Cryptographic authentication (Galileo OS-NMA) |
| Spoofing (Sophisticated) | Very Low | 5 | Hard | Multi-sensor cross-check |
| Meaconing | Low | 4 | Easy (time-tag check) | Time-of-arrival consistency |
| Solar Radio Burst | Rare | 5 | Easy (NOAA alerts) | Wait + INS holdover |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that *spoofing* is fundamentally more dangerous than *jamming* for a robot, because jamming is obvious (the robot loses fix), while spoofing silently corrupts the position estimate and the robot may not trigger any safety fallback.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between GNSS jamming and GNSS spoofing. Which is more dangerous for an autonomous robot, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Jamming | Spoofing |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Transmission of high-power RF noise to deny GNSS signal reception | Transmission of counterfeit GNSS signals to deceive the receiver |
| Effect on Robot | Complete loss of position fix (obvious) | Receiver computes a *wrong* position silently |
| Detection | Easy (C/NŌéĆ drops) | Difficult (signal appears valid) |
| Robot Response | Falls back to INS/SLAM | Robot may continue on attacker-chosen path |

**Spoofing is more dangerous** because the robot remains unaware of the corruption, leading to silent mission failure. (3 marks)

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**What is multipath error in GNSS, and how does a choke-ring antenna mitigate it?**

**Model Answer:**
Multipath error occurs when the GNSS signal arrives at the receiver via multiple paths due to reflection from ground or nearby structures, causing pseudo-range error of 1ŌĆō100 m. A **choke-ring antenna** uses concentric metallic rings (depth Ōēł $\lambda$/4) to create a ground plane that rejects signals arriving from below the horizon (low elevation), thereby suppressing the dominant multipath contributors. (3 marks)

#### Ō£” Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 4, Q Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major GNSS threats. With a neat block diagram, explain the resilient localization architecture of an outdoor mobile robot that must operate under jamming and spoofing conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **effective radius of a GNSS jammer** in terms of satellite EIRP, jammer power, antenna gains, and required J/S margin. A ground robot is at a range of 20,200 km from a GPS satellite with satellite EIRP = 26.8 dBW. A jammer of 10 W with antenna gain 8 dBi is in the vicinity. Receiver gains are 0 dBi toward both satellite and jammer. Required J/S margin = 30 dB. Calculate the jammer's effective range.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of GNSS Threats** (4 marks for classification, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for mitigation):

1. **Unintentional:** Multipath, ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, signal blockage.
2. **Intentional:** Jamming (CW, narrowband, broadband), spoofing, meaconing.
3. **Natural:** Solar radio bursts, geomagnetic storms, ionospheric scintillation.
4. **System-level:** Clock anomalies, ephemeris errors, ground-segment cyber attacks.

**Resilient Localization Architecture (Block Diagram):**

```
[GNSS Rx]  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
[IMU    ]  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöż
[Wheel  ]  ŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [EKF / UKF Fusion Filter] ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [Robot Pose]
[LiDAR  ]  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöż            Ō¢▓
[Camera ]  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöś            Ōöé
                          Ōöé
                  [Threat Monitor: C/NŌéĆ, RAIM, IMU-GNSS residual]
                          Ōöé
                          Ō¢╝
                  [Degraded Mode Controller]
                  INS-only / SLAM-only / STOP
```

The **threat monitor** continuously evaluates receiver health. If C/NŌéĆ < 28 dB-Hz for multiple satellites, or the **IMU-GNSS innovation residual** in the EKF exceeds 3Žā for > 2 seconds, the controller transitions the robot into **degraded mode** (INS-odometry aided by LiDAR/visual SLAM) and reduces its maximum velocity by 50% as a safety margin.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation** (4 marks for derivation, 3 marks for numerical):

From the link-budget derivation in Section 3.1:

$$R_j = R_s \cdot 10^{\frac{(P_j+G_{jt}+G_{rj}) - (P_t+G_{st}+G_r) - (J/S)_{req}}{20}}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_j + G_{jt} &= 10\log_{10}(10) + 8 = 10 + 8 = 18 \text{ dBW} \\
P_t + G_{st} &= 26.8 \text{ dBW} \\
G_r = G_{rj} &= 0 \text{ dBi} \\
R_s &= 2.02 \times 10^7 \text{ m} \\
(J/S)_{req} &= 30 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Exponent evaluation:**

$$\dfrac{18 - 26.8 - 0 - 30}{20} = \dfrac{-38.8}{20} = -1.94$$

**Final calculation:**

$$R_j = 2.02 \times 10^7 \times 10^{-1.94} = 2.02 \times 10^7 \times 1.148 \times 10^{-2} \approx 231{,}900 \text{ m} \approx 232 \text{ km}$$

**Answer:** The 10 W jammer can deny GPS reception within a radius of approximately **232 km** around the robot.

#### Ō£” Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 4, Q Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four main categories of GNSS threats with suitable examples. Discuss why GNSS is inherently vulnerable to low-power jamming, citing the relevant power levels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With mathematical expressions, explain **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)** and the **Position Dilution of Precision (PDOP)**. How does a robot use these metrics to validate GNSS fixes?

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Four GNSS Threat Categories** (5 marks for explanation, 2 marks for power-level justification):

1. **Unintentional Interference:** Multipath (1ŌĆō100 m error), ionospheric delay (5ŌĆō50 m), signal blockage in urban canyons.
2. **Intentional Jamming:** Continuous-wave, narrowband, broadband, or swept jamming. Example: a 1 W personal privacy device can jam GPS within 10ŌĆō20 km.
3. **Spoofing & Meaconing:** Adversary transmits counterfeit signals; meaconer rebroadcasts delayed real signals.
4. **Space-Weather & System Threats:** Solar radio bursts (can elevate noise floor by 20ŌĆō40 dB), satellite clock anomalies, ground-segment cyber attacks.

**Why GNSS is Inherently Vulnerable (Power-Level Argument):**

- Authentic GPS L1 signal at receiver: **ŌłÆ160 dBW** (= $10^{-16}$ W).
- Thermal noise floor in 20 MHz bandwidth: **Ōēł ŌłÆ101 dBm** (= $7.9 \times 10^{-14}$ W).
- The signal sits **~30 dB below** the noise floor.
- The receiver recovers it only because of the **43 dB** spread-spectrum processing gain from the C/A code correlation.
- Therefore, a jammer transmitting only **ŌłÆ130 dBm** (10Ōü╗┬╣ŌüČ W) at the antenna ŌĆö i.e., a milliwatt-scale source ŌĆö can overpower the satellite signal once the processing gain is overcome.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**PDOP Derivation (4 marks for math, 3 marks for robot logic):**

The position estimate is:

$$\mathbf{x} = (G^T G)^{-1} G^T \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

where $G$ is the **geometry matrix** built from line-of-sight unit vectors to each satellite, and $\boldsymbol{\rho}$ is the pseudo-range vector.

The covariance of the position estimate is:

$$\text{cov}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma_{URE}^2 (G^T G)^{-1} = \sigma_{URE}^2 H$$

The **PDOP** is defined as:

$$\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{H_{11} + H_{22} + H_{33}}$$

where $H_{ii}$ are the diagonal elements of $H = (G^T G)^{-1}$. PDOP < 3 indicates good geometry; PDOP > 6 is unusable for robotics.

**RAIM Test Statistic:**

Given $n$ satellites and 4 unknowns ($x, y, z, t_b$), the sum of squared residuals is:

$$\text{SSE} = \boldsymbol{\rho}^T (I - G(G^T G)^{-1} G^T) \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

The detection statistic is:

$$T = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSE}}{\lambda_1}}$$

where $\lambda_1$ is the smallest eigenvalue of the residual projector. A satellite-fault is declared if $T > T_{thresh}$ (Ōēł 5.33 for false-alarm probability $10^{-6}$).

**Robot Validation Logic:**

1. Compute PDOP from current satellite geometry; reject fixes with PDOP > 4.
2. Run RAIM FDE (Fault Detection & Exclusion) to identify and remove a faulty satellite.
3. Cross-check the GNSS fix with the EKF prediction from IMU/odometry.
4. If innovation residual > 3Žā for > 2 s, declare threat and switch to degraded mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** write the formula $R_j$ using `|` notation in your answer sheet; use the explicit exponential form to avoid sign errors.
> - **Do not** confuse **PDOP** (geometry-driven) with **$\sigma_{URE}$** (signal-driven); examiners specifically test whether you know that $\sigma_{pos} = \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{URE}$.
> - **Do not** describe spoofing as "another form of jamming" ŌĆö it is a fundamentally different threat (deception vs. denial).
> - In 14-mark answers, **always** include the block diagram for resilient localization; the architecture carries **at least 2ŌĆō3 marks** by itself.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **GNSS = GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + IRNSS/NavIC + BeiDou.** All share the same fundamental vulnerability: signal is **~30 dB below thermal noise**.
- [ ] **Four threat families:** Unintentional, Intentional (Jamming), Spoofing/Meaconing, Space-Weather/Systemic.
- [ ] **Spoofing > Jamming in danger level** because the receiver is silently deceived.
- [ ] **Multipath** is the most common threat; mitigated by **choke-ring antennas** and **RAIM**.
- [ ] **Jammer effective range** scales as $\sqrt{P_j / P_t}$ ŌĆö a 5 W jammer denies GPS within ~130 km.
- [ ] **PDOP** quantifies satellite geometry: < 3 good, > 6 unacceptable.
- [ ] **$\sigma_{pos} = \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{URE}$** is the central equation for GNSS accuracy.
- [ ] **Resilient localization** = GNSS + IMU + Odometry + LiDAR/Camera + Threat Monitor + Degraded-Mode Controller.
- [ ] **Dual-frequency GNSS** eliminates first-order ionospheric delay using the ionosphere-free linear combination.
- [ ] **RAIM** provides autonomous fault detection using the **sum of squared residuals (SSE)** test statistic.
- [ ] Standard metrics to memorize: GPS L1 = **1575.42 MHz**, $\lambda \approx$ **0.19 m**, min received power = **ŌłÆ160 dBW**.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, always pair the **threat classification** with the **resilient architecture block diagram** for full marks.
